### [1] [EDEN: A Large-Scale Corpus of Clinical Notes for Italian](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12569)
*Tiziano Labruna,Guido Bertolini,Pietro Ferrazzi,Bernardo Magnini*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: EDEN is a large-scale, anonymized Italian emergency department clinical notes corpus (~4M notes) with a manually annotated subset (~6K notes) using a structured CRF of 132 items. It introduces CRF-filling as a new structured IE benchmark and provides zero-shot baselines with Gemma-27B and MedGemma-27B.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To fill a critical gap in freely available Italian-language clinical data that can support the development and application of Large Language Models in concrete medical settings, particularly for structured information extraction from emergency department notes.

Method: The authors constructed a corpus of ~4M anonymized clinical notes from Italian EDs via an on-site anonymisation pipeline. A subset of ~6K notes was manually annotated by multiple clinicians using a structured Case Report Form (CRF) with 132 items covering dyspnea and loss of consciousness. The annotation underwent iterative revision to resolve ambiguities. The CRF items span numerical, categorical, binary, and mixed types. Zero-shot baselines were established using Gemma-27B and MedGemma-27B on the CRF-filling task.

Result: The EDEN corpus is the largest freely available Italian clinical notes corpus. The annotated subset is richly structured but highly imbalanced. Zero-shot CRF-filling baselines were provided using Gemma-27B and MedGemma-27B, establishing initial performance benchmarks.

Conclusion: EDEN addresses a major resource gap for Italian clinical NLP. The CRF-filling task proposed serves as a novel structured information extraction benchmark. The dataset enables both development and evaluation of LLMs for real-world emergency medicine applications.

Abstract: We present EDEN (Emergency Department Electronic Notes), a new and unique large-scale corpus of clinical notes produced in Emergency Departments of Italian hospitals. The corpus, in its current version, is composed of approximately 4 million clinical notes fully anonymized, covering diverse phases of patient care during the stay in the emergency department. In addition, a subset of about six thousand notes has been manually annotated by clinical experts through a structured Case Report Form (CRF) containing 132 items relevant for two patient situations in emergency departments, dyspnea and loss of consciousness. Items may assume numerical values (e.g., for blood saturation), categorical (e.g., for level of consciousness ), binary (e.g., for presence of traumas), and mixed value types. The annotation process involved multiple clinicians and underwent iterative revision to resolve ambiguities in item formulation, resulting in a richly structured (although high imbalanced) resource. The dataset aims to fill a relevant gap of data able to support both the development and the use of Large Language Models in concrete medical applications. We describe the data collection protocol, the on-site anonymisation pipeline, corpus statistics, and the annotation scheme. Finally, we propose CRF-filling as a novel structured information extraction benchmark, and provide zero-shot baseline resulting from Gemma-27B and MedGemma-27B. To the best of our knowledge, the EDEN dataset is the largest freely available corpus of clinical notes existing for the Italian language.

</details>


### [2] [Helping Figures Tell their Story! Paper-Grounded Video Generation Explaining Complex Scientific Figures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12576)
*Ishani Mondal,Javad Baghirov,Jordan Boyd-Graber*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces paper-grounded figure-to-video generation — creating narrated walkthrough videos from scientific figures and their source papers. The authors propose MINARD, a pipeline that generates paper-faithful narrations and sequentially grounds them to figure regions, and release FigTalk, a benchmark with novel sequential and component-level grounding metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current video generation systems and benchmarks lack the capability to produce paper-grounded, step-by-step narrations aligned with visual highlights over scientific figures. Understanding scientific figures requires narration that references the paper and sequentially directs attention to relevant figure regions — a gap the authors aim to fill.

Method: The authors propose MINARD (Multimodal Interpretation of Narrated Architecture via Region Decomposition), a pipeline that takes a scientific figure and its accompanying paper as input. MINARD generates paper-grounded narrations and then sequentially grounds those narration segments to specific figure regions. They also construct FigTalk, a benchmark dataset, and derive new evaluation metrics for sequential and component-level grounding.

Result: On the FigTalk benchmark, MINARD produces narrations that are humanlike and faithful to the source paper. It also outperforms existing approaches on narration-conditioned figure spatial grounding, as demonstrated through both automatic metrics and human evaluation.

Conclusion: MINARD successfully addresses the gap in paper-grounded figure-to-video generation, delivering narrations and region grounding that are both humanlike and paper-faithful. FigTalk and its derived metrics provide a standardized framework for evaluating this emerging task.

Abstract: Scientific figures compress complex pipelines into a single canvas, yet understanding them requires paper-grounded, step-by-step narration aligned with visual highlights a capability missing from current video generation systems and benchmarks. To address this, we introduce paper-grounded figure-to-video generation: generating narrated, region-grounded walkthrough videos from a figure and its paper. We propose MINARD (Multimodal Interpretation of Narrated Architecture via Region Decomposition), a pipeline that generates paper-grounded narrations and sequentially grounds them to figure regions. We also release FigTalk, a benchmark with new sequential and component-level grounding metrics derived. On FigTalk, MINARD generates humanlike, paper-faithful narrations and outperforms narration-conditioned figure spatial grounding compared to existing approaches in both automatic and human evaluation

</details>


### [3] [MARD: Mirror-Augmented Reasoning Distillation for Mechanism-Level Drug-Drug Interaction Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12578)
*Mohammadreza Riyazat,Vian Lelo,Rameen Jafri,Yumna Khan,Abeer Badawi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A 7B-parameter model (MARD) for mechanism-level drug-drug interaction prediction is introduced, combining structured taxonomy, leakage-safe evaluation, and novel training techniques. It outperforms GPT-4o and 31 other systems, exhibits anti-memorization behavior, and costs ~1% of frontier API pricing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing DDI prediction treats the task as flat binary/interaction-type classification, ignoring the mechanistic axes (which enzyme/pharmacodynamic target), directionality, and evidence provenance. There is also a need for reproducible, leakage-safe evaluation protocols and auditable reasoning metrics.

Method: The authors introduce: (1) a 7-family/147-subtype structured DDI taxonomy; (2) leakage-safe cold-split protocols; (3) Mirror-Augmented Reasoning Distillation (MARD) for a 7B model with three innovations — single-token KL divergence on direction tags, per-loss PRM-weighted DPO with programmatic hard negatives, and a mechanism-aware retrieval channel; (4) process-reward step labels automatically verified against DrugBank fields (no human/LLM judges).

Result: On the April-2026 DrugBank release, MARD-7B is the only system among 32 whose accuracy survives drug-pair novelty, beating the best baseline by +13.9 pp and GPT-4o by +6.7 pp. An anti-memorisation signature shows accuracy improving on rarely seen drugs, indicating structured pharmacological reasoning rather than frequency-based memorization.

Conclusion: Mechanism-level DDI prediction with MARD-7B demonstrates that structured reasoning distillation with verifiable process rewards can outperform large frontier models at a fraction of the cost, while avoiding memorization pitfalls via genuine pharmacological reasoning. The authors release all data, the DDI-PRM, retrieval index, and training code.

Abstract: Mechanism-level drug-drug interaction (DDI) prediction requires identifying which enzyme or pharmacodynamic axis is implicated, in which direction, and with which evidence -- not merely whether two drugs interact. We introduce a reproducible mechanism-level DDI labelling and evaluation protocol with a structured 7-family/147-subtype taxonomy, leakage-safe cold-split protocols, and auditable reasoning metrics for evaluating pharmacological prediction beyond flat interaction classification. We propose a pipeline that produces a 7B reasoning MARD (Mirror-Augmented Reasoning Distillation), combining three training innovations: a single-token KL divergence on direction tag that ties the model's prediction, per-loss PRM-weighted DPO with programmatic hard negatives, and a leakage-safe mechanism-aware retrieval channel. Process-reward step labels are automatically verifiable against DrugBank-structured fields, requiring no human or LLM judges. On the April-2026 DrugBank release, our MARD-7B is the only system in a 32-system comparison whose accuracy survives drug-pair novelty, beating the best baseline by +13.9 pp and GPT-4o by +6.7 pp at ~1% of frontier API cost. Further analysis reveals an anti-memorisation signature where accuracy improves on rarely seen drugs, suggesting that gain comes from structured pharmacological reasoning rather than drug-frequency memorisation. We release corpus, DDI-PRM, retrieval index, and training code.

</details>


### [4] [Constrained Semantic Decompression in LLMs through Persian Proverb-Conditioned Story Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12599)
*Zahra Habibzadeh,Paria Khoshtab,Amir Mesbah,Yadollah Yaghoobzadeh*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper frames proverb-to-story generation as "constrained semantic decompression," introduces a Persian proverb-story dataset (PAND), and reveals that LLMs exhibit a "decompression gap" — fluent but morally unfaithful narratives — which explicit reasoning partially mitigates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Proverbs compress rich cultural and moral knowledge into dense, abstract forms. Transforming them into faithful narratives requires deep semantic grounding that current LLMs may lack. The authors aim to systematically study this abstraction-to-realization challenge using proverb-conditioned story generation as a testbed.

Method: The authors construct PAND (Proverb Aligned Narrative Dataset), a Persian dataset pairing proverbs with human-written stories and explicit meanings. They evaluate LLM performance using a hybrid framework combining human-calibrated LLM-as-a-Judge with structural metrics, and test multiple prompting regimes including explicit reasoning and iterative refinement.

Result: A persistent "decompression gap" is identified: LLMs produce surface-level fluency but fail to faithfully instantiate the moral and causal structure of proverbs. Explicit reasoning and iterative refinement partially mitigate these failures, indicating errors stem from difficulty translating abstract meaning into narrative form rather than a lack of relevant knowledge.

Conclusion: The constrained semantic decompression task reveals fundamental limitations in how LLMs handle culturally compressed knowledge. The task naturally extends to other forms of compressed cultural knowledge beyond Persian proverbs.

Abstract: Transforming a dense, abstract proverb into an engaging and morally faithful narrative requires deep cultural understanding and robust semantic grounding. We frame this problem as a \emph{constrained semantic decompression} task and study proverb-conditioned story generation as a testbed for abstraction-to-realization in large language models (LLMs). Focusing on Persian, we introduce the Proverb Aligned Narrative Dataset (PAND), pairing proverbs with human-written stories and explicit meanings. By a hybrid evaluation framework that combines human-calibrated LLM-as-a-Judge with structural metrics, we analyze model behavior across multiple prompting regimes. Our findings reveal a persistent \emph{decompression gap}: current LLMs often achieve strong surface-level fluency while failing to faithfully instantiate the underlying moral and causal structure encoded in proverbs. We further show that explicit reasoning and iterative refinement can partially mitigate these failures, suggesting that many decompression errors arise from difficulties in translating abstract meaning into narrative form rather than a complete lack of relevant knowledge. Our proposed task naturally extends to other forms of compressed cultural knowledge.

</details>


### [5] [Shopping Reasoning Bench: An Expert-Authored Benchmark for Multi-Turn Conversational Shopping Assistants](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12608)
*Shuxian Fan,Seonwoo Min,Youna Hu,Botao Xia,Jayakrishnan Unnikrishnan,Rowan Musselmann,Yifan Gao,Qingyu Yin,Priyanka Nigam,Bing Yin*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Shopping Reasoning Bench is an expert-authored benchmark of 525 shopping missions with 10,863 importance-weighted binary rubrics. Evaluation of nine models (GPT, Claude, Gemini) shows pass rates of only 57–77%, with multi-turn performance degrading 4–18 points as conversations progress, revealing that current models fall short of expert-level shopping advice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: No existing benchmark jointly evaluates the open-ended multi-turn reasoning, domain expertise, and criterion-level quality required by real-world shopping conversations. Current e-commerce and general-purpose benchmarks lack the subjective preference balancing, budget constraint handling, and cross-product trade-off reasoning essential to shopping assistance.

Method: The authors construct Shopping Reasoning Bench, comprising 525 missions (232 single-turn, 293 multi-turn) with 10,863 importance-weighted binary rubrics authored by retail domain experts. Criteria are organized under a taxonomy of five reasoning categories and fifteen subcategories (e.g., preference refinement, trade-off analysis, compatibility assessment). Nine models across three families (GPT, Claude, Gemini) are evaluated against these rubrics.

Result: Overall pass rates across all models reach only 57–77%. On multi-turn missions, all models score 13–29 points lower on optional "above-and-beyond" criteria than on required ones. Performance degrades 4–18 points as conversations progress through turns, indicating that while models can handle basic shopping assistance, they fail to deliver expert-level advisory quality.

Conclusion: Shopping Reasoning Bench exposes a significant gap between current model capabilities and the demands of expert shopping assistance, particularly in multi-turn settings requiring sustained reasoning about preferences, trade-offs, and compatibility. It provides a challenging, domain-specific testbed to drive future development of shopping assistants.

Abstract: Conversational shopping assistants now serve hundreds of millions of customers, yet no existing benchmark jointly evaluates the open-ended multi-turn reasoning, domain expertise, and criterion-level quality that real shopping conversations demand. Shopping reasoning is unique among language model applications. Unlike factual question answering or verifiable code generation, it requires balancing subjective preferences, budget constraints, and cross-product trade-offs across multi-turn dialogue, capabilities absent from previous e-commerce and general-purpose benchmarks. We introduce the Shopping Reasoning Bench, an expert-authored benchmark of 525 missions (232 single-turn, 293 multi-turn) with 10863 importance-weighted binary rubrics authored by retail domain experts. These criteria are organized under a taxonomy of five reasoning categories and fifteen subcategories covering diverse demands such as preference refinement, trade-off analysis, and compatibility assessment. An evaluation of nine models across three families (GPT, Claude, Gemini) shows that pass rates reach only 57--77% overall. On multi-turn missions, all models score 13--29 points lower on optional above-and-beyond criteria than on required ones, and performance degrades 4--18 points as conversations progress. These gaps show that current models handle basic shopping assistance but fall short of expert-level advice, making Shopping Reasoning Bench a challenging testbed for future shopping assistant development.

</details>


### [6] [MentalMARBERT: Domain-Adaptive Pre-training and Two-Stage Fine-Tuning for Arabic Mental Health Disorders Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12649)
*Fatimah Almalki,Areej Alhothali,Lulwah Alharigy,Abdulrahman Aladeem*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper presents a two-phase framework for detecting mental health disorders from Arabic social media text using domain-adapted pretrained language models, with a hierarchical two-stage classifier achieving a macro-F1 of 0.861 on a newly constructed annotated dataset of 50,670 tweets spanning six categories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Arabic mental health NLP lags behind English due to dialectal variation, informal language, scarce annotated resources, and severe class imbalance. Multi-class disorder classification for Arabic remains underexplored, motivating the need for robust domain adaptation and classification architectures tailored to this language.

Method: A two-phase framework: Phase 1 applies Domain-Adaptive Pretraining (DAPT) and Task-Adaptive Pretraining (TAPT) to three Arabic PLMs (AraBERT, CAMeLBERT, MARBERT) on a large unlabeled Arabic mental health tweet corpus, evaluated to select the best backbone (MentalMARBERT). Phase 2 compares four configurations: single-stage vs. hierarchical two-stage classification, each combined with full fine-tuning or LoRA. A new annotated dataset of 50,670 Arabic tweets across six mental health categories is introduced (Krippendorff's Alpha = 0.733).

Result: Domain-adapted MARBERT (MentalMARBERT) significantly outperforms baselines in accuracy and macro-F1. The hierarchical two-stage architecture with full fine-tuning yields the best results: macro-F1 of 0.861 and accuracy of 0.877.

Conclusion: Domain-specific adaptive pretraining and hierarchical classification are highly effective for Arabic mental health disorder detection, demonstrating that combining DAPT/TAPT with a two-stage architecture substantially improves performance over standard approaches.

Abstract: Detecting mental health disorders from Arabic social media text remains challenging due to dialectal variation, informal language, limited high-quality annotated resources, and severe class imbalance. While English mental health natural language processing (NLP) has progressed substantially, Arabic multi-class disorder classification remains insufficiently studied. This study proposes a two-phase framework for Arabic mental health text classification. In phase 1, three Arabic pre-trained language models, AraBERT, CAMeLBERT, and MARBERT, undergo Domain-Adaptive and Task-Adaptive Pretraining (DAPT and TAPT) using a large-scale corpus of unlabeled Arabic mental health tweets. The adapted models are evaluated under a unified protocol to identify the most effective backbone model. In phase 2, the selected model is assessed across four configurations combining single-stage and hierarchical two-stage classification architectures with full fine-tuning and Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA). To support this study, we constructed a novel annotated Arabic mental health dataset comprising 50,670 tweets across six categories, with strong inter annotator agreement (Krippendorff's Alpha = 0.733, average pairwise agreement = 0.797). Experimental results show that the domain-adapted MARBERT (MentalMARBERT) achieves statistically significant improvements over baseline models in both accuracy and macro-F1. The hierarchical two-stage architecture combined with full fine-tuning achieves the best overall performance, reaching a macro-F1 of 0.861 and an accuracy of 0.877. These findings demonstrate the effectiveness of domain-specific adaptive pretraining and hierarchical classification for Arabic mental health disorder detection.

</details>


### [7] [Observable Patterns Are Not Explanations: A Causal-Geometric Analysis of Latent Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12689)
*Darpan Aswal,Thomas Palmeira Ferraz,Yongxin Zhou,Maxime Peyrard*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper critically evaluates claims about latent reasoning in LRMs (Coconut and CODI). The authors show that observable latent-state patterns (BFS frontiers, decodable arithmetic) also appear in control models lacking the proposed recurrence or curriculum, and do not always causally affect behavior. Using causal interventions, they demonstrate that latent-thought utilization is graded rather than binary, with behavioral influence concentrated in low-rank directions exhibiting structured step-to-step geometry. They argue that decodability or attention patterns alone cannot establish mechanism, advocating for matched controls and causal tests in LRM interpretability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent LRM work treats interpretable latent-state patterns as evidence of internal reasoning mechanisms, but these claims have not been subjected to rigorous causal validation. The authors aim to determine whether such patterns genuinely constitute mechanisms or are merely correlational artifacts.

Method: The authors evaluate two LRMs (Coconut and CODI) alongside carefully constructed control models that lack the proposed recurrence mechanisms or training curricula. They employ: (1) pattern comparison between LRMs and controls to test necessity of the hypothesized mechanisms, (2) causal intervention experiments to assess whether latent thoughts causally affect behavior, measuring graded (rather than binary) utilization, and (3) geometric analysis using low-rank decomposition to characterize how step-to-step latent geometry relates to behavioral influence.

Result: Key findings: (1) BFS-like frontiers and decodable arithmetic computation appear in control models lacking the proposed recurrence/curriculum, undermining claims that these patterns are evidence of specific reasoning mechanisms. (2) Causal interventions show latent-thought utilization is graded, not binary — a thought's influence scales with its causal effect on behavior. (3) Behavioral influence concentrates in low-rank directions, and the step-to-step geometry of these directions becomes increasingly structured as their behavioral influence grows.

Conclusion: Latent thoughts should be conceptualized as hidden computation, not hidden explanation. Neither decodability, attention patterns, nor static structural properties alone can establish mechanistic claims about latent reasoning. Rigorous LRM interpretability requires matched control models and causal intervention tests to distinguish genuine mechanisms from correlational artifacts.

Abstract: Latent reasoning models (LRMs) replace explicit chain-of-thought with continuous thoughts. Recent work treats observable latent-state patterns, such as BFS-like frontiers and decodable arithmetic computation, as evidence for internal reasoning mechanisms. Evaluating two LRMs (Coconut and CODI) against controls lacking the proposed recurrence or curriculum, we find these patterns also appear in the controls and do not always causally affect behavior. Causal interventions reveal that latent-thought utilization is not binary but graded, scaling with a thought's causal effect on model behavior. Geometric analyses reveal this effect concentrates in low-rank directions whose step-to-step geometry grows more structured as their behavioral influence increases. Latent thoughts should therefore be treated as hidden computation, not hidden explanation: decodability, attention, or static structure alone cannot establish mechanism. LRM interpretability thus requires matched controls and causal tests.

</details>


### [8] [AfriSUD: A Dependency Treebank Collection for Evaluating Models on African Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12708)
*Happy Buzaaba,Cheikh Mouhamadou Bamba Dione,David Ifeoluwa Adelani,Sylvain Kahane,Kim Gerdes,Bruno Guillaume,Kevin Guan,Aremu Anuoluwapo,Naome A. Etori,Shamsuddeen Hassan Muhammad,Utitofon Inyang,Peter Nabende,David Sabiiti Bamutura,Andiswa Bukula,Chinedu Uchechukwu,Rooweither Mabuya,Idris Akinade,Christiane Fellbaum*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: AfriSUD introduces the first large-scale, syntactically annotated treebank collection for nine Sub-Saharan African languages using the SUD framework. Despite evaluating various models, results reveal a persistent syntax gap, indicating current architectures inadequately capture African-language syntactic diversity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: African languages are linguistically diverse and globally significant, yet severely underrepresented in NLP resources and research. No prior large-scale syntactically annotated treebanks exist across multiple African language families, creating a resource gap that hinders both linguistic understanding and NLP development for these languages.

Method: The authors introduce AfriSUD, a community-led, native-speaker-verified collection of Surface-Syntactic Universal Dependencies (SUD) treebanks covering nine African languages from major families across Sub-Saharan Africa. The annotations capture typological features such as agglutination and tone. They then benchmark a range of models — non-transformer baselines, multilingual pretrained encoders, and LLMs — on part-of-speech tagging and dependency parsing tasks.

Result: All evaluated models exhibit clear limitations across the nine languages, revealing a significant "syntax gap." Existing model architectures, including state-of-the-art multilingual encoders and LLMs, fail to adequately handle the structural diversity of African-language syntax.

Conclusion: AfriSUD provides a critical resource for African NLP, but the persistent syntax gap underscores that current modeling paradigms are insufficient for capturing typologically diverse syntactic structures. This calls for new architectures and approaches specifically designed to handle the structural properties of African languages.

Abstract: Despite their linguistic diversity and global significance, African languages remain underrepresented in research and resources to support NLP. We aim to bridge this gap by introducing AfriSUD, the first large-scale collection of syntactically annotated treebanks for nine diverse African languages spanning major language families and regions across Sub-Saharan Africa. Using the Surface-Syntactic Universal Dependencies (SUD) framework, our community-led effort provides high-quality, native-speaker verified data that capture typological key features such as agglutination and tone. We evaluate a range of models on AfriSUD for part-of-speech tagging and dependency parsing including non-transformer baselines, multilingual pretrained encoders, and LLMs. Our results reveal a significant syntax gap, where models still show clear limitations across the nine languages, suggesting that existing architectures may not fully capture the structural diversity of African-language syntax.

</details>


### [9] [How Fine-Grained Should a RAG Benchmark Be? A Hierarchical Framework for Synthetic Question Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12789)
*Chase M. Fensore,Kaustubh Dhole,Jason Fan,Eugene Agichtein,Joyce C. Ho*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: HieraRAG introduces a hierarchical framework to determine optimal granularity for RAG benchmark construction, finding that the ideal categorization level varies by dimension (complexity, answer type, linguistic variation) and introducing a Coherence Ratio metric to validate whether fine-grained splits meaningfully subdivide parent categories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Practitioners lack empirical guidance on which dimensions to vary and at what granularity when constructing RAG evaluation benchmarks, making it difficult to design benchmarks that effectively discriminate between system configurations.

Method: The authors propose HieraRAG, a hierarchical framework that defines optimal granularity as the level maximizing discriminative power (standard deviation of generation quality across categories). They generate 5,872 synthetic QA pairs from FineWeb-10BT across three dimensions (Question Complexity, Answer Type, Linguistic Variation) at three granularity levels (2, 4, and 8 categories), evaluate using a BM25+Falcon-3-10B pipeline, and introduce a Coherence Ratio metric to assess whether fine-grained splits cleanly subdivide parent categories. Human evaluation on 110 stratified QA pairs validates synthetic quality.

Result: Optimal granularity is dimension-dependent: Question Complexity benefits from fine-grained distinctions (discriminative power: 0.053), while Answer Type and Linguistic Variation peak at medium granularity. Coherence Ratios reveal structural differences (Question Complexity: 0.40 vs. Answer Type: 1.44). Human evaluation confirms synthetic QA quality.

Conclusion: HieraRAG provides a portable, empirically-grounded procedure for practitioners to determine appropriate evaluation granularity in their own RAG settings, though the specific numerical findings are tied to the single configuration tested.

Abstract: Evaluating retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems requires benchmarks that capture diverse question characteristics, yet practitioners lack empirical guidance on which dimensions to vary and at what granularity. We present HieraRAG, a hierarchical framework for studying granularity in RAG benchmark construction, defining optimal granularity as the level that maximizes discriminative power (the standard deviation of generation quality across categories) within a given RAG configuration. As a case study, we generate 5,872 synthetic question-answer (QA) pairs from FineWeb-10BT across 3 dimensions (Question Complexity, Answer Type, Linguistic Variation) at 3 granularity levels (2, 4, and 8 categories). With a BM25+Falcon-3-10B pipeline, optimal granularity varies by dimension: complexity benefits from fine-grained distinctions (discriminative power: 0.053) while answer type and linguistic variation peak at medium granularity. We introduce a Coherence Ratio metric to quantify whether fine-grained splits cleanly subdivide parent categories, revealing structural differences across dimensions (Question Complexity: 0.40 vs. Answer Type: 1.44). Human evaluation of 110 stratified QA pairs confirms synthetic quality. While these specific findings reflect a single configuration, HieraRAG provides a portable procedure and validation metric for practitioners to determine evaluation granularity within their own RAG settings.

</details>


### [10] [RogueAI: A Reverse Turing Test for Detecting Licensed AI Deception in Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13310)
*Sara Candussio,Emanuele Ballarin,Lorenzo Bonin,Sandro Junior Della Rovere,Luca Bortolussi*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper presents RogueAI, a "Turing Test 2.0" where humans must identify which of two LLM agents is deceptive, and AutoRogueAI, where players co-design scenarios with a narrator. A pilot study reveals a 75.6% heuristic accuracy vs. 56.6% human accuracy gap, suggesting humans ignore diagnostic linguistic signals of deception.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The original Turing Test's epistemological question — "is this agent human?" — has become obsolete as LLMs pass casual conversational benchmarks. The authors argue the modern variant should ask "can this agent be trusted?" — shifting focus from human-likeness to honesty detection, with implications for AI safety, scalable oversight, and deception research.

Method: RogueAI is structured as a one-on-two interrogation game: a human player questions two indistinguishable LLM agents (one honest, one licensed to deceive) within a shared fictional scenario, and must identify the deceptive agent before a turn budget expires. AutoRogueAI extends this procedurally — a narrator LLM co-designs a custom scenario with the player and secretly adopts its own deception strategy. The pilot deployed the system over three days, collecting 467 initiated sessions (415 completed, 1,876 interaction turns in Italian). A simple heuristic based on differential helpfulness, brevity, and hedging was evaluated against human accuracy.

Result: The deceptive agent carries a reliable linguistic signature — reduced helpfulness, shorter responses, and more hedging — that a simple heuristic exploits at 75.6% accuracy. Human players achieved only 56.6% accuracy, failing to leverage the most diagnostic signal. This 19-percentage-point gap persisted across the pilot deployment.

Conclusion: The gap between heuristic (75.6%) and human (56.6%) accuracy reveals that humans systematically overlook available deception cues, positioning RogueAI as valuable for data collection on human-AI trust dynamics, as a teaching tool for AI literacy, and as an evaluation harness for honesty-trained models. The work contributes to scalable oversight, social-deduction benchmarking, and LLM deception research.

Abstract: The original Turing Test asks a human judge to distinguish a machine from a person through dialogue. Three quarters of a century later, conversational systems pass this test in casual settings; the interesting epistemological question has shifted. We argue that the relevant modern variant asks not whether a dialogue partner is artificial, but whether it can be trusted. We present RogueAI, an interactive webapp that operationalizes this revisited test as a one-on-two interrogation game: a human player questions two indistinguishable Large Language Model agents, knowing that exactly one of them has been licensed to deceive within a shared fictional scenario. The player's task is to identify the deceptive agent and "shut it off" before a turn budget is exhausted. We further introduce AutoRogueAI, a procedural extension in which players co-design a custom scenario with a narrator agent that secretly chooses its own deception strategy. We describe the framing, sketch the abstract architecture and gameplay loop, and situate the artifact within recent work on LLM deception, social-deduction benchmarks, and scalable oversight via debate. A three-day pilot deployment (467 initiated sessions, 415 completed, 1876 interaction turns in Italian) provides early feasibility evidence and surfaces a concrete tension: the deceptive agent carries a reliable, locally-present linguistic signature - differential helpfulness, brevity, hedging - that a simple heuristic exploits at 75.6% accuracy, yet human players achieved only 56.6%, consistent with ignoring the most diagnostic signal entirely. We discuss what this gap implies for the artifact's use as a data-collection vehicle, a teaching tool, and an evaluation harness for honesty-trained models.

</details>


### [11] [Does AI Reviewer See the Full Picture? Attacking and Defending Multimodal Peer Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12716)
*Xinyu Zhao,Rana Muhammad Shahroz Khan,Zhen Xu,Zhen Tan,Tianlong Chen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: PaperGuard introduces a benchmark to evaluate and defend against cross-modal adversarial attacks on AI peer-review systems, where attackers manipulate both text and figures to induce targeted failures (e.g., score inflation). It offers a multimodal dataset, unified attack suite (black-box prompt injection, white-box GCG/PGD), and a chunk-based embedding defense, demonstrating pervasive vulnerability in SOTA models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI peer-review systems using LLMs/MLLMs are vulnerable to adversarial manipulation, yet existing robustness studies are text-only. Scientific papers are inherently multimodal (text + figures), and peer-review attacks are domain-specific and targeted (e.g., inflating scores) rather than general jailbreaking. No practical defenses exist for this cross-modal, domain-specific threat.

Method: PaperGuard is a three-pillar framework: (1) a new multimodal peer-review dataset across multiple scientific domains; (2) a unified attack suite combining black-box prompt injections with white-box perturbations — GCG for text and PGD for figures; (3) a practical defense using chunk-based embedding search to localize and neutralize harmful instructions within long-context academic papers.

Result: Extensive experiments across state-of-the-art models confirm that AI reviewers are pervasively vulnerable to the proposed cross-modal attacks, and the chunk-based embedding defense provides practical mitigation.

Conclusion: PaperGuard establishes the foundational benchmark, protocols, and actionable defense necessary for trustworthy, attack-resilient AI-assisted scholarly reviewing, filling a critical gap in multimodal adversarial robustness for peer-review systems.

Abstract: The integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) and Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) into scientific peer-review workflows introduces novel and significant risks for adversarial manipulation, especially given the multimodal nature of scientific papers where figures, not just text, convey core evidence. This creates a significant gap: current robustness studies on AI peer-review are overwhelmingly text-only. Moreover, the problem is distinct from standard jailbreaking, as a peer-review attack seeks to induce a domain-specific, targeted failure (e.g., "inflate this score") rather than a general safety policy violation, for which no practical defenses exist. To address this, we introduce PaperGuard, the first comprehensive benchmark designed to systematically evaluate and defend AI-generated peer-review against these domain-specific, cross-modal attacks. Our framework is built on three pillars: (1) a new multimodal peer-review dataset spanning multiple scientific domains; (2) a unified suite of attacks, including black-box prompt injections and white-box perturbations, specifically designed to target both text (GCG) and figures (PGD); and (3) a practical defense, motivated by the long-context challenge of academic papers, that uses chunk-based embedding search to efficiently localize and mitigate harmful instructions. Our extensive experiments, conducted across state-of-the-art models, confirm that AI reviewers are pervasively vulnerable. PaperGuard establishes the foundational benchmark, protocols, and actionable defense necessary to pioneer trustworthy, attack-resilient AI-assisted scholarly reviewing.

</details>


### [12] [Agent-based models for the evolution of morphological alternation patterns](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12748)
*Aravinth Kulanthaivelu,Richard Sproat*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper presents a multi-agent simulation modeling how morphological stem and inflection alternations (like "go/went") emerge and spread through populations. It introduces an LLM-based "AI Historical Linguist" to evaluate simulation realism, finding that scale-free networks and random Bernoulli adoption produce more plausible morphologies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Suppletive and irregular morphological alternations (e.g., "go/went") are cross-linguistically common, persistent, yet seemingly non-functional for communication or learning. The authors aim to computationally model how such alternations emerge, spread, and become entrenched through social diffusion, addressing a gap where prior simulations lacked naturalistic lexicons, realistic phonology, and large agent populations.

Method: A multi-agent simulation where agents possess lexicons with hundreds/thousands of entries and realistic phonological rules. When an agent hears a novel form (arising from phonological change or lexical alternatives associated with a subpopulation), they probabilistically adopt it, potentially spreading the form to other paradigm slots. The system supports various network topologies, diffusion patterns, and adoption policies. For evaluation, the authors introduce the "AI Historical Linguist," an LLM-driven system simulating a debate between two historical linguists to assess the plausibility of evolved morphologies against real and disguised morphologies. Three case studies model attested historical changes with counterfactual testing.

Result: Scale-free social networks and random Bernoulli adoption of novel forms produce morphologies judged as more plausible by the AI Historical Linguist, compared to alternative network topologies and adoption policies. The three attested historical change case studies demonstrate the model's capacity to replicate real diachronic developments and explore counterfactual scenarios.

Conclusion: Morphological alternations can emerge and stabilize through social diffusion mechanisms without requiring functional advantages. The combination of multi-agent simulation with LLM-based evaluation offers a novel, scalable methodology for assessing computational models of language change. Scale-free networks and Bernoulli adoption appear to be key factors in producing realistic morphological patterns.

Abstract: Why is the past of English "go" the apparently unrelated "went"? Such alternations are frequent in languages. They neither aid communication nor learnability, yet they can be persistent, surviving over centuries or millennia.
  We present a multi-agent simulation of the emergence of morphological stem and inflection alternations. Alternate forms arise by phonological changes or, as with "go/went", from lexical alternatives associated with a subset of the population. When an agent 'hears' another agent use a novel form for a slot in the paradigm of a word (say, the past tense of go), they will with some probability adopt that form, possibly spreading its use to other slots in the paradigm that shared the same original form. Thus alternative forms can spread through the population and become entrenched as stem or inflectional marker alternants. Unlike many previous computational studies, our system allows for naturalistic lexical forms, realistic phonological rules, lexicons with hundreds or thousands of entries, and agent populations in the tens or hundreds. It supports several network topologies, diffusion patterns and agent adoption policies.
  One issue with such simulations is evaluation: how realistic is the resulting morphology compared to those of real languages? We introduce the AI Historical Linguist, a novel Large Language Model-driven system that models a debate between two historical linguists. We use this to compare a set of real language morphologies, disguised morphologies, and experimentally evolved morphologies. The results suggest that among the factors that favor more plausible morphologies are scale-free social networks and random Bernoulli adoption of forms.
  We also present three case studies modeling attested historical changes, allowing us to test what might have happened if history had been different.
  All code and data are released.

</details>


### [13] [LLMs Can Better Capture Human Judgments--With the Right Prompts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12754)
*Danica Dillion,Chen Cecilia Liu,Baihui Wang,Daniele Barolo,Tanmay Rajore,Niket Tandon,Pranathi Ravikumar,Kurt Gray*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Simple prompting strategies—reporting standard deviations/response proportions and leveraging human confusion ratings—improve LLMs' ability to capture the full distribution of human moral judgments and enhance cross-wording stability, though LLMs remain poorly calibrated about their own error.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Two commonly cited limitations of LLMs are their failure to capture full response distributions and instability across wording variations. This paper aims to test whether these limitations can be mitigated through improved elicitation techniques rather than model-scale or architectural changes.

Method: The authors evaluate prompting strategies across two datasets: (1) a U.S.-representative set of 144 moral scenarios, and (2) 38 moral belief items from the ISSP's Family and Changing Gender Roles module spanning 32 countries. They compare standard prompting against techniques that ask LLMs to output standard deviations and response proportions, and incorporate human confusion ratings as a signal for scenario clarity.

Result: Prompting LLMs to report standard deviations and response proportions recovers the full range of human responses better than common strategies. Ensuring scenarios are clear (as measured by human confusion ratings) boosts alignment, and LLMs can track these ratings. However, LLMs' self-reported error estimates are poorly calibrated, though they predict human variability reasonably well.

Conclusion: Asking better questions to LLMs yields better answers. Simple elicitation techniques can substantially mitigate perceived limitations in capturing human judgment distributions, suggesting that prompt engineering is a viable path to improved AI-human alignment without requiring architectural changes.

Abstract: Are large language models (LLMs) bad at capturing human judgment? Two commonly stated limitations are that LLMs fail to capture full distributions of responses, and that their judgments are unstable across wording variations. We demonstrate simple prompting strategies that mitigate these limitations. Across two datasets--a U.S.-representative set of 144 moral scenarios and 38 moral beliefs from the International Social Survey Programme's Family and Changing Gender Roles module covering 32 countries--we show how simple elicitation techniques help improve AI-human alignment. First, prompting models to report standard deviations and response proportions recovers the full range of human responses better than common strategies. Second, ensuring scenarios are clear to human participants--as reflected in human confusion ratings--boosts model alignment, and LLMs can track human confusion ratings. At the same time, we find that LLMs' estimates of their own error are poorly calibrated, though they can predict human variability relatively well. These results suggest that asking better questions to LLMs can yield better answers.

</details>


### [14] [Rigel: Reverse-Engineering the Metal 4.1 Tensor Compute Path on the Apple M4 Max GPU](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12765)
*Ramchand Kumaresan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Rigel empirically reverse-engineers Apple's undocumented Metal 4.1 tensor compute path on M4 Max, revealing that fp8 matmul2d is emulated rather than hardware-accelerated, executes entirely on GPU shader cores without dedicated matrix hardware or ANE routing, accumulates in ≥fp32, and uses a reconstructable 8×8 cooperative_tensor fragment layout. A hand-fused GEMM+bias+GELU kernel leveraging these findings outperforms the decomposed path by 6.5–12.9%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Apple's Metal Performance Primitives (MPP) matmul2d over cooperative_tensor fragments has a documented interface but deliberately hidden hardware behavior — undisclosed hardware acceleration status, physical execution location, accumulator width, and thread-to-fragment mapping. Developers cannot make informed optimization decisions without knowing whether operations are truly accelerated, where they run, or how data is partitioned.

Method: Rigel employs a checksum-gated, provenance-tracked microbenchmark harness with three-signal triangulation: (1) throughput ceiling measurement comparing fp8 vs fp16 at varying problem sizes, (2) comparison against simdgroup_matrix operations on the same shader cores, and (3) per-rail power attribution using Apple's power monitoring interfaces. The method recovers the opaque 8×8 cooperative_tensor fragment layout through systematic probing of thread-to-element mappings.

Result: Eleven hidden or contradicted facts about the Metal 4.1 specification were recovered. Key findings: fp8 (E4M3) matmul2d sustains only 0.94× fp16 throughput despite halved operand bytes, proving emulation rather than acceleration; execution occurs entirely on GPU shader cores with no dedicated matrix datapath or ANE involvement; accumulation uses ≥fp32 precision; the undocumented 8×8 cooperative_tensor fragment layout was fully reconstructed. A hand-fused GEMM+bias+GELU kernel built on these insights achieves +6.5–12.9% improvement in the cache-resident regime.

Conclusion: On pre-neural-accelerator Apple Silicon (M4 Max), Metal 4.1's tensor compute path is a memory-footprint optimization rather than a compute-throughput one for fp8. The characterization methodology and open-source artifacts (MIT-licensed code, per-cell CSVs) enable developers to write more efficient kernels by understanding the true hardware behavior that Apple's documentation obscures.

Abstract: Apple's Metal 4.1 exposes a tensor compute path: the Metal Performance Primitives (MPP) matmul2d operation over cooperative_tensor fragments, whose interface is documented but whose hardware behavior is deliberately hidden. The specification states which data-type rows are supported, never whether they are hardware-accelerated, where the operation physically executes, what its accumulator width is, or how it partitions matrix fragments across threads. We present Rigel, an empirical characterization of this path on a single Apple M4 Max (a pre-neural-accelerator generation). Using a checksum-gated, provenance-tracked microbenchmark harness, Rigel recovers eleven facts the v4.1 specification hides or contradicts. The headline finding: the Metal 4.1 fp8 (E4M3) matmul2d is emulated, not accelerated: it sustains 0.94x the throughput of fp16 despite reading half the operand bytes, so on M4 it is a memory-footprint feature, not a performance feature. We further show, via a three-signal triangulation (throughput ceiling, comparison against simdgroup_matrix, and per-rail power attribution), that matmul2d executes entirely on the GPU shader cores with no dedicated matrix datapath and no evidence of Apple Neural Engine routing; that it accumulates in >=fp32; and we reconstruct the opaque 8x8 cooperative_tensor fragment layout Apple documents nowhere. Acting on the characterization, a hand-fused GEMM + bias + GELU kernel beats the decomposed path by +6.5-12.9% in the cache-resident regime. All findings are reproducible from committed MIT-licensed code and per-cell CSVs.

</details>


### [15] [GENIE: A Fine-Grained Measure for Novelty](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12790)
*Ramya Namuduri,Manya Wadhwa,Anshun Asher Zheng,Greg Durrett,Junyi Jessy Li*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes GENIE, a fine-grained evaluation metric that measures novelty of model-generated text along task-specific features, showing that holistic metrics fail to capture the multi-dimensional nature of novelty, and uses GENIE to analyze where existing creativity-enhancement methods actually improve novelty.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work on LLM creativity focuses on whether models can generate creative outputs at all, but lacks understanding of what specifically makes generated content novel. Existing holistic metrics cannot capture the high-dimensional nature of novelty or reveal which specific properties they target.

Method: GENIE is a fine-grained evaluation metric that measures response novelty along task-specific features relative to a population of responses. It decomposes novelty across multiple feature dimensions rather than producing a single holistic score.

Result: Holistic metrics struggle to capture the high-dimensionality of novelty and provide no insight into which properties they actually target. GENIE successfully measures the effectiveness of various creativity mitigation methods, revealing where these methods improve novelty across different task-specific features.

Conclusion: Fine-grained, feature-level evaluation via GENIE provides more actionable insights into model novelty than holistic metrics, enabling better understanding and targeted improvement of creativity in LLM outputs.

Abstract: Large Language Models have consistently demonstrated a lack of creativity and diversity across tasks. Prior work has focused on addressing whether models are capable of generating creative outputs. Here, we aim to consider novelty and investigate what makes model-generated content novel or not novel in a task-specific manner. We propose a fine-grained evaluation metric GENIE to measure the novelty of responses along task-specific features with respect to a population of responses. We show that unlike GENIE, holistic metrics struggle to capture the high-dimensionality of novelty and do not provide insight on which properties they target. Finally, we use GENIE to measure the effectiveness of mitigation methods that address creativity to better understand where these methods can improve novelty.

</details>


### [16] [Detect, Remask, Repair: Diffusion Editing for Faithful Summarization of Evolving Contexts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12807)
*Hao Zou,Zachary Horvitz,Chandhru Karthick,Zhou Yu,Kathleen McKeown*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes DETECT-REMASK-REPAIR, a diffusion-based framework that identifies and repairs outdated spans in summaries rather than regenerating them entirely, enabling controllable faithfulness-speed-preservation tradeoffs in evolving-context summarization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Summaries become outdated as contexts evolve; full regeneration is wasteful, obscures changes, and is unnecessary when only a few claims are unsupported. The authors aim to enable localized, targeted repair of outdated summary spans while preserving still-supported content.

Method: The DETECT-REMASK-REPAIR framework uses a three-stage pipeline: (1) DETECT identifies outdated spans via faithfulness assessment, (2) REMASK masks those spans, and (3) REPAIR uses masked diffusion language models to infill corrected content. The approach is evaluated on DialogSum and a new synthetic benchmark called StreamSum, with experiments on faithfulness-steered repair, one-step repair for latency reduction, and post-hoc correction of autoregressive outputs.

Result: Localized diffusion repair provides a controllable alternative to full rewriting. Faithfulness-steered repair improves early drafts, one-step repair reduces repair cost to under half a second, and the framework enables faithfulness-speed-preservation tradeoffs. It also serves as a post-hoc correction step that improves faithfulness for autoregressive systems.

Conclusion: Diffusion-based localized repair is an effective, efficient, and controllable approach for updating summaries in evolving contexts, offering practical advantages over full regeneration and serving as a general post-hoc correction mechanism.

Abstract: Summaries of real-world events can become outdated as contexts evolve and new information arrives. A common response is to generate a new summary from the updated context, but full regeneration discards the previous draft, can obscure what changed, and may be unnecessary when only a few claims are unsupported. We study localized faithfulness repair: updating outdated spans in an existing summary while preserving supported content. We propose DETECT-REMASK-REPAIR, a diffusion-based framework that identifies, remasks, and repairs outdated regions with masked diffusion language models. To evaluate evolving-context summarization, we introduce StreamSum, a benchmark of synthetic event timelines. Experiments on DialogSum and StreamSum show that localized diffusion repair provides a controllable alternative to full rewriting: faithfulness-steered repair improves early drafts, one-step repair reduces repair cost to under half a second, with the framework enabling faithfulness-speed-preservation tradeoffs across datasets. We also find that the framework can provide a post-hoc correction step that improves faithfulness for autoregressive systems.

</details>


### [17] [Localizing Anchoring Pathways in Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12818)
*Hillary N. Owusu,Sarah Wiegreffe,Naomi H. Feldman*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Language models exhibit anchoring bias where irrelevant numbers in prompts skew numerical reasoning. Using logit-difference metrics and attribution-based circuit localization, the authors identify where this anchor-sensitive signal resides in 7B–8B Qwen and Llama models, finding edge-level methods outperform node-level methods and that circuits transfer strongly within a model but weakly across base vs. instruction-tuned variants.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To mechanistically understand where and how anchoring effects — a cognitive bias where irrelevant numerical anchors distort judgments — are internally represented and processed within language models during numerical reasoning tasks.

Method: A controlled multiple-choice numerical reasoning setup with shared answer options. Authors define a logit-difference metric (correct answer logit minus anchor-corresponding answer logit) to track behavioral anchoring. They then apply attribution-based circuit localization using both node-level and edge-level methods on 7B–8B parameter Qwen and Llama models (both base and instruction-tuned), analyzing circuit transferability across anchor directions and model variants.

Result: Edge-level attribution methods recover the anchor-sensitive signal more faithfully than node-level methods. Low- and high-anchor circuits transfer strongly within the same model across anchor directions, suggesting shared pathway structure. However, cross-variant transfer (base ↔ instruction-tuned) is sparse and unreliable, indicating post-training alters which pathways are most relevant.

Conclusion: The study provides a mechanistic account of anchoring in language models: anchor-sensitive decision signals are localized to specific circuits best identified via edge-level methods, with pathways that are direction-invariant but sensitive to post-training modifications.

Abstract: Irrelevant numbers in a prompt can shift language model judgments, producing anchoring effects in numerical reasoning. We study where this anchor-sensitive signal is carried inside language models using a controlled multiple-choice setup with shared answer options. We define a logit-difference metric comparing the correct answer option with the answer option corresponding to the anchor, and validate that it tracks behavioral anchoring. Using attribution-based circuit localization on 7B--8B Qwen and Llama base and instruction-tuned models, we find that edge-level methods recover this signal more faithfully than node-level methods. Low- and high-anchor circuits transfer strongly within a model, suggesting shared pathway structure across anchor direction. However, sparse transfer across base and instruction-tuned variants is less reliable, indicating that post-training changes which pathways matter most. Overall, our results provide a mechanistic account of how anchoring-related decision signals are carried inside language models.

</details>


### [18] [LoHoSearch: Benchmarking Long-Horizon Search Agents Beyond the Human Difficulty Ceiling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12837)
*Jiarui Zhao,Rongzhi Zhang,Lingchuan Liu,Hao Yang,Xunliang Cai,Xi Su*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LoHoSearch is a new benchmark for long-horizon search agents, constructed via an automated knowledge-graph pipeline (7M+ Wikipedia entities) that systematically maximizes search space size and structural complexity. The best model achieves only 34.74% accuracy, and context management strategies yield marginal gains (+6.8%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing search agent benchmarks (e.g., BrowseComp) have rapidly saturated (>90% accuracy) because they are human-authored, limiting search space size and structural complexity. A difficulty ceiling has emerged that existing benchmarks cannot break.

Method: LoHoSearch is built via an automated pipeline over a knowledge graph of 7M+ Wikipedia entities. The pipeline selects relations with large search spaces and assembles them into structurally complex questions, with each answer uniquely verifiable against the KG. The benchmark comprises 544 human-verified questions across 11 domains.

Result: The strongest model achieves only 34.74% accuracy on LoHoSearch. Existing context management strategies provide only a +6.8% improvement, far smaller gains than observed on prior benchmarks.

Conclusion: LoHoSearch provides a significantly more demanding standard for evaluating long-horizon reasoning and context management in search agents, addressing the saturation problem of prior benchmarks.

Abstract: Search agent benchmarks exemplified by BrowseComp have rapidly saturated over the past year, with the strongest models surpassing 90% accuracy. Since these benchmarks are predominantly human-authored, annotators lack a global perspective on entity statistics and cannot systematically maximize search space size and structural complexity. This creates a difficulty ceiling that is hard to break. To address this, we introduce LoHoSearch (Long-Horizon Search Agents), a challenging benchmark comprising 544 human-verified questions across 11 domains. LoHoSearch is constructed via an automated pipeline built upon a knowledge graph covering over 7 million Wikipedia entities, which selects relations with large search spaces and assembles them into structurally complex questions with KG-verified unique answers. Our evaluation demonstrates that even the strongest model achieves only 34.74% accuracy, and existing context management strategies (best +6.8%) yield far smaller gains than on prior benchmarks. LoHoSearch provides a more demanding standard for evaluating long-horizon reasoning and context management in search agents.

</details>


### [19] [Small LLMs for Biomedical Claim Verification: Cost-Effective Fine-Tuning, Structural Dataset Shortcuts, and Cross-Domain Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12854)
*Gaurav Kumar*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Small LLMs fine-tuned with QLoRA on only 1,008 examples for biomedical claim verification can surpass GPT-4o and GPT-5 by up to 12% F1 at a fraction of the cost, while also revealing a structural artifact in SciFact that inflates in-domain scores.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large proprietary models like GPT-4o/GPT-5 achieve strong zero-shot biomedical claim verification but are costly and opaque, limiting scalable deployment. The authors aim to demonstrate that small, open-weight LLMs fine-tuned with parameter-efficient QLoRA can match or exceed GPT performance at dramatically lower cost.

Method: Three small LLMs (Phi-3-mini 3.8B, Qwen2.5-3B, Mistral-7B) are fine-tuned via QLoRA on the SciFact and HealthVer datasets using only 1,008 training examples. Bidirectional cross-domain evaluation (SciFact→HealthVer and HealthVer→SciFact) is conducted at matched model sizes to disentangle dataset structure from data quantity effects. Comparisons are made against GPT-4o, GPT-5, and fine-tuned BioLinkBERT encoders.

Result: Mistral-7B with QLoRA surpasses both GPT-4o and GPT-5 with up to 12% F1 gain. A previously unreported structural artifact in SciFact is identified that artificially inflates in-domain scores. Bidirectional out-of-domain evaluation shows that training on structurally sound data (HealthVer) enables robust cross-domain transfer.

Conclusion: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning of small LLMs offers a cost-effective, transparent, and high-performing alternative to large proprietary models for biomedical claim verification. Dataset quality and structure matter more than sheer data quantity for cross-domain generalization.

Abstract: Large Language Models such as GPT-4o and GPT-5 achieve strong zero-shot performance on biomedical claim verification, but cost and opacity limit scalable use. We fine-tune three small LLMs: Phi-3-mini (3.8B), Qwen2.5-3B, and Mistral-7B, via QLoRA on SciFact and HealthVer, providing the first study of QLoRA models against GPT-4o and fine-tuned BioLinkBERT encoders. Mistral-7B QLoRA surpasses both GPT-4o and GPT-5 (up to 12% F1 gain) at a fractional cost using just 1,008 training examples. We conduct extensive in-domain and cross-domain evaluation: models trained on SciFact tested on HealthVer and vice versa, at matched sizes to isolate dataset structure from data quantity. We identify a previously unreported structural artifact in SciFact that inflates in-domain scores, and show through bidirectional out-of-domain evaluation that training on structurally sound data enables robust cross-domain transfer. We plan to release all code and adapter checkpoints.

</details>


### [20] [Direct Preference Optimization for Chatbot Fine-Tuning: An Empirical Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12881)
*Yvonne Qiu,Dezhi Yu,ShuoJia Fu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Presents a DPO-based fine-tuning approach for LLMs that simplifies training, boosts computational efficiency, and achieves competitive performance, though training instability requires further study.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) pipelines are complex and computationally demanding; DPO offers a simpler alternative by directly optimizing preferences without an explicit reward model.

Method: Fine-tuning large language models using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), a reinforcement learning technique that bypasses explicit reward modeling by directly optimizing the policy from preference data. Evaluation employs BLEU, ROUGE, and cosine similarity metrics.

Result: DPO simplifies the training pipeline, improves computational efficiency, and achieves competitive performance. BLEU, ROUGE, and cosine similarity metrics confirm effective learning and convergence.

Conclusion: DPO is a viable and efficient alternative for LLM fine-tuning, but observed training instability warrants further investigation to ensure robust deployment.

Abstract: We present an approach to fine-tuning large language models using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), a reinforcement learning technique. Our experimental results demonstrate that DPO simplifies the training pipeline, improves computational efficiency, and achieves competitive performance. The evaluation using BLEU, ROUGE, and cosine similarity metrics indicates effective learning and convergence, though further investigation is needed to address observed training instability.

</details>


### [21] [SafeLLM: Extraction as a Hallucination-Resistant Alternative to Rewriting in Safety-Critical Settings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12897)
*Julia Ive,Felix Jozsa,Evridiki Georgaki,Nabeel Sheikh,Emma Cattell,Nick Jackson,Paulina Bondaronek,Ciaran Scott Hill,Richard Dobson*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper evaluates extraction-based methods as hallucination-resistant alternatives to rewriting-based RAG for accessing organisational guidelines. Line-number-based source selection outperforms other strategies, achieving up to 95% term recall with strong source alignment. Safety-focused approaches improve precision but introduce omissions, and performance varies by document structure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs used in RAG systems for organisational documentation (SOPs, HR policies, clinical guidelines) are prone to hallucinations and unstable completeness-conciseness trade-offs when using free-form rewriting. In safety- and compliance-critical settings, these failures are unacceptable, motivating an exploration of extraction-based approaches that preserve fidelity to source text.

Method: The authors compare multiple prompting strategies: (1) line-number-based source selection, (2) extraction of relevant guideline sentences with explicit safety annotations, and (3) a multi-stage pipeline that refines draft answers using supporting evidence. Experiments use both frontier-scale and locally deployable models on NHS acute care, oncology, and NICE guidelines. Evaluation employs automatic metrics and human expert assessment of relevance and completeness.

Result: Line-number selection achieves the strongest overall performance across model scales, with up to 95% term recall and close source alignment. Safety-oriented strategies improve precision but cause systematic omissions; multi-stage filtering amplifies this precision-recall trade-off. Line-based extraction excels on protocol-like documents, while alternative strategies reach up to 97% term recall on more verbose content.

Conclusion: Extraction-based methods, particularly line-number selection, offer a viable hallucination-resistant alternative to rewriting-based RAG for compliance-critical organisational documentation, though optimal strategy selection depends on document structure and the desired precision-recall balance.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to access organisational documentation, including standard operating procedures (SOPs), HR policies and institutional guidelines. However, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems that rely on free-form rewriting can introduce hallucinations and unstable trade-offs between completeness and conciseness, particularly in safety- and compliance-critical settings. Objectives: To evaluate extraction as a hallucination-resistant alternative to rewriting-based RAG and compare strategies that balance precision, recall and safety across document types and model scales. Methods: We compare multiple prompting strategies, including line-number-based source selection, extraction of relevant guideline sentences with explicit safety annotations, and a multi-stage pipeline that refines draft answers using supporting evidence from source guidelines. Experiments are conducted on documents of varying length and structure, including local NHS acute care and oncology guidelines and UK-wide NICE guidelines, using both frontier-scale and locally deployable models. Performance is assessed using automatic metrics and human expert evaluation of relevance and completeness. Results: Line-number selection achieves the strongest results, outperforming direct copying and safety-focused strategies across both large and small models while maintaining high term recall (up to 95%) and close alignment with source text. Safety-oriented approaches improve precision but introduce systematic omissions, while multi-stage filtering further amplifies this trade-off. Performance varies with document structure: line-based extraction excels in protocol-like content, whereas alternative strategies perform better on more verbose documents (up to 97% term recall).

</details>


### [22] [PRISM: Prosody-Integrated Multi-Agent Reasoning Framework for Empathetic Spoken Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12902)
*Wen Zhang,Xiaocui Yang,Zhuoyue Gao,Shi Feng,Daling Wang,Yifei Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: PRISM is a multi-agent framework for empathetic spoken dialogue that decouples speech perception, response generation, and speech synthesis, using a prosody-to-language translation mechanism and on-demand external knowledge tools to improve empathy, prosodic appropriateness, and response quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing empathetic spoken dialogue systems face two key limitations: (1) cascade pipelines lose acoustic/prosodic cues during ASR-based speech-to-text conversion, and (2) end-to-end speech models lack interpretable control over emotion and knowledge integration. Both paradigms fail to adequately preserve and leverage prosodic information critical for empathetic interaction.

Method: PRISM decouples the pipeline into three coordinated components — speech perception, response generation, and speech synthesis — within a multi-agent framework. Key innovations include: (1) a prosody-to-language translation mechanism that converts acoustic prosodic features into textual representations to stabilize LLM reasoning, and (2) an on-demand external knowledge invocation system for enriching empathetic dialogue generation.

Result: PRISM achieves consistent improvements across objective and subjective metrics in three dimensions: empathy, prosodic appropriateness, and text response generation quality.

Conclusion: The decoupled multi-agent architecture with prosody-to-language translation effectively bridges the gap between acoustic signal preservation and controllable empathetic response generation, offering a promising direction for building more natural and emotionally aligned spoken dialogue systems.

Abstract: Empathetic spoken dialogue systems require not only semantically appropriate responses but also emotionally aligned prosodic expression. However, cascade pipelines often discard acoustic cues during speech-to-text conversion, while end-to-end speech models lack interpretable control over emotion and knowledge integration. To address these challenges, we propose PRISM, a multi-agent framework for empathetic spoken dialogue that decouples speech perception, response generation, and speech synthesis into coordinated components. PRISM introduces a prosody-to-language translation mechanism to stabilize large language model reasoning and enables on-demand invocation of external knowledge tools for empathetic dialogue generation. Experimental results demonstrate that PRISM achieves consistent improvements in empathy, prosodic appropriateness, and text response generation quality across objective and subjective metrics. Our code is available at: https://github.com/Bxzfrm/PRISM.

</details>


### [23] [X-MADAM-RAG: Diagnosing and Handling Chinese-English Evidence Conflict in Retrieval-Augmented Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12903)
*Yongqi Kang,Yu Fu,Yong Zhao*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Introduces X-RAMDocs-ZHEN, a Chinese-English benchmark for diagnosing evidence conflict in RAG, and X-MADAM-RAG, a conflict-aware pipeline. While X-MADAM-RAG performs well on the controlled benchmark, a rule-only extractor achieves perfect scores, revealing template regularity. A naturalized stress test exposes that document-level extraction remains the main bottleneck, with both methods struggling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RAG systems often encounter mutually contradictory evidence, especially in multilingual settings where Chinese and English documents may support incompatible answers. Existing benchmarks lack controlled diagnosis of this evidence conflict problem, motivating the creation of a targeted benchmark and analysis pipeline.

Method: (1) X-RAMDocs-ZHEN: a controlled Chinese-English benchmark with 300 examples across six conditions (monolingual support, bilingual agreement, reversed conflict, conflict with noise). (2) X-MADAM-RAG: an interpretable pipeline with four stages — per-document candidate extraction, visible-evidence repair, deterministic candidate grouping, and conflict-aware aggregation. (3) A deterministic naturalized stress test (100 samples) that removes answer templates while preserving candidate strings to probe template overfitting.

Result: On the controlled benchmark with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, X-MADAM-RAG achieves 0.9667 strict accuracy and 0.9767 conflict-aware success, outperforming baselines. However, a zero-call rule-only extractor reaches 1.0000, indicating strong template regularity. On the naturalized stress test, rule-only extraction drops to 0.0000 and X-MADAM-RAG to 0.3000, below naive and evidence-normalized baselines. A privileged oracle remains perfect, identifying document-level extraction as the bottleneck.

Conclusion: X-RAMDocs-ZHEN and X-MADAM-RAG serve as diagnostic tools for controlled evidence conflict rather than general hallucination detection or natural retrieval robustness. The template regularity in the controlled benchmark and the performance collapse on the naturalized stress test reveal that document-level candidate extraction remains the primary bottleneck.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems may receive evidence that is not merely noisy but mutually contradictory. This issue becomes particularly salient in multilingual settings, where retrieved Chinese and English evidence may support incompatible answer candidates. We study this problem through X-RAMDocs-ZHEN, a controlled Chinese-English benchmark derived from RAMDocs for diagnosing evidence conflict in RAG. The benchmark contains 300 examples across six balanced conditions, including monolingual support, bilingual agreement, reversed conflict directions, and conflict with optional noise. We further examine X-MADAM-RAG, an interpretable pipeline that decomposes evidence handling into per-document candidate extraction, visible-evidence repair, deterministic candidate grouping, and conflict-aware aggregation. On the original controlled benchmark with Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, X-MADAM-RAG achieves 0.9667 strict accuracy and 0.9767 conflict-aware success, outperforming an evidence-normalized single-call baseline. However, a zero-call rule-only extractor reaches 1.0000 on the same benchmark, revealing strong template regularity. To probe this limitation, we construct a deterministic naturalized stress test that removes explicit answer templates while preserving candidate strings. On its 100-sample subset, rule-only extraction falls to 0.0000, but X-MADAM-RAG also drops to 0.3000 strict accuracy, below both naive and evidence-normalized baselines. A privileged oracle remains perfect, indicating that document-level extraction is the main bottleneck. These findings position X-RAMDocs-ZHEN and X-MADAM-RAG as diagnostic tools for controlled evidence conflict rather than as evidence of general hallucination detection or robustness to natural retrieval.

</details>


### [24] [SENTINEL: Failure-Driven Reinforcement Learning for Training Tool-Using Language Model Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12908)
*Ziyi Wang,Yuxuan Lu,Yimeng Zhang,Qun Liu,Chen Luo,Jiri Gesi,Hanqing Lu,Yisi Sang,Manling Li,Jing Huang,Dakuo Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SENTINEL is a failure-driven RL framework that converts an agent's rollout failures into targeted training tasks via a Controller–Proposer–Solver loop. By identifying error patterns, generating tasks that stress those weaknesses, and training on them, it improves Pass¹ from 66.4 to 74.9 on Tau2-Bench Retail, outperforming standard RL on synthetic tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training reliable tool-using language model agents is challenging because fixed pre-training task distributions become mismatched with the agent's evolving capabilities during RL. Many rollouts are wasted on tasks the agent already handles well, while specific weaknesses remain unaddressed.

Method: SENTINEL uses a three-component loop: (1) the Controller analyzes failed trajectories to identify recurring error patterns, (2) the Proposer generates new executable tasks designed to stress these specific weaknesses, and (3) the Solver is trained via RL on these targeted tasks.
