This creates an adaptive curriculum that scales with the agent's failures.

Result: On Tau2-Bench Retail with Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507, SENTINEL improves Pass¹ from 66.4 to 74.9 and outperforms RL trained on general synthetic tasks across Pass^k metrics, demonstrating that model failures provide an effective, scalable source of targeted training signal.

Conclusion: Model failures are a rich, scalable signal for improving tool-using LM agents. Failure-driven task generation adapts the training distribution to the agent's current weaknesses, overcoming the distribution mismatch problem in standard RL training.

Abstract: Language model agents are increasingly effective in solving realistic tasks through multi-turn tool use. However, training reliable tool-using agents remains challenging in practice. While reinforcement learning provides an on-policy paradigm for improving agents from their own environment interactions, its effectiveness depends heavily on the training task distribution. When tasks are fixed before training, the task distribution can become increasingly mismatched with the policy's evolving capabilities, causing many rollouts to be spent on uninformative tasks. We propose SENTINEL, a failure-driven reinforcement learning framework that turns the Solver's rollout failures into targeted training tasks. SENTINEL follows a Controller--Proposer--Solver loop: the Controller analyzes failed trajectories and summarizes recurring error patterns, the Proposer generates executable tasks that stress these weaknesses, and the Solver is trained on the targeted tasks. On Tau2-Bench Retail with Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507, SENTINEL improves Pass\^{}1 from 66.4 to 74.9 and outperforms RL on general synthetic tasks across Pass\^{}k metrics. These results demonstrate that model failures provide an effective and scalable source of targeted training signal for improving tool-using language model agents.

</details>


### [25] [PiDA: Phonetically-Informed Data Augmentation for Robust Vietnamese Speech Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12911)
*Giang Son Nguyen,Tung X. Nguyen,Hieu Minh Truong,Nhu Vo,Wray Buntine,Dung D. Le*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper systematically categorizes ASR errors in Vietnamese speech translation by phonetic cause, quantifies their impact on NMT via linear mixed-effects modeling, and proposes Phonetically-Informed Data Augmentation (PiDA) to improve translation robustness against phonetic ASR errors, achieving up to +2.04 BLEU improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cascaded speech translation systems suffer from error propagation when ASR produces incorrect transcripts. While this is a known problem, no prior work had systematically categorized ASR errors by phonetic cause for Vietnamese ST or quantified the specific impact of phonetic confusions on downstream NMT performance. The authors aim to fill this gap and leverage phonetic insights to design targeted mitigation strategies.

Method: (1) Systematic categorization of ASR substitution errors by phonetic cause (vowels, tones, consonants, or random). (2) Linear Mixed-Effects Modelling to quantify the impact of each error category on NMT quality. (3) PiDA: Phonetically-Informed Data Augmentation that corrupts clean text by substituting words with phonetically similar alternatives using phonetic word embeddings. (4) Fine-tuning NMT models on PiDA-augmented FLEURS Vietnamese-English data.

Result: Most ASR substitution errors originate from phonetic confusions rather than random noise. Phonetic errors significantly degrade ST quality. PiDA-augmented fine-tuning improves translation of erroneous ASR outputs by up to +2.04 BLEU over standard fine-tuning, while also yielding slight improvements on clean-text translation.

Conclusion: Phonetic confusions are the dominant source of ASR substitution errors in Vietnamese and disproportionately harm ST performance. Data augmentation that mimics these phonetic errors (PiDA) effectively enhances NMT robustness to ASR noise without sacrificing clean-text quality, validating the utility of phonetically-informed training strategies for cascaded ST.

Abstract: Cascaded speech translation (ST) systems suffer from error propagation when Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) outputs incorrect transcripts. We present the first systematic categorization of ASR errors for Vietnamese ST, classifying substitution errors by phonetic cause and quantifying their impact on downstream Neural Machine Translation (NMT) performance using Linear Mixed-Effects Modelling. We confirm that most ASR substitution errors arise from phonetic confusions rather than random noise, and that these phonetic errors significantly degrade ST quality. Motivated by this finding, we propose Phonetically-Informed Data Augmentation (PiDA), which generates ASR-like corruptions by substituting words with phonetically similar alternatives using phonetic word embeddings. Fine-tuning on a PiDA-augmented version of FLEURS Vietnamese-English improves translation of erroneous ASR outputs (up to +2.04 BLEU over standard fine-tuning) while also slightly improving clean-text performance.

</details>


### [26] [Polar: A Benchmark for Evaluating Political Bias in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12922)
*Sangho Kim,Heejin Kim,Yoonhee Park,Hyunggeun Jeon,Jaejin Lee*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Polar is a 4,026-instance multiple-choice benchmark measuring political bias in LLMs via option-level likelihoods across U.S. and South Korean contexts. Evaluation of 38 models reveals systematic bias variation by political context, with all models leaning left-progressive on U.S. content but showing more centered patterns on South Korean content; presentation language alone can shift measured bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Political bias in LLMs is increasingly consequential but difficult to measure reproducibly across different political systems and languages. Existing bias evaluation methods rely on prompt-based generation, which introduces variability. There is a gap in cross-contextual, multilingual benchmarks that can systematically compare bias across distinct political environments.

Method: The authors introduce Polar, a 4,026-instance multiple-choice benchmark derived from the Manifesto Project covering two ideological axes and eight issue categories. Instead of prompt-based generation, Polar measures political bias through option-level likelihoods (token probabilities assigned to each answer choice). The benchmark evaluates models in parallel across U.S. and South Korean political contexts, with translation experiments to isolate the effect of presentation language.

Result: Across 38 LLMs, measured bias varies systematically with political context, issue category, model group, and presentation language. All models lean left-progressive on U.S. political content. On South Korean content, models show more centered and ideologically mixed patterns. Translation experiments demonstrate that merely changing the presentation language can shift measured political bias, even when the underlying semantic content remains constant.

Conclusion: The findings underscore the necessity of multilingual and cross-contextual evaluation frameworks for political bias in LLMs. Monolingual, single-context assessments yield an incomplete and potentially misleading picture of model bias, as both the political context of the content and the language of presentation independently influence bias measurements.

Abstract: Political bias in large language models (LLMs) is increasingly significant, but difficult to measure reproducibly across political and linguistic contexts. We introduce Polar, a 4,026-instance multiple-choice benchmark that measures political bias through option-level likelihoods rather than prompt-based generation. Polar covers two ideological axes and eight issue categories derived from the Manifesto Project, and evaluates models in parallel across U.S. and South Korean political contexts. Across 38 LLMs, measured bias varies systematically with political context, issue category, model group, and presentation language. All models lean left-progressive on U.S. political content, but show more centered and mixed patterns on South Korean content. Translation experiments further show that presentation language alone can shift measured bias. These findings highlight the need for multilingual and cross-contextual evaluation of political bias in LLMs.

</details>


### [27] [Multi-Turn Reasoning When Context Arrives in Pieces: Scalable Sharding and Memory-Augmented RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12941)
*Shu Tong Luo,Wenqin Liu,Rui Liu,Mingming Gong,Jiaxian Guo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LLMs suffer up to 65% accuracy degradation when task-critical information is fragmented across multiple conversation turns. The authors introduce a memory-augmented training approach using a compact rolling memory instead of full conversation history, and propose a sharding pipeline to convert single-turn QA datasets into multi-turn episodes for scalable training. Memory-trained models generalize zero-shot to harder tasks and outperform full-history baselines even with full context at test time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs exhibit a "Lost in Conversation" phenomenon where accuracy degrades significantly (up to 65%) when users reveal task-critical information incrementally across multiple turns — even when the full context is available. Existing solutions relying on full conversation history are insufficient, and manually creating multi-turn datasets is prohibitively expensive. The authors aim to address both the performance degradation and the data scarcity problem simultaneously.

Method: (1) A low-cost sharding pipeline that automatically converts single-turn QA datasets (e.g., GSM8K) into multi-turn fragmented-information episodes, eliminating manual annotation. (2) Training models to maintain a compact rolling memory — a compressed representation that is iteratively updated — instead of attending to the full growing conversation history. The policy is trained solely on sharded GSM8K data.

Result: Memory-augmented models significantly improve multi-turn accuracy compared to full-history baselines. The approach generalizes zero-shot to harder math problems and out-of-domain long-context QA tasks. Strikingly, memory-trained models outperform full-history baselines even when the full history is provided at test time, indicating that learning to compress fosters more robust incremental reasoning.

Conclusion: Training LLMs with a compact rolling memory mechanism effectively mitigates the "Lost in Conversation" degradation. The sharding pipeline provides a scalable way to generate multi-turn training data. Crucially, the benefit of memory compression transcends mere context management — it induces fundamentally more capable incremental reasoning that surpasses even full-context exposure.

Abstract: When a user reveals task-critical information across several conversation turns, LLM accuracy drops by up to 65% despite full context availability. We show that this Lost in Conversation degradation can be substantially mitigated by training models to maintain a compact rolling memory instead of attending to a growing history. To make such training scalable, we introduce a low-cost sharding pipeline that converts single-turn QA datasets into multi-turn fragmented-information episodes, eliminating the need for hours of manual annotation. Training only on sharded GSM8K, our memory-augmented policy significantly improves multi-turn accuracy and generalises zero-shot to harder math and out-of-domain long-context QA. Moreover, memory-trained models outperform full-history baselines even when given the full history at test time, suggesting that learning to compress induces more robust incremental reasoning than full-context exposure alone.

</details>


### [28] [SkillChain: Closing the Loop on Skill Evolution for Image-Based E-Commerce AI Assistants](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12984)
*Yimin Hu,Mengtao Xu,Hao Guo,Yuheng Song,Xiaoyong Zhu,Bo Zheng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SkillChain automates the lifecycle of Skills (intent-specific behavioral constraints) for LLM-based e-commerce image assistants via a three-stage pipeline: Skill Creator, Route Optimizer, and Body Refiner. Deployed at production scale, it significantly improves response quality, user engagement, content consumption, and long-term retention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Image-based AI assistants on e-commerce platforms must handle heterogeneous user intents (product search, style recommendation, visual encyclopedia, utility calls) behind a single image upload. Each intent demands distinct response formats, tool invocations, and domain knowledge. Without per-intent behavioral constraints, LLM-based systems conflate these modes, falling short of domain quality standards. The breadth and dynamism of the intent space make manual engineering infeasible, necessitating an automated approach.

Method: SkillChain is a closed-loop system that automates the Skill lifecycle through three stages: (1) Skill Creator — bootstraps Skills from task specifications and trajectories; (2) Route Optimizer — aligns intent routing to appropriate Skills; (3) Body Refiner — iteratively refines Skill content (the behavioral constraints/response logic) using dual-path LLM-Judge evaluation. The system closes the production feedback loop, allowing Skills to evolve continuously based on real-world usage.

Result: Deployed on a production-scale e-commerce image assistant, SkillChain substantially improved aggregate response quality, with the strongest gains in structural compliance and content quality. A one-week online A/B experiment confirmed statistically significant improvements in user engagement, content consumption, and long-term retention metrics.

Conclusion: Automating the Skill lifecycle via SkillChain is a viable and effective approach for managing the heterogeneous intent space of production image assistants. Closing the feedback loop enables continuous improvement without manual engineering overhead, yielding measurable gains in both response quality and key business metrics.

Abstract: Image-based AI assistants are now deployed at production scale on e-commerce platforms, where a single uploaded image can trigger fundamentally different user intents: product search, style recommendation, visual encyclopedia, or utility tool calls, each demanding its own response format, tool invocation, and domain knowledge. Without per-intent behavioral constraints, LLM-based systems conflate these heterogeneous modes and fall short of domain quality standards, while the breadth and dynamism of the intent space render manual engineering infeasible. To address this, we present SkillChain, which closes the production feedback loop on Skill evolution, automating the lifecycle of Skills through three stages: Skill Creator for bootstrapping from task specs and trajectories, Route Optimizer for routing alignment, and Body Refiner for iterative Skill Body refinement via dual-path LLM-Judge evaluation. Deployed on a production-scale e-commerce image assistant, SkillChain substantially improves aggregate response quality, with the strongest gains on structural compliance and content quality; a one-week online A/B experiment further confirms significant gains in user engagement, content consumption, and long-term retention.

</details>


### [29] [No Hidden Prompts Needed! You Can Game AI Peer Review with Presentation-Only Revisions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13044)
*Xu Yang,Zhizhou Sha,Junbo Li,Jian Yu,Yifan Sun,Matthew Zhao,Jinrui Fang,Xinyue Guo,Yining Wu,Xu Hu,Yifu Luo,Qiang Liu,Zhangyang Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Adversarial repackaging is a novel attack where only presentation-level content (abstract, framing, related work, discussion) is modified—without touching scientific evidence—guided by AI-reviewer feedback. It achieves 75.1% success rate and +1.21/10 score gain. Key insight: AI reviewers confuse the appearance of addressing limitations with actually resolving them, making paper presentation an optimization surface.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing robustness research on AI reviewers focuses on explicit attacks (hidden instructions, prompt injection). This paper identifies a harder, more policy-relevant failure mode: no hidden text or altered scientific content, only presentation-level modifications that game AI reviewer scores. The motivation is to expose a structural vulnerability where presentation polishing, not scientific merit, drives score inflation.

Method: The authors introduce "adversarial repackaging," a closed-loop attack framework that iteratively revises presentation-level content (abstract, contribution framing, related work, discussion, narrative structure) using AI-reviewer feedback as an optimization signal, while keeping all scientific evidence (methods, experiments, figures, equations, proofs, numerical results) fixed. Three mainstream AI reviewers are tested. Ablations distinguish surface edits (polishing, formatting) from deeper strategic edits (related-work repositioning, analytical discussion expansion).

Result: Adversarial repackaging achieves 75.1% attack success rate and mean score gain of +1.21/10 across three AI reviewers. Strategic edits like related-work repositioning substantially outperform surface edits. Two structural failure modes emerge: (1) AI reviewers are easier to impress (highlighting strengths) than to convince (dissolving weaknesses often backfires); (2) AI reviewers confuse the appearance of addressing a limitation with actually resolving it. A contamination-free rolling benchmark and attack framework are released.

Conclusion: The deployment risk of AI reviewers extends beyond malicious hidden instructions to the emergence of paper presentation as an optimizable surface. AI reviewers lack robust anchoring to scientific content, enabling unchanged evidence to be reinterpreted as stronger contributions through presentation manipulation alone. This calls for new evaluation standards that test whether AI reviewers remain grounded in scientific content under presentation-only edits.

Abstract: As AI-generated reviews move from experimental tools into peer-review infrastructure, most robustness concerns have focused on explicit attacks such as hidden instructions and prompt injection. We study a harder and more policy-relevant failure mode: no hidden text, no prompt injection, and no changes to methods, experiments, figures, equations, proofs, or numerical results. The attacker modifies only presentation-level content, such as the abstract, contribution framing, related work, discussion, and narrative structure. We introduce adversarial repackaging: a closed-loop attack that uses AI-reviewer feedback to search for presentation-level revisions while keeping the scientific evidence fixed. Across three mainstream AI reviewers, adversarial repackaging achieves a 75.1% attack success rate and a mean score gain of +1.21/10. The effect is not explained by ordinary prose polishing. We also reveal that strategies that change how the reviewer interprets the paper, such as related-work repositioning and analytical discussion expansion, substantially outperform surface edits such as local polishing, table formatting, and algorithm boxes.
  Our analysis reveals two deeper structural failure modes. First, AI reviewers are easier to impress than to convince: highlighting strengths reliably increases perceived merit, while attempts to dissolve weaknesses frequently backfire. Second, AI reviewers can confuse the appearance of addressing a limitation with actually resolving it, allowing unchanged evidence to be reinterpreted as stronger scientific contribution. These results show that the deployment risk is not only malicious hidden instructions, but the emergence of paper presentation itself as an optimization surface. We release a contamination-free rolling benchmark and attack framework for testing whether AI reviewers remain anchored to scientific content under presentation-only edits.

</details>


### [30] [sebis at CRF Filling 2026: A Two-Stage Local LLM Pipeline for Medical CRF Filling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13082)
*Katharina Sommer,Tristan Till,Florian Matthes*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A privacy-preserving, fully local pipeline using MedGemma-27B with a two-stage architecture (binary classification + value extraction) achieves a macro-F1 of 0.55 on the CL4Health 2026 CRF task, securing second place among open-source, on-premise submissions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extracting structured clinical data from unstructured EHR notes remains a bottleneck. LLMs offer strong performance but face barriers in clinical deployment: privacy risks, high inference costs, and hallucination beyond textual evidence. The authors aim to address these by building a fully local, data-sovereign alternative.

Method: A two-stage pipeline using the MedGemma-27B model: (1) binary presence classification determines whether a clinical finding is present, negated, uncertain, or unknown; (2) value extraction extracts the specific value only when presence is confirmed. The approach uses item-specific, few-shot in-context learning, requires no external API calls or fine-tuning, and enforces strict textual grounding to prevent hallucination.

Result: The pipeline achieved a macro-F1 score of 0.55 on the official English test track of the CL4Health 2026 CRF task, placing second among all locally-hosted, open-source submissions.

Conclusion: Privacy-preserving, on-premise LLM pipelines using domain-adapted models can achieve near-competitive performance with proprietary frontier models, offering a practical, data-sovereign framework for clinical NLP without sacrificing compliance or evidence fidelity.

Abstract: The extraction of structured clinical information from unstructured EHR notes is a persistent bottleneck in healthcare informatics. While large language models (LLMs) offer high performance, their deployment in clinical settings is hindered by privacy risks, inference costs, and the tendency to hallucinate beyond textual evidence. We address these challenges for the CL4Health 2026 Case Report Form (CRF) filling task by proposing a fully local, domain-adapted pipeline using the MedGemma-27B model. Our two-stage architecture, which separates binary presence classification from value extraction, enforces strict adherence to textual evidence and ensures deterministic outputs for negated, uncertain, or unknown states. By leveraging item-specific, few-shot in-context learning without external API calls or fine-tuning, our approach achieves a macro-F1 score of 0.55 on the official English test track. This result secures second place among all locally-hosted, open-source submissions. Our work demonstrates that privacy-preserving, on-premise LLM pipelines can achieve near-competitive performance with proprietary frontier models, providing a practical, data-sovereign framework for clinical NLP.

</details>


### [31] [LEDGER: A Long-Context Benchmark of Corporate Annual Reports for Grounded Financial Retrieval and Extraction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13100)
*Charles Moslonka,Amaury de Vitry,Arthur Garnier,Hicham Randrianarivo,Emmanuel Malherbe*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LEDGER is a benchmark dataset of 4,999 digitized corporate annual reports with 31 labeled financial KPIs, designed to evaluate long-context capabilities of LLMs across three tasks: KPI retrieval, needle-in-a-haystack lookup, and full KPI extraction, accompanied by OCR-quality annotations and tooling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current financial NLP benchmarks reduce financial reporting to plain-text SEC 10-K filings with limited QA pairs, failing to leverage the very-long-context capabilities of modern LLMs. There is a pressing need for rigorous, multi-modal, long-document evaluation in the financial domain that includes figures, tables, and narrative—not just regulatory text.

Method: The authors construct LEDGER, comprising 4,999 digitized corporate annual reports. Each report is annotated with 31 consolidated financial KPIs linked to market reactions at earnings dates. Three benchmarks of increasing difficulty are derived: (1) a page-level KPI retrieval task with TREC-style relevance judgments over 118,048 natural language questions; (2) a conversational needle-in-a-haystack single-value lookup; and (3) a full KPI extraction task from long, numerically dense reports. Human OCR-quality annotations with inter-annotator agreement metrics and a complete extraction/validation/scoring toolchain are provided.

Result: The paper releases the LEDGER corpus and its associated benchmarks, annotations, and toolchain. A case study demonstrates the dataset's research utility by linking CEO-letter rhetoric to post-publication market impact.

Conclusion: LEDGER fills a critical gap in financial NLP evaluation by providing a rigorous, multi-task, long-context benchmark grounded in real corporate annual reports, enabling more meaningful assessment of LLMs' document understanding capabilities in finance.

Abstract: Finance reporting is a natural proving ground for large language models, and the very-long-context capabilities of recent models across all sizes make rigorous evaluation in this domain an increasingly pressing need. Yet most public financial resources reduce the task to plain-text SEC 10-K filings paired with a handful of question-answer items. We release LEDGER (Long-context Evaluation of Documents for Grounded Extraction and Retrieval), a corpus of 4,999 digitized corporate annual reports - full documents with figures, tables, and narrative, not just regulatory filings. Each report is labeled with 31 consolidated financial KPIs to be extracted and linked to the market's reaction at the earnings date. From this data we derive three evaluation benchmarks spanning the difficulty spectrum: a pure page-level KPI retrieval task with TREC-style relevance judgments over 118,048 questions in natural language, a conversational "needle-in-a-haystack" single-value lookup, and a full KPI extraction task, both from long, numerically dense reports. We additionally provide human OCR-quality annotations with inter-annotator agreement and the complete extraction, validation, and scoring toolchain. We further demonstrate the dataset's research utility with a case study linking CEO-letter rhetoric to post-publication market impact.

</details>


### [32] [MÖVE: A Holistic LLM Benchmark for the German Public Sector](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13111)
*Camilla Dalerci,Thilo Michael,Robin Schaefer,Daniel Weinland*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: MÖVE is a holistic benchmark for evaluating 39 LLMs in the German public sector across both performance (summarization, QA, topic extraction) and governance criteria (hallucination, energy use, transparency, constitutional alignment). No single model excels across all tasks, and model size poorly predicts quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM adoption in public administration lacks principled model selection guidance. Existing benchmarks are English/US-centric, focus solely on task performance, and ignore governance dimensions critical for public-sector deployment, such as transparency, energy consumption, and alignment with constitutional values.

Method: Evaluation of 39 LLMs across 10 German-language datasets (including custom gold- and silver-standard public-administration datasets). Performance is measured via summarization, question answering, and topic extraction using classical NLP metrics, embedding-based methods, and LLM-as-a-judge. Governance is assessed through hallucination rates, energy consumption estimates, provider transparency, and alignment with German constitutional values and political party positions.

Result: No single model dominates across all criteria; top performers vary by task. Model size is a poor predictor of quality. The benchmark itself demonstrates statistical precision, reliable LLM judging, sensitivity to prompt formulation, and validates energy estimates. Private datasets affect model rankings, underscoring the need for domain-specific evaluation.

Conclusion: MÖVE demonstrates that holistic, multi-dimensional evaluation is essential for responsible LLM adoption in the public sector. The benchmark is publicly available and maintained as a living resource to guide evidence-based model selection in German public administration.

Abstract: We present MÖVE (Modelle für die Öffentliche Verwaltung Evaluieren), a holistic benchmark for evaluating large language models (LLMs) in the context of the German public sector. While LLMs are increasingly adopted in public administration, model selection remains largely ad hoc, and existing benchmarks offer limited guidance: they are predominantly English-centric, US-centric in content, and focus exclusively on task performance. MÖVE addresses these gaps by evaluating 39 models across two complementary dimensions. Performance criteria cover summarization, question answering, and topic extraction. Governance criteria assess hallucination tendencies, energy consumption, provider transparency, and alignment with German constitutional values and knowledge about positions by German political parties. In total, we utilize ten German-language datasets, including gold- and silverstandard datasets that we constructed to reflect public-administration domains. We employ a multi-metric evaluation strategy combining classical NLP metrics, embedding-based methods, and LLM-as-a-judge approaches. Our results show that no single model dominates across all criteria: top performers differ between tasks, and model size alone is a poor predictor of quality. We further evaluate the benchmark itself, analyzing its statistical precision, LLM judge reliability, the impact of our private datasets on model rankings, the sensitivity of our results to prompt formulation, and the validity of our energy consumption estimates. MÖVE is designed as a living benchmark under active development; results are publicly available at https://moeve.bundesdruckerei.de/.

</details>


### [33] [G-Long: Graph-Enhanced Memory Management for Efficient Long-Term Dialogue Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13115)
*Minjun Choi,Yoonjin Jang,Sangwon Youn,Youngjoong Ko*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: G-Long is a graph-enhanced framework that uses a fine-tuned small LM for structured triplet extraction and an attention-aware importance scoring mechanism via T5 to maintain long-term conversational consistency, achieving SOTA on response generation and memory retrieval with significantly lower computational cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs struggle with long-term consistency in open-domain dialogue due to poor long-context reasoning and the inefficiency of processing extensive raw text. Existing solutions either use unstructured memory (prone to information loss) or rely on costly LLMs (high latency). There is a need for a structured, efficient memory framework.

Method: G-Long employs two key components: (1) a fine-tuned small Language Model (sLM) that extracts structured triplets from dialogue and performs associative retrieval over a graph-based memory, and (2) a novel attention-aware importance scoring mechanism that leverages the intrinsic cross-attention signals from a T5 summarizer to identify and prioritize salient memories.

Result: G-Long achieves state-of-the-art performance across diverse benchmarks: up to 9.8% improvement in response quality on MSC and 40.8% improvement in retrieval recall on LME, while significantly reducing computational overhead compared to LLM-based alternatives.

Conclusion: Combining structured graph memory with attention-aware importance scoring on a small LM offers a highly effective and efficient solution for long-term conversational consistency, outperforming both unstructured memory approaches and expensive LLM-based methods.

Abstract: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have advanced open-domain dialogue systems, maintaining long-term consistency remains a challenge due to inherent limitations in long-context reasoning and the inefficiency of processing extensive raw text. Existing approaches typically rely on either unstructured memory storage, which is prone to information loss, or computationally expensive LLMs that incur high latency. To address these limitations, we propose G-Long, a graph-enhanced framework that utilizes a fine-tuned small Language Model (sLM) for structured triplet extraction and associative retrieval, significantly reducing operational costs. Furthermore, we introduce the novel attention-aware importance scoring mechanism that leverages the intrinsic cross-attention signals of a T5 summarizer to identify salient memories. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that G-Long achieves state-of-the-art performance in both response generation and memory retrieval, yielding performance gains of up to 9.8% in response quality on MSC and 40.8% in retrieval recall on LME, while significantly minimizing computational overhead.

</details>


### [34] [EvoBrowseComp: Benchmarking Search Agents on Evolving Knowledge](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13120)
*Yunhan Wang,Jiaan Wang,Lianzhe Huang,Xianfeng Zeng,Fandong Meng*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: EvoBrowseComp introduces an evolving, contamination-free benchmark for search-augmented LLMs, synthesized via a three-agent framework that traverses the live web. It features 800 complex QA pairs (400 English, 400 Chinese) and supports fully automated updates to prevent memorization shortcuts, establishing a scalable paradigm for future-proof evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for search agents (e.g., BrowseComp) rely on static knowledge, making them susceptible to test-set contamination and parametric memorization. Models can exploit fact recall rather than genuine retrieval, obscuring true browsing competence via reasoning shortcuts. This creates an urgent need for future-proof, contamination-resistant evaluation benchmarks.

Method: A three-agent collaborative framework: (1) a QA Synthesis Agent retrieves fresh knowledge from the live web to generate QA pairs; (2) an Information Filtering Agent filters retrieved content by credibility and popularity to block parametric shortcuts; (3) a High-Level Guidance Agent formalizes questions into reasoning graphs to reduce logical redundancy and eliminate shortcut-friendly patterns. The fully automated pipeline enables regular benchmark updates. The resulting EvoBrowseComp dataset contains 400 English and 400 Chinese questions.

Result: Extensive experiments confirm EvoBrowseComp's high difficulty, requiring broad horizontal search across the live web. The benchmark effectively prevents shortcut-based solving, forcing models to demonstrate genuine retrieval and reasoning capabilities rather than relying on memorized knowledge.

Conclusion: EvoBrowseComp establishes a scalable, auto-updatable paradigm for high-difficulty benchmarking that keeps pace with evolving world knowledge and advancing agent capabilities, offering a sustainable solution to the contamination problem in search-agent evaluation.

Abstract: Search Agents -- large language models augmented with search tools -- have intensified the need for future-proof evaluation benchmarks. Existing benchmarks such as BrowseComp rely on static knowledge, making them vulnerable to test-set contamination and parametric memorization. Consequently, models can achieve high scores through fact recall rather than genuine retrieval, obscuring true browsing competence via reasoning shortcuts.
  In this paper, we introduce EvoBrowseComp, an evolving benchmark of 400 English and 400 Chinese contamination-free complex questions synthesized via live-web traversal. To collect these questions, we design a three-agent collaborative framework: (1) a QA synthesis agent that retrieves fresh knowledge from the live web to synthesize QA pairs; (2) an information filtering agent that filters retrieved knowledge in terms of credibility and popularity to block parametric shortcuts; and (3) a high-level guidance agent that formalizes questions into reasoning graphs to reduce logical redundancy and shortcuts in synthesized QA pairs. Because the framework supports fully automated synthesis, EvoBrowseComp can be regularly updated to prevent data contamination and maintain temporal freshness. Extensive experiments confirm its great difficulty, requiring broad horizontal search. It establishes a scalable paradigm for auto-updatable, high-difficulty benchmarking that keeps pace with both evolving world knowledge and advancing agent capabilities.

</details>


### [35] [NaturalFlow: Reducing Disruptive Pauses for Natural Speech Flow in Simultaneous Speech-to-Speech Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13121)
*Dongwook Lee,Youngho Cho,Sangkwon Park,Heeseung Kim,Sungroh Yoon*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A fluency-aware optimization framework for simultaneous speech-to-speech translation that reduces inter-chunk silences by leveraging linguistic diversity and speech duration variability, achieving natural speech flow while maintaining low latency and translation quality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Simultaneous speech-to-speech translation systems pursue extremely low latency, but this causes fragmented, chunk-wise speech output with frequent unnatural pauses that increase listener cognitive load. The goal is to find an optimal balance between the low latency of simultaneous translation and the natural acoustic flow of consecutive translation.

Method: A fluency-aware optimization framework that minimizes inter-chunk silences using two model-internal signals: (1) linguistic diversity and (2) induced temporal variability in speech durations. The framework searches for a sweet spot between latency and fluency.

Result: Experiments on both short-form and long-form benchmarks demonstrate that the proposed framework produces natural speech flow while maintaining competitive latency and translation quality compared to existing approaches.

Conclusion: The fluency-aware optimization framework successfully bridges the gap between simultaneous and consecutive translation, delivering natural-sounding speech output without sacrificing the low-latency advantages of simultaneous systems.

Abstract: Simultaneous speech-to-speech translation aims to enable near-real-time communication by minimizing latency, offering a compelling, real-time alternative to the high latency of consecutive translation. However, the excessive pursuit of low latency often results in fragmented chunk-wise speech. Consequently, listeners are subjected to an unnatural acoustic flow punctuated by frequent pauses, which could increase their cognitive load. To bridge this gap, we introduce a fluency-aware optimization framework designed to discover the sweet spot between the low-latency benefits of simultaneous translation and the natural flow of consecutive translation. Our framework minimizes inter-chunk silences by leveraging model-internal signals, including linguistic diversity and induced temporal variability in speech durations. Experiments on short- and long-form benchmarks show that our framework produces natural speech flow while maintaining competitive latency and translation quality.

</details>


### [36] [HyPE: Category-Aware Hypergraph Encoding with Persistent Edge Embeddings for Persona-Grounded Dialogue](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13142)
*Sangwon Youn,Yoonjin Jang,Youngjoong Ko*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: HyPE uses hypergraph neural networks with structured persona quadruples (Core, Expression, Sentiment, Category) and Persistent Edge Embeddings to improve persona consistency in dialogue generation, outperforming flat sentence-level baselines across multiple backbone models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing persona-grounded dialogue systems treat persona sentences as flat, independent units, ignoring high-order relational structure such as shared topical categories among persona attributes. This limits the model's ability to capture richer persona representations.

Method: HyPE decomposes each persona sentence into a (Core, Expression, Sentiment, Category) quadruple and organizes these elements into a hypergraph where hyperedges connect elements sharing category labels. An HyperGCN propagates structural information into a persona summary vector and a soft-memory bank for conditioning the response generator. Persistent Edge Embeddings (PEE) are introduced as learnable per-category priors fused during message passing.

Result: Under greedy decoding on PersonaChat, HyPE consistently outperforms sentence-level pooling baselines with GPT-2, LLaMA-3.2-3B, and Qwen2.5-3B backbones, showing that structured hyperedge-level encoding transfers across model scales.

Conclusion: Structured hyperedge-level persona encoding via hypergraph neural networks provides a scalable, transferable advantage for persona-grounded dialogue generation, outperforming flat aggregation methods.

Abstract: Persona-grounded dialogue systems aim to produce responses consistent with a speaker's persona, yet existing methods treat personas as a flat set of sentences and fail to model the high-order relations among persona attributes-e.g., that several persona sentences share a topical category. We propose HyPE (Hypergraph Persona Encoder), a framework that (i) analyzes each persona-bearing text as a (Core, Expression, Sentiment, Category) quadruple, and (ii) organizes persona elements into a hypergraph whose hyperedges are induced by shared category labels. An HyperGCN hypergraph neural network propagates this structure into a persona summary vector and a soft-memory bank that condition the response generator. We further propose Persistent Edge Embeddings (PEE), lightweight per-category learnable priors fused into the HyperGCN message-passing step. On PersonaChat under greedy decoding, HyPE consistently outperforms sentence-level pooling baselines across GPT-2, LLaMA-3.2-3B, and Qwen2.5-3B backbones by demonstrating that structured hyperedge-level persona encoding provides a transferable advantage across model scales.

</details>


### [37] [NTS-CoT: Mitigating Hallucinations in LLM-based News Timeline Summarization with Chain-of-Thought Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13171)
*Feng Lyu,Huiqin Yan,Sijing Duan,Hao Wu,Shuang Gu,Xue Qiao,Weixu Zhang,Haolun Wu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: NTS-CoT is a Chain-of-Thought framework for timeline summarization that mitigates two types of LLM hallucinations — unfaithful content in news summarization and information omission in date-event summarization — using Element-CoT, Date Selection, and Causal-CoT modules, outperforming baselines on three benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM-based timeline summarization (TLS) suffers from hallucinations, which are understudied. The authors identify two key hallucination types: (1) unfaithful content generation during individual news summarization, and (2) information omission when aggregating events along a timeline. Bridging this gap motivates the development of a dedicated hallucination-mitigation framework.

Method: NTS-CoT employs three modules: (i) Element-CoT uses Chain-of-Thought prompting to extract essential news elements (who, what, when, where, why) for faithful summarization; (ii) Date Selection combines temporal saliency and event prominence to select key timestamps; and (iii) Causal-CoT infers causal relationships between events to reduce omissions in date-event summarization.

Result: Quantitative experiments on three TLS benchmarks and human evaluation show NTS-CoT outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, effectively reducing both types of hallucinations and improving overall TLS performance.

Conclusion: NTS-CoT demonstrates that structured Chain-of-Thought reasoning — targeting elemental extraction, temporal saliency, and causal inference — is an effective strategy for mitigating hallucinations in LLM-based timeline summarization.

Abstract: The rapid updates of online news make tracking event developments challenging, highlighting the need for timeline summarization (TLS). Hallucinations, where LLM-generated content deviates from source news, still remain a critical issue in LLM-based TLS and are not well studied in existing works. To bridge this gap, we identify two primary types of hallucinations: unfaithful content during news summarization and information omission in date-event summarization. Then, we propose NTS-CoT, a novel framework that leverages Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning to mitigate hallucinations in TLS. The framework consists of three key modules: i) Element-CoT to capture essential news elements for faithful summarization, ii) Date Selection to combine temporal saliency and event prominence for timestamp selection, and iii) Causal-CoT to infer causal relationships and reduce omissions in date-event summarization. Extensive experiments, including quantitative analysis on three TLS benchmarks and human evaluation, demonstrate that NTS-CoT outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, effectively mitigating hallucinations and improving LLM-based TLS performance. Our source code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/NTS-CoT .

</details>


### [38] [MemRefine: LLM-Guided Compression for Long-Term Agent Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13177)
*Minjae Kim,Jinheon Baek,Soyeong Jeong,Sung Ju Hwang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: MemRefine is an LLM-guided memory management framework that keeps a long-term agent's memory store within a fixed storage budget by using similarity-based candidate pairing and LLM-judged delete/merge/preserve decisions, outperforming rule-based baselines under tight budgets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents in long-term interactions face unbounded memory growth with redundant entries, inflating storage costs and degrading retrieval quality. This is especially problematic on resource-constrained platforms with hard memory budgets, yet no existing work addresses storage-budgeted memory management for already constructed memory stores.

Method: MemRefine uses surface similarity only to propose candidate pairs from the memory store, then defers the actual delete, merge, or preserve decisions to an LLM judge that evaluates factual content. The process iterates over candidate pairs until the target storage budget is met, ensuring only information useful for future interactions is retained.

Result: Across multiple memory frameworks and long-term conversation benchmarks, MemRefine consistently meets target storage budgets while preserving downstream task performance, significantly outperforming rule-based baselines, particularly under tight budget constraints.

Conclusion: MemRefine provides an effective solution for storage-budgeted memory management in LLM agents, demonstrating that LLM-based factual reasoning for memory curation is superior to surface-similarity heuristics, enabling deployment on resource-constrained platforms without sacrificing performance.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly expected to operate over long-term interactions, where information from past dialogues must be preserved and recalled to support future tasks. However, as interactions accumulate, the memory store grows without bound and fills with redundant entries that inflate storage cost and degrade retrieval by crowding out the most useful evidence. Furthermore, this is especially limiting on resource-constrained platforms with hard memory budgets, motivating us to formulate storage-budgeted memory management, the task of keeping an already constructed memory store within a fixed budget while preserving information useful for future interactions. To this end, we then propose MemRefine, an LLM-guided framework that, since surface similarity poorly reflects factual value, uses similarity only to propose candidate pairs and defers delete, merge, and preserve decisions to an LLM judge based on factual content, iterating until the budget is met. Across multiple memory frameworks and long-term conversation benchmarks, MemRefine consistently meets target budgets while preserving downstream performance and outperforming rule-based baselines under tight budgets.

</details>


### [39] [LAUKIN: A Multi-jurisdictional Common Law Contract Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13184)
*Amrita Singh,Aditya Joshi,Jiaojiao Jiang,Hye-young Paik,May Fong Cheong*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: LAUKIN is a cross-jurisdictional legal equivalence dataset spanning Australia, UK, and India, comprising 14,727 clause pairs (3,000 manually labelled) from 204 contracts. A multi-stage retrieval-reranking pipeline was used for construction, and 12 models evaluated achieved a best macro-F1 of 65.11%, revealing that cross-jurisdictional equivalence classification is challenging even among jurisdictions sharing legal heritage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multinational companies need cross-jurisdictional contract review, but existing legal NLP datasets are restricted to single jurisdictions, limiting the development of models capable of determining whether clauses from different jurisdictions are legally equivalent.

Method: A multi-stage retrieval and reranking pipeline constructs initial clause pair mappings across three jurisdictions (AU-UK, UK-IN, IN-AU). A subset is manually annotated by legal experts for boolean legal equivalence (Equivalent/Not Equivalent). The final dataset contains 3,000 labelled pairs (900 train, 600 dev, 1,500 test) and 11,727 unlabelled pairs. Twelve models across four techniques are benchmarked on the dataset.

Result: The best-performing model achieves a macro-F1 of 65.11%, indicating the task is challenging. Results demonstrate that despite shared legal heritage (common law tradition), drafting conventions diverge significantly across jurisdictions, making cross-jurisdictional equivalence classification non-trivial.

Conclusion: LAUKIN establishes a challenging benchmark for cross-jurisdictional legal clause equivalence. The significant divergence in drafting conventions across Australia, UK, and India — despite shared legal heritage — underscores the difficulty of the task. The inclusion of unlabelled data also paves the way for future semi-supervised learning research in legal NLP.

Abstract: Multinational companies increasingly require cross-jurisdictional contract review, yet existing legal NLP datasets are largely restricted to a single jurisdiction. We introduce LAUKIN (Legal equivalence dataset of Australia, UK, and INdia), a dataset of clause pairs (AU-UK, UK-IN, IN-AU) labelled for boolean legal equivalence. We develop a novel multi-stage retrieval and reranking pipeline to construct the initial clause pair mapping, with a subset of clause pairs subsequently annotated by legal experts as Equivalent or Not Equivalent. The dataset comprises 14,727 clause pairs from 204 contracts across 8 agreement types, of which 3,000 are manually labelled: 900 train, 600 dev, and 1,500 test. We evaluate 12 models across 4 techniques, achieving a best macro-F1 of 65.11%, establishing LAUKIN as a challenging benchmark. Results reveal that, despite shared legal heritage, drafting conventions diverge significantly across jurisdictions, making cross-jurisdictional equivalence classification non-trivial. LAUKIN also includes 11,727 unlabelled training pairs to support future semi-supervised learning research in legal NLP.

</details>


### [40] [A Context-Aware Dataset for Stance Detection in Bioethical Controversies on Reddit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13187)
*Hu Huang,Genan Dai,Fuqiang Niu,Yi Yang,Zhaoya Gong,Bowen Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: BioStance is a new dataset of 39,600 annotated Reddit Post-Comment pairs for stance detection in bioethical discourse, covering six controversial targets across three ethical dimensions with high inter-annotator reliability (α=0.82).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Bioethical debates are increasingly conducted on social media, but stance detection research lacks large-scale, domain-specific datasets that capture the context-dependent nature of such discourse. Existing resources fail to address the unique challenges of bioethical argumentation.

Method: The authors constructed BioStance by collecting Post-Comment pairs from Reddit discussions on bioethics, covering six targets across three dimensions: (1) fundamental value conflicts, (2) individual liberty vs. collective responsibility, and (3) technological uncertainty. Each instance preserves hierarchical conversational context and is triple-annotated using a three-class scheme (Favor, Against, None).

Result: The dataset comprises 39,600 annotated pairs. Three independent annotators per instance achieved a mean Krippendorff's α of 0.82, indicating substantial inter-annotator reliability.

Conclusion: BioStance, with its thematic diversity, conversational structure, and high-quality annotations, provides a valuable resource for advancing context-aware stance detection, argument mining, and computational analysis of bioethical discourse on social media.

Abstract: Bioethical debates increasingly unfold on social media, yet stance detection research lacks large-scale, domain-specific resources for modeling such context-dependent discourse. We present BioStance, a context-aware dataset of 39,600 annotated Post-Comment pairs from Reddit bioethical discussions. BioStance covers six controversial targets across three dimensions of bioethical controversy: fundamental value conflicts, individual liberty versus collective responsibility, and technological uncertainty. Each instance preserves hierarchical conversational context and is labeled by three independent annotators using a three-class stance scheme: Favor, Against, and None. The annotations achieve a mean Krippendorff's $α$ of 0.82, indicating substantial reliability. By combining thematic diversity, conversational structure, and high-quality human annotation, BioStance supports research on context-aware stance detection, argument mining, and computational analysis of bioethical discourse.

</details>


### [41] [SICI: A Semantic-Pragmatic Complexity Index Reveals Regime Shifts in LLM Stance Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13189)
*Fuqiang Niu,Bowen Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces SICI, a seven-dimensional metric measuring semantic-pragmatic complexity in stance detection tasks. SICI reliably predicts LLM accuracy and reveals phase-transition-like error patterns across model scales, while showing that common interventions (prompting, retrieval, debate) merely shift models along an attribution–abstention axis rather than resolving high-complexity failures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based stance detection struggles with hard examples that resist improvement from clearer instructions, reasoning prompts, retrieval augmentation, or debate mechanisms. There is a lack of diagnostic tools to quantify exactly what makes an example hard, motivating a principled complexity measure grounded in semantic-pragmatic theory.

Method: SICI (Stance Inference Complexity Index) is a seven-dimensional diagnostic measure capturing the semantic-pragmatic burden of a target–text pair. It is validated against SemEval-2016 and VAST datasets, tested for cross-scorer reliability (Krippendorff's α=0.771), and used to analyze error regimes across GPT-3.5, GPT-4o-mini, DeepSeek-V3, and GPT-4o. A 15-method intervention study examines how prompting, retrieval, and debate strategies affect model behavior along the complexity axis.

Result: SICI outperforms surface-level proxies in predicting LLM accuracy. LLM errors exhibit a phase-transition structure: low-complexity examples suffer from over-attribution (especially Against), intermediate complexity forms an unstable decision boundary, and high complexity concentrates on None predictions. This pattern is consistent across all tested models, though stronger models shift the phase boundaries. Intervention experiments reveal that prompting, retrieval, and debate primarily shift models along the attribution–abstention axis without eliminating the high-complexity bottleneck.

Conclusion: SICI provides a principled, reliable framework for diagnosing stance detection difficulty. The phase-transition error structure suggests fundamental limitations in current LLM reasoning that are not overcome by existing intervention methods, pointing to the need for new approaches targeting the high-complexity regime.

Abstract: Prompt-based LLMs are increasingly used for stance detection, but harder examples are not always repaired by clearer instructions, reasoning prompts, retrieval, or debate. We introduce SICI (Stance Inference Complexity Index), a seven-dimensional diagnostic measure of the semantic-pragmatic burden imposed by a target--text pair. Across SemEval-2016 and VAST, SICI predicts LLM accuracy better than surface proxies and shows substantial cross-scorer reliability ($α=0.771$). More importantly, LLM errors change regime as SICI increases: low-complexity examples invite over-attribution, especially Against predictions; intermediate examples form an unstable boundary; and high-complexity examples rapidly concentrate on None. This phase-transition-like structure persists across GPT-3.5, GPT-4o-mini, DeepSeek-V3, and GPT-4o, although stronger models move the boundaries. A 15-method intervention study further shows that prompting, retrieval, and debate often shift models along the attribution--abstention axis rather than removing the high-complexity bottleneck.

</details>


### [42] [Layer-Resolved Optimal Transport for Hallucination Detection in NMT and Abstractive Summarization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13216)
*Mariia Onyshchuk,Maksym-Vasyl Tarnavskyi,Marta Sumyk*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper extends optimal transport (OT) analysis of cross-attention beyond NMT hallucination detection to abstractive summarization faithfulness, finding that OT is a reliable detector only when the failure mode is "source disengagement" (attention wandering), but fundamentally limited when unfaithful summaries attend correctly yet misrepresent content — a downstream failure invisible to attention-based metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work showed OT on cross-attention can detect NMT hallucinations without supervision. The authors aim to: (1) conduct a deeper layer-wise analysis of this phenomenon across all decoder layers and hallucination types, and (2) test whether the geometric signal generalizes to a different task — abstractive summarization faithfulness detection — probing the fundamental limits of attention-based faithfulness metrics.

Method: The authors analyze the Fairseq DE-EN NMT model (N=3,414) layer-by-layer using Wasserstein-to-Uniform and Wasserstein-to-Data distances on cross-attention distributions, characterizing detection performance per layer and per hallucination type. They then construct an unsupervised OT-based faithfulness detector evaluated on the AggreFact benchmark (N=1,116) for CNN/DM and XSum summaries, comparing against the supervised MiniCheck-Flan-T5-L baseline. Structural experiments on T5-base examine layer-wise concentration patterns and the effect of dropping individual cross-attention layers on generation quality.

Result: In NMT: Wass-to-Unif and Wass-to-Data are complementary detectors specialized across hallucination types; detection concentrates in layers L1–L4, with L5 anti-predictive for subtler hallucinations; correct translations exhibit an "exploratory attention phase" from the first decoding step that hallucinated translations lack. In summarization: the unsupervised OT detector achieves 57.2%/57.6% balanced accuracy on CNN/XSum, well above chance but substantially below the supervised MiniCheck baseline (69.9%/74.3%). Structural experiments on T5-base reveal Layer 3 has peak concentration, Layer 12 is most critical for generation quality, and consistent decoder organization across depth.

Conclusion: OT on cross-attention is a principled, interpretable tool for detecting source disengagement failures, but is fundamentally limited when unfaithfulness arises from correct-attention-yet-incorrect-content failures downstream of the attention mechanism. This establishes both the value and the inherent ceiling of purely attention-geometric approaches to faithfulness detection.

Abstract: Optimal transport (OT) has been shown to detect hallucinations in neural machine translation (NMT) by measuring the geometric distance between cross-attention distributions and a reference distribution, without any supervision. We extend this analysis to all six decoder layers of the Fairseq DE-EN model ($N=3{,}414$), showing that Wass-to-Unif and Wass-to-Data are complementary detectors specialised across hallucination types, that detection is concentrated in layers L1--L4 with L5 anti-predictive for subtler types, and that hallucinated translations lack the exploratory attention phase present in correct translations from the first decoding step. We further evaluate whether the geometric signal transfers to abstractive summarization faithfulness detection: our unsupervised OT detector on AggreFact ($N=1{,}116$) achieves $57.2\%$/$57.6\%$ balanced accuracy on CNN/XSum -- above chance but substantially below supervised MiniCheck-Flan-T5-L($69.9\%$/$74.3\%$). This gap is principled: unlike NMT hallucinations, unfaithful summaries can attend correctly to source tokens while misrepresenting their content, a failure mode invisible to concentration-based OT metrics by construction. Structural experiments on T5-base confirm consistent decoder organisation across depth, with Layer~3 showing peak concentration and Layer~12 being most critical for generation quality. Together, the results establish OT on cross-attention as a reliable detector when the failure mode is source disengagement, a principled interpretability tool regardless of task, and fundamentally limited when faithfulness failures occur downstream of attention.

</details>


### [43] [When Similar Means Different: Evaluating LLMs on Arabic--Hebrew Cognates](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13218)
*Junhong Liang,Noor Abo Mokh,Bashar Alhafni*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SemCog Bench is a benchmark of 1,858 Arabic–Hebrew word pairs testing LLMs on cognate identification and semantic disambiguation. Models rely heavily on surface-form similarity, performing well on true cognates but poorly on false friends and loanwords, with sentence context providing only modest gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Arabic and Hebrew share many true cognates, false friends, and loanwords due to their close Semitic relationship. This lexical overlap challenges LLMs' cross-lingual semantic understanding, yet no rigorous benchmark existed to systematically evaluate this capability.

Method: The authors introduce SemCog Bench, a curated benchmark of 1,858 Arabic–Hebrew word pairs with sentence-level annotations. They evaluate multiple open-source and commercial LLMs across four input representations (raw, diacritized, Romanized, phonetic) on two tasks: cognate identification and semantic disambiguation.

Result: LLMs achieve high accuracy on true cognates but performance drops significantly on false friends and loanwords, indicating over-reliance on surface-form similarity. Sentence-level context provides only modest improvements, showing that contextual cues alone cannot overcome misleading form-based signals.

Conclusion: Current LLMs exhibit a fundamental limitation in resolving cross-lingual form–meaning conflicts between Arabic and Hebrew. SemCog Bench serves as a rigorous benchmark for evaluating and improving multilingual semantic reasoning in LLMs.

Abstract: Arabic and Hebrew, as closely related Semitic languages, share a substantial lexicon of true cognates, misleading false friends, and modern loanwords. This overlap poses a challenge for cross-lingual semantic understanding in large language models (LLMs). To evaluate this capability, we introduce SemCog Bench, a curated benchmark of 1,858 Arabic--Hebrew word pairs with sentence-level annotations for cognate identification and semantic disambiguation. We evaluate open-source and commercial LLMs across multiple input representations (raw, diacritized, Romanized, and phonetic) and reveal a critical gap in cross-lingual reasoning. While models achieve high accuracy on true cognates, performance drops sharply on false friends and loanwords, reflecting a strong reliance on surface-form similarity. Furthermore, sentence-level context yields only modest improvements, suggesting that contextual cues alone are insufficient to overcome misleading form-based signals. These findings reveal a fundamental limitation of current LLMs in resolving cross-lingual form--meaning conflicts and establish SemCog Bench as a rigorous benchmark for multilingual semantic reasoning. Our code and data are publicly available.

</details>


### [44] [PolyAlign: Conditional Human-Distribution Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13227)
*L. D. M. S. Sai Teja,Ufaq Khan,Sathira Silva,Xiao Wu,Muhammad Haris Khan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: PolyAlign is a distribution-aware alignment framework that moves beyond global assistant behavior by aligning language models to conditional human response distributions based on language, task, and dialogue context. It combines Bucket-Aware SFT with Human-Distribution Preference Optimization (HDPO) to improve naturalness and distributional faithfulness in bilingual settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard SFT and preference optimization align models toward a single global assistant behavior, suppressing the natural variation in human responses across languages, tasks, and dialogue settings. The authors argue that models should match the human response distribution appropriate to the current interaction context rather than a universal style.

Method: PolyAlign organizes bilingual interaction data into buckets defined by language, interaction track, response family, and length, establishing bucket-specific human reference distributions. It employs two components: (1) Bucket-Aware SFT, which balances optimization across heterogeneous buckets to prevent dominant distributions from overwhelming minority ones; and (2) Human-Distribution Preference Optimization (HDPO), which regularizes preference learning by using a critic-estimated distance to the bucket-specific human support, penalizing generations that deviate from the target distribution.

Result: Across a bilingual evaluation suite covering English and Chinese single- and multi-turn settings, PolyAlign improves conditional naturalness and distributional faithfulness while preserving competitive task utility compared to global alignment baselines.

Conclusion: Post-training should move beyond global alignment objectives toward interaction-aware alignment that respects the diversity of human response distributions across different contexts.

Abstract: Post-training methods such as supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and preference optimization typically align language models toward a single global assistant behavior. While effective for improving average helpfulness, this can suppress the natural variation of human responses across languages, tasks, and dialogue settings. We study this problem as conditional human-distribution alignment: models should match the human response distribution appropriate to the current interaction context, rather than a universal response style. We introduce PolyAlign, a distribution-aware alignment framework that organizes bilingual interaction data into bucket-specific human reference distributions defined by language, interaction track, response family, and length. PolyAlign combines Bucket-Aware SFT, which balances optimization across heterogeneous buckets, with Human-Distribution Preference Optimization (HDPO), which regularizes preference learning using critic-estimated distance to bucket-specific human support. Across a bilingual evaluation suite covering English and Chinese single- and multi-turn settings, PolyAlign improves conditional naturalness and distributional faithfulness while preserving competitive task utility. The results suggest that post-training should move beyond global alignment objectives toward interaction-aware alignment with human response distributions.

</details>


### [45] [Evaluating Pluralism in LLMs through Latent Perspectives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13254)
*Laura Majer,Jan Šnajder,Martin Tutek*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper introduces a domain-agnostic, multi-layered framework for unsupervised extraction of perspectives from text, evaluates it on book reviews, and finds that while some LLMs and prompting techniques approach broad perspective coverage, rarer perspectives remain disproportionately underrepresented compared to human-written text.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The growing demand for pluralistic LLM generation lacks clear operationalization. Identifying perspectives in text would provide concrete guidance for pluralistic alignment and help quantify the "pluralistic gap" — the divergence between LLM-generated and human-produced distributions of viewpoints. Prior work on homogeneity in LLM outputs has relied mainly on multiple-choice questionnaires or high-level text characteristics, rather than directly extracting fine-grained perspectives from free-form text.

Method: The authors propose a domain-agnostic, multi-layered framework for unsupervised extraction of perspectives from text. The framework is evaluated on book reviews — a highly opinionated dataset with diverse viewpoints. Multiple LLMs and prompting strategies are compared against human-written reviews to assess how well generated text covers the spectrum of perspectives, with particular attention to rare or minority viewpoints.

Result: Some models and prompting techniques come close to covering a broad spectrum of perspectives, but rare perspectives remain disproportionately underrepresented in LLM-generated text. This leads to perspective distributions that diverge significantly from those found in human-written reviews.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively surfaces the pluralistic gap in LLM generation by revealing systematic underrepresentation of minority perspectives. This highlights an important limitation in current LLM alignment and generation practices, and provides a tool for diagnosing and potentially mitigating perspective homogenization.

Abstract: The growing need to represent diverse perspectives has increased interest in pluralistic LLM generation. Although difficult to operationalize, identifying perspectives expressed in text would provide clear guidance on pluralistic alignment and more clearly articulate the pluralistic gap in LLM generation. While models have been shown to reduce the diversity of training data and generate homogeneously, this has been demonstrated primarily on multiple-choice questionnaires or using high-level characteristics of free-form text. In this paper, we introduce and implement a domain-agnostic multi-layered framework for unsupervised extraction of perspectives suitable for identifying the pluralistic gap in LLM-generated text. We evaluate our framework on book reviews, a highly opinionated dataset representing diverse perspectives, and compare various prompts and models. Our results show that while some models and prompting techniques come close to covering a broad spectrum of perspectives, rarer perspectives remain disproportionately underrepresented, resulting in distributions that diverge from human text.

</details>


### [46] [SkillCAT: Contrastive Assessment and Topology-Aware Skill Self-Evolution for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13317)
*Kunfeng Chen,Qihuang Zhong,Juhua Liu,Bo Du*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SkillCAT is a training-free framework for LLM agent skill self-evolution that introduces three novel stages: Contrastive Causal Extraction (multi-trajectory comparison), Assessment-Augmented Evolution (patch validation before merging), and Topology-Aware Task Execution (routable sub-skill topology for efficient inference), achieving up to 40.40% improvement over baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current skill self-evolution pipelines for LLM agents suffer from three key limitations: (1) learning from only one trajectory per task limits robustness, (2) merging candidate skill patches before verifying them introduces noise, and (3) loading the full skill corpus at inference time is inefficient. These shortcomings motivate a more principled, staged approach.

Method: SkillCAT is a training-free framework with three stages: (1) Contrastive Causal Extraction (CCE) — samples multiple trajectories per task and contrasts same-task success/failure pairs to isolate outcome-relevant evidence; (2) Assessment-Augmented Evolution (AAE) — replays each candidate skill patch on source-task clones, retaining only patches that maintain or improve outcomes before hierarchical merging; (3) Topology-Aware Task Execution (TTE) — compiles evolved skills into a routable sub-skill topology, loading only task-relevant capability nodes during inference.

Result: SkillCAT was evaluated on SpreadsheetBench, WikiTableQuestions, and DocVQA, along with cross-model and out-of-distribution generalization tests. It raised the average score over baselines by up to 40.40%.

Conclusion: SkillCAT demonstrates that reliable skill evolution for LLM agents can be achieved without model training by systematically improving trajectory sampling, patch validation, and inference-time skill retrieval through its three-stage design.

Abstract: Skill self-evolution methods for LLM agents aim to turn execution trajectories into reusable skill documents, but current pipelines typically learn from one trajectory per task, merge candidate skill patches before checking them, and load the full skill corpus before inference. We propose SkillCAT, a training-free framework that separates this process into three stages. Contrastive Causal Extraction (CCE) samples multiple trajectories for each task and compares same-task success/failure pairs to identify evidence that explains outcome differences. Assessment-Augmented Evolution (AAE) replays each candidate patch on source-task clones and keeps only patches that improve or preserve task outcomes before hierarchical skill patch merging. Topology-Aware Task Execution (TTE) compiles the evolved skills into a routable sub-skill topology, so inference loads only the capability nodes relevant to the task. We evaluate SkillCAT on common agent benchmarks, including SpreadsheetBench, WikiTableQuestions, and DocVQA, and further test cross-model and out-of-distribution generalization. Across these settings, SkillCAT raises the average score over baselines by up to 40.40%, demonstrating reliable skill evolution without model training.

</details>


### [47] [Low-Latency Real-Time Audio Game Commentary System via LLM-Based Parallel Text Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13322)
*Ryota Kawamatsu,Anum Afzal,Yuki Saito,Shinnosuke Takamichi,Graham Neubig,Katsuhito Sudoh,Hiroya Takamura,Tatsuya Ishigaki*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A real-time audio game commentary system that parallelizes text generation with speech playback to reduce inter-utterance silence from 9.6s to 0.3s, significantly improving speaking rhythm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional sequential pipelines for live game commentary (capture→generate text→synthesize speech, then wait for playback to finish before next generation) introduce accumulated latency resulting in long, unnatural silences between utterances — a critical flaw for real-time commentary where timing and rhythm matter.

Method: Parallel architecture where text generation runs concurrently with speech playback. The system buffers multiple candidate utterances ahead of time, enabling immediate speech synthesis at playback boundaries rather than waiting for each generation-playback cycle to complete sequentially.

Result: Mean inter-utterance silence reduced from 9.6s (sequential baseline) to 0.3s. Over 40% improvement in similarity to professional speaking silence-timing patterns. A user study with 120 experienced gamers confirmed significantly better perceived speaking rhythm.

Conclusion: Parallelizing text generation with speech playback via utterance buffering effectively eliminates the latency bottleneck in end-to-end live commentary systems, making real-time AI commentary practically viable for fast-paced game scenarios.

Abstract: We present a low-latency real-time audio game commentary system that generates spoken commentary directly from live gameplay video. In this end-to-end setting, a key bottleneck is accumulated waiting time; conventional pipelines capture frames, generate text, and synthesize speech sequentially for each utterance, and do not request the next generation until speech playback has completed. This strict sequentiality causes long and unnatural silence between utterances. To address this latency bottleneck, our system runs text generation in parallel with speech playback and buffers multiple candidate utterances ahead of time, enabling immediate synthesis at playback boundaries. Experiments on fast-paced game videos show that our parallel design reduces the mean inter-utterance silence from 9.6 seconds to 0.3 seconds compared to sequential baselines. It also improves similarity to professional speaking--silence timing patterns by over 40 %, and a user study with 120 experienced game players confirms significantly improved perceived speaking rhythm. Our demo video is available at: https://youtu.be/pmrRUlvav8M.

</details>


### [48] [IVIE: A Neuro-symbolic Approach to Incremental and Validated Generation of Interactive Fiction Worlds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13348)
*Micaela Vaucher,Santiago Silveira,Santiago Góngora,Luis Chiruzzo*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: IVIE is a neuro-symbolic system for generating interactive fiction that combines LLM-driven creativity with symbolic validation to produce coherent, playable worlds. It uses a four-stage incremental pipeline, achieving high player engagement but facing challenges with LLM inconsistencies and validation gaps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interactive Fiction generation faces a trade-off: LLMs produce creative narratives but lack world coherence, while symbolic systems ensure consistency but are inflexible. There is a need for an approach that balances creative flexibility with narrative coherence.

Method: IVIE extends the PAYADOR neuro-symbolic framework with a four-stage incremental generation pipeline: (1) setting and character creation, (2) puzzle design, all delegated to LLMs, while grounding world state through symbolic validation. The system produces worlds with interconnected locations, functional items, NPCs, and coherent puzzles around a goal-oriented architecture.
