Result: Human evaluation showed IVIE generates immersive, thematically coherent worlds with high player engagement. The neuro-symbolic approach successfully balances flexibility with narrative coherence. However, LLM inconsistencies occasionally bypass puzzle constraints, and objective validation gaps allow some structurally impossible goals.

Conclusion: Neuro-symbolic methods effectively balance generative freedom and narrative coherence for interactive fiction, but challenges remain with LLM reliability and validation completeness. The authors identify design considerations for future systems, particularly regarding LLM capabilities and limitations.

Abstract: Computational creativity in Interactive Fiction faces a fundamental tension: Large Language Models (LLM) may produce creative narratives but struggle with world coherence, while symbolic systems ensure consistency but lack creative flexibility. We present IVIE (Incremental & Validated Interactive Experiences), a neuro-symbolic approach to generating complete and playable interactive fiction worlds from scratch. Building upon PAYADOR's neuro-symbolic framework, IVIE implements a four-stage incremental generation pipeline that delegates creative decisions--setting and character creation, puzzle design--to LLMs while grounding the world state through symbolic validation. The system generates worlds with interconnected locations, functional items, non-player characters, and coherent puzzles, all structured around a central goal-oriented architecture. Human evaluation shows the approach generates immersive, thematically coherent worlds with high player engagement. Results seem to indicate that the neuro-symbolic approach successfully balances flexibility with narrative coherence: symbolic validation grounds LLM generation without eliminating generative freedom. However, challenges remain: LLM inconsistencies occasionally bypass puzzle constraints, and objective validation gaps allow some structurally impossible goals. We identify key design considerations for future neurosymbolic interactive storytelling systems, particularly regarding LLM capabilities and their limitations.

</details>


### [49] [From Passive Generation to Investigation: A Proactive Scientific Peer Review Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13349)
*Haishuo Fang,Yue Feng,Iryna Gurevych*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ProReviewer introduces an LLM-based peer review agent that uses a Markov Decision Process framework with a structured review log to proactively investigate papers, achieving state-of-the-art review quality by outperforming larger frontier models and fine-tuned baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based peer review systems lack the ability to proactively investigate suspicious parts of a paper based on accumulating evidence — a behavior characteristic of human reviewers. This limits their capacity to generate in-depth, evidence-backed reviews.

Method: The paper formulates proactive investigation as a Markov Decision Process (MDP) and proposes ProReviewer, an agent guided by a structured review log that serves as a workspace to track evidence and intermediate findings. The model uses an 8B backbone, trained via supervised fine-tuning and optimized with reinforcement learning.

Result: ProReviewer achieves the highest average score across five quality dimensions, outperforming prompt-based methods with much larger frontier LLMs by up to 39% relatively and the strongest fine-tuned baseline by 16% relatively. It also attains the highest win rates in human evaluation.

Conclusion: Enabling LLM-based review agents to proactively investigate papers via an MDP formulation and a structured review log significantly improves review depth and evidence support, yielding reviews that rival or surpass those from much larger models.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have shown promise in automating scientific peer review. However, existing approaches often struggle to generate in-depth reviews supported by concrete evidence. We argue that a key limitation is the lack of flexibility to proactively investigate suspicious parts of a paper based on accumulated evidence, as human reviewers do. In this paper, we explore how to enable an LLM-based review agent to perform such proactive investigation. We find that this can be naturally formulated as a Markov Decision Process (MDP), and propose ProReviewer, a scientific peer review agent that proactively reviews a paper guided by a maintained, structured review log. The structured review log serves as a workspace for the agent to track evidence and intermediate findings collected during review. Experiments show that ProReviewer with an 8B backbone, trained by supervised fine-tuning and optimized by reinforcement learning, achieves the highest average score across five quality dimensions, outperforming prompt-based methods with much larger frontier LLMs by up to 39% and the strongest fine-tuned baseline by 16% relatively. It also attains the highest win rates against baselines in human evaluation.

</details>


### [50] [An End-to-End Hybrid Framework for Rumour Detection in Low-Resources Algerian Dialect](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13411)
*Dihia Lanasri,Fatima Benbarek*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper proposes an end-to-end hybrid framework for rumour detection in Algerian dialectal social media content, addressing challenges of informality, code-switching, and resource scarcity. A domain-specific annotated dataset is built using real posts, synthetic data, and the FASSILA corpus, with a transliteration pipeline for Arabizi. The best-performing model combines transformer embeddings with a classical classifier (F1=0.84), and domain-specific pre-training proves more critical than model size.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rumour spread on social media is a growing problem, but detecting it in the Algerian context is uniquely challenging due to: (1) the informal, code-switched nature of dialectal content; (2) a severe scarcity of annotated datasets for Algerian dialect; and (3) the poor performance of standard Arabic NLP tools when applied to dialectal text. These resource and linguistic barriers motivate the need for a dedicated detection framework.

Method: An end-to-end hybrid framework is developed with several components: (1) construction of a domain-specific annotated dataset by combining real social media posts, synthetically generated data, and the FASSILA corpus, with automatic labeling via a similarity-based annotation process; (2) a transliteration pipeline that generates parallel datasets in both Arabic script and Arabizi; (3) evaluation of multiple approaches spanning classical ML, deep learning, transformer-based models, and hybrid architectures that combine transformer embeddings with classical classifiers.

Result: The hybrid approach combining transformer embeddings with a classical classifier achieved the highest F1-score of 0.84. Domain-specific pre-training on social media data proved more impactful than raw model size, with social-media-trained models consistently outperforming larger models pre-trained on formal Arabic corpora.

Conclusion: Rumour detection in low-resource Algerian dialect settings is feasible when supported by targeted dataset construction, transliteration pipelines, and domain-adapted models. The findings underscore that domain relevance in pre-training outweighs scale, offering a practical roadmap for similar low-resource dialect NLP tasks.

Abstract: The rapid growth of social media has intensified the spread of rumours. This issue is more challenging in the Algerian context due to the informal and code-switched nature of dialectal content, the scarcity of annotated resources, and the limited effectiveness of standard Arabic NLP tools on dialect text.
  This paper presents an end-to-end rumour detection hybrid framework for Algerian dialect social media content. We build a domain-specific annotated dataset by combining real social media posts, synthetic data, and the FASSILA corpus, with automatic labeling based on a similarity-based annotation process. A transliteration pipeline is also introduced to generate parallel datasets in Arabic script and Arabizi.
  We evaluate multiple approaches, including classical machine learning, deep learning, transformers, and hybrid models. Experimental results show that a hybrid approach combining transformer embeddings with a classical classifier achieves the best performance, reaching an F1-score of 0.84. We also find that domain-specific pre-training is more important than model size, with social media-trained models outperforming larger models trained on formal Arabic corpora.
  These results demonstrate the feasibility of rumour detection in low-resource Algerian dialect settings.

</details>


### [51] [S-GBT: Smooth Growth Bound Tensor for Certified Robustness Against Word Substitution Attacks in NLP](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13439)
*Mohammed Bouri,Mohammed Erradi,Adnane Saoud*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: S-GBT introduces second-order curvature regularization via element-wise Hessian bounding, achieving up to 23.4% improvement in certified robust accuracy against word substitution attacks for LSTM and CNN models while maintaining competitive clean accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing defenses against word substitution attacks only consider first-order sensitivity (gradient magnitude), ignoring curvature — how gradients change under perturbation. When gradients vary sharply, models remain vulnerable despite first-order defenses. A second-order approach capturing Hessian information is needed for tighter certified robustness bounds.

Method: The Smooth Growth Bound Tensor (S-GBT) computes element-wise bounds on the Hessian matrix. The output change under word substitution is bounded by both a linear (first-order) and quadratic (second-order) term. A regularization term minimizing these bounds is integrated directly into the training objective. Formal theoretical proofs establish the resulting robustness guarantees. The method is derived for both LSTM and CNN architectures.

Result: Combining first- and second-order regularization improves certified robust accuracy by up to 23.4% over prior methods across multiple benchmark datasets, while clean accuracy remains competitive.

Conclusion: Controlling both the gradient and its variation (curvature) is a promising direction for building more robust NLP models. Second-order sensitivity provides strictly tighter certification than first-order methods alone.

Abstract: Despite recent progress in Natural Language Processing (NLP), models remain vulnerable to word substitution attacks. Most existing defenses focus on first order sensitivity and measure how much the output changes when the input is slightly perturbed. However, they ignore how this sensitivity evolves, which is described by curvature. When gradients vary sharply, models can still fail. This paper introduces the Smooth Growth Bound Tensor (S-GBT), a second order method that bounds the Hessian element-wise, for which we provide formal theoretical proofs on the resulting robustness bounds. A regularization term is added during training to minimize these bounds. This yields tighter certified robustness against word substitution attacks. The change in the output under word substitution is bounded by both a linear term and a quadratic term. S-GBT is derived for two architectures: Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN). The method is integrated directly into the training objective. Its effectiveness is evaluated on multiple benchmark datasets. The results show that combining first and second order regularization improves certified robust accuracy by up to 23.4% compared to prior methods, while clean accuracy remains competitive. These findings indicate that controlling both the gradient and its variation is a promising direction for building more robust models.

</details>


### [52] [Ontology Memory-Augmented ASR Correction for Long Text-Speech Interleaved Conversations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13464)
*Xinxin Li,Huiyao Chen,Meishan Zhang,Yunxin Li,Zulong Chen,Zhibo Ren,Xiaoqing Dong Baotian Hu,Min Zhang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Proposes an ontology memory-augmented ASR correction framework for long, text-speech interleaved conversations, using a dynamically updatable ontology to store entities, variants, and relations as retrievable context nodes, outperforming direct correction on the new RAMC-Corr benchmark dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional ASR correction focuses on short utterances, but modern interactions feature long interleaved text and speech. Existing methods that concatenate raw dialogue history suffer from redundancy and noise, making it hard to locate sparse correction evidence. There is a need for structured, conversation-level contextual evidence to correct context-dependent ASR errors.

Method: An ontology memory-augmented framework that organizes preceding interaction history into a dynamically updatable ontology memory. This memory stores entities, terminology, surface variants, potential ASR confusions, and semantic relations as retrievable nodes. A new dataset, RAMC-Corr, is constructed from MAGIC-RAMC to evaluate long-range ASR correction with grounded context.

Result: The proposed method outperforms direct correction baselines in 9 out of 10 paired backbone-setting combinations on RAMC-Corr, yielding more selective and evidence-grounded corrections for context-dependent ASR errors.

Conclusion: Structuring dialogue history into an ontology memory provides effective, grounded context for ASR correction in long interleaved conversations, significantly improving over naive history concatenation approaches.

Abstract: Automatic speech recognition (ASR) correction has traditionally focused on isolated utterances or short local contexts. However, as text and speech become increasingly interleaved in long interactions, ASR correction requires conversation-level contextual evidence. Existing ASR correction methods often rely on the current hypothesis or concatenate raw dialogue history. In such contexts, sparse correction evidence can be difficult to locate amid redundancy and noise. Addressing these challenges, we propose an ontology memory-augmented ASR correction framework for long text-speech interleaved conversations. The framework organizes preceding interaction history into a dynamically updatable ontology memory, where entities, terminology, surface variants, potential ASR confusions, and semantic relations are stored as retrievable nodes for context-grounded correction. To evaluate this setting, we construct RAMC-Corr, a dataset derived from MAGIC-RAMC for long-range ASR correction with grounded context. Experiments on RAMC-Corr show that our method improves over direct correction in 9 out of 10 paired backbone-setting combinations and encourages more selective and evidence-grounded corrections for context-dependent ASR errors.

</details>


### [53] [Leveraging Audio-LLMs to Filter Speech-to-Speech Training Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13507)
*Qixu Chen,Satoshi Nakamura*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A two-stage Rank-to-Distill strategy trains an audio-language model to filter noisy speech-to-speech translation (S2ST) data directly from raw paired speech, yielding up to +1.4 ASR-BLEU improvements on CVSS-C and SpeechMatrix benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale mined S2ST corpora contain noise, misalignment, and semantic errors that degrade translation performance. Manual labeling is infeasible at scale, necessitating an automated, scalable filtering method that can assess both acoustic fidelity and cross-lingual semantic consistency directly from audio.

Method: A two-stage Rank-to-Distill framework: (1) a lightweight ranker generates pseudo-labels (keep/drop) from noisy speech pairs; (2) these pseudo-labels supervise an audio large language model (Audio LLM) to predict keep/drop decisions directly from raw paired speech, jointly capturing acoustic fidelity and cross-lingual semantic consistency.

Result: The method achieves consistent improvements over unfiltered training on CVSS-C and SpeechMatrix datasets, with gains of up to +1.4 ASR-BLEU for end-to-end S2ST.

Conclusion: Distilling ranking-based filtering decisions into an audio-language model provides an effective, scalable approach to cleaning noisy S2ST corpora, significantly boosting downstream speech translation quality without requiring manual annotations.

Abstract: Large-scale mined corpora provide abundant training data for end-to-end speech-to-speech translation (S2ST) but may contain noise, misalignment, and semantic errors. Filtering noisy data is crucial to maintain robust speech translation performance. We study how to train an audio-language model to make keep/drop decisions on paired speech directly from audio. To obtain reliable supervision without manual labels, we adopt a scalable two-stage Rank-to-Distill strategy. A lightweight ranker generates keep/drop pseudo-labels from noisy speech pairs, then trains an audio large language model to predict keep/drop directly from raw paired speech. The resulting model jointly captures acoustic fidelity and cross-lingual semantic consistency for the selection of speech-conditioned data. Experiments on CVSS-C and SpeechMatrix show consistent improvements over unfiltered training, yielding up to +1.4 ASR-BLEU for end-to-end S2ST.

</details>


### [54] [When Does Mixing Help? Analyzing Query Embedding Interpolation in Multilingual Dense Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13537)
*Tongyao Zhu,Chao-Ming Huang,Min-Yen Kan*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: A ratio-controlled study on mMARCO shows that mixing query language embeddings improves dense retrieval performance, with an optimal mix ratio outperforming monolingual queries in most cases. However, a strong asymmetry exists: English dominance makes pure English queries best for English indices, while mixing always helps non-English retrieval. English is the best mixing partner across all non-English languages, and mixing gains inversely correlate with typological distance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mixed-language querying is common in multilingual communities, yet the sensitivity of dense retrievers to such mixed queries is poorly understood. The authors aim to systematically characterize how embedding-level language mixing affects retrieval performance.

Method: Ratio-controlled experiments on mMARCO using BGE-M3, where mixed queries are constructed via embedding-level interpolation of parallel monolingual query translations. The mixing proportion is systematically varied, and retrieval is evaluated across different document index languages. Patterns are validated across model families and scales.

Result: (1) Optimal mixing ratio beats best monolingual endpoint in 88/105 cases. (2) Strong asymmetry: mixing uniformly helps when retrieving from non-English indices, but pure English queries are best for English-containing indices. (3) English is the strongest mixing partner for every non-English document language. (4) After controlling for English dominance, mixing gains negatively correlate with typological distance between languages.

Conclusion: Language-mix sensitivity in dense retrievers is structured and predictable, not arbitrary. The findings are robust across model families and scales, offering practical guidance for multilingual retrieval system design.

Abstract: While mixed-language querying is ubiquitous in multilingual communities, the sensitivity of dense retrievers to such queries remains poorly understood. We present a ratio-controlled study on mMARCO that systematically evaluates retrieval performance by varying the mixing proportion of parallel query translations via embedding-level mixing -- constructing mixed queries as an interpolation of monolingual embeddings. Experiments with BGE-M3 demonstrate that an optimal mixing ratio outperforms the best monolingual endpoint in 88/105 cases. We uncover a distinct asymmetry driven by English dominance: mixing is uniformly beneficial when retrieving from non-English document indices, whereas indices containing English are best served by pure English queries. Furthermore, English acts as the strongest mixing partner for every non-English document language. Finally, when controlling for English dominance, mixing gains correlate negatively with typological distance. We conclude that language-mix sensitivity is structured and predictable, and we validate the robustness of these patterns across model families and scales.

</details>


### [55] [ArogyaSutra: A Multi-Agent Framework for Multimodal Medical Reasoning in Indic Languages](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13572)
*Tanmoy Kanti Halder,Akash Ghosh,Subhadip Baidya,Arijit Roy,Sriparna Saha*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: ArogyaBodha is a large-scale multilingual multimodal medical QA dataset (English + 7 Indic languages), and ArogyaSutra is an actor-critic multi-agent framework with tool grounding and dual-memory for medical reasoning. Together they improve multilingual medical VQA in low-resource settings like rural India.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MLLMs perform poorly in specialized healthcare settings, particularly for multilingual and low-resource scenarios where patients use native Indic languages and multimodal inputs (e.g., medical images). This limits equitable AI-driven healthcare access in regions like rural India.

Method: (1) ArogyaBodha: a large-scale multilingual multimodal medical QA dataset from 8 heterogeneous sources, covering 31 body systems, 6 imaging modalities, and 21 clinical domains across English + 7 Indic languages. (2) ArogyaSutra: an actor-critic multi-agent framework integrating tool grounding with dual-memory mechanisms for step-wise reasoning-aware decision making; uses stored actor-critic simulation trajectories for distillation.

Result: Experiments show improved multilingual medical reasoning accuracy across all Indic languages. Ablation studies validate the contribution of each component.

Conclusion: The proposed dataset (ArogyaBodha) and framework (ArogyaSutra) effectively address the gap in multilingual, multimodal medical reasoning, enabling more equitable AI healthcare assistance in low-resource, non-English settings.

Abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown promising reasoning capabilities in general domains, yet their performance remains limited in specialized settings such as healthcare, especially in multilingual and low-resource scenarios. This gap is critical in regions like rural India, where patients often express complex medical queries in native Indic languages and rely on multimodal inputs such as medical images. Existing English-centric MLLMs struggle to support such use cases, limiting equitable access to AI-driven healthcare assistance. To address this challenge, we introduce ArogyaBodha, a large-scale multilingual multimodal medical question-answer dataset constructed from eight heterogeneous sources, covering 31 body systems, six imaging modalities, and 21 clinical domains across English and seven major Indian languages. We further propose ArogyaSutra, an actor-critic-based multi-agent framework that integrates tool grounding with dual-memory mechanisms for step-wise, reasoning-aware decision making, and uses stored actor-critic simulation trajectories for distillation. Experiments show that our dataset and framework improve multilingual medical reasoning accuracy across all Indic languages, with ablations validating the contribution of each component. The source code and dataset are available at: https://iitp-cse.github.io/ ArogyaSutra/

</details>


### [56] [LabVLA: Grounding Vision-Language-Action Models in Scientific Laboratories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13578)
*Baochang Ren,Xinjie Liu,Xi Chen,Yanshuo Liu,Chenxi Li,Daqi Gao,Zeqin Su,Jintao Xing,Zirui Xue,Rui Li,Xiangyu Zhao,Shuofei Qiao,Minting Pan,Wangmeng Zuo,Lei Bai,Dongzhan Zhou,Ningyu Zhang,Huajun Chen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper addresses the gap between AI-driven experimental reasoning and physical lab execution by introducing RoboGenesis (a simulation-based data engine for generating laboratory robot demonstrations) and LabVLA (a vision-language-action model with a two-stage training recipe). LabVLA achieves state-of-the-art success rates on the LabUtopia benchmark under both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI systems can reason about experiments (reading literature, generating hypotheses, planning protocols) but cannot physically execute them—human operators are still required at the bench. Existing VLA models are trained on household/tabletop tasks and lack exposure to lab instruments, transparent liquids, and fixed protocol workflows. Two critical bottlenecks are identified: (1) lack of laboratory-specific training data across diverse robot embodiments, and (2) lack of a unified learning framework for lab execution.

Method: Two main contributions: (1) **RoboGenesis**, a simulation-based data engine that composes lab workflows from atomic skills, validates rollouts, and exports structured demonstrations for various robot profiles. (2) **LabVLA**, a VLA model trained with a two-stage recipe: FAST action token pretraining on Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct to make the backbone action-aware before continuous control learning, followed by flow matching posttraining that attaches a DiT action expert under knowledge insulation to prevent catastrophic forgetting.

Result: On the LabUtopia benchmark, LabVLA achieves the highest average success rate among all evaluated baselines, outperforming them in both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings.

Conclusion: Closing the gap between AI reasoning and physical lab execution requires addressing both data and embodiment bottlenecks. RoboGenesis provides scalable, embodiment-diverse lab demonstrations, and LabVLA's two-stage training recipe (FAST + flow matching with knowledge insulation) yields a unified policy that generalizes effectively to diverse lab tasks and robot morphologies.

Abstract: Scientific laboratories increasingly rely on AI systems to reason about experiments, but the physical act of doing science remains largely outside their reach. AI can help read literature, generate hypotheses, and plan protocols, yet the execution of those protocols at the bench still requires a human operator. Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models provide one possible interface between written protocols and robot execution, but existing policies are trained mostly on household and tabletop demonstrations and rarely encounter the instruments, transparent liquids, or fixed protocol workflows found in scientific laboratories. Closing this gap requires both laboratory-specific supervision and a unified learning framework that can accommodate the diverse robot embodiments used to execute experimental protocols. We therefore identify data and embodiment as central bottlenecks alongside model design. To address the data side, we build RoboGenesis, a simulation-based workflow and data engine that composes configured laboratory workflows from atomic skills, validates and filters rollouts, and exports structured demonstrations across supported robot profiles. On the policy side, we present LabVLA, trained with a two-stage recipe: FAST action token pretraining first makes the Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct backbone action aware before any continuous control is learned, and flow matching posttraining then attaches a DiT action expert under knowledge insulation. On the LabUtopia benchmark, LabVLA achieves the highest average success rate among all evaluated baselines under both in-distribution and out-of-distribution settings.

</details>


### [57] [One Polluted Page Is Enough: Evaluating Web Content Pollution in Generative Recommenders](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13610)
*Minghao Luo,Liang Chen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Search-augmented LLMs are vulnerable to recommending fake products when polluted web content appears in retrieval results. The FORGE benchmark, covering 225 products across 15 categories, shows fooled rates up to 27% from a single polluted page and 73.8% with top-3 replacement. Reasoning often backfires by fabricating social proof, and standard defenses like skepticism prompting can worsen the problem.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As search-augmented LLMs increasingly mediate consumer recommendations, they risk consuming polluted web content (e.g., fake reviews, promotional pages) from live retrieval. The authors aim to quantify the extent to which these models unwittingly promote fake products due to contaminated search results—a previously underexplored risk at the intersection of retrieval-augmented generation and adversarial web pollution.

Method: The authors introduce FORGE, a benchmark that simulates web-content pollution by locally rewriting real products in retrieved web pages into fake ones. The benchmark spans 225 real-world products across 15 categories and 5 consumer scenarios. Given an upstream search result, FORGE replaces real product mentions with fake counterparts and measures LLM recommendation rates for the fake products. Experiments cover 12 commercial and open-weights LLMs. Three defenses are evaluated: skepticism prompting, consensus filtering over model priors, and cross-document evidence filtering.

Result: All 12 LLMs tested are vulnerable. A single polluted page yields fooled rates up to 27%, while replacing the top-3 retrieved results with fake products raises fooled rates to 73.8%. Vulnerability varies by product category, with models lacking stable prior knowledge being more susceptible. Reasoning models do not resist better; instead, they often generate spurious social proof to justify false recommendations. Among defenses, skepticism prompting can exacerbate vulnerability (similar to reasoning), while consensus filtering reduces fake recommendations but risks suppressing legitimate products.

Conclusion: Search-augmented LLMs are systematically vulnerable to fake-product promotion through polluted retrieval content. The FORGE benchmark demonstrates that this is a pervasive issue across model types, with reasoning offering no protection and simplistic defenses introducing new trade-offs. The authors release FORGE publicly to facilitate further research on robust retrieval-augmented recommendation.

Abstract: Search-augmented LLMs increasingly mediate everyday consumer recommendations by retrieving live web content. This creates a new risk: generative recommenders may consume polluted web content, such as fake reviews and promotional pages crafted to mislead recommendations. We ask: to what extent do search-augmented LLMs become unwitting promoters of fake products when consuming polluted retrieval results? To answer this, we introduce FORGE (Fake Online Recommendations in Generative Environments), a benchmark for measuring fake-product promotion under controlled web-content pollution. Given an upstream search result, FORGE locally rewrites real products in retrieved web pages into fake ones to simulate web-content pollution, and measures how often the LLM recommends the fake product. FORGE covers 225 real-world products across 15 categories and 5 consumer scenarios. Across 12 commercial and open-weights LLMs, all models are vulnerable: a single polluted page yields fooled rates of up to 27%, while the full top-3 replacement raises this to 73.8%. Vulnerability varies substantially across categories, increasing when models lack stable prior knowledge of the relevant products. Reasoning does not mitigate this vulnerability; instead, it often generates spurious social proof to justify false recommendations. We evaluate three defenses: skepticism prompting and consensus filtering (over model priors or cross-document evidence). Skepticism can exacerbate vulnerability, much like reasoning, while filtering risks suppressing legitimate products. We release FORGE at https://github.com/leoluolol/forge-benchmark.

</details>


### [58] [Beyond Uniform Tokens: Adaptive Compression for Time Series Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13624)
*Jialin Gan,Xin Qiu,Guangzhe Chen,Xue Wang*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper proposes an adaptive token budgeting framework for time series LLMs that compresses TS tokens via frequency-domain analysis and progressively prunes prompt tokens across layers, achieving up to 7.68× inference acceleration while improving performance in 78% of evaluated tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TS tokens and prompt tokens have fundamentally different information structures, making uniform token processing in LLMs inefficient. The authors identify two inefficiencies: (1) TS tokens exhibit uneven spectral contributions with redundant frequency patterns, and (2) prompt-token influence attenuates with model depth.

Method: An adaptive token budgeting framework with two components: (1) frequency-domain structure-aware compression for TS tokens that exploits uneven spectral contributions, and (2) progressive prompt token reduction across layers based on diminishing influence with depth.

Result: Up to 7.68× inference acceleration with performance gains in 78% of evaluated settings across forecasting, classification, imputation, and anomaly detection tasks.

Conclusion: Asymmetric token compression — treating TS tokens and prompt tokens differently based on their distinct information properties — is effective for building scalable, efficient time series foundation models.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have enabled time series (TS) analysis by jointly modeling numerical observations and textual context through a shared token interface. However, TS tokens and prompt tokens exhibit fundamentally different information structures, making uniform token processing inefficient. In this paper, we study token efficiency in TS language modeling from an asymmetric-token perspective. We show that TS tokens have highly uneven spectral contributions, where many tokens share redundant frequency patterns while a small subset preserves critical temporal evidence. We also observe that prompt-token influence attenuates with model depth, suggesting that full prompt retention across all layers is unnecessary. Based on these findings, we develop an adaptive token budgeting framework that compresses TS tokens via frequency-domain structure and progressively reduces prompt tokens across layers. Experiments across forecasting, classification, imputation, and anomaly detection demonstrate up to \textit{\textbf{7.68$\times$}} inference acceleration and performance gains in \textit{\textbf{78\%}} of evaluated settings, showing the effectiveness of asymmetric token compression for scalable TS foundation models.

</details>


### [59] [From Tokens to Faces: Investigating Discrete Speech Representations for 3D Facial Animation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13630)
*Pedro Correa,Olivier Perrotin,Samir Sadok,Paula Costa,Thomas Hueber*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper evaluates four families of speech representations (SSL, neural codecs, ASR-style, and others) for speech-driven 3D facial animation, finding that phonetic-class encoding benefits prediction accuracy. The authors also introduce an Audio Visual Text-to-Speech (AVTTS) pipeline using discrete representations as a shared space for decoding both speech and 3D facial motion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The choice of speech representation is critical for 3D facial animation, yet different representations encode fundamentally different information — segmental/semantic cues, acoustic reconstruction latents, or label-based phonetic spaces. There is a need to systematically understand which representation family best serves facial motion prediction and why.

Method: The authors evaluate four speech representation families across two facial decoders using objective metrics and perceptual evaluation. They conduct probing analyses to relate tokenized representations to phonetic units and articulatory deformations. They then introduce an AVTTS pipeline that uses discrete representations as a shared latent space to jointly decode speech and 3D facial motion.

Result: Encoding phonetic classes proves beneficial for accurate facial animation prediction. Both semantic (SSL) and label-based (ASR-style) representations achieve comparable facial animation quality. The proposed AVTTS pipeline successfully leverages discrete representations as a shared space for speech and facial motion decoding.

Conclusion: Phonetic-class encoding is a key factor for high-quality speech-driven 3D facial animation. Label-based discrete representations, in particular, offer a practical shared space for multimodal tasks such as the introduced AVTTS pipeline, enabling simultaneous speech and facial motion synthesis.

Abstract: The choice of speech representation is critical in speech-driven 3D facial animation. Representations differ in what they encode: SSL features emphasize segmental and semantic cues, neural codecs yield latents optimized for acoustic reconstruction, and ASR-style objectives produce label-based spaces. We evaluate four speech representation families for 3D facial synthesis, comparing their facial reconstruction quality across two facial decoders using objective metrics and a perceptual evaluation. We additionally conduct probing analyses that relate tokenized representations to phonetic units and to articulatory deformations. We found that encoding phonetic classes is beneficial for accurate facial animation prediction on both semantic and label-based representations with comparable facial animation quality. From the latter, we introduce an Audio Visual Text-to-Speech (AVTTS) pipeline that leverages, as a shared space, discrete representations to decode speech and 3D facial motion.

</details>


### [60] [Operads for compositional reasoning in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13634)
*Nathaniel Bottman,Kyle Richardson*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: This paper proposes operads as a rigorous mathematical framework for modeling question decomposition in LLM reasoning, defining a "questions operad" Q where operations are question templates and composition is sub-answer substitution. It introduces operadic consistency — measuring whether a QA model's answers agree across partial collapses of a decomposition tree — which is empirically validated in a companion paper as strongly correlated with accuracy, outperforming temperature-based self-consistency baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Question decomposition is widely used to improve LLM reasoning for complex queries, but currently lacks a rigorous mathematical foundation. The authors seek to provide this foundation and, in doing so, uncover new principled methods for analyzing and improving multi-step reasoning reliability.

Method: The authors adopt operads — algebraic structures from category theory that model many-in, one-out operations and their hierarchical compositions — as the framework. They define the questions operad Q, where operations correspond to question templates and composition corresponds to substituting answers of sub-queries into parent templates. QA models are then interpreted as algebras over Q. From this, they derive the notion of operadic consistency: measuring agreement of a model's answers across different partial collapses of a question decomposition tree.

Result: The operadic framework successfully formalizes existing question decomposition practices. The companion paper (Bottman, Liu, and Richardson, 2026) empirically demonstrates that operadic consistency is strongly correlated with accuracy across twelve LLMs and four multi-hop QA datasets, outperforming standard temperature-based self-consistency baselines.

Conclusion: Operads provide the natural mathematical home for question decomposition. Invariants like operadic consistency open new directions for analyzing and improving the reliability of multi-step reasoning in LLMs.

Abstract: Question decomposition, i.e. breaking a complex query into simpler sub-queries whose answers are composed to produce a final answer, is a widely used strategy for improving LLM reasoning, yet it currently lacks a rigorous mathematical foundation. In this paper, we propose operads, mathematical structures that model many-in, one-out operations and compositions thereof, as a natural framework for describing question decomposition. We define the questions operad $Q$, in which operations correspond to question templates and composition corresponds to substitution of sub-answers, and show how QA models can be interpreted as algebras over $Q$. Beyond reframing existing practice, this operadic perspective points toward new methods, in particular a notion of operadic consistency, which measures whether a QA model's answers agree across the partial collapses of a question decomposition tree. Empirical evaluation of operadic consistency is reported in our companion paper (Bottman, Liu, and Richardson, 2026), which finds it strongly correlated with accuracy across twelve LLMs and four multi-hop QA datasets and outperforming standard temperature-based self-consistency baselines. We argue that operads are the natural mathematical home for question decomposition, and that invariants such as operadic consistency open new directions for analyzing and improving the reliability of multi-step reasoning.

</details>


### [61] [Recursive Agent Harnesses](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13643)
*Elias Lumer,Sahil Sen,Kevin Paul,Vamse Kumar Subbiah*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Recursive Agent Harness (RAH), a pattern where a parent agent spawns parallel subagent harnesses via executable scripts for long-context reasoning. RAH extends recursive language models by using full agent tooling (filesystem, code execution, planning) as the recursive unit. Using GPT-5, RAH improves coding-agent performance from 71.75% to 81.36% on Oolong-Synthetic, and reaches 89.77% with Claude Sonnet 4.5.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Two converging trends motivate this work: (1) recursive language models (RLMs) have demonstrated that recursion over model calls is effective for long-context reasoning, and (2) production coding agents increasingly spawn subagents at scale (e.g., Anthropic's dynamic workflows). The authors observe that the recursive unit in these production systems is a full agent harness — with filesystem tools, code execution, and planning — rather than a bare model call. They aim to formalize, name, and evaluate this underexplored pattern bridging these two lines of work.

Method: The authors propose RAH ("harness recursion"), a code-first extension of model recursion. A parent agent generates and executes a script that spawns multiple subagent harnesses operating in parallel, each handling fine-grained workloads. Structured function calls are used for smaller subtasks. The evaluation is a controlled experiment on long-context reasoning using the Oolong-Synthetic benchmark (199 samples across 13 context-length buckets up to 4M tokens). Backbone models are held fixed: GPT-5 for direct comparison against published Codex and RLM baselines, with additional experiments using Claude Sonnet 4.5 to test a stronger backbone.

Result: With GPT-5 as the backbone, RAH improves the Codex coding-agent baseline from 71.75% to 81.36% on Oolong-Synthetic — a ~9.6 percentage point gain attributed to the harness design rather than model capability. With Claude Sonnet 4.5, the same RAH design achieves 89.77%, demonstrating that harness recursion scales with stronger backbone models.

Conclusion: Harness recursion — where the recursive unit is a full agent harness rather than a bare model call — is a distinct and effective pattern for long-context reasoning. RAH formalizes this pattern and demonstrates significant gains over both vanilla coding agents and model-level recursion, suggesting that code-first, tool-equipped recursive agent architectures are a promising direction for scaling reasoning capabilities.

Abstract: Recursive language models (RLMs) showed that recursion over model calls is an effective strategy for long-context reasoning, and production coding agents have begun to write code that spawns subagents at scale, most recently in Anthropic's dynamic workflows. We name and study the pattern between these two lines of work, where the recursive unit is a full agent harness with filesystem tools, code execution, and planning rather than a model call with no tools. We call this the Recursive Agent Harness (RAH) and frame it as harness recursion, the code-first extension to the model recursion of RLMs. A parent agent generates and runs an executable script that spawns subagent harnesses in parallel for fine-grained workloads and uses structured function calls for small subtasks. We provide a controlled evaluation on long-context reasoning. With the backbone held fixed at GPT-5 to match the published Codex and RLM baselines, RAH improves the Codex coding-agent baseline from 71.75% to 81.36% on Oolong-Synthetic (199 samples, 13 context-length buckets up to 4M tokens), a gain attributable to the harness rather than the model. With a stronger backbone, Claude Sonnet 4.5, the same design reaches 89.77%.

</details>


### [62] [SkMTEB: Slovak Massive Text Embedding Benchmark and Model Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13647)
*Marek Šuppa,Andrej Ridzik,Daniel Hládek,Natália Kňažeková,Viktória Ondrejová*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: SkMTEB is the first comprehensive Slovak text embedding benchmark (31 datasets, 7 task types). Evaluation of 31 models shows multilingual instruction-tuned models perform best, while Slovak NLU models transfer poorly. The authors release two efficient, vocabulary-trimmed Slovak E5 models (45M and 365M parameters) that achieve competitive performance with proprietary APIs while being locally deployable for semantic search and RAG.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Slovak is a low-resource West Slavic language with insufficient benchmark coverage for text embeddings. Existing multilingual benchmarks offer only shallow Slovak coverage (~4× less than SkMTEB). Additionally, there is a need for efficient, locally-deployable Slovak embedding models suitable for semantic search and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), without reliance on proprietary APIs.

Method: The authors construct SkMTEB, a benchmark modeled after the Massive Text Embedding Benchmark (MTEB), comprising 31 datasets across 7 task types (classification, clustering, pair classification, reranking, retrieval, semantic textual similarity, and summarization). They evaluate 31 embedding models, including multilingual models and Slovak-specific NLU models. To create efficient Slovak embeddings, they apply vocabulary trimming (removing tokens irrelevant to Slovak) to Multilingual E5 models, then fine-tune on Slovak data, producing e5-sk-small (45M params) and e5-sk-large (365M params), achieving up to 62% size reduction.

Result: Large instruction-tuned multilingual models achieve the strongest overall performance on SkMTEB. Slovak-specific models trained for NLU tasks transfer poorly to embedding tasks. The vocabulary-trimmed and fine-tuned e5-sk-small and e5-sk-large models achieve competitive performance with proprietary APIs despite significant parameter reductions. The models remain effective for semantic search and RAG while being locally deployable.

Conclusion: SkMTEB fills a critical benchmarking gap for Slovak text embeddings. The vocabulary-trimming plus fine-tuning approach offers a replicable and cost-effective path for developing efficient embedding models for other under-resourced languages. All artifacts — benchmark, models, datasets, and code — are released openly to facilitate further research.

Abstract: We introduce SkMTEB, the first comprehensive MTEB-style text embedding benchmark for Slovak, a low-resource West Slavic language, comprising 31 datasets across 7 task types -- nearly 4$\times$ the depth of existing multilingual benchmark coverage for Slovak. Our evaluation of 31 embedding models reveals that large instruction-tuned multilingual models achieve the strongest performance, while existing Slovak-specific models trained for NLU tasks transfer poorly to embedding tasks. To address the need for efficient, locally-deployable Slovak embeddings, we develop \texttt{e5-sk-small} (45M parameters) and \texttt{e5-sk-large} (365M) by applying vocabulary trimming and fine-tuning to Multilingual E5 models. Despite size reductions of up to 62\%, our open-source models achieve competitive performance with proprietary APIs while remaining locally deployable for semantic search and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). We release the benchmark, models, datasets, and code openly, hoping our approach offers a replicable path for other under-resourced languages.

</details>


### [63] [Operadic consistency: a label-free signal for compositional reasoning failures in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13649)
*Nathaniel Bottman,Yinhong Liu,Kyle Richardson*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Operadic consistency (OC) checks whether an LLM's direct answer to a compositional question matches the answer derived by composing its answers to sub-questions. OC strongly correlates with accuracy (r ≥ 0.85 across all datasets), outperforms baselines like chain-of-thought self-consistency on harder multi-hop QA tasks, and improves selective prediction when used alongside existing confidence signals.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Detecting LLM reasoning failures at inference time without ground-truth labels is critical for trustworthiness. Existing confidence baselines (self-consistency, semantic entropy, P(True)) rely on within-question sampling and self-evaluation. The authors propose a complementary, decomposition-based diagnostic inspired by operad theory: exploiting the compositional structure of queries to surface inconsistencies that indicate likely errors.

Method: Operadic consistency (OC): For a given query, decompose it into sub-questions, obtain the model's direct answer to the full query, then independently obtain answers to sub-questions and compose them according to the decomposition tree. OC measures whether the direct answer agrees with the composed answer. The method is evaluated across 12 instruction-tuned LLMs (4B–671B parameters) on four multi-hop QA datasets (HotpotQA, DROP, MuSiQue, StrategyQA). For frontier thinking models, the decomposition is extracted from the model's own chain of thought rather than externally provided.

Result: OC achieves Pearson r ∈ [0.86, 0.94] with accuracy across all datasets, and is the only signal with r ≥ 0.85 uniformly. CoT-SC matches OC on HotpotQA and DROP (r = 0.93, 0.87) but drops to r ≈ 0.45 on MuSiQue and StrategyQA. OC contributes statistically significant complementary information beyond CoT-SC and semantic entropy (cluster-robust p ≤ 10⁻¹⁶). At equal K=3 budget, OC yields selective-prediction lifts of +0.086 to +0.096 AUARC and +0.092 to +0.164 AUROC over tuned CoT-SC. On five frontier thinking models with self-extracted decompositions, positive lift is observed on all 16 tested (dataset, budget, metric) cells, with 12 of 16 showing 95% CIs excluding zero.

Conclusion: Operadic consistency provides a robust, complementary confidence signal for detecting LLM reasoning failures, particularly effective on compositional multi-hop queries where simpler consistency checks degrade. It generalizes across model scales and types, and remains effective even when decompositions are extracted from the model's own reasoning traces, making it practical for deployment with frontier thinking models.

Abstract: Detecting LLM reasoning failures at inference time without ground-truth labels has motivated a wide range of confidence baselines, including self-consistency, semantic entropy, and P(True), built on within-question sampling and self-evaluation. Operad theory, the formalism for systems built by iterated substitution, suggests a complementary diagnostic: a model's direct answer to a compositional query should agree with the answer it produces by composing a stated decomposition of the same query. We instantiate this idea as operadic consistency (OC), a per-question signal. Across twelve instruction-tuned LLMs (4B to 671B parameters, open-weights and closed-source) on four multi-hop QA datasets, OC is strongly correlated with accuracy on every dataset (Pearson $r \in [0.86, 0.94]$, all $p \leq 0.0004$), and is the only signal we evaluate with $r \geq 0.85$ uniformly across all four datasets. Chain-of-thought self-consistency (CoT-SC; Wang et al., 2023) matches OC on HotpotQA and DROP ($r = 0.93, 0.87$) but drops to $r \approx 0.45$ on MuSiQue and StrategyQA. At the per-question level, OC contributes information beyond CoT-SC and semantic entropy on every dataset (cluster-robust $p \leq 10^{-16}$ for the OC coefficient), and the conclusion is robust to additionally controlling for constructed decomposition-aware baselines ($p \leq 10^{-13}$). The same signal yields selective-prediction improvements (accuracy at fixed coverage) over a tuned CoT-SC baseline at the equal-cost $K = 3$ budget (AUARC lifts of +0.086 to +0.096 and AUROC lifts of +0.092 to +0.164; 95% CIs exclude zero on every cell). On five frontier thinking models, where the decomposition is extracted from the model's own chain of thought, the same equal-cost comparison gives positive selective-prediction point-estimate lift on all 16 (dataset, budget, metric) cells tested, with 95% CIs excluding zero on 12 of the 16.

</details>


### [64] [HyperTool: Beyond Step-Wise Tool Calls for Tool-Augmented Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13663)
*Yaxin Du,Yifan Zhou,Yujie Ge,Jiajun Wang,Xianghe Pang,Shuo Tang,Tuney Zheng,Bryan Dai,Jian Yang,Siheng Chen*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: HyperTool introduces a unified MCP-style interface that allows LLM agents to execute multi-step tool workflows within a single code block, collapsing deterministic subroutines and reducing context consumption from step-wise atomic tool calls.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing tool-augmented LLM agents suffer from an "execution-granularity mismatch": atomic, step-wise tool calls unfold deterministic workflows into repeated model-visible decisions, wasting context window space and forcing the model to manage low-level dataflow. The authors aim to raise the abstraction level of tool execution to match the logical granularity of subroutines.

Method: The authors propose HyperTool, a unified executable MCP-style tool interface. A model invokes HyperTool with a code block that can call existing tools via their original schemas, manipulate return values, and pass intermediate results locally — effectively folding deterministic tool subroutines into a single outer call. Training data is synthesized by generating HyperTool-format trajectories from cross-tool compositional tasks and verifying them in real MCP environments.

Result: On the MCP-Universe benchmark, HyperTool raises average accuracy from 15.69% to 35.29% on Qwen3-32B and from 9.93% to 33.33% on Qwen3-8B. It also surpasses GPT-OSS and Kimi-k2.5 on average accuracy, demonstrating substantial improvements in multi-step tool use.

Conclusion: HyperTool effectively addresses the execution-granularity mismatch by elevating the model-visible unit of tool execution, leading to significant accuracy gains in compositional multi-step tool use across different model scales and outperforming strong baselines.

Abstract: Tool-augmented LLM agents commonly rely on step-wise atomic tool calls, where each invocation, observation, and value transfer is exposed in the main reasoning trace. This creates an \emph{execution-granularity mismatch}: locally deterministic tool workflows are unfolded into repeated model-visible decisions, consuming context and forcing the model to manage low-level dataflow in the trace. We introduce \textbf{HyperTool}, a unified executable MCP-style tool interface that changes the model-visible unit of tool execution. A model invokes HyperTool with a code block that can call existing tools through their original schemas, manipulate returned values, and pass intermediate results locally, folding deterministic tool subroutines into a single outer call. To train models to use this interface, we synthesize HyperTool-format trajectories from cross-tool compositional tasks and verify them in real MCP environments. On MCP-Universe, HyperTool improves average accuracy from 15.69\% to 35.29\% on Qwen3-32B and from 9.93\% to 33.33\% on Qwen3-8B, and surpass GPT-OSS and Kimi-k2.5 on average accuracy, showing that our HyperTool can substantially improve multi-step tool use.

</details>


### [65] [Influcoder: Distilling Decoders' Gradient Influence Rankings into an Encoder for Data Attribution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13668)
*Dimitri Kachler,Damien Sileo,Pascal Denis*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: Influcoder proposes a fast, cost-effective method for influence-based Data Attribution at scale, addressing the speed and storage limitations of traditional influence functions when applied to large LLM training datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM capabilities grow, there is increasing demand to curate high-quality training data by identifying which samples cause specific model behaviors (e.g., toxic outputs). Existing influence-function-based Data Attribution methods are effective but suffer from prohibitive processing speed and storage requirements, making them impractical for large-scale datasets.

Method: The authors propose Influcoder, a novel approach that enables influence-based Data Attribution at scale. While the abstract does not detail the technical mechanism, the method is characterized as quick and cost-effective, likely involving approximations or architectural innovations to overcome the computational and storage bottlenecks of traditional influence functions.

Result: The abstract does not provide specific quantitative results, but positions Influcoder as a practical solution for influence-based Data Attribution on large datasets, implying significant improvements in processing speed and storage compactness over existing methods.

Conclusion: Influcoder offers a viable path to applying influence-based Data Attribution at the scale required by modern LLM training pipelines, enabling efficient identification of training samples that precondition undesirable model behavior.

Abstract: With the growth of LLMs' (Large Language Models) capabilities, there has been an increasing push to curate high quality datasets by filtering samples in the training data. In general, Data Attribution (DA) methods aim to estimate how individual samples in a training dataset can precondition a model to generate certain outputs. As an example, one might be interested in which samples in the data could be the source of toxic behavior after training the LLM. Many methods quantify this conditioning through the paradigm of influence functions. While methods of this family are effective in its function, they lack the necessary processing speed and storage compactness to be practically implemented on large datasets. We propose a method, Influcoder, as a quick and cost-effective approach to influence-based Data Attribution at scale.

</details>


### [66] [Learning to Reason by Analogy via Retrieval-Augmented Reinforcement Fine-Tuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13680)
*Zilin Xiao,Qi Ma,Chun-cheng Jason Chen,Xintao Chen,Avinash Atreya,Hanjie Chen,Vicente Ordonez*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: RA-RFT proposes a post-training framework that combines a reasoning-aware retriever (trained via gold-relevance distillation) with reinforcement fine-tuning, enabling LMs to learn from retrieved analogous demonstrations. It outperforms standard RL fine-tuning (e.g., GRPO) on mathematical reasoning benchmarks by surfacing complementary solution strategies rather than relying on semantic similarity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional RAG retrieves contexts based on lexical or semantic similarity, but this fails for complex reasoning: similar problems may require different strategies, while superficially different problems may share identical reasoning patterns. There is a need for retrieval that prioritizes reasoning benefit over semantic overlap.

Method: RA-RFT operates in two stages: (1) Gold-relevance distillation trains a retriever to rank contexts by expected reasoning benefit rather than semantic similarity, using verifiable outcome rewards as supervision. (2) The policy model is fine-tuned via reinforcement fine-tuning (RFT) using retrieved analogous demonstrations, so it learns to leverage reasoning traces under outcome-based rewards. The framework also analyzes retrieval diversity to surface complementary solution strategies.

Result: RA-RFT consistently outperforms standard RFT methods (e.g., GRPO) on challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks. On AIME 2025, it improves average@32 accuracy by 7.1 points for Qwen3-1.7B and 2.8 points for Qwen3-4B over GRPO. The gains are shown to be orthogonal to advances in reward design or training curricula.

Conclusion: Reasoning-aware retrieval is a complementary axis of improvement for language model reasoning, independent of reward design or curriculum advances. Teaching models to reason by analogy through RA-RFT yields consistent and significant gains over standard reinforcement fine-tuning approaches.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) has become a standard mechanism for grounding language models in external knowledge, yet conventional retrieval based on lexical or semantic similarity is poorly suited for complex reasoning tasks: a semantically similar problem may demand an entirely different solution strategy, while a superficially different problem may share the same underlying reasoning pattern. We propose Retrieval-Augmented Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (RA-RFT), a post-training framework that teaches language models to reason by analogy. RA-RFT uses gold-relevance distillation to train a retriever that ranks contexts by expected reasoning benefit rather than semantic overlap, and then fine-tunes the policy model via reinforcement fine-tuning methods with retrieved analogous demonstrations, so the model learns to leverage reasoning traces under verifiable outcome rewards. We further analyze the diversity of retrieved contexts and find that reasoning-aware retrieval surfaces complementary solution strategies that provide distinct reasoning scaffolds for individual problems. Across challenging mathematical reasoning benchmarks, RA-RFT consistently outperforms standard reinforcement fine-tuning methods. For example, it improves AIME 2025 average@32 accuracy by 7.1 and 2.8 points over GRPO for Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen3-4B respectively -- suggesting that reasoning-aware retrieval is a complementary axis of improvement and orthogonal to advances in reward design or training curricula.

</details>


### [67] [EvoArena: Tracking Memory Evolution for Robust LLM Agents in Dynamic Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13681)
*Jundong Xu,Qingchuan Li,Jiaying Wu,Yihuai Lan,Shuyue Stella Li,Huichi Zhou,Bowen Jiang,Lei Wang,Jun Wang,Anh Tuan Luu,Caiming Xiong,Hae Won Park,Bryan Hooi,Zhiyuan Hu*

Main category: cs.CL

TL;DR: EvoArena is a benchmark for evaluating LLM agents in dynamic, evolving environments across terminal, software, and social domains. The authors propose EvoMem, a patch-based memory system that tracks environmental changes as structured update histories, improving agent performance on both their new benchmark and existing ones like GAIA and LoCoMo.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Most LLM agent evaluations assume static environments, but real-world deployment involves continuous changes in environments, tasks, and user preferences. Agents must adapt to this evolution, yet no benchmarks systematically model such environmental dynamics. This gap motivates both a new evaluation framework and a memory mechanism tailored to evolving conditions.

Method: The paper introduces EvoArena, a benchmark suite that models environmental changes as progressive updates across three domains: terminal (command-line), software (application interaction), and social (preference-aligned conversation). To address these challenges, they propose EvoMem, a patch-based memory paradigm that stores memory evolution as structured update histories, allowing agents to reason about how and when the environment changed. The memory records diffs/patches rather than full snapshots, enabling efficient tracking of state evolution.

Result: Current agents average only 39.6% accuracy on EvoArena across all three domains. EvoMem yields a 1.5% average improvement on EvoArena, and boosts performance on GAIA by 6.1% and LoCoMo by 4.8%. It also improves chain-level accuracy (consecutive evolutionary subtasks) by 3.7%. Mechanistic analysis reveals EvoMem enhances evidence capture in memory, better preserving complete evolving environment states.

Conclusion: Modeling environmental evolution is critical for both evaluation and memory in LLM agent systems. EvoArena provides a needed benchmark for dynamic settings, and EvoMem demonstrates that structured, patch-based memory tracking of environmental changes significantly improves agent reliability and adaptability.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents have achieved strong performance on a wide range of benchmarks, yet most evaluations assume static environments. In contrast, real-world deployment is inherently dynamic, requiring agents to continually align their knowledge, skills, and behavior with changing environments and updated task conditions. To address this gap, we introduce EvoArena, a benchmark suite that models environment changes as sequences of progressive updates across terminal, software, and social domains. We further propose EvoMem, a patch-based memory paradigm that records memory evolution as structured update histories, enabling agents to reason about environmental evolution through changes in their memory. Experiments show that current agents struggle on EvoArena, achieving an average accuracy of 39.6% across evolving terminal, software, and social-preference domains. EvoMem consistently improves performance, yielding an average gain of 1.5% on EvoArena and also improving standard benchmarks such as GAIA and LoCoMo by 6.1% and 4.8%. Beyond individual tasks, EvoMem further improves chain-level accuracy by 3.7% on EvoArena, where success requires completing a consecutive sequence of related evolutionary subtasks. Mechanistic analysis shows that EvoMem improves evidence capture in the memory, indicating better preservation of complete evolving environment states. Our results highlight the importance of modeling evolution in both evaluation and memory for reliable agent deployment.

</details>


<div id='cs.LG'></div>

# cs.LG [[Back]](#toc)

### [68] [Quickest Detection of Hallucination Onset: Delay Bounds and Learned CUSUM Statistics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12476)
*Igor Itkin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Token-level hallucination detectors are conventionally evaluated via AUC classification metrics, which obscure temporal detection delay. The authors reframe hallucination onset detection as a quickest change detection problem, derive a theoretical lower bound (~1.3 tokens delay), and demonstrate that a causal recurrent labeler (behaving as a learned CUSUM) achieves 11–13 token detection delay vs. 31 for a linear baseline. An information-rate analysis reveals the gap to optimality stems from the learned score capturing only ~1/4.5 of the available feature divergence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluation of token-level hallucination detectors uses aggregate classification metrics (e.g., AUC over all tokens), which fail to capture the temporally critical aspect of streaming monitoring: how quickly a hallucination is detected after onset. The paper aims to make detection delay measurable and optimizable through the lens of sequential change-point detection theory.

Method: The authors formulate hallucination onset detection as a quickest change detection (QCD) problem with a first-order Markov latent state model (faithful → hallucinated), validated on the RAGTruth corpus. They derive Lorden's lower bound on detection delay at a fixed false-alarm rate. A causal recurrent labeler is shown to implement a CUSUM procedure with a learned increment. Performance is decomposed via a controlled comparison into contributions from per-token scoring quality vs. temporal accumulation, and an information-rate bound (Donsker–Varadhan type) quantifies the gap between learned and optimal score functions.

Result: Lorden's bound gives a theoretical minimum delay of ~1.3 tokens at 0.01 false-alarm rate. The causal recurrent detector achieves 11–13 token delay vs. 31 tokens for a linear per-token baseline at matched false-alarm rates. Decomposition shows the advantage is primarily from better per-token scoring rather than temporal integration. The learned score realizes only ~1/4.5 of the feature divergence; this deficit cannot be fixed by recalibration and explains the remaining order-of-magnitude gap to optimality, with the residual attributed to finite-horizon effects.

Conclusion: Classification metrics conceal the temporal structure of hallucination detection; sequential analysis exposes it. The substantial gap between the learned detector and the theoretical lower bound (1.3 vs. 11–13 tokens) is largely due to suboptimal per-token scoring that captures insufficient information from available features, not a failure of temporal accumulation. This reframes hallucination detector evaluation and points to improving per-token score functions as the primary path forward.

Abstract: Token-level hallucination detectors are evaluated as classifiers, by AUC over all tokens, yet a streaming monitor is judged by its reaction time: the number of tokens that pass between the onset of a hallucination and the alarm. We formulate hallucination onset detection as a quickest change detection problem. A first-order Markov model of the latent faithful/hallucinated state, validated on RAGTruth, places the task inside classical change-point theory and yields Lorden's lower bound on detection delay: about 1.3 tokens at a false-alarm rate of 0.01. We then show that a causal recurrent labeler acts as a CUSUM with a learned increment; at a matched false-alarm rate it detects in 11-13 tokens, against 31 for a linear per-token baseline, and a controlled decomposition attributes most of this advantage to a better per-token score rather than to temporal accumulation. An information-rate optimality theorem of Donsker-Varadhan type explains the remaining order-of-magnitude gap: the learned score realizes only 1/4.5 of the divergence the features carry, a deficit that recalibration cannot remove, with the remainder a finite-horizon effect. Classification metrics conceal this delay structure; sequential analysis makes it measurable

</details>


### [69] [Boltzmann Attention: Learnable Ising Couplings for Cooperative Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12478)
*Gilhan Kim,Daniel K. Park*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes Boltzmann attention, which augments standard attention with learnable pairwise couplings via an Ising model formulation, enabling explicit modeling of inter-position interactions. It improves over softmax attention on language modeling and bracket matching tasks, especially for longer sequences, and demonstrates that quantum annealing can serve as a practical training method.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard attention computes relevance through individual query-key similarities with only implicit competition via softmax normalization. It lacks explicit, learnable parameterization of cooperative or antagonistic interactions between attention decisions, limiting the ability to directly model structured co-attention patterns.

Method: Boltzmann attention formulates attention as an energy-based model governed by an interacting Ising model. Beyond data-dependent local fields (standard query-key scores), it introduces learnable pairwise couplings between positions. Attention patterns are sampled from the resulting Boltzmann distribution. The authors also explore diabatic quantum annealing as an alternative sampling strategy for training.

Result: On character-level language modeling and synthetic bracket matching, Boltzmann attention consistently outperforms standard softmax attention in a Transformer architecture, with gains increasing with sequence length. A four-way ablation confirms the improvement is attributable to the learnable pairwise couplings. Diabatic quantum annealing matches the performance of exact Boltzmann computation.

Conclusion: Explicit inter-position interactions provide a principled enhancement for attention mechanisms. The Ising formulation not only improves modeling capacity but also opens a pathway toward leveraging quantum computing for efficient sampling during training.

Abstract: Attention mechanisms are central to modern sequence models, yet standard attention computes relevance primarily through individual query--key similarities. Although softmax normalization introduces competition among positions, a standard attention layer does not explicitly parameterize learnable interactions between attention decisions. This limits its ability to directly model cooperative or antagonistic co-attention structure within the attention mechanism itself. We propose Boltzmann attention, an energy-based generalization in which attention patterns are governed by an interacting Ising model. The method augments the usual data-dependent local fields with learnable pairwise couplings, allowing the model to represent inter-position correlations beyond those captured by softmax or sigmoid attention. Experiments on character-level language modeling and synthetic bracket matching show that Boltzmann attention consistently improves over standard softmax attention within a standard Transformer architecture, with the advantage becoming more pronounced as sequence length increases. A four-way ablation confirms that the improvement arises from the learnable pairwise couplings. These results suggest that explicit inter-position interactions provide a principled enhancement for attention-based sequence modeling. Moreover, the Ising formulation opens a natural path toward quantum-computing-based sampling strategies: we demonstrate that diabatic quantum annealing provides a practical training method while maintaining competitive performance with exact Boltzmann computation.

</details>


### [70] [ReCal: Reward Calibration for RL-based LLM Routing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12479)
*Qihang Yu,Hanwen Tong,Zhengqi Zhang,Bo Zheng,Feng Wei,Shengyu Zhang,Zemin Liu,Fei Wu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ReCal is a reward calibration framework for RL-based LLM routing that addresses ambiguous credit assignment and optimization bias via hierarchical reward decomposition and distribution-aware optimization, achieving consistent routing improvements across seven datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current RL-based LLM routing methods aggregate multiple objectives (correctness, format, etc.) into a single scalar reward, causing ambiguous credit assignment and conflicting optimization signals. Additionally, reward variability across instances biases optimization toward trivial samples over informative ones. No existing method addresses these reward calibration issues in the routing context.

Method: ReCal introduces two main components: (1) Hierarchical reward decomposition with component-wise advantage estimation, which disentangles the scalar reward into distinct sub-rewards for cleaner credit assignment and computes per-component advantages using a dedicated value function; and (2) Distribution-aware optimization that applies variance-aware reweighting to modulate optimization impact based on reward variability and per-dataset normalization to align reward scales across heterogeneous task distributions.

Result: Experiments on seven datasets show that ReCal consistently improves routing performance and training stability over baseline RL-based routing methods.

Conclusion: ReCal effectively resolves the reward calibration challenges in RL-based LLM routing, enabling more stable and effective policy learning across diverse tasks through hierarchical decomposition and distribution-aware optimization.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM) routing has emerged as an effective paradigm for leveraging the complementary strengths of multiple LLMs through dynamic model and reasoning-strategy selection. Recent reinforcement learning (RL)-based routing methods further improve routing quality by optimizing routing policies from interaction feedback. However, they still struggle to provide informative and comparable learning signals under heterogeneous tasks with varying difficulty. In practice, multiple objectives (e.g., correctness, format behavior) are aggregated into a single scalar reward, leading to ambiguous credit assignment and conflicting optimization signals. Moreover, reward signals exhibit significant variability across instances, where some instances produce higher or more variable rewards, introducing optimization bias that favors trivial samples over informative ones. To address these issues, we propose \textbf{ReCal}, a \textbf{\underline{Re}}ward \textbf{\underline{Cal}}ibration framework for RL-based LLM routing. We first introduce a hierarchical reward decomposition mechanism with component-wise advantage estimation. We further propose a distribution-aware optimization strategy that calibrates optimization variability through variance-aware reweighting and per-dataset normalization. Experiments on seven datasets demonstrate that ReCal consistently improves routing performance, and training stability over baselines. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ReCal.

</details>


### [71] [Representing Time Series as Structured Programs for LLM Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12481)
*Jaeho Kim,Changhun Oh,Seokhyun Lee,Irina Rish,Changhee Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: T2SP introduces a deterministic, training-free method that converts time series into structured symbolic programs (capturing trends, periods, events), enabling off-the-shelf LLMs to reason about temporal data without fine-tuning, yielding improved performance, faster reasoning, and lower failure rates across editing, captioning, and QA tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs excel at reasoning but natively operate on text/code, not numerical time series. Existing approaches either serialize raw numbers or require costly fine-tuning, forcing LLMs to extract temporal structure themselves — a modality mismatch that degrades performance on long sequences and incurs high computational overhead.
