Method: T2SP (Time-Series-to-Structured-Program) is a deterministic, training-free representation method that decomposes a time series into three components — trends, periods, and salient events — and expresses them as a structured symbolic program. This shifts temporal-structure extraction from the model to the representation, aligning the input format with LLMs' native textual/code modalities.

Result: Evaluated on three reasoning tasks (editing, captioning, question answering), T2SP consistently improves performance, reduces reasoning time, and lowers failure rates compared to raw-string numerical representations.

Conclusion: T2SP provides an effective interface between time series and LLMs, enabling off-the-shelf models to leverage their inherent reasoning capabilities for temporal analysis without modality mismatch or fine-tuning overhead.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong reasoning and instruction-following capabilities, making them potentially powerful tools for time-series analysis. However, time series lie outside their native textual modality, raising a fundamental question: how should time series be represented so that LLMs can reason about them effectively? Existing work typically serializes raw numerical sequences or fine-tunes pre-trained LLMs on time-series data. These approaches place the burden of extracting temporal structure directly on the LLM, creating a modality mismatch that often degrades performance on long sequences and introduces substantial computational overhead. In this work, we introduce Time-Series-to-Structured-Program representation (T2SP), a deterministic, training-free method that represents a time series as a structured symbolic program. T2SP decomposes time series into trends, periods, and salient events, expressing them in a program-friendly format aligned with the textual and code-like modalities on which LLMs are natively trained. By shifting temporal-structure extraction from the model to the representation itself, T2SP enables off-the-shelf LLMs to leverage their existing reasoning capabilities for time-series understanding. We evaluate T2SP on three reasoning tasks -- editing, captioning, and question answering -- where it consistently improves performance, reduces reasoning time, and lowers failure rates compared with raw-string representations. Our results demonstrate that T2SP provides an effective interface between time series and LLMs.

</details>


### [72] [Scalable anomaly detection via a univariate Christoffel function](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12483)
*Florian Grivet,Didier Henrion,Jean-Bernard Lasserre,Louise Travé-Massuyès*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes UCF (Univariate Christoffel Function), a scalable variant of Christoffel function-based anomaly detection that overcomes the exponential matrix inversion bottleneck. By using squared distances to support points, UCF retains the on-off support dichotomy and support shape capture while achieving state-of-the-art performance on ADBench, outperforming 14 baselines in Average Precision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Christoffel function-based anomaly detection methods, while theoretically appealing and computationally frugal compared to deep learning, suffer from a critical scalability limitation: they require inverting a matrix whose size grows exponentially with data dimension, making them intractable for even moderate-dimensional datasets. This work aims to resolve this dimensionality bottleneck while preserving the method's core theoretical properties.

Method: The authors introduce UCF (Univariate Christoffel Function), which reformulates the Christoffel function using the squared distance between the query point and the support points, reducing the problem to a univariate setting. This avoids the exponential matrix size growth while preserving the on-off support dichotomy behavior and accurate support shape capture properties of the original Christoffel function.

Result: Extensive experiments on the ADBench benchmark show that UCF consistently outperforms 14 state-of-the-art baselines in terms of Average Precision, demonstrating both scalability and superior anomaly detection performance.

Conclusion: By resolving the scalability bottleneck of the Christoffel function, this work expands the anomaly detection toolkit with a robust, theoretically grounded, and universally applicable approach, making polynomial optimization-based methods viable for real-world, moderate-to-high-dimensional anomaly detection tasks.

Abstract: Anomaly detection plays a critical role in identifying unusual patterns across domains such as fraud detection, network intrusion, and system fault diagnosis. Recently, Christoffel function-based methods, rooted in polynomial optimization, have emerged as promising alternatives to deep learning due to their strong mathematical foundations and computational frugality. However, their practical applicability is hindered by the need to invert a matrix whose size grows exponentially with the data dimension, rendering the method intractable even for moderate-dimensional datasets. This paper addresses the dimensionality limitations of Christoffel function-based anomaly detection while preserving its key theoretical properties, i.e., the on-off support dichotomy behavior and the accurate support shape capture. We introduce UCF, a univariate Christoffel function which is based on the squared distance between the query point and the support points. Extensive experiments on the ADBench benchmark demonstrate that UCF consistently outperforms 14 state-of-the-art baselines in terms of Average Precision. By resolving the scalability bottleneck of the Christoffel Function, this work expands the toolkit of anomaly detection methods with a robust, theoretically grounded, and universally applicable approach.

</details>


### [73] [Speculative Rollback Correction for Quality-Diverse Web Agent Imitation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12485)
*Longkun Hao,Hongyu Lin,Hao Li,Zhichao Yang,Haojie Hao,Dongshuo Huang,Haitao Yang,Hongyu Ge,Ming jie Xie,Yanjun Wu,Zi Hao Yin,Yan Bai,Yihang Lou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SRC proposes a branch-level imitation learning framework where student agents execute short speculative segments before teacher review, with rollback to the first harmful deviation. This balances intervention timing, improving recovery-versus-query tradeoffs and collecting diverse high-quality trajectories for fine-tuning web agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The core challenge is determining optimal expert intervention timing in imitation learning for web agents. Intervention that is too late allows cascading errors to push environments into irrecoverable states, while intervention that is too early or frequent causes over-reliance on expert policies and convergence to rigid local optima with a single trajectory pattern.

Method: SRC uses fixed-horizon branch review: (1) the student agent executes a short speculative segment autonomously; (2) the teacher reviews only when local progress breaks, localizing the first harmful deviation; (3) rollback preserves the useful prefix up to that deviation point; (4) successful rollouts are filtered by a hard verifier and stored in a lightweight quality-diversity archive; (5) the collected data supports next-action supervised fine-tuning on both localized corrections and verifier-passing trajectories.

Result: On WebArena-Infinity, SRC collected 977 verifier-passing trajectories and 9,183 next-action examples. Fixed-horizon review demonstrated an improved recovery-versus-query tradeoff compared to step-level review while retaining verifier-passing solution variants.

Conclusion: SRC effectively addresses the intervention timing dilemma in imitation learning for resettable web agent environments, enabling efficient data collection for supervised fine-tuning without trapping agents in local optima or allowing irrecoverable state degradation.

Abstract: Training interactive web agents through imitation learning from expert trajectories has emerged as a highly effective approach. However, determining the optimal timing for expert intervention presents a critical challenge in this context. Delayed intervention often leads to the accumulation of early-stage errors, pushing the page state into an irrecoverable regime. Conversely, premature or excessive intervention causes the agent to become overly reliant on expert policies, trapping the model in local optima characterized by a single, rigid trajectory. We propose Speculative Rollback Correction (SRC), a branch-level imitation framework for resettable agent environments. Instead of requesting teacher labels at every visited state or correcting only after a completed trajectory, SRC uses fixed-horizon branch review: the student executes a short speculative segment before teacher review, and the teacher localizes the first harmful deviation only when local progress breaks. Rollback preserves useful prefixes, while successful rollouts are filtered by a hard verifier and retained in a lightweight quality-diversity archive. The resulting data supports next-action supervised fine-tuning on both localized corrections and verifier-passing trajectories. On WebArena-Infinity, SRC collects 977 verifier-passing trajectories and 9,183 next-action examples; fixed-horizon review improves the recovery-versus-query tradeoff over step-level review while retaining verifier-passing solution variants. Code is available at https://github.com/LongkunHao/SRC_gui_agent.

</details>


### [74] [An Empirical Study on Predictive Maintenance for Component X in Heavy-Duty Scania Trucks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12486)
*Valeriu Dimidov,Sasan Jafarnejad,Raphaël Frank*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A PdM methodology for truck fleets models wear as a monotonic time series, converts recent observations into tabular data, and applies AutoML for classification, outperforming SOTA on the Scania Component X dataset in cost reduction and simplicity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Implementing condition-based PdM for truck fleets is difficult due to massive data volumes, the complexity of failure detection from sensor data, and the challenge of finding cost-effective trade-offs. Existing approaches lack a practical, simplified yet cost-efficient framework.

Method: The methodology assumes component wear-and-tear follows a monotonically non-decreasing time series. Only the most recent observations are selected and transformed into a tabular format. Machine learning models designed for tabular data, coupled with AutoML, are then used for classification.

Result: The proposed methodology reduces costs on the Scania Component X dataset compared to current state-of-the-art approaches.

Conclusion: The monotonic wear assumption, combined with recent-observation selection, tabular transformation, and AutoML-based classification, provides a simpler and more cost-effective condition-based PdM solution for truck fleets than existing SOTA methods.

Abstract: Condition-based Predictive Maintenance (PdM) for truck fleets has gained momentum in recent years. This maintenance strategy aims to minimize unplanned downtimes and reduce costs by monitoring the health status of vehicles and taking proactive action based on their condition. However, the implementation of condition-based PdM systems is challenging due to the large volume of data generated by the trucks, the inherent complexity of detecting failures through sensor data and the difficulties in finding cost-effective trade-offs in the solution's implementation. In this paper, we define and validate a condition-based PdM methodology built on the assumption that the wear-and-tear state of the monitored component can be represented as a monotonically non-decreasing time series. It involves selecting only the most recent observations from the time series and transforming them into a tabular format for classification using machine learning (ML) models designed for tabular data. Our results indicate that the proposed methodology reduces costs on the Scania Component X dataset compared to current state-of-the-art (SOTA) approaches, while also simplifying the modeling process through AutoML.

</details>


### [75] [DynamicPTQ: Mitigating Activation Quantization Collapse via Residual-Stream Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12487)
*Zimo Zhao,Maolin Wang,Bowen Yu,Bowen Liu,Xiao Han,Xiangyu Zhao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DynamicPTQ addresses 4-bit activation quantization instability in LLMs by identifying that massive activations follow a phase-wise pattern across layers. It proposes a mixed-precision strategy that assigns 8-bit precision only to quantization-sensitive layers identified via residual-stream dynamics, improving perplexity and QA performance with minimal throughput overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PTQ methods use static transformation-based smoothing (orthogonal rotations, affine scaling) to handle massive activations but fail to account for cross-layer residual-stream dynamics, where massive activations emerge and disappear in phases, causing newly injected layer updates to dominate quantization scales and degrade historical residual information.

Method: The authors introduce two metrics — Jump Ratio and Historical Feature SNR — to characterize dynamic quantization instability from residual-stream changes. They then propose DynamicPTQ, a phase-aware mixed-precision policy that detects quantization-sensitive layers from residual-stream dynamics and selectively assigns 8-bit activation precision to those layers while keeping weights, KV caches, and other activations at 4-bit (W4A4KV4). The method integrates directly with existing PTQ baselines (QuaRot, SpinQuant, FlatQuant).

Result: Experiments on LLaMA-2 and LLaMA-3 demonstrate consistent improvements in perplexity and zero-shot QA under W4A4KV4 quantization, with 1.05–1.07× throughput improvement and modest memory overhead.

Conclusion: DynamicPTQ offers a practical path toward robust low-bit LLM inference by dynamically addressing phase-wise massive activation patterns through mixed-precision quantization, outperforming purely static smoothing approaches.

Abstract: Post-training quantization (PTQ) is essential for efficient large language model inference, but reliably quantizing activations remains challenging when weights, activations, and KV caches are all quantized to 4-bit precision. A key difficulty lies in massive activations, whose extreme values dominate the activation range and amplify quantization errors. State-of-the-art methods mainly mitigate massive activations through transformation-based smoothing, such as orthogonal rotations and affine scaling, but overlook the cross-layer dynamics of the residual stream. In this paper, we show that massive activations emerge and disappear in a phase-wise pattern across network depth, triggering large residual changes. These changes cause newly injected layer-wise updates to dominate the 4-bit quantization scale and weaken historical residual information. To characterize this behavior, we introduce Jump Ratio and Historical Feature SNR. This suggests that static transformation-based smoothing cannot fully resolve dynamic quantization instability caused by cross-layer residual changes. Based on this analysis, we propose DynamicPTQ, a Dynamic Post-Training Quantization policy for phase-aware mixed-precision activation quantization. DynamicPTQ identifies quantization-sensitive layers from residual-stream dynamics and assigns 8-bit activation precision only to these layers, while keeping weights, KV caches, and other activations in 4-bit precision. It can be directly integrated with strong PTQ baselines such as QuaRot, SpinQuant, and FlatQuant. Experiments on LLaMA-2 and LLaMA-3 show that DynamicPTQ consistently improves perplexity and zero-shot QA performance under W4A4KV4 quantization, while achieving 1.05 to 1.07 times throughput improvement with modest memory overhead. These results demonstrate a practical path toward robust low-bit LLM inference.

</details>


### [76] [A Stationary (and Therefore Compatible) Representation is All You Need](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12488)
*Niccolò Biondi,Federico Pernici,Simone Ricci,Alberto Del Bimbo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proves that d-Simplex fixed classifiers yield stationary, compatible representations, allowing seamless model updates without reprocessing gallery data. It further shows that combining cross-entropy with contrastive loss captures higher-order dependencies beyond first-order statistics, achieving state-of-the-art in retrieval under sequential fine-tuning and model replacement scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: When retrieval models are updated over time, new feature representations are typically incompatible with previously indexed gallery features, necessitating costly reprocessing. The authors aim to learn representations that remain compatible across model updates, enabling uninterrupted retrieval services. Prior work with d-Simplex fixed classifiers only aligns first-order statistics, missing higher-order dependencies.

Method: The authors formally prove that d-Simplex fixed classifiers imply compatible (stationary) representations. To address the limitation of first-order-only alignment, they propose training with a convex combination of cross-entropy loss and a contrastive loss under a d-Simplex fixed classifier. They show this combination is theoretically equivalent to cross-entropy learning with explicit compatibility constraints, capturing higher-order feature dependencies. Experiments cover sequential fine-tuning and a new scenario where a pre-trained model is periodically replaced with an improved version.

Result: The proposed method enables uninterrupted retrieval without reprocessing gallery images during model updates and replacements. It achieves state-of-the-art performance by improving both compatibility and retrieval accuracy compared to baselines that use cross-entropy alone under d-Simplex fixed classifiers.

Conclusion: Stationary representations induced by d-Simplex fixed classifiers provide a principled foundation for learning compatible representations. Augmenting cross-entropy with contrastive loss effectively captures higher-order dependencies, yielding superior compatibility and retrieval performance during sequential model updates and replacements.

Abstract: Learning compatible representations aims to learn feature representations that can be used interchangeably over time whenever a model undergoes updates. In this paper, we demonstrate that stationary representations learned by d-Simplex fixed classifiers imply compatibility as in its formal definition. This result establishes a foundation for future works and can be directly exploited in practical learning scenarios. We address the challenge of learning compatibility using $d$-Simplex fixed classifiers when the model is sequentially fine-tuned. Learning according to a d-Simplex fixed classifier with the cross-entropy loss aligns feature distributions at the first-order statistics. Consequently, it may not fully capture higher-order dependencies in the representation between model updates. To address this issue, we demonstrate that training the model using a $d$-Simplex fixed classifier through a convex combination of the cross-entropy loss and a contrastive loss not only captures higher-order dependencies, but is also equivalent to learning with the cross-entropy under the compatibility constraints. We confirm our findings with extensive experiments also considering a new scenario where a pre-trained model is sequentially fine-tuned and occasionally replaced with an improved model. We show that stationary representations enable uninterrupted retrieval services (without reprocessing gallery images) while improving performance during model updates and replacements, achieving state-of-the-art. Code at https://github.com/miccunifi/iamcl2r.

</details>


### [77] [Robustness Verification of Recurrent Neural Networks with Abstraction Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12490)
*Li-Jen Lin,Chih-Duo Hong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes an abstraction-refinement framework for certified robustness verification of RNNs. It partitions pre-activation intervals that cross zero to eliminate dominant relaxation errors from nonlinear bound propagation. A SHAP-guided timestep selection strategy ranks hidden states by their contribution to the verification objective, refining only the most critical timesteps to control combinatorial splitting costs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Certified local robustness verification for RNNs suffers from approximation errors introduced by nonlinear relaxations (ReLU, tanh, sigmoid) that propagate through recurrent connections and accumulate over time. Standard linear bound propagation methods become overly conservative, especially when many pre-activation intervals cross zero, causing them to fail on actually robust inputs.

Method: An abstraction-refinement framework that partitions pre-activation intervals crossing zero. On each refined branch, ReLU becomes exact, and smooth activations (tanh, sigmoid) get tighter linear envelopes. To manage combinatorial explosion in long sequences, a SHAP-guided timestep selection strategy ranks hidden states by their contribution to the verification objective and refines only the most critical timesteps in temporal order.

Result: Experiments on CIFAR10 and MNIST stroke benchmarks show consistent improvements in verification success and robustness-margin tightness over abstraction-only baselines. Clear runtime trade-offs between ReLU and tanh models are exposed.

Conclusion: Abstraction-refinement with SHAP-guided splitting effectively reduces the conservativeness of RNN certification, improving verification rates while offering a tunable trade-off between precision and computational cost.

Abstract: Certified local robustness verification for recurrent neural networks (RNNs) is challenging because approximation errors introduced by nonlinear relaxations can propagate through recurrent connections and accumulate over time. As a result, scalable linear bound propagation methods often become overly conservative and fail to certify inputs that are in fact robust, especially when many pre-activation intervals cross zero. We propose an abstraction-refinement framework for RNN verification that partitions such intervals to remove the dominant relaxation error: on each refined branch, ReLU becomes exact, and smooth activations such as tanh and sigmoid admit substantially tighter linear envelopes. To control the combinatorial cost of splitting in long sequences, we introduce a SHAP-guided timestep selection strategy that ranks hidden states by their contribution to the verification objective and refines only the most critical timesteps in temporal order. Experiments on CIFAR10 and MNIST stroke benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in verification success and robustness-margin tightness over abstraction-only baselines, while exposing clear runtime trade-offs between ReLU and tanh models.

</details>


### [78] [Net-Ev$^2$: A Generative Simulator for Network Event Evolution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12494)
*Guangyu Wang,Zhaonan Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Net-Ev² is a two-stage generative simulator for modeling how disturbance events propagate impacts across real-world networks, combining structure-guided masked pre-training with a topology-aware diffusion process. It accepts natural-language event input and preserves network topology during simulation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches fail to jointly model both structured attributes and unstructured semantics of events while capturing topological structures when simulating network event evolution. There is a need for a simulator that can model how disturbance events (e.g., accidents) propagate their impacts across real-world networks.

Method: Net-Ev² uses a two-stage framework: (1) structure-guided masked pre-training, and (2) a topology-aware diffusion process with U-Net-like graph downsampling and upsampling during denoising. At inference, it generates simulations from natural-language event input only. The authors also introduce Net-Ev²-6.5M, a multimodal benchmark of aligned event and network traffic data across four large-scale road networks, and JL-MMD, a topology-aware metric for evaluating topological fidelity in generated network dynamics.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance and strong generalization ability of Net-Ev² compared to existing approaches.

Conclusion: Net-Ev² effectively addresses the challenging task of simulating event-driven network evolution by jointly leveraging event cues and preserving network topology, achieving superior performance with flexible natural-language inference capabilities.

Abstract: Reducing real-world trial and error has long been a central goal of decision making, and generative simulators advance this goal by modeling the evolution of future states. An even more challenging yet meaningful task is simulating how disturbance events (e.g., accidents) propagate their impacts across real-world networks. The existing approaches fall short of modeling both structured attributes and unstructured semantics of events, and capturing topological structures in simulating network event evolution. Therefore, we are motivated to propose Net-Ev$^2$ ($\underline{\textbf{Net}}$work $\underline{\textbf{Ev}}$ent $\underline{\textbf{Ev}}$olution), a novel generative simulator that jointly leverages event cues while preserving network topology in simulations. Specifically, the framework consists of two stages, namely structure-guided masked pre-training and topology-aware diffusion process, which is achieved by U-Net-like graph downsampling and upsampling during denoising. At inference time, Net-Ev$^2$ can generate simulations using natural-language event input only, with greater flexibility for practical usage. Furthermore, we introduce Net-Ev$^2$-6.5M, a multimodal benchmark of aligned event and network traffic data across four large-scale road networks, as well as a new topology-aware metric, namely JL-MMD, to evaluate topological fidelity in generated network dynamics. Extensive experiments demonstrate the state-of-the-art performance and strong generalization ability of Net-Ev$^2$. Code is made available at https://github.com/Guangyu4/Net-Ev-2.

</details>


### [79] [$μ$VLA: On Recurrent Memory for Partially Observable Manipulation in VLA Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12497)
*Egor Cherepanov,Nikita Kachaev,Daniil Zelezetsky,Aydar Bulatov,Artem Pshenitsyn,Yuri Kuratov,Alexey Skrynnik,Aleksandr I. Panov,Alexey K. Kovalev*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: μVLA introduces minimal recurrence into pretrained VLA backbones via learnable memory tokens updated through self-attention, with no auxiliary losses or architectural changes. It isolates recurrence's contribution, showing significant gains under partial observability (0.42→0.84 on training tasks) while maintaining full-observability performance (96.2% on LIBERO), and identifies where memory structure is vs. isn't needed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing memory-augmented VLAs conflate recurrence with retrieval, compression, hierarchical memory, and auxiliary objectives, making it unclear what recurrence alone contributes. Under partial observability, VLA models that assume decisions from current observations alone fail when information is no longer visible. The authors aim to isolate recurrence's contribution through a controlled study.

Method: μVLA augments a pretrained OpenVLA-OFT transformer with a small set of learnable memory tokens carried across timesteps and updated via self-attention. Training uses end-to-end truncated backpropagation through time (TBPTT) with no auxiliary losses. Variants are parameterized by memory width (m), TBPTT length (K), and memory update rule (cross-step gradients vs. detached EMA), ensuring recurrence is the only varying factor. Evaluated on MIKASA-Robo and LIBERO benchmarks.

Result: On MIKASA-Robo, μVLA improves average success rate on five training tasks from 0.42 to 0.84 (strongest setting); held-out task performance reaches 0.23 vs. 0.07 baseline. On tasks requiring different memory structure, performance stays near baseline. On LIBERO (full observability), the strongest recurrent variant achieves 96.2%, showing no regression.

Conclusion: Minimal in-backbone recurrence is sufficient for many partial-observability tasks, but certain tasks require additional memory structure. The results calibrate the capability envelope of recurrence alone, identifying where it suffices and where structured memory becomes necessary.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models predict chunks of future actions from the current observation, an assumption that fails under partial observability, where decisions depend on information no longer visible. Existing memory-augmented VLAs simultaneously introduce recurrence, retrieval, compression modules, auxiliary objectives, hierarchical memory, or task-specific architectural changes, so the contribution of recurrence itself remains entangled with surrounding machinery. We present a controlled isolation study of recurrence in a strong pretrained VLA backbone. Our formulation augments the transformer with a small set of learnable memory tokens carried across timesteps and updated through self-attention, trained end to end with truncated backpropagation through time, with no auxiliary losses and no architectural changes. We instantiate this as $μ$VLA, a family of OpenVLA-OFT variants parameterized by memory width m, TBPTT length K, and the memory update rule (cross-step gradients or a detached EMA), so that recurrence is the only varying factor. On MIKASA-Robo, $μ$VLA improves average success rate on five training tasks from 0.42 to 0.84 at the strongest setting and reaches 0.23 on held-out tasks with the same memory structure versus 0.07 for the memoryless baseline. On tasks requiring different memory structure, performance remains near baseline. On LIBERO, the strongest recurrent variant achieves 96.2% average success, indicating no regression under full observability. We interpret these results as a calibration of the capability envelope of minimal in-backbone recurrence, identifying the regime in which it is sufficient and the regime where additional memory structure is required. Demos and videos can be found in https://avanturist322.github.io/mu-vla/.

</details>


### [80] [Improving Crash Frequency Prediction from Simulated Traffic Conflicts Using Machine Learning Based Microsimulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12500)
*Xian Liu,Carlo G. Prato,Gustav Markkula*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether machine learning-based driver behavior models in traffic microsimulation improve crash frequency predictions compared to traditional rule-based models. Using surrogate safety measures and Extreme Value Theory at five UK intersections, the ML model produced crash predictions consistent with real-world data without location-specific calibration, while the rule-based model failed. However, directly using ML-generated crashes yielded poor predictions, indicating ML models currently capture conflicts well but not actual crashes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing microsimulation-based safety studies rely on simplified rule-based behavior models that fail to generate realistic conflict dynamics, limiting crash prediction accuracy. Recent ML-based behavior models trained on large-scale trajectory data offer a potential solution, but their effectiveness for crash frequency prediction had not been rigorously evaluated against real-world crash data.

Method: Traffic microsimulation was conducted at five signalized intersections in Leeds, UK, using both a standard rule-based model and a state-of-the-art ML model. Simulated vehicle trajectories were analyzed using a two-dimensional Time-to-Collision (TTC) metric to identify conflicts. These conflicts were then modeled using Extreme Value Theory (EVT) to predict crash frequency. Predictions from both models were compared against real-world crash data.

Result: Conflicts from the ML model yielded crash predictions consistent with real-world crash data, whereas the rule-based model failed to produce meaningful predictions due to lack of location-specific calibration. Directly using ML-generated simulated crashes to predict real-world crash frequency performed poorly, indicating ML models can realistically reproduce conflicts but not yet crashes.

Conclusion: ML-based behavior models are promising for improving crash prediction from simulated conflicts without requiring location-specific calibration. The findings highlight a clear future direction: advancing ML models to better generate realistic crashes in microsimulation.

Abstract: Traffic microsimulation combined with surrogate safety measures has increasingly been used as a proactive alternative to historical crash data for predicting crash frequency for current or planned road infrastructure designs. However, existing microsimulation-based safety studies have adopted simplified rule-based behaviour models, which reproduce traffic flow reasonably well but often fail to generate realistic conflict dynamics, limiting crash prediction accuracy. Recent advances in machine learning (ML)-based behaviour models offer a promising opportunity to potentially improve microsimulation realism and crash frequency predictions by learning human driving behaviour directly from large-scale trajectory datasets. To investigate this possibility, traffic microsimulation was conducted for five real-world signalised intersections in Leeds, UK, using both a standard rule-based model and a state-of-the-art ML model. Simulated vehicle trajectories were analysed using a two-dimensional Time-to-Collision metric to identify simulated conflicts, which were then modelled using Extreme Value Theory to predict crash frequency. Results show that conflicts from the ML model yielded crash predictions in line with the real-world crash data, whereas the rule-based model did not permit meaningful predictions, presumably due to a lack of model calibration to the specific simulated intersections. Directly using ML-generated simulated crashes to predict real-world crash frequency also yielded poor results, suggesting that while current ML models can realistically reproduce conflicts, they are not yet able to generate realistic crashes. Overall, the findings demonstrate that ML-based behaviour models are promising for improving crash prediction from simulated conflicts, without a need for location-specific model calibration, and suggest clear future directions for ML-based traffic microsimulation.

</details>


### [81] [Policy-driven Conformal Prediction for Trustworthy QoT Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12501)
*Kiarash Rezaei,Omran Ayoub,Paolo Monti,Carlos Natalino*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Conformal QoT is a policy-driven framework that integrates statistically guaranteed quality-of-transmission (QoT) estimation with operational decision policies, achieving reliable lightpath-feasibility predictions under domain shift and boosting accuracy from 92% to 99.6% on open datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate QoT estimation is critical for optical network planning, but existing methods lack statistical guarantees and suffer performance degradation under domain shift (distributional mismatch between training and deployment data). The authors aim to provide reliable, uncertainty-aware lightpath-feasibility predictions with formal coverage guarantees.

Method: The framework, Conformal QoT, combines conformal prediction (a distribution-free uncertainty quantification technique) with policy-driven operational decision rules. Conformal prediction produces prediction sets with finite-sample coverage guarantees, while the decision policy layer translates these sets into actionable feasibility decisions (e.g., accept, reject, or flag for manual inspection), balancing risk and operational constraints under domain shift.

Result: On open datasets, Conformal QoT improved lightpath-feasibility prediction accuracy from 92% to 99.6%, while maintaining the statistical coverage guarantees of conformal prediction even under domain shift conditions.

Conclusion: The integration of conformal prediction with operational decision policies yields a robust, statistically grounded framework for QoT estimation that significantly outperforms conventional methods, especially under distributional shift, making it suitable for real-world optical network operations where reliability is paramount.

Abstract: We propose Conformal QoT, a policy-driven framework that combines statistically guaranteed QoT estimation with operational decision policies, enabling reliable lightpath-feasibility predictions under domain shift and improving accuracy from 92\% to 99.6\% on open datasets.

</details>


### [82] [Dolph2Vec: Self-Supervised Representations of Dolphin Vocalizations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12503)
*Chiara Semenzin,Faadil Mustun,Roberto Dessi,Pierre Orhan,Alexis Emanuelli,Yair Lakretz,Gonzalo de Polavieja,German Sumbre*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces Dolph2Vec, a species-specific self-supervised learning model trained on five years of longitudinal dolphin recordings. It significantly outperforms general-purpose SSL baselines on signature whistle classification and detection tasks, while its learned representations reveal interpretable acoustic units aligned with dolphin whistle structure, positioning SSL as both a modeling and scientific discovery tool for animal communication research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current SSL models for bioacoustics prioritize broad cross-species generalization and are not optimized for capturing fine-grained, species-specific communication structures. There is a need for species-dedicated SSL models that can uncover interpretable acoustic units within individual communication systems.

Method: The authors collected a novel longitudinal dataset spanning over five years from five known dolphins in a semi-naturalistic marine environment. They adapt the Wav2Vec2.0 architecture to train Dolph2Vec exclusively on this species-specific data — the first large-scale SSL model of its kind. The model is benchmarked on signature whistle classification and whistle detection tasks, with analysis of learned embeddings and codebook structure to assess interpretability.

Result: Dolph2Vec significantly outperforms general-purpose SSL baselines on both signature whistle classification and whistle detection tasks. Furthermore, the learned embeddings and codebook structure capture interpretable acoustic units that correspond to dolphin whistle categories and potentially sub-whistle structures, enabling fine-grained communication analysis.

Conclusion: Species-specific SSL can serve a dual role — as a high-performance model for bioacoustic tasks and as a scientific tool for generating and exploring hypotheses about animal communication systems, revealing structure that general-purpose models miss.

Abstract: Self-supervised learning (SSL) has opened new opportunities in bioacoustics by enabling scalable modeling of animal vocalizations without the need for expensive manual annotation. However, current SSL models in this domain prioritize broad generalization across species and are not optimized for uncovering the fine-grained structure of individual communication systems. In this work, we collect and release a novel dataset of over five years of longitudinal recordings, from five known dolphins in a semi-naturalistic marine environment, an unprecedented resource for studying dolphin communication. We adapt the Wav2Vec2.0 Baevski et al. (2020) architecture to this domain and introduce Dolph2Vec, the first large-scale, species-specific SSL model trained exclusively on this data. We benchmark our model on two biologically relevant tasks: signature whistle classification and whistle detection. Dolph2Vec significantly outperforms general-purpose baselines in both tasks. Beyond performance, we show that learned embeddings and codebook structure capture interpretable acoustic units aligned with dolphin whistle categories and possibly sub-whistle structure, enabling fine-grained analysis of communication patterns. Our findings demonstrate how SSL can serve as both a model and a scientific tool to explore hypotheses in animal communication research.

</details>


### [83] [Boosting Direct Preference Optimization with Penalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12505)
*Pengwei Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DPOP extends DPO by adding a gated penalty on reference-greedy responses, activated when the policy still prefers rejected over chosen responses, yielding notable win-rate improvements on AlpacaEval 2.0.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing pairwise preference optimization methods (e.g., DPO, SimPO) only use chosen and rejected responses from a static dataset, ignoring the informative signal from what the reference model itself would generate for the same prompt. DPOP aims to exploit this overlooked signal.

Method: DPOP augments the standard DPO loss with a penalty term on reference-greedy responses (the most likely response under the reference model). Crucially, a gating mechanism activates this penalty only when the current policy assigns lower likelihood to the chosen response than to the rejected one, preventing unnecessary regularization when the model already ranks preferences correctly.

Result: On AlpacaEval 2.0, DPOP improves length-controlled win rate over DPO, SimPO, and AlphaDPO on Llama-3-8b-it (5.3% relative gain) and Gemma-2-9b-it (4.4% relative gain). Ablations show that a SimNPO-style length-normalized penalty outperforms both NPO and token-level unlikelihood in this setting.

Conclusion: The reference-greedy response provides a valuable and previously unused training signal. DPOP's gated penalty effectively leverages this signal, yielding consistent gains across models and outperforming related penalty-based variants.

Abstract: Offline preference optimization has become a practical substitute for reinforcement learning from human feedback, but pairwise objectives such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) and its variants use only the chosen and rejected responses stored in a static dataset. This leaves a useful signal unused: the response that the reference model itself would generate for the same prompt. We propose Direct Preference Optimization with Penalization (DPOP), a simple extension of DPO that augments the base preference loss with a gated penalty on reference-greedy responses. DPOP activates this penalty only when the current policy still assigns a lower likelihood to the preferred response than to the rejected response. On AlpacaEval 2.0, DPOP improves length-controlled win rate over DPO, SimPO, and AlphaDPO on both Llama-3-8b-it and Gemma-2-9b-it, achieving relative gains of 5.3\% and 4.4\% over baselines on the two models, respectively. Ablations further show that a SimNPO-style length-normalized penalty is stronger than NPO and token-level unlikelihood in this setting.

</details>


### [84] [Rubric-Guided Self-Distillation: Post-Training Without Rubric Verifiers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12507)
*MohammadHossein Rezaei,Anas Mahmoud,Zihao Wang,Utkarsh Tyagi,Advait Gosai,Razvan-Gabriel Dumitru,Aakash Sabharwal,Bing Liu,Yunzhong He*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RGSD distills rubric-conditioned policy outputs into an unconditioned student, providing dense per-token signals that replace costly LLM-judge verifiers while achieving comparable rubric satisfaction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing rubric-based training methods (e.g., GRPO) require an LLM verifier to score rollouts against rubrics, causing three problems: (1) substantial training-time computational overhead, (2) exposure to verifier-specific biases, and (3) reduction of rich rubric feedback to a sparse, end-of-trajectory reward signal. The authors seek a verifier-free alternative that retains dense, rubric-aligned learning signals.

Method: RGSD is a self-distillation framework where the base policy is conditioned on the rubric to act as a teacher, generating a target token distribution. An unconditioned student policy then learns by minimizing the KL divergence (token-by-token) against this teacher distribution across all timesteps. This replaces sparse trajectory-level rewards from an LLM judge with dense per-token supervision, entirely removing the verifier from the training loop. The method uses only one on-policy rollout per prompt.

Result: Evaluated on Qwen-2.5 (3B, 7B) and Qwen3-Thinking (4B, 8B) models across medical and science domains, RGSD achieves rubric satisfaction rates comparable to judge-based GRPO, while requiring no training-time verifier calls. Ablation studies reveal: (1) raw rubrics provide stronger teacher enrichment than self-generated reference responses; (2) a stronger GRPO judge can outperform RGSD in some settings, positioning RGSD as a complementary verifier-free alternative.

Conclusion: RGSD is a viable verifier-free training method for rubric-guided alignment, particularly advantageous when verifier cost or reliability is the primary bottleneck. It is positioned as complementary to judge-based methods like GRPO rather than a strict replacement.

Abstract: Rubrics have emerged as an alternative to RLVR in open-ended domains where a single ground-truth final answer is not available. Existing rubric-based training methods rely on an LLM verifier that scores each rollout against rubrics. This introduces substantial training-time overhead, exposes optimization to verifier-specific biases, and reduces rubric feedback to a sparse end-of-trajectory signal. We propose Rubric-Guided Self-Distillation (RGSD), a verifier-free training method in which the base policy, conditioned on the rubric, serves as the teacher for the unconditioned student. RGSD distills the rubric-conditioned teacher distribution into the student token-by-token, replacing sparse trajectory-level rewards with dense per-token learning signals and removing the LLM judge from the training loop entirely. Across Qwen-2.5 (3B, 7B) and Qwen3-Thinking (4B, 8B) models on medical and science domains, RGSD achieves rubric satisfaction comparable to judge-based GRPO while using one on-policy rollout per prompt and no training-time verifier calls. Ablations show that raw rubrics provide a stronger teacher enrichment signal than self-generated reference responses, while a stronger GRPO judge can outperform RGSD in some settings, positioning RGSD as a complementary verifier-free alternative when verifier cost or reliability is the bottleneck.

</details>


### [85] [Crossing the Validation Crisis: Cross-Validation Reduces Benchmarking Variance Surprisingly Well](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12552)
*Célestin Eve,Gaël Varoquaux,Thomas Moreau*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Cross-validation with multiple splits substantially improves the reliability of ML benchmarking by reducing variance; the authors introduce "sample gain" to quantify this virtual data augmentation and propose a dynamic early-stopping procedure based on early-fold estimates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Machine learning evaluation suffers from a validation crisis: limited test samples and algorithmic stochasticity make performance estimates unreliable, obscuring genuine advances. Cross-validation is underutilized—its potential to mitigate this variance through multiple splits has not been formally quantified.

Method: The authors introduce the concept of "sample gain," which quantifies the effective increase in sample size achieved by cross-validation splits in terms of variance reduction. They analyze how multiple splits reduce benchmarking variance on synthetic and real-world datasets (histopathology and NLP fine-tuning). They also propose a dynamic early-stopping procedure that estimates from the first few folds whether subsequent folds will yield substantial sample gains, avoiding diminishing returns.

Result: Multiple cross-validation splits substantially improve reliability and stability of performance estimates. Diminishing returns from additional splits set in later than conventionally expected. The proposed early-stopping procedure can effectively determine when further splitting is unnecessary.

Conclusion: Pushing cross-validation further on available samples is a practical and effective strategy for achieving robust, reliable benchmarking in modern machine learning, directly addressing the validation crisis.

Abstract: Modern machine learning progresses through empirical work, benchmarking new methods to evaluate relative performance. However, the statistical variability inherent to evaluation - exacerbated by the stochastic nature of many algorithms - often makes performance estimation unreliable due to the limited test samples available, leading to a validation crisis in which genuine advances are difficult to discern. In this work, we show that cross-validation improves markedly confidence when evaluating and comparing learning algorithm performances. We introduce the concept of sample gain, which quantifies the virtual data augmentation achieved by using multiple cross-validation splits to reduce benchmarking variance. Experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets (histopathologic scans and NLP fine-tuning) demonstrate that multiple splits can substantially improve the reliability and stability of performance estimates, with diminishing returns often setting in later than expected. We also introduce a procedure to dynamically early-stop cross-validation by estimating from the first few folds if subsequent folds will bring large sample gains. Our findings highlight the value of pushing cross-validation on available samples to achieve robust and reliable benchmarking.

</details>


### [86] [Emerging Flexible Designs for Geospatial Multimodal Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12595)
*Philipe Dias,Waqwoya Abebe,Abhishek Potnis,Aristeidis Tsaris,Dan Lu,Xiao Wang,Dalton Lunga*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A controlled, apples-to-apples comparison of leading foundation model architectures for geospatial multimodal reasoning, standardizing pretraining and evaluation to reveal design trade-offs in flexibility, modality alignment, and downstream performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Foundation models for Earth observation exhibit significant architectural diversity (encoder-only, encoder-decoder, masked autoencoding), making consistent performance comparison difficult. There is a need for practical guidance on architecture selection for next-generation geospatial FMs capable of robust multimodal reasoning across varied spectral band configurations.

Method: The authors standardize pretraining using identical self-supervised learning objectives and training datasets across multiple leading FM architectures. All models are parameterized consistently and evaluated on the GEOBench benchmark, covering both classification and segmentation tasks, with particular attention to flexibility across different spectral band configurations.

Result: The study reveals key design trade-offs between model flexibility, modality alignment, and downstream task performance. Specific architectural strengths and limitations are identified under controlled conditions, providing actionable insights for FM design in geospatial contexts.

Conclusion: Architectural choices in geospatial foundation models involve meaningful trade-offs between flexibility, cross-modal alignment, and task performance. The findings offer practical guidance for developing next-generation geospatial FMs with robust multimodal reasoning capabilities.

Abstract: Foundation models are rapidly transforming Earth observation by enabling scalable pretraining across diverse unlabeled geospatial modalities. However, their architectural diversity ranging from encoder-only to encoder-decoder and masked autoencoding paradigms makes it challenging to assess performance trade offs in a consistent manner. In this work, we present an apples-to-apples comparison of leading FM architectures designed for geospatial multimodal reasoning, with a particular focus on flexibility across varied spectral band configurations. We standardize pretraining using identical self supervised learning objectives and training datasets, and evaluate all models under consistent parameterization on the GEOBench benchmark across classification and segmentation tasks. Our results offer new insights into the design trade-offs between model flexibility, modality alignment, and downstream task performance. By highlighting architectural strengths and limitations under controlled conditions, this study provides practical guidance for building next generation geospatial foundation models capable of robust multimodal reasoning.

</details>


### [87] [Viral Proteins Reveal Geometry of Protein Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12609)
*Arthur Bigot,Harmon Bhasin,Core Francisco Park,Eugene Shakhnovich,Dianzhuo Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Protein language models (pLMs) trained on imbalanced datasets exhibit a "nativeness axis" in embedding space that orders sequences from well-modeled cellular proteins to viral proteins to random sequences. Scaling contracts this axis unevenly across viral families, yet viral-specific signal persists, enabling linear separability beyond what perplexity or shallow features offer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how protein language models represent underrepresented biological sequences — specifically viral proteins — given that pLMs are trained on highly imbalanced datasets dominated by cellular sequences.

Method: Using viral proteins as a case study across ESM model families, the authors analyze embedding spaces by measuring alignment with masked reconstruction perplexity, examining how scaling affects this alignment across viral families, and testing linear separability of viral proteins.

Result: A dominant nativeness axis was identified in embedding space that orders sequences from cellular (well-modeled) → viral → shuffled/random sequences. Scaling contracts this axis unevenly across viral families. Viral proteins remain linearly separable beyond what zero-shot perplexity and shallow sequence features can capture.

Conclusion: pLM representations are structured by a general notion of nativeness while preserving information specific to distinct biological groups, suggesting that even underrepresented sequences retain meaningful, group-specific signals in the embedding space.

Abstract: Protein language models are trained on highly imbalanced datasets, raising the question of how they represent underrepresented biological sequences. Using viral proteins as a case study across ESM model families, we identify a dominant nativeness axis in embedding space, aligned with masked reconstruction perplexity, that orders sequences from well-modeled cellular proteins through viral proteins to shuffled and random sequences. Scaling contracts this axis unevenly across viral families. Despite this, protein language model embeddings retain viral-specific signal: viral proteins remain linearly separable beyond zero-shot perplexity and shallow sequence features. Together, these results suggest that pLM representations are structured by a general notion of nativeness while preserving information specific to distinct biological groups.

</details>


### [88] [The Mathematics of AI Winters: The mathematical Taxonomy of Paradigm Fragility in AI Winter](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12610)
*Miquel Noguer i Alonso,David Pacheco Aznar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper argues that the first and second AI winters were driven not only by engineering failures and inflated expectations but also by genuine formal mathematical barriers—including limits in representation, optimization, complexity, statistical learnability, and high-dimensional approximation. It synthesizes these bottlenecks through key theoretical results (Minsky-Papert perceptron limits, Blum-Rivest NP-hardness, Stone's minimax rates, vanishing gradients, and VC/Valiant learning theory) and shows how later breakthroughs mitigated rather than eliminated them.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To complement the standard narrative that AI winters resulted solely from engineering shortcomings and commercial hype by demonstrating that the dominant paradigms of those periods also encountered provable formal barriers, thereby offering a richer, theoretically grounded explanation of AI's historical stumbles.

Method: A synthetic (non-archival) analysis that maps central disappointments in early AI onto mathematically precise bottlenecks, surveyed through landmark theoretical results: Minsky and Papert's perceptron impossibility theorems, Blum and Rivest's complexity-theoretic hardness of neural-network training, Stone's minimax rates for nonparametric high-dimensional estimation, vanishing-gradient analyses by Hochreiter and Bengio et al., and statistical learning theory of Vapnik-Chervonenkis, Valiant, and Blumer et al.

Result: The identified formal barriers align closely with key AI disappointments: perceptron limitations undermined early neural approaches; NP-hardness of training constrained optimization; minimax rates exposed the curse of dimensionality; vanishing gradients crippled deep networks; and classical learning theory revealed fundamental sample-complexity limits. Later breakthroughs (deep architectures, ReLU activations, dropout, large datasets, GPU compute, etc.) mitigated these barriers but did not eliminate them.

Conclusion: The AI winters were not merely episodes of irrational disillusionment but partly rational responses to genuine formal limitations of the era's paradigms. Understanding these mathematical bottlenecks provides a deeper, more nuanced historical narrative and may inform current research by highlighting which barriers have been merely mitigated versus truly resolved.

Abstract: Two major periods of reduced funding and confidence in artificial intelligence research, commonly called the first and second AI winters, are usually explained through engineering failure, commercial disappointment, and inflated expectations. This article develops a complementary thesis: that the dominant paradigms of those periods also met genuine formal barriers, including limitations of representation, optimisation, computational complexity, statistical learnability, and high-dimensional approximation. The contribution is synthetic rather than archival. We do not claim that particular theorems mechanically caused the winters; rather, we show that several central disappointments of early AI were aligned with mathematically precise bottlenecks. We analyse these bottlenecks through the perceptron impossibility results of Minsky and Papert, the complexity-theoretic hardness of exact neural-network training established by Blum and Rivest, minimax rates for nonparametric estimation in high dimension due to Stone, vanishing-gradient analyses by Hochreiter and by Bengio and collaborators, and classical statistical learning theory in the tradition of Vapnik and Chervonenkis, Valiant, and Blumer and collaborators. We then relate these barriers to the later breakthroughs that mitigated, rather than eliminated, them.

</details>


### [89] [Evaluation of AutoML Frameworks for IDS under Imbalanced Data Conditions of the NSL-KDD Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12611)
*Wiliane Carolina Silva,Evandro César Vilas Boas,Felipe A. P. de Figueiredo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Evaluates nine open-source AutoML frameworks on the original five-class NSL-KDD dataset with severe imbalance, finding that ensemble-based, imbalance-aware frameworks like PyCaret (66% macro-F1) outperform others; accuracy alone is insufficient for rare attack classes (R2L, U2R).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior AutoML studies for network intrusion detection simplify the problem via binary classification or minority-class removal, masking the real challenge of severe multiclass imbalance. A realistic, standardized evaluation of how AutoML frameworks handle highly underrepresented attacks (R2L, U2R) is needed.

Method: Nine open-source AutoML frameworks were tested under a unified, reproducible protocol on the original five-class NSL-KDD dataset. The analysis covered architectural design, ensemble strategies, validation procedures, hyperparameter optimization, and native imbalance-handling mechanisms, with macro-F1 as the primary metric.

Result: PyCaret achieved the best macro-F1 at 66%, followed by AutoGluon at 55%. Frameworks with ensemble learning and imbalance-aware optimization showed superior minority-class discrimination. Accuracy-oriented optimization proved insufficient, as high-weighted metrics coexisted with poor rare-attack generalization. Frameworks lacking native balancing support exhibited significant degradation on R2L and U2R detection.

Conclusion: Native integration of imbalance-aware optimization, resampling, and stratified evaluation into AutoML pipelines is essential for realistic multiclass intrusion detection. The work establishes a standardized benchmark and identifies key architectural gaps in current AutoML frameworks for severely imbalanced IDS scenarios.

Abstract: This work investigates the impact of severe class imbalance on the performance of automated machine learning (AutoML) frameworks for multiclass network intrusion detection using the NSL-KDD dataset. Unlike previous studies that simplify the problem through binary classification or minority-class removal, we preserve the original five-class distribution, including highly underrepresented attacks such as R2L and U2R, enabling a realistic evaluation of imbalance-sensitive learning behavior. Nine open-source AutoML frameworks were analyzed under a unified and reproducible experimental protocol, considering differences in architectural design, ensemble strategies, validation procedures, hyperparameter optimization, and imbalance-handling mechanisms. The results demonstrate that frameworks incorporating ensemble learning and imbalance-aware optimization achieve better minority-class discrimination. PyCaret obtained the best overall performance, reaching 66\% macro-F1, followed by AutoGluon with 55\%, whereas frameworks lacking native balancing support exhibited significant degradation in minority-class detection capability. The analysis further shows that accuracy-oriented optimization alone is insufficient for highly imbalanced IDS scenarios, since high-weighted metrics may coexist with poor generalization on rare attack categories. As a contribution, this work establishes a standardized benchmark for AutoML-based intrusion detection under severe multiclass imbalance, highlighting current architectural limitations and the need for native integration of imbalance-aware optimization, resampling, and stratified evaluation strategies into automated learning pipelines. The source code is publicly available.

</details>


### [90] [Fed-FBD: Federated Functional Block Diversification for Isolation, Privacy, and Surgical Unlearning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12679)
*Weijie Chen,Alan B. McMillan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Fed-FBD proposes a modular federated learning architecture that decomposes ResNet into independently tracked functional blocks, enabling block-level adversarial isolation, privacy-by-design against membership inference, and surgical machine unlearning — all with modest accuracy trade-offs compared to FedAvg.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard FedAvg treats each client as a black box, offering no mechanisms for isolating adversarial contributors, auditing per-client influence, or supporting the right to be forgotten. This motivates a modular architecture that provides these guarantees by design.

Method: Fed-FBD decomposes a ResNet backbone into six functional blocks (stem, four residual groups, classification head) and maintains a warehouse of N color variants. Each variant is assembled from independently tracked, contributor-stamped blocks. The architecture enforces block-level isolation structurally, provides membership inference privacy indistinguishable from random chance without additional mechanisms, and enables sub-second surgical unlearning of a participant's blocks without retraining.

Result: On six MedMNIST-2D datasets, PathMNIST (224×224), and CIFAR-10, Fed-FBD shows a 0.3%–3.1% IID accuracy gap on adequately sized datasets. It remains within 0.8%–4.0% of FedAvg at Dirichlet α=1.0 on three of four datasets. All six adversarial attacks are confined to the poisoned client's blocks with at most ±0.01 AUC drift on clean colors.

Conclusion: Fed-FBD successfully trades a modest accuracy gap for three critical guarantees absent in FedAvg: block-level adversarial isolation, privacy-by-design, and efficient surgical unlearning, making it a practical and robust alternative for privacy-sensitive federated learning deployments.

Abstract: Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training without sharing raw patient data, but standard approaches such as FedAvg treat each client as a black box and provide no mechanism for isolating an adversarial contributor, auditing per-client influence, or honoring a departed participant's right to be forgotten. We present Fed-FBD (Federated Functional Block Diversification), a modular federated architecture that decomposes a ResNet backbone into six functional blocks (the stem, four residual groups, and the classification head) and maintains a warehouse of N color variants, each assembled from independently tracked and contributor-stamped blocks. Fed-FBD provides three capabilities absent in FedAvg: (i) architecturally guaranteed block-level isolation, so that an adversarial or mislabelled client cannot contaminate the clean colous; (ii) privacy-by-design, where membership inference advantage is already indistinguishable from chance before any privacy mechanism is applied; and (iii) surgical machine unlearning of a departed participant's contribution at sub-second cost and without retraining. Experiments on six MedMNIST-2D datasets, PathMNIST at 224x224, and CIFAR-10 show that Fed-FBD trades a modest 0.3%-3.1% IID accuracy gap on the adequately sized datasets for these guarantees, remains within 0.8%-4.0% of FedAvg at Dirichlet alpha=1.0 on three of four datasets, and confines all six adversarial attacks we study to the poisoned client's own blocks with at most +/-0.01 AUC drift on the clean colors.

</details>


### [91] [Towards Provably Fair Machine Learning: Bayesian Approaches For Consistent and Transparent Predictions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12615)
*Owen O'Neill,Fintan Costello*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a Fair Bayesian classifier that enforces determinism and statistical consistency across all subgroups, abstaining when consistent predictions are impossible. On benchmark datasets, it achieves zero consistency error while exceeding baseline accuracy and multicalibration, providing a principled fairness framework grounded in frequentist inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ML classifiers in high-stakes domains produce predictions that vary systematically in quality across subgroups, especially granular intersections of features. Predictions often contradict observed evidence, and regularization exacerbates this by collapsing small subgroups—disproportionately harming demographic minorities. Addressing unreliable frequentist inference in small subgroups is framed as a necessary step toward fair ML.

Method: The authors define two requirements: (1) determinism—identical individuals receive identical predictions; and (2) statistical consistency—at significance level α, one cannot reject the hypothesis that subgroup predictions are drawn from the Bayesian optimal target distribution. From these, they derive the Fair Bayesian classifier, which enforces both requirements across all groups/subgroups simultaneously and abstains when no consistent deterministic prediction exists.

Result: On Adult, COMPAS, and Bank Marketing datasets, standard classifiers produce statistically inconsistent predictions for a substantial proportion of subgroups. The Fair Bayesian classifier achieves zero consistency error by construction and exceeds baseline accuracy and multicalibration on every dataset tested.

Conclusion: Statistical consistency provides a principled foundation for prediction quality with direct implications for algorithmic fairness. By enforcing Bayesian consistency at the finest data-supported resolution, the classifier demonstrates that exhaustive subgroup fairness with principled abstention is practically achievable.

Abstract: ML classifiers deployed in high-stakes domains produce predictions whose quality varies systematically across subgroups. For granular subgroups defined by intersections of multiple features, predictions are often inconsistent with the observed data: the model's outputs contradict the evidence available for that subgroup. This problem is exacerbated by regularisation, which improves aggregate performance by collapsing small subgroups into larger groups, disproportionately affecting demographic minorities. We define two requirements for consistent prediction: determinism (identical individuals receive identical predictions) and statistical consistency (we cannot reject, at significance level alpha, the hypothesis that the predictions for a subgroup were drawn from the Bayesian optimal target distribution inferred for that subgroup). From these requirements we derive the Fair Bayesian classifier, which enforces both across every group and subgroup simultaneously and abstains whenever no consistent deterministic prediction is possible. On three benchmark datasets (Adult, COMPAS, and Bank Marketing), standard classifiers produce statistically inconsistent predictions for a substantial proportion of subgroups. Our classifier achieves zero consistency error by construction while exceeding baseline accuracy and multicalibration on every dataset tested. Statistical consistency provides a principled foundation for prediction quality with direct implications for algorithmic fairness. Minority demographics are disproportionately concentrated in small subgroups, precisely where frequentist inference is least reliable; addressing this inference problem is therefore a necessary step toward fair ML. By enforcing Bayesian consistency at the finest resolution the data supports, the our classifier demonstrates that exhaustive subgroup fairness with principled abstention is achievable in practice.

</details>


### [92] [Bag of Dims: Training-Free Mechanistic Interpretability via Dimension-Level Sign Patterns](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12629)
*Varun Reddy Nalagatla*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Transformer hidden states, in their standard basis, inherently function as a "Bag of Dims" — individual dimensions act as independent binary registers encoding semantic features (via sign) and confidence (via magnitude), requiring no training or architectural modification to discover interpretable features across the entire compute pathway.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the prevailing assumption that interpretable features in transformer models require post-hoc training (e.g., sparse autoencoders, probing), and to demonstrate that the standard neuron basis already constitutes a viable, training-free feature representation that extends throughout the entire transformer (hidden states, attention projections, and FFN weights).

Method: The authors propose the "Bag of Dims" framework, treating each hidden state dimension as an independent binary register (sign = semantic direction, magnitude = confidence). They conduct four progressive experiments across Qwen 3.5-4B, Gemma 3-4B, and Mistral 7B: (1) replacing all magnitudes with unity and measuring next-token accuracy via the LM head; (2) Hamming distance scoring without any decoder; (3) single-token type caching to discover semantic categories via per-dimension sign consistency over 50 anchor tokens, validated by AUC against a trained probe; (4) extending discovery to K/V attention projections and linking features to individual FFN writer neurons via static weight inspection and neuron coalitions. Unsupervised scaling to 1500 features with pairwise MI is also evaluated.

Result: Sign patterns alone achieve 72–93% top-5 next-token accuracy; pure Hamming scoring reaches 80–90% top-4096. Single-token type caching discovers 175 categories with mean AUC 0.80 (trained probe adds only +0.018). All 175 categories remain discoverable in K and V projections. Static FFN weight inspection links ~20% of features to individual writer neurons (>0.70 agreement; random baseline: 0%), with top-200 neuron coalitions achieving >0.70 agreement on 99.9% of prototypes. Unsupervised discovery yields 1500 features at 100% yield and 99% sparsity across all models, with pairwise mutual information of 0.0014 bits confirming near-zero inter-dimension coupling.

Conclusion: The standard basis is already sufficient for interpretable feature extraction across the entire transformer compute pathway. No training, optimization, or substantial compute (beyond one forward pass per vocabulary token) is required, fundamentally reframing how we should approach mechanistic interpretability of language models.

Abstract: We show that the standard basis of transformer hidden states already provides a training-free, architecture-general feature basis. Individual dimensions encode semantic content via their signs and confidence via their magnitudes, functioning as independent binary registers. We validate this Bag of Dims framework across three model families (Qwen 3.5-4B, Gemma 3-4B, Mistral 7B) through four progressive experiments. Sign patterns alone carry predictive content: replacing all magnitudes with unity achieves 72-93% top-5 next-token accuracy through the LM head, and pure Hamming scoring without any decoder reaches 80-90% top-4096. These sign patterns organize into semantic features: using a single-token type cache (one forward pass per vocabulary token, no context), we discover 175 categories via per-dimension sign consistency (mean AUC 0.80) from 50 anchors with zero training. A trained probe adds only +0.018 AUC and converges to axis-aligned weights, confirming negligible cross-dimension structure. This structure extends to attention: all 175 categories remain discoverable in K and V projections. On the write side, static FFN weight inspection links 20% of features to individual writer neurons (>0.70 agreement; random controls: 0%), with top-200 neuron coalitions achieving >0.70 agreement on 99.9% of prototypes via majority vote. Fully unsupervised discovery (random seeds, no labels) scales to 1500 features at 100% yield and 99% sparsity across all three models, with pairwise MI of 0.0014 bits confirming low inter-dimension coupling. These results establish that the standard basis already suffices for feature reading throughout the transformer compute pathway, requiring no training, no optimization, and no GPU-days beyond a single forward pass per vocabulary token.

</details>


### [93] [Detecting Functional Memorization in Code Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12764)
*Matthieu Meeus,Anil Ramakrishna,Matthew Grange,Zheng Xu,Luca Melis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates "functional memorization" in LLMs — where models reproduce the functional logic of training code without textual overlap. Using a counterfactual setup with Olmo-3-32B, the authors show that a midtrained model exposed to target code generates functionally equivalent outputs compared to an unexposed reference model, demonstrating that verbatim-based auditing metrics are insufficient for detecting code memorization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work on training data memorization has focused on textual overlap between training examples and model outputs. However, code can be functionally equivalent while being textually dissimilar (e.g., different variable names, restructuring). This creates a blind spot in current auditing methods, which may fail to detect cases where models reproduce the functional logic of training data without verbatim copying. The authors aim to expose this gap.

Method: The authors employ a counterfactual design using Olmo-3-32B. A midtrained version of the model (exposed to target code during training) is compared against a pretrained reference model (not exposed to the same code). Both models are prompted with Python function signatures. Memorization is assessed along two axes: (1) textual similarity using traditional overlap metrics, and (2) functional similarity using LLM-as-a-judge evaluations and execution-based tests that check behavioral equivalence of generated code.

Result: The midtrained model shows clear evidence of functional memorization — it generates code that is functionally equivalent to the training examples even when textual similarity is low or absent. This confirms that models can internalize and reproduce the functional logic of training code in ways that verbatim-based detection methods miss.

Conclusion: Functional memorization is a real phenomenon that verbatim auditing metrics fail to capture. The findings underscore the need for auditing frameworks that incorporate functional and execution-based similarity measures to reliably assess training data leakage in code-generating LLMs.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to generate code at scale. Meanwhile, prior work has investigated whether training data may be recoverable from model outputs, by auditing the textual overlap between training examples and model generations. Code, however, can be functionally equivalent while textually dissimilar. In this work, we study functional memorization: extraction of functional logic beyond what verbatim metrics detect. We construct a counterfactual setup for Olmo-3-32B, comparing a midtrained model (exposed to target code) against a pretrained reference (not exposed). We prompt both models with Python function signatures and measure both textual and functional similarity (i.e., LLM-as-a-judge, execution-based). Our results show clear evidence of functional memorization, highlighting the need for auditing metrics that go beyond textual overlap.

</details>


### [94] [Keep Policy Gradient in Charge: Sibling-Guided Credit Distillation for Long-Horizon Tool-Use Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12634)
*Tianyu Ding,Jianhong Xin,Juan Pablo De la Cruz Weinstein*

Main category: cs.LG
