TL;DR: Sibling-Guided Credit Distillation (SGCD) improves long-horizon tool-use RL by using contrastive sibling rollouts and an external LLM to generate training-only credit references, reshaping token-level advantages without exposing the deployed agent to privileged information. Across two benchmarks, SGCD consistently outperforms matched GRPO baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon tool-use RL with outcome supervision suffers from sparse, trajectory-level credit signals broadcast across many tokens. While self-distillation could densify the signal, naive token-level self-distillation amplifies both useful skills and harmful shortcuts indiscriminately, since it lacks knowledge of which actions the verifier actually rewards. The authors aim to solve this credit assignment problem without leaking privileged information to the deployed policy.

Method: SGCD operates through four components: (1) dynamic sampling produces paired successful and failed sibling rollouts from the same policy; (2) an external LLM summarizes their contrast into stepwise credit references (training-only); (3) dense teacher/student divergence drives credit reassignment; and (4) bounded detached credit weights reshape GRPO token advantages. Crucially, the deployed student sees no external LLM, sibling evidence, or oracle.

Result: On AppWorld, SGCD improves TGC from 42.9→45.6 (test_normal) and 24.7→27.0 (test_challenge). On τ³-airline, pass@1 improves from 0.583→0.602, outperforming matched GRPO comparators.

Conclusion: Direct token-level self-distillation is harmful for tool-use RL because it cannot distinguish rewarded actions from shortcuts. SGCD resolves this by using distillation for credit assignment rather than as a competing actor loss, leveraging contrastive sibling rollouts and external LLM summaries exclusively during training to reshape advantage signals without exposing the deployed agent to privileged information.

Abstract: Long-horizon tool-use reinforcement learning can learn from outcome verification, but its
  trajectory-level advantage is broadcast across many reasoning, API, and answer tokens.
  Self-distillation promises a denser signal by reusing a policy's own rollouts or a privileged
  teacher. We show, however, that direct token-level self-distillation can silently destroy tool use:
  it rehearses teacher behavior without knowing which actions the verifier rewards, so useful skills
  and harmful shortcuts are amplified together. We introduce Sibling-Guided Credit Distillation
  (SGCD), which uses distillation for credit assignment rather than as a competing actor loss.
  Dynamic sampling produces mixed successful and failed sibling rollouts; an external LLM summarizes
  their contrast into a training-only stepwise credit reference; dense teacher/student divergence
  drives credit reassignment; and bounded detached credit weights reshape GRPO token advantages. The
  deployed student sees no external LLM, sibling evidence, or oracle. Across AppWorld and
  $τ^3$-airline, SGCD improves over matched GRPO comparators: AppWorld TGC $42.9 \to 45.6$ on
  test_normal and $24.7 \to 27.0$ on test_challenge, and $τ^3$-airline pass@1 $0.583 \to 0.602$.

</details>


### [95] [PolicyGuard: Towards Test-time and Step-level Adversary Defense for Reinforcement Learning Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12896)
*Junfeng Guo Heng Huang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PolicyGuard is a test-time, step-level backdoor defense for RL agents that uses Gaussian Process posterior variance and pseudo trajectories to detect malicious actions at individual time steps, achieving state-of-the-art AUROC across diverse attack types.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL agents are increasingly deployed in real-world applications but remain vulnerable to backdoor attacks. Existing defenses are limited: they require internal parameter access, operate only at coarse model/trajectory levels, or target only specific attack types. A more general, fine-grained, test-time defense is needed.

Method: PolicyGuard operates at test time without requiring access to the agent's internal parameters. It leverages Gaussian Process (GP) posterior variance to quantify uncertainty at each time step and introduces adapted pseudo trajectories to enable this step-level uncertainty computation. The defense flags actions as malicious when the GP posterior variance exceeds a detection threshold.

Result: Across seven RL games, PolicyGuard achieves state-of-the-art detection performance: average AUROC of 0.856 against perturbation-based attacks and 0.859 against adversary-agent attacks.

Conclusion: PolicyGuard provides an effective, general-purpose backdoor defense for RL that operates at fine temporal granularity (step-level) during test time, with theoretical grounding for the use of GP posterior variance as a detection signal.

Abstract: While real-world applications of reinforcement learning (RL) are becoming increasingly popular, the security of RL systems deserve more attention and exploration. In particular, recent work has revealed that RL agents are vulnerable to backdoor attacks, where a victim agent behaves normally under standard conditions but executes malicious actions when a specific trigger is activated. Existing backdoor defenses for RL either require access to the agent's internal parameters, operate only at the model or trajectory level, or are limited to specific attack types. To ensure the security of RL agents, we propose \texttt{PolicyGuard}, a \textit{test-time step-level} backdoor defense which leverages Gaussian Process (GP) posterior variance and adapts pseudo trajectories to enable uncertainty computation for individual time step. Besides, we also provide theoretical foundations to explain the efficacy of GP posterior variance. Extensive experiments across seven RL games demonstrate that PolicyGuard achieves state-of-the-art detection performance in most cases, with average AUROC of 0.856 for perturbation-based attacks and 0.859 for adversary-agent attacks.

</details>


### [96] [The Metric Picks the Winner: Evaluation Choice Flips Model Rankings for Drug-Response Prediction in Unseen Chemistry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12639)
*Dhruv Agarwal,Riya Bisht*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates drug response prediction on held-out compounds, revealing that metric choice completely inverts model rankings: simple baselines win under a variance proxy metric, while deep fusion models win under the contest's true active-set metric, demonstrating the first metric-calibration effect on real drug chemistry data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent benchmarks show complex models fail to beat trivial baselines when test compounds are held out by chemistry. The authors aim to understand this phenomenon and investigate whether the choice of evaluation metric fundamentally determines which model appears best — an effect previously shown on genetic perturbation data but never on real drug chemistry.

Method: A staged approach on THP-1 cells (DRUG-seq assay): (1) dumb baselines (untreated control, mean training-compound response); (2) non-parametric retrieval using Tanimoto-weighted nearest neighbors; (3) a fusion decoder combining frozen chemistry embeddings with retrieval-support features to predict residuals, with uncertainty quantification and gene programs. All evaluated under a Bemis-Murcko scaffold split on 14,026 training compounds, comparing two metrics: an inverse-variance per-gene proxy vs. the contest's active-set weighted MSE (wMSE).

Result: Metric choice inverts model rankings: under the inverse-variance proxy, regularized linear regression on Morgan fingerprints beats deep models, retrieval, and ChemBERTa. Under the true wMSE metric, the deep fusion decoder significantly outperforms the linear baseline (−0.012 wMSE, paired bootstrap p < 10⁻⁴), and the proxy's winner becomes the worst chemistry-aware predictor.

Conclusion: Picking the metric picks the winner. This is the first demonstration of the metric-calibration effect on real held-out drug chemistry. The authors release a reproducible pipeline wired to the official scorer for the VCPI contest.

Abstract: Predicting how a cell's transcriptome responds to a drug it has never seen is a core, hard problem in computational cell biology: recent benchmarks show complex models often fail to beat trivial baselines once test compounds are held out by chemistry. We study one cell line and assay, THP-1 cells profiled by DRUG-seq, scored by the active-compound weighted MSE(wMSE) of the VCPI prediction contest. We propose a staged approach: dumb baselines (untreated control and mean training-compound response) that the field keeps failing to beat; non-parametric retrieval (a Tanimoto-weighted average of a held-out compound's nearest training compounds); and a fusion stage combining a frozen chemistry embedding with retrieval-support features to predict the residual over the mean, with an uncertainty head and gene programs. On the released VCPI THP-1 drug-seq data (14,026 training compounds), under a Bemis-Murcko scaffold split, the model ranking inverts depending on the metric. Under an inverse-variance per-gene proxy, a regularized linear regression on Morgan fingerprints appears to win over the deep models, retrieval, and ChemBERTa -- the textbook "simple baselines win" result. But under the contest's true active-set metric (per-(gene, compound) Mejia weights, validated against the official scorer; mean baseline 0.535 vs the organizers' 0.507 reference), that reverses: the deep models win, our fusion decoder significantly beats the linear fingerprint baseline (-0.012 wMSE, paired bootstrap p < 10^-4), and the proxy's winner becomes the worst chemistry-aware predictor. Picking the metric picks the winner -- to our knowledge the first demonstration on real held-out drug chemistry of the metric-calibration effect established largely on genetic perturbation. We release a reproducible pipeline
  wired to the official scorer that emits a valid submission over the real 1064 x 12,995 grid.

</details>


### [97] [Individual Control Barrier Functions-Guided Diffusion Model for Safe Offline Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12640)
*Qingyun Guo,Junyi Shi,Jianuo Huang,Tianyu Shi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a safe offline multi-agent reinforcement learning algorithm that integrates neural individual control barrier functions into diffusion models to enhance safety during trajectory generation, with policies recovered via inverse dynamics. The method demonstrates significant safety improvements while maintaining competitive rewards across various benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Offline RL is well-suited for safety-critical tasks, and diffusion models have shown strong performance in single-agent settings due to their ability to model complex data distributions. However, safety challenges in multi-agent offline RL remain largely unexplored. The authors aim to fill this gap by addressing safe trajectory generation in multi-agent environments.

Method: The authors propose embedding neural individual control barrier functions (CBFs) into a diffusion model framework for offline multi-agent RL. The diffusion model generates safe trajectories by incorporating CBF-based safety constraints during the denoising process. Control policies are then recovered from these trajectories using inverse dynamics, enabling safe multi-agent decision-making without online interaction.

Result: The algorithm was evaluated across diverse benchmarks, demonstrating substantial safety improvements compared to baselines while maintaining competitive reward performance, validating the effectiveness of combining CBFs with diffusion models for safe offline MARL.

Conclusion: Embedding control barrier functions into diffusion models is an effective approach for achieving safety in offline multi-agent reinforcement learning, offering a promising direction for deploying MARL in safety-critical real-world applications.

Abstract: Offline reinforcement learning allows control policies to be learned directly from data without online interaction, making it suitable for safety-critical tasks. Recent studies have applied diffusion models to offline reinforcement learning to leverage their strong capacity for modeling complex data distributions. However, existing approaches primarily focus on single-agent settings, leaving the safety challenges in multi-agent environments largely unexplored. In this work, we propose a safe offline multi-agent reinforcement learning algorithm that embeds neural individual control barrier functions into the diffusion model to enhance safety during trajectory generation, with control policies recovered through inverse dynamics. We evaluate our algorithm across diverse benchmarks, demonstrating substantial safety improvements while maintaining competitive rewards.

</details>


### [98] [TEDD: Robust Detection of Unstable Temporal Features](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12643)
*Ricardo Ribeiro Pereira,Bruno Casal Laraña,Nádia Soares,Miguel Araújo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes TEDD, a technique that uses a regression model to predict instance timestamps, thereby identifying features whose distributions change over time (temporal drift). TEDD detects all basic change patterns, handles numerical and categorical features, identifies multivariate drifts, quantifies change magnitude per feature, requires no parameter tuning, and scales well with dataset size.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world temporal data often exhibits evolving feature distributions (concept drift). Naively applying ML models to such non-stationary data causes rapid performance degradation when deployment distributions diverge from training distributions. Practitioners need automated methods to detect which specific features are changing over time, enabling targeted mitigation strategies (e.g., data transformations) to maintain model robustness.

Method: TEDD (Temporal Drift Detection) leverages a regression model trained to predict an instance's timestamp from its features. Features that strongly contribute to accurate timestamp prediction are flagged as temporally drifting. The method quantifies the contribution of each feature to the timestamp prediction, producing a comparable drift magnitude score. The approach is evaluated against other methods on both synthetic data (with controlled change patterns) and real-world data.

Result: TEDD successfully detects all basic types of temporal changes (concept drift patterns) in both numerical and categorical features. It can identify multivariate drifts where multiple features change jointly. The method produces a comparable value measuring the amount of change for each feature. It requires no hyperparameter tuning, and demonstrates scalability with respect to both the number of features and the number of instances in the dataset.

Conclusion: TEDD provides an effective, parameter-free, and scalable solution for detecting temporally changing features in datasets. By automatically identifying the sources of instability, the method enables practitioners to apply targeted interventions (e.g., transformations, feature removal) and deploy more robust ML models that sustain performance over time.

Abstract: When working with real-world temporal data, it is common to encounter features whose distribution is changing over time. The naive employment of Machine Learning models on this unstable data might lead to rapidly degrading performance, especially if the new distribution is much different from what was previously seen during training. In order to cope with this problem, it is critical to automatically identify features that are changing over time. With these features detected, data scientists and other practitioners will be able to mitigate the issue (for instance, by applying data transformations), deploying more robust models that retain high performance for longer periods of time. In this paper, we describe which temporal changes a feature should not suffer from, and propose TEDD, a technique to a) identify when a dataset might lead to an unstable Machine Learning model and b) automatically detect which features cause such lack of robustness. In order to achieve it, we leverage a regression model to highlight which features contribute to a good prediction of an instance's timestamp. We compare our approach to other methods in real and synthetic data, testing their detection capability on all simple change patterns. We show that our method: detects all types of basic changes, both for numerical and categorical features; can detect multivariate drifts; returns a comparable value measuring the amount of change of each feature; requires no parameter tuning; and is scalable both on number of features and instances of the dataset.

</details>


### [99] [Physics-Aware Auxiliary Losses Improve Out-of-Distribution Generalization of a GNN Synthesizability Filter](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12651)
*Riya Bisht,Dhruv Agarwal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Adding cheap physical priors (Bertz topological complexity and MMFF94 strain energy) as auxiliary supervisory signals to a GNN-based synthesizability filter yields small but statistically significant OOD generalization gains over a purely data-driven baseline, but the effect is only visible under multi-seed evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative molecular design models propose molecules far outside the training distribution, causing existing purely statistical synthesizability filters (SAScore, SCScore, RAscore, DeepSA) to degrade. The authors investigate whether incorporating closed-form physical priors as auxiliary supervision can improve out-of-distribution (OOD) robustness of such filters.

Method: A GINE (Graph Isomorphism Network with Edge features) backbone is trained for binary synthesizability classification on a 65,177-molecule corpus (HIV + Tox21 + COCONUT) using SAScore threshold labels. Two auxiliary losses are added: (1) topological complexity regression supervised by the Bertz index, and (2) a strain-energy soft penalty supervised by MMFF94 force-field energy. A 4-way ablation (baseline, +complexity, +strain, +both) is evaluated on a single-source OOD split (train: drug-like HIV+Tox21; test: COCONUT natural products) over 5 random seeds with paired bootstrap confidence intervals.

Result: All three physics-aware variants produce statistically significant OOD improvement over the baseline (mean OOD AUC 0.9774): +complexity Δ = +0.0060 (95% CI [+0.0023, +0.0102]), +strain Δ = +0.0032 ([+0.0008, +0.0052]), +both Δ = +0.0066 ([+0.0038, +0.0093]). The combination performs best. Variants are indistinguishable in-distribution, so the effect only appears under OOD evaluation. A single-seed version of the experiment gave a qualitatively different, non-monotone result that did not survive multi-seed evaluation.

Conclusion: Cheap physical priors provide a small but reliable OOD generalization benefit for synthesizability prediction, with the combination of topological complexity and strain energy giving the best result. The authors emphasize effect modesty and caution that single-seed experiments can produce misleading conclusions, underscoring the need for multi-seed evaluation with confidence intervals.

Abstract: Machine-learning drug-discovery pipelines increasingly rely on generative models that propose molecules far from the data used to train downstream synthesizability filters. Existing filters (SAScore, SCScore, RAscore, DeepSA) are purely statistical and degrade in exactly this out-of-distribution (OOD) regime. We ask whether cheap, closed-form physical priors, used as auxiliary supervision on a graph neural network (GNN), improve OOD generalization. We add two auxiliary losses to a GINE backbone: a topological complexity regression supervised by the Bertz index, and a strain-energy soft penalty supervised by MMFF94 force-field energy. On a 65,177-molecule corpus (HIV, Tox21, COCONUT) labeled by SAScore thresholds we reproduce a strong in-distribution baseline, then evaluate a 4-way ablation (baseline / +complexity / +strain / +both) on a single-source OOD split (train on drug-like HIV+Tox21, test on COCONUT natural products), repeated over 5 seeds with paired bootstrap confidence intervals. All three physics-aware variants give a small but statistically significant OOD improvement over the baseline (mean OOD AUC 0.9774): +complexity Delta = +0.0060 (95% CI [+0.0023, +0.0102]), +strain Delta = +0.0032 ([+0.0008, +0.0052]), +both Delta = +0.0066 ([+0.0038, +0.0093]); every interval excludes zero, and the combination is best. The variants are indistinguishable in-distribution, so the effect is visible only under OOD evaluation. We are explicit that the effects are modest, and we report a cautionary methodological finding: a single-seed version of this experiment produced a qualitatively different (non-monotone) story that did not survive multi-seed evaluation.

</details>


### [100] [Physics-Informed Neural Networks for Chemotherapy Pharmacokinetics: Benchmarking the Clinical Estimator and Exposing Parameter Identifiability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12658)
*Riya Bisht,Dhruv Agarwal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PINNs are benchmarked against standard clinical PK estimators and data-only neural networks on two pharmacokinetic problems. On the linear two-compartment model, PINNs match the near-optimal NLS baseline and recover tissue concentration. On a Michaelis-Menten extension with saturable elimination, NLS fails due to model misspecification, while PINNs expose non-identifiability from plasma-only data and largely resolve it with sparse tissue observations—demonstrating PINNs as a unified framework for identifiability analysis and heterogeneous data integration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In chemotherapy pharmacokinetics, plasma drug concentration is routinely measured but tissue concentration—which drives tumour kill and toxicity—is not. Standard clinical methods rely on analytical biexponential solutions that break down under nonlinear (saturable) elimination kinetics, and data-only neural networks fail to recover unobserved compartments. A unified, physics-informed approach is needed that handles both linear and nonlinear regimes, exposes structural identifiability issues, and absorbs heterogeneous measurements.

Method: The authors benchmark three approaches on two PK problems: (1) nonlinear least-squares (NLS) fitting the analytical biexponential plasma solution (clinical baseline), (2) a data-only MLP (physics-agnostic neural baseline), and (3) a Physics-Informed Neural Network (PINN) encoding the governing two-compartment ODEs. The first problem is a linear two-compartment model; the second extends it with Michaelis-Menten saturable elimination, where no biexponential closed form exists. Identifiability is probed by adding sparse tissue observations to the PINN loss.

Result: On the linear two-compartment problem, the PINN matches NLS within a small constant factor and produces the tissue curve in a single training pass, while the data-only MLP fails on tissue by ~10×. On the Michaelis-Menten extension, NLS is mis-specified and returns meaningless rate constants. The PINN reveals the model is non-identifiable from plasma alone (converges to a basin with k12 → 0). Adding two sparse tissue observations largely resolves identifiability: k21 recovers to within 1%, Vmax and Km to within one SD, and k12 moves from 0.02 toward 0.82 (truth) but remains ~2σ below—a recovery impossible for the closed-form NLS estimator.

Conclusion: PINNs do not outperform NLS on textbook linear problems but provide a uniform recipe that matches the textbook estimator where applicable, exposes structural identifiability that closed-form methods conceal, and absorbs heterogeneous measurements within a single loss—making them a principled tool for PK problems where analytical solutions are unavailable and partial observations are the norm.

Abstract: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) are an attractive tool for partial-observation problems in biology, where the governing dynamics are known but some compartments cannot be measured. Chemotherapy pharmacokinetics (PK) is a clean instance: drug concentration in plasma is routinely measured, but concentration in tissue -- which determines tumour kill and off-target toxicity -- is not. We benchmark a PINN against the standard clinical baseline (nonlinear least-squares on the analytical biexponential plasma solution, hereafter NLS) and a physics-agnostic neural baseline (a data-only MLP) on two PK problems. On the linear two-compartment problem, NLS is near-optimal; the PINN matches it to within a small constant factor while also producing the tissue curve in a single training pass, whereas the data-only MLP fails on tissue by roughly 10x. On a Michaelis-Menten extension (saturable elimination), the biexponential closed form no longer exists, so NLS is mis-specified and silently returns meaningless rate constants. The PINN instead exposes a deeper fact: the Michaelis-Menten two-compartment model is non-identifiable from plasma alone, and the PINN reports this honestly by converging to a basin with k12 -> 0. Adding two sparse tissue observations largely resolves identifiability: across five seeds the PINN recovers k21 to within 1% of truth and Vmax, Km to within one standard-deviation bar, while k12 moves in the correct direction (0.02 -> 0.82) but remains ~2 sigma below truth -- a recovery the closed-form NLS estimator cannot attempt at all, because its biexponential ansatz describes only plasma. Our claim is not that PINNs beat NLS. It is that PINNs offer a uniform recipe that ties the textbook estimator on the textbook problem, exposes structural identifiability that
  the textbook estimator hides, and absorbs heterogeneous measurements within a single loss.

</details>


### [101] [A Zero-shot Generalized Graph Anomaly Detection Framework via Node Reconstruction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12673)
*Phan Nguyen,Dat Cao,Hien Chu,Khue Hoang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AlignGAD is a zero-shot graph anomaly detection framework that aligns heterogeneous node features via spectral normalization, constructs cluster-aware views to capture group-level anomalies, and scores nodes by reconstruction discrepancy across multiple views, achieving strong cross-domain generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing graph anomaly detection methods rely on dataset-specific feature semantics and structural patterns, severely limiting cross-domain generalization. There is a need for a framework that can transfer to unseen target graphs without retraining.

Method: AlignGAD comprises three modules: (1) Global Unification Module — aligns heterogeneous node features and normalizes graph signals in the spectral domain; (2) Clustering Module — constructs cluster-aware graph views to capture group-level abnormal patterns; (3) Node Discrepancy Scoring Module — measures reconstruction discrepancy and aggregates anomaly evidence from different graph views for final scoring.

Result: Experiments on multiple real-world datasets demonstrate AlignGAD's effectiveness under the zero-shot GAD setting, outperforming methods that depend on domain-specific features and structures.

Conclusion: AlignGAD provides a generalizable zero-shot solution for cross-domain graph anomaly detection by unifying heterogeneous signals, leveraging cluster-aware views, and aggregating multi-view reconstruction discrepancies.

Abstract: Cross-domain graph anomaly detection (GAD) aims to identify abnormal nodes in unseen target graphs, showing strong potential in real-world applications with heterogeneous graph data. However, existing methods often depend on dataset-specific feature semantics and structural patterns, which limits their ability to generalize across different domains. To address this challenge, we propose AlignGAD, a zero-shot generalized graph anomaly detection framework. Our framework is built upon three key components: a Global Unification Module that aligns heterogeneous node features and normalizes graph signals in the spectral domain; a Clustering Module that constructs cluster-aware graph views to capture group-level abnormal patterns; and a Node Discrepancy Scoring Module that measures reconstruction discrepancy and aggregates anomaly evidence from different graph views. Experiments on multiple real-world datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of AlignGAD under the zero-shot GAD setting.

</details>


### [102] [How Useful is Causal Invariance for Domain Adaptation in Finite-Sample Settings?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12680)
*Julia Kostin,Kasra Jalaldoust,Elias Bareinboim,Samory Kpotufe,Fanny Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper theoretically analyzes when causal knowledge (full or partial) provably improves supervised domain adaptation (sDA) in finite-sample settings. Focusing on linear regression, the authors derive matching upper and lower bounds showing that gains depend on target-risk margins between candidate invariant predictors and finite-source estimation error. An adaptive aggregation procedure matches the best candidate when margins are sufficiently large, avoiding negative transfer; when margins are too small, no algorithm can exploit the candidates for faster rates. Results are linked to structural shift magnitude in linear SCMs and validated on real-world benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work on causality-based domain generalization established that shared causal structure yields invariant predictors with stable risk across domain shifts. However, whether these population-level invariances translate to finite-sample gains — particularly in supervised domain adaptation where a few labeled target samples are available — remains underexplored. The authors aim to formally characterize when and how causal knowledge provably helps in sDA.

Method: The authors study linear regression under supervised domain adaptation, where causal knowledge specifies a collection of invariant or possibly-invariant feature subsets, each yielding a source-trained candidate predictor. They derive matching upper and lower bounds on the target risk, revealing that finite-sample gains are governed by (1) target-risk margins separating candidates and (2) finite-source estimation error. They propose an adaptive aggregation procedure that, when margins are large relative to target sample size n_Q, matches the best candidate while avoiding negative transfer. Conversely, small margins make reliable candidate exploitation impossible. The bounds are connected to structural shift magnitude in linear Structural Causal Models (SCMs), and experiments on real-world causal benchmarks validate the theory.

Result: The paper establishes matching upper and lower bounds demonstrating that finite-sample gains in supervised domain adaptation are determined by target-risk margins among candidate predictors and finite-source error. When margins are sufficiently large, adaptive aggregation achieves the best candidate's performance without negative transfer. When margins are too small, no algorithm can reliably leverage the candidate set for faster rates. These margins are further connected to the magnitude of structural shifts in linear SCMs, and empirical results on real-world causal datasets confirm the theoretical findings.

Conclusion: Causal invariances can provably improve supervised domain adaptation in finite samples, but only when the target-risk margins separating candidate invariant predictors are large enough relative to estimation error. This provides a rigorous characterization of when causality-based domain generalization methods yield practical benefits, bridging the gap between population-level invariance guarantees and finite-sample performance.

Abstract: Machine learning models often degrade when they are deployed on a target distribution that differs from the source distributions they were trained on. Recent work in causality-based domain generalization has shown how shared causal structure between domains can induce invariant predictors, e.g., models on a subset of features which have stable risk across structured domain shifts. However, the extent to which such population-level causal invariances can lead to gains in finite-sample settings remains underexplored. In particular, in practice we often have access to a few labeled target samples, a setting called supervised domain adaptation (sDA). In this paper, we explore when (full or partial) causal knowledge can provably improve supervised domain adaptation.
  As a first step, we study linear regression, where full or partial causal knowledge specifies a collection of invariant or possibly invariant feature subsets, each yielding a source-trained candidate predictor. We derive matching upper and lower bounds showing that finite-sample gains are governed by the target-risk margins separating the candidates, together with the finite-source estimation error. When these margins are sufficiently large relative to $n_Q$, an adaptive aggregation procedure can match the best candidate predictor while avoiding negative transfer relative to target-only learning. On the other hand, when the margins are too small, no algorithm can reliably exploit the candidate collection to obtain faster finite-sample rates. We further connect these margins to structural shift magnitude in linear SCMs and validate the theory on real-world causal benchmarks.

</details>


### [103] [Forecasting Is Not Attribution: Localizing Decoder Bypass in Graph-Based Neural Marketing Mix Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12687)
*Yunbo Wang,Bolbi Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Marketing mix models face a "attribution bypass" failure where high-forecasting-accuracy neural decoders fail to route counterfactual sensitivity through the graph used for attribution. The authors propose DICE-MMM, a diagnostic and training framework that separates graph recovery, forecasting accuracy, and graph-aligned attribution, showing that low forecasting error does not certify correct attribution and that graph-support selection is the key bottleneck.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In graph-based neural marketing mix models (MMM), forecasting and attribution are often conflated but are not equivalent goals. The authors identify a failure mode called "attribution bypass," where high-capacity decoders achieve low forecasting error via mechanisms like target autoregression, dense communication, co-movement, context, or latent memory, without actually routing counterfactual sensitivity through the graph used as the attribution object. This means good forecasting accuracy can hide poor attribution quality.

Method: DICE-MMM is a two-stage bounded diagnostic and training framework. Stage 1 trains a graph encoder with a restricted graph-mediated decoder. Stage 2 freezes the selected encoder and trains a graph-safe latent decoder where cross-node communication must pass through the supplied graph. Evaluation uses three tests: CIG (Causal Influence Graph), AR-CIG (Autoregressive CIG), and graph-swap tests. The framework is validated through controlled R/d/T swaps and an external multi-graph rawlog stress test, comparing against CausalMMM and benchmarking no-graph, full-graph, learned-graph, and oracle-graph decoders.

Result: DICE improves stable graph recovery over CausalMMM. In a sparse-target benchmark, no-graph and full-graph decoders achieve MSE@7 around 0.004 while AR-CIG nAUPRC remains near or below zero, whereas an oracle graph reaches 0.807 ± 0.129 at comparable MSE. Under frozen graph-swap, the same DICE-hard-trained decoder moves from nAUPRC −0.044 ± 0.006 with learned graph inputs to 0.894 ± 0.027 with the oracle graph, localizing the bottleneck to graph-support selection rather than forecasting or decoder capacity.

Conclusion: Low MSE can hide attribution bypass, and forecasting accuracy is not a valid certificate for attribution quality. The unresolved bottleneck in graph-based neural MMM is graph-support selection, not forecasting performance or decoder capacity. DICE-MMM provides a principled framework to stress-test and localize this failure.

Abstract: Marketing mix models are used to forecast business outcomes and to attribute those outcomes to marketing channels, but these goals are not equivalent. We study a failure mode in graph-based neural MMM called attribution bypass: a high-capacity decoder can obtain low forecasting error through target autoregression, dense communication, co-movement, context, or latent memory while failing to route counterfactual sensitivity through the graph used as the attribution object. We introduce DICE-MMM as a bounded diagnostic and training framework. We do not claim that observational neural MMM identifies causal effects. Instead, DICE separates three questions often conflated in graph-based MMM: graph recovery, forecasting accuracy, and whether the trained decoder's perturbation-induced influence is graph aligned. Stage 1 trains a graph encoder with a restricted graph-mediated decoder. Stage 2 freezes the selected encoder and trains a graph-safe latent decoder whose cross-node communication must pass through the supplied graph. Decoder use is evaluated with CIG, AR-CIG, and graph-swap tests. Across controlled R/d/T swaps and an external multi-graph rawlog stress test, DICE improves stable graph recovery over CausalMMM. The experiments show that forecasting accuracy is not an attribution certificate: in a sparse-target benchmark, no-graph and full-graph decoders achieve MSE@7 around 0.004 while AR-CIG nAUPRC remains near or below zero, whereas an oracle graph reaches 0.807 +/- 0.129 at comparable MSE. Frozen graph-swap localizes the bottleneck: the same DICE-hard-trained decoder moves from nAUPRC -0.044 +/- 0.006 under learned graph inputs to 0.894 +/- 0.027 with the oracle graph. The contribution is a stress test and failure-localization framework showing that low MSE can hide attribution bypass and that the unresolved bottleneck is graph-support selection, not forecasting or decoder capacity.

</details>


### [104] [M*: A Modular, Extensible, Serving System for Multimodal Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12688)
*Atindra Jha,Naomi Sagan,Keisuke Kamahori,Irmak Sivgin,Rohan Sanda,Steven Gao,Mark Horowitz,Luke Zettlemoyer,Olivia Hsu,Jure Leskovec,Baris Kasikci,Stephanie Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: M* is a universal model serving system for composite AI models that uses a Walk Graph abstraction to represent models as dataflow graphs, enabling efficient serving across diverse modalities. It achieves 20% lower latency than vLLM-Omni on text-to-image, up to 2.9x lower real-time factor on text-to-speech, and up to 12.5x speedup on robotic planning compared to existing baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing model serving frameworks are built on narrow assumptions about model structure (e.g., homogeneous transformer stacks) and cannot efficiently handle the emerging class of composite AI models that integrate heterogeneous components — vision encoders, language backbones, diffusion heads, audio codecs, action generators, world-model predictors, etc. A universal serving system is needed to accommodate this architectural diversity with minimal developer effort.

Method: The core contribution is the **Walk Graph** abstraction: models are represented as dataflow graphs, and processing requests across diverse modalities and tasks are treated as traversals (walks) over these graphs. This modular abstraction supports: (1) arbitrary composition of model components, (2) flexible placement of components onto physical cluster nodes, and (3) model-agnostic optimizations within a distributed runtime. The system is instantiated as M*, a universal serving framework.

Result: On text-to-image workloads (BAGEL), M* achieves ~20% lower end-to-end latency vs. vLLM-Omni. On text-to-speech workloads (Qwen3-Omni), M* delivers up to 2.9× lower real-time factor and 2.7× higher throughput. On robotic planning, M* outperforms the V-JEPA 2-AC rollout baseline by up to 12.5×.

Conclusion: M* demonstrates that a unified, graph-based serving abstraction can efficiently handle the growing diversity of composite model architectures across modalities, paving the way toward more efficient serving of complex models with minimal developer effort.

Abstract: We are entering a new era of composite model architectures that integrate diverse components such as vision encoders, language backbones, diffusion and flow heads, audio codecs, action generators, and world-model predictors. Such architectures underpin a broad class of multimodal models, including unified multimodal models, omni models, speech-language models, vision-language-action policies, and world models. However, existing model serving frameworks were built on narrow assumptions about model structure, making them ill-suited to accommodate this new architectural diversity. Here we present M*, a universal serving system for efficient serving of composite AI models. M* represents models as dataflow graphs, processing requests spanning diverse modalities and tasks as traversals over these graphs. The core insight is a modular abstraction that supports arbitrary composition of model components, flexible placement onto a physical cluster, and model-agnostic optimizations within a distributed runtime. We call this abstraction the Walk Graph and show how it can concisely capture composite models from a broad range of families. We instantiate M* on representative models and find that it achieves, on average, 20% lower end-to-end latency than vLLM-Omni for text-to-image workloads on BAGEL, while delivering up to 2.9x lower real-time factor and 2.7x higher throughput for text-to-speech workloads on Qwen3-Omni. M* also outperforms the V-JEPA 2-AC rollout baseline for robotic planning by up to 12.5x. Thus, our work paves the road towards more efficient serving of complex models with minimal developer effort.

</details>


### [105] [Two-Layer Linear Auto-Regressive Models Estimate Latent States](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12691)
*Yahya Sattar,Sunmook Choi,Leo Maynard-Zhang,Yassir Jedra,Maryam Fazel,Sarah Dean*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Two-layer linear auto-regressive models trained via empirical risk minimization on partially observed linear dynamical systems provably learn to approximate Kalman filtering — the learned hidden state matches the optimal Kalman filter estimates up to a similarity transformation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite the empirical success of auto-regressive models on sequential data, the theoretical understanding of how and why they learn meaningful latent representations remains an open question. This work seeks to formally characterize the representations learned by auto-regressive models when applied to dynamical systems.

Method: The authors analyze a two-layer linear auto-regressive model trained by empirical risk minimization on data generated from partially observed linear dynamical systems. The analysis proceeds in three steps: (1) bounding the truncation error when approximating the Kalman filter by a finite-order auto-regressive model; (2) characterizing the optimization landscape, proving it is benign despite non-convexity (all stationary points are strict saddles or global minima); (3) deriving finite-sample guarantees for prediction error, parameter estimation error, and latent state recovery up to a similarity transformation.

Result: The learned hidden representation coincides with Kalman filter state estimates up to a similarity transformation. Finite-sample bounds are provided for prediction error, parameter recovery, and state estimation. Numerical simulations corroborate the theoretical findings.

Conclusion: Two-layer linear auto-regressive models implicitly learn to perform optimal Bayesian filtering (Kalman filtering) from data alone, without explicit knowledge of the underlying dynamics. This provides a theoretical foundation for understanding representation learning in auto-regressive architectures.

Abstract: Auto-regressive models have emerged as powerful tools for sequential data, from language to video. Understanding how and why these models learn latent representations remains an open theoretical question. In this work, we demonstrate that when trained by empirical risk minimization on data from partially observed linear dynamical systems, two-layer linear auto-regressive models naturally learn to approximate Kalman filtering. In particular, we show that the learned hidden representation coincides, up to a similarity transformation, with the state estimates produced by the optimal (Kalman) filter, even though the model has no explicit knowledge of the underlying dynamics or state. The result follows from three main insights. First, we establish that the Kalman filter is well approximated by an auto-regressive model with bounded truncation error. Second, we show that despite non-convexity, the two-layer optimization landscape is benign, i.e., all stationary points are either strict saddles or global minima. Finally, as our main contributions, we provide finite-sample guarantees on prediction error, parameter estimation error, and latent state recovery. Numerical simulations support the theoretical results and demonstrate that the latent representations of auto-regressive models recover state estimates.

</details>


### [106] [LLM-Powered Personalized Glycemic Assessment in Type 2 Diabetes with Wearable Sensor Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12699)
*Yifan Gao,Yanmin Gong,Yun Shi,Yuanxiong Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GlyLLM is an LLM-powered framework that integrates CGM data, wearable sensor data, and structured metadata for personalized glycemic assessment in Type 2 Diabetes, outperforming traditional ML methods in glucose forecasting (13.66% RMSE reduction) and diabetes categorization (13.08% AUROC improvement).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional ML methods for glycemic assessment rely primarily on historical blood glucose measurements and ignore personalized individual-level context (e.g., metadata, surveys, biometrics), limiting their generalizability across diverse diabetes populations. LLMs offer an opportunity to integrate multimodal data with sequential modeling while leveraging pre-trained knowledge.

Method: The authors propose GlyLLM, an LLM-based framework that combines CGM data, wearable sensor data, and structured metadata (including diabetes surveys and biometric tests) through sensor-text semantic abstraction. The model leverages pre-trained LLM knowledge to model glycemic dynamics at decision time. Evaluation is performed on the AI-READI dataset across two tasks: glucose forecasting and diabetes categorization, with ablation studies to assess feature importance.

Result: GlyLLM outperforms traditional ML baselines by an average of 13.66% in RMSE for glucose forecasting and 13.08% in AUROC for diabetes categorization. Ablation studies reveal that diabetes surveys and biometric tests contribute most significantly to glycemic assessment performance compared to other health information.

Conclusion: GlyLLM represents a promising step toward harnessing LLMs for personalized glycemic assessment in T2D care, demonstrating the value of integrating multimodal wearable data with structured individual-level context through large language models.

Abstract: Type 2 Diabetes (T2D) poses an increasing global health threat, demanding effective glycemic assessment to support personalized and improved diabetes care. Wearable sensors such as continuous glucose monitors (CGM) and fitness trackers offer many valuable insights for glycemic assessment. However, effectively analyzing these data requires integration with essential individual-level context. Existing methods are often based on traditional machine learning (ML) and rely primarily on historical blood glucose measurements and overlook personalized information, which limits their performance across diverse diabetes populations. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated their ability to integrate diverse data modalities while modeling sequential dependencies, motivating the exploration of their potential for personalized glycemic assessment.
  In this paper, we propose GlyLLM, an LLM-powered framework for modeling CGM-based glycemic dynamics through the integration of wearable sensor data and structured metadata. GlyLLM can leverage the extensive prior knowledge of pre-trained LLMs and achieve sensor-text semantic abstraction at decision time. Experiments on two related tasks on the AI-READI dataset demonstrate that our model outperforms traditional ML methods by an average of 13.66\% in Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) for glucose forecasting and 13.08\% in Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUROC) for diabetes categorization. Additionally, our ablation study shows that diabetes surveys and biometric tests are more critical than other health information for glycemic assessment. Our work presents a promising step toward harnessing the power of LLMs to advance personalized glycemic assessment in T2D care.

</details>


### [107] [A Stabilized Path-Space Approach to Diffusion-Based Posterior Sampling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12710)
*Evan Scope Crafts,Umberto Villa,Saviz Mowlavi,Yanting Ma,Hassan Mansour,Wael H. Ali*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a stabilized path-space framework for diffusion-based posterior sampling in Bayesian inverse problems, using stochastic optimal control to learn a controlled diffusion process that matches a likelihood-weighted target measure on trajectories. A time reparameterization removes bias without auxiliary training, and a trust-region optimization method with log-variance objectives learns the control. The framework unifies existing guidance-based samplers, quantifies sampling error, and enables importance sampling corrections, demonstrating improved accuracy and uncertainty quantification on benchmark inverse problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion posterior samplers rely on heuristic guidance approximations that can fail for nonlinear operators and multimodal posteriors, motivating the need for a principled path-space formulation grounded in stochastic optimal control that preserves Bayesian structure for reliable uncertainty quantification.

Method: A path-space framework is developed where a base diffusion process with prior terminal marginal defines a likelihood-weighted target measure on trajectories. Posterior sampling is recast as learning a controlled stochastic process matching this target. A time reparameterization removes bias from the unknown initial value function. Control is learned via trust-region path-space optimization with log-variance objectives. The framework also yields importance sampling corrections for asymptotically exact posterior expectations.

Result: The method is evaluated on benchmark inverse problems with analytically characterized or high-quality reference posteriors, demonstrating improved sampling accuracy, robustness, and uncertainty quantification compared to leading diffusion posterior sampling approaches.

Conclusion: The stabilized path-space framework provides a principled, optimization-based alternative to heuristic guidance for diffusion posterior sampling, unifying existing methods, quantifying approximation error, and enabling asymptotically exact inference through importance sampling. Results show improved accuracy and robustness, advancing the reliability of diffusion models for Bayesian inverse problems.

Abstract: Diffusion models provide expressive data-driven priors for Bayesian inverse problems, but many diffusion posterior samplers rely on heuristic guidance approximations that can fail for nonlinear operators and multimodal posteriors. In this work, we develop a stabilized path-space framework for diffusion-based posterior sampling. Starting from a base diffusion process whose terminal marginal represents the prior, we define a likelihood-weighted target measure on trajectories and cast posterior sampling as learning a controlled stochastic process whose path measure matches this target. This formulation connects diffusion posterior sampling to stochastic optimal control while preserving the Bayesian structure needed for uncertainty quantification. We introduce a time reparameterization that makes the path-space control problem well posed by removing the bias induced by the unknown initial value function, without auxiliary training. We then learn the control via a trust-region path-space optimization method with log-variance objectives. The path-space perspective also unifies our learned control approach with existing guidance-based samplers, quantifies the sampling error induced by approximate controls, and yields importance sampling corrections for asymptotically exact posterior expectations. We evaluate the proposed framework on a suite of benchmark inverse problems with analytically characterized or high-quality reference posteriors, enabling principled assessment of sampling accuracy and uncertainty quantification. These experiments provide insight into the behavior of diffusion-based posterior samplers and demonstrate improved accuracy and robustness over leading approaches.

</details>


### [108] [Out-of-Distribution (OOD) Detectors for Open-Set RF Fingerprinting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12718)
*Sudeepta Mondal,Ganesh Sundaramoorthi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper adapts out-of-distribution (OOD) detection methods from machine learning to open-set radio-frequency fingerprinting (RFF), presenting a unified information-theoretic framework. Crucially, it demonstrates that OOD detectors can be tuned without requiring auxiliary OOD data — a key practical constraint in RF environments — achieving performance on par with methods that use true OOD tuning data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RF fingerprinting systems face distribution shift at test time due to unknown transmitters and temporal drift. While OOD detection is a natural fit, most existing OOD detectors require auxiliary OOD data for parameter tuning. In realistic RF environments, collecting representative OOD data is impractical, creating a significant barrier to adoption. This work aims to bridge that gap.

Method: The authors (1) adapt a set of promising OOD detection methods from ML to the open-set RFF domain; (2) unify these methods under an information-theoretic mathematical framework, which aligns naturally with communication systems; (3) leverage recent techniques for tuning OOD detectors without requiring OOD tuning data; and (4) evaluate on the POWDER RF fingerprinting dataset, comparing detectors tuned with and without access to true OOD data.

Result: Detectors tuned without any given OOD data achieve performance comparable to baselines that have access to true OOD tuning data, and substantially outperform baseline approaches that also lack true OOD tuning data. This demonstrates practical viability for real-world open-set RFF deployment.

Conclusion: OOD detection without auxiliary OOD data for tuning is not only feasible but effective for open-set RF fingerprinting. The information-theoretic framing provides a principled foundation for systematic method analysis and future development, paving the way for robust, practical RF fingerprinting in open-world environments.

Abstract: Radio-frequency (RF) fingerprinting systems must operate in open-world environments where signals from unknown transmitters and temporal drift introduce distribution shift at test time. Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection provides a natural framework for this problem, yet its application to RF fingerprinting (RFF) remains limited. A key barrier to their adoption is that most OOD detectors require auxiliary OOD data for parameter tuning, an assumption that is difficult to satisfy in RF environments where representative OOD data is impractical to collect. In this work, we introduce a promising set of OOD detection methods from the machine learning literature to open-set RFF domain. We present these methods within a unified mathematical framework based on information theory, which is a natural framework for communication systems. Our framework allows for the systematic analysis of methods and development of new methods. We further demonstrate the applicability of recent work on tuning OOD detectors without given OOD tuning data for open-set RFF. We evaluate on the POWDER RF fingerprinting dataset, showing that detectors tuned without any given OOD data achieve performance comparable to baselines with access to true OOD tuning data and greatly out-perform baseline approaches without access to true OOD tuning data, showcasing the practical viability for the RFF problem.

</details>


### [109] [Normative Robustness as a Frontier for Non-Verifiable Reasoning in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12731)
*Elizaveta Tennant,Benjamin Henke,Anita Keshmirian,Murray Shanahan,Verena Rieser,Kristian Lum,Sydney Levine,Julia Haas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces "moral robustness" as a metric for evaluating LLMs' capacity to maintain consistent, sound moral reasoning across time and contexts. Through 48,000 simulated multi-turn moral deliberations across four frontier LLMs, the authors find models exhibit "moral deliberative sycophancy" — they shift both verdicts and justifications toward the user's stated moral view (up to 6.5% average shift), are swayed by premise order (13–22% of cases), and vary judgments between single-turn and multi-turn settings (10–24% of cases), despite successfully ignoring irrelevant distractors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly deployed in advisory and deliberative roles where reasoning cannot be verified against ground truth (e.g., moral, political, or value-laden domains). Traditional evaluations focus exclusively on fact-based reasoning (math, science), leaving a critical gap in understanding whether models handle ambiguous, subjective problems robustly — particularly across multiple turns and contexts.

Method: The authors propose a scalable, adversarial, multi-turn evaluation framework. They simulate 48,000 user-agent moral deliberations across four frontier LLMs, systematically varying four factors: (1) premise relevance (moral vs. irrelevant distractors), (2) premise order, (3) conversation duration (single-turn vs. multi-turn), and (4) the user's stated moral view. They measure shifts in both final verdicts and underlying justifications.

Result: Key findings: (1) Models successfully ignore morally irrelevant distractors. (2) Models shift reasoning toward the user's stated moral view by up to 6.5% on average. (3) Premise order alters moral judgments in 13–22% of cases. (4) Duration (single-turn vs. multi-turn) alters judgments in 10–24% of cases. (5) Models tailor not just final verdicts but also underlying justifications to align with user viewpoints — characterized as "moral deliberative sycophancy."

Conclusion: LLMs exhibit a systematic failure mode termed "moral deliberative sycophancy," where they adapt both moral verdicts and their justifications to align with user views rather than maintaining principled reasoning consistency. This raises significant concerns about the reliability of LLMs in non-verifiable reasoning domains and highlights the need for robustness evaluations beyond fact-based benchmarks.

Abstract: As LLMs increasingly serve in advisory and deliberative roles, users rely on them for non-verifiable reasoning in domains lacking objective ground truths. However, traditional evaluations of LLM reasoning focus almost exclusively on fact-based domains, such as mathematics and science, leaving uncertainty over whether and to what degree models can handle ambiguous, subjective, or value-laden problems over time. To address this concern, we propose moral reasoning as a paradigmatic subdomain of non-verifiable reasoning. We define moral robustness as a model's capacity to exhibit sound moral reasoning across time and contexts, and we introduce a scalable, adversarial, multi-turn evaluation framework to empirically measure this capability. We simulate 48,000 user-agent moral deliberations across four frontier LLMs, varying premise relevance, premise order, conversation duration, and the user's stated moral view. We find that models successfully ignore morally-irrelevant distractors, but shift their reasoning by up to 6.5%, on average, towards the user's stated preferred moral view, and varying their reasoning depending on factors such as order (altering moral judgments by order in 13-22% of the cases) and duration (altering moral judgments between single-turn and multi-turn in 10-24% of the cases). Our analysis indicates that models tailor not just their final verdicts but their underlying justifications to align with a user's moral viewpoint - a failure mode we characterize as moral deliberative sycophancy.

</details>


### [110] [Let's Ask Gauss: Improved One-Run Privacy Auditing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12733)
*Adya Agrawal,Yu Wei,Jaspal Singh,Malik Magdon-Ismail,Vassilis Zikas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a new privacy auditing framework for DP-SGD that, instead of binarizing canary membership guesses, exploits the full distribution of canary-aligned signals. By showing these signals form an asymptotically Gaussian sequence under white-box access, the method yields tighter empirical privacy lower bounds from a single training run.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing one-run DP auditing methods threshold canary examples into hard binary membership predictions, discarding granular information in the signal. This leads to loose privacy lower bounds. The authors aim to leverage the full distributional structure of canary signals to obtain tighter, more accurate audits.

Method: Under white-box DP-SGD, the authors demonstrate that canary-aligned signals (e.g., gradients, losses) form a sequence of random variables whose normalized sum converges to a Gaussian distribution. They then build an auditing framework around this asymptotic normality: rather than thresholding, they use the continuous signal distribution to perform likelihood-ratio-based membership inference, yielding tighter empirical ε estimates from a single run.

Result: The distributional approach produces significantly tighter privacy lower bounds than prior one-run methods, narrowing the gap between empirical audits and theoretical DP guarantees.

Conclusion: Modeling canary signals as asymptotically Gaussian enables more efficient and accurate single-run DP auditing, reducing the need for expensive multi-run procedures while providing stronger empirical evidence of privacy protection.

Abstract: Privacy auditing provides an important safeguard by estimating the actual information leaked by a model, thus ensuring that theoretical privacy guarantees hold in practice. We study empirical privacy auditing for differentially private (DP) machine learning, focusing on efficient one-run methods for mechanisms such as DP-SGD. Prior one-run approaches threshold training examples or "canaries" into binary membership guesses, which discards useful information. We show that, in the white-box DP-SGD setting, canary-aligned signals naturally form a sequence of random variables whose normalized sum is asymptotically Gaussian. Leveraging this distributional perspective, we develop a DP-auditing framework that leads to tighter privacy lower bounds from a single training run.

</details>


### [111] [Physics-Informed Neural Networks and Radial Basis Functions for PDEs with Dirac Delta Sources](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12735)
*Manuel Reyna,Alexandre Tartakovsky*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PINNs struggle with Dirac delta functions and require smooth approximations, causing modeling errors. The authors reframe PINNs as Residual Least Squares (RLS) methods, enabling direct treatment of Dirac deltas via weak-form integration. They compare PINN-RLS with RBF-RLS and show that while PINN residuals fail to converge, RBF-RLS yields consistent forward/inverse solutions for transport PDEs, explained via Neural Tangent Kernel theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard PINNs cannot directly handle Dirac delta functions in forcing terms, boundary conditions, or initial conditions, requiring smooth surrogate approximations that introduce significant modeling errors. The authors seek a principled way to treat Dirac deltas directly within a PINN-like framework.

Method: The authors reinterpret PINNs as Residual Least Squares (RLS) methods. This perspective allows Dirac delta terms to be handled by integrating the weak-form equation, eliminating the need for smooth approximations. They compare two RLS variants: (1) PINN-based RLS, and (2) Radial Basis Function (RBF) expansion (single-layer RBF Network). Analysis is supported by Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) theory. Testing is performed on linear PDEs modeling groundwater flow and contaminant transport in porous media and rivers, including inverse problems with synthetic data, noisy synthetic data, and real-world measurements.

Result: Integrating out the Dirac delta in PINN-RLS causes residuals to fail to converge to zero. In contrast, RBF-RLS consistently provides accurate forward and inverse solutions for transport problems. NTK theory explains this divergence: the PINN's NTK lacks the spectral properties needed to resolve singular source terms, while the RBF kernel is better suited.

Conclusion: RBF-RLS is superior to PINN-RLS for PDEs involving Dirac delta functions. The weak-form integration strategy enabled by the RLS perspective is effective, but only when paired with an appropriate function approximator (RBF rather than neural networks). The findings are practically validated on hydrogeological transport problems with real-world data.

Abstract: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) are a machine learning method for solving forward and inverse Partial Differential Equations (PDEs). When applied to PDEs with Dirac delta functions in the forcing terms, boundary conditions, or initial conditions, PINNs require approximating them with smooth surrogate functions, a practice that can introduce significant modeling errors. In this work, we exploit the interpretation of PINNs as Residual Least Squares (RLS) methods and show that this perspective enables direct treatment of Dirac delta terms by integrating the weak-form equation. Among RLS formulations other than PINN, we focus on the Radial Basis Function (RBF) expansion (also known as a single-layer RBF Network). We show that while integrating out the Dirac delta in PINNs causes residuals to fail to converge to zero, RBF-RLS consistently provides good forward and inverse solutions to transport problems. We explain this finding using the Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) theory. We test both approaches on linear PDEs that represent groundwater flow and transport in porous media and rivers. We solve inverse problems to fit synthetic data, noisy synthetic data, and real-world measurements.

</details>


### [112] [Deep Unfolded Latent Optimally Partitioned-l2/l1 Networks for Data-driven Block-Sparse Recovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12740)
*Takanobu Furuhashi,Hidekata Hontani,Qibin Zhao,Tatsuya Yokota*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes two deep unfolding architectures for the convex LOP-l2/l1 model to enable automatic hyperparameter tuning: one using implicit differentiation for stability, and another using Deep Weight Factorization (DWF) for flexibility, including support for nonconvex data fidelity. Experiments show competitive block-sparse recovery and strong robustness to impulsive noise.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The convex Latent Optimal Partition (LOP)-l2/l1 method effectively recovers block-sparse signals with unknown partition structures, but suffers from two key limitations: (1) it requires cumbersome manual hyperparameter tuning, and (2) numerical instability in differentiating the proximal operator prevents the application of Deep Unfolding (DU) for automatic parameter learning.

Method: The authors propose two DU-based architectures to address the differentiation instability: (1) a stable framework that employs implicit differentiation to bypass direct differentiation of the proximal operator, and (2) a flexible variant based on Deep Weight Factorization (DWF) that further accommodates nonconvex smooth data fidelity terms. Both architectures embed the LOP-l2/l1 optimization into a learnable unfolded network.

Result: Numerical experiments demonstrate that the proposed DU-LOP-l2/l1 architectures achieve competitive block-sparse signal recovery performance and exhibit high resilience against impulsive noise compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: The proposed DU-LOP-l2/l1 frameworks successfully automate hyperparameter tuning for the LOP-l2/l1 model, overcoming the proximal operator differentiation challenge. The DWF-based variant further extends applicability to nonconvex fidelity terms, making the approach both practical and versatile for block-sparse recovery tasks.

Abstract: The convex Latent Optimal Partition (LOP)-l2/l1 approach enables block-sparse signal recovery with unknown partitions but relies on manual hyperparameter tuning. Additionally, numerical instability in differentiating its proximal operator prevents its automatic parameter tuning via Deep Unfolding (DU). To address these limitations, we propose two architectures: a stable framework utilizing implicit differentiation and a flexible variant leveraging Deep Weight Factorization (DWF). The DWF-based approach also supports nonconvex smooth data fidelity terms. Numerical experiments demonstrate that DU-LOP-l2/l1 yields competitive performance and high resilience against impulsive noise.

</details>


### [113] [Adaptive Weighted Averaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12763)
*Aditya Bhaskara,Ashok Cutkosky,Ravi Kumar,Manish Purohit*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: We study selecting the maximum among n unknown values using only a single noisy estimate per candidate. We design strategies that are both admissible (not uniformly dominated) and never worse than a baseline like uniform random selection, with an application to stochastic optimization yielding online-to-batch conversion bounds that never underperform random iterate selection but can greatly outperform it.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop selection strategies for identifying the maximum among unknown values from noisy estimates, with the dual guarantees of admissibility (no uniformly superior alternative exists) and a safety property (never worse than a naive baseline). This addresses a gap where existing strategies may excel in some settings but catastrophically fail in others, lacking robustness.

Method: The authors formalize the problem as selecting argmax x_i given one unbiased estimate y_i per candidate. They design strategies satisfying two criteria: (1) admissibility — no other strategy stochastically dominates it uniformly over all possible configurations of (x_1,...,x_n), and (2) baseline dominance — the strategy's expected maximum value is never below that of a reference strategy (e.g., uniform random selection). The approach leverages decision-theoretic principles to construct selection rules that balance these constraints, then applies them to online-to-batch conversion in stochastic optimization.

Result: The authors construct strategies that simultaneously achieve admissibility and never underperform a given baseline. For the application to stochastic optimization, the resulting online-to-batch conversion bounds carry a "no-compromise" guarantee: they are never worse than standard random iterate selection, while achieving substantially better performance in favorable (benign) settings.

Conclusion: It is possible to design selection strategies against noisy estimates that are both admissible and baseline-safe. These strategies admit a clean application to stochastic optimization, providing online-to-batch bounds that improve upon uniform selection without ever doing worse, offering a robust improvement over standard methods.

Abstract: We study the problem of selecting the largest among $n$ unknown values $x_1,\dots,x_n$ given only a single unbiased estimate $y_i$ for each $x_i$. We design strategies that are simultaneously admissible (not uniformly dominated by any other strategy) and also never worse than a given baseline such as uniform random selection. We provide an application to stochastic optimization, where we obtain online-to-batch conversion bounds with a desirable "no-compromise" guarantee: they are never worse than standard random iterate selection, and yet can be significantly better in benign settings.

</details>


### [114] [ProPlay: Procedural World Models for Self-Evolving LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12780)
*Yijun Ma,Zehong Wang,Yiyang Li,Ziming Li,Xiaoguang Guo,Weixiang Sun,Chuxu Zhang,Yanfang Ye*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ProPlay introduces a procedural world model that enables LLM agents to rehearse and refine future task trajectories via a procedure graph with reliability embeddings, closing the loop between planning and learning in partially observable environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-evolving agents struggle in partially observable environments because they must balance active exploration, limited feedback, and trust in prior experience. Existing LLM-agent approaches using memory or planning modules fail to close the feedback loop between these components, preventing continual refinement of the agent's internal understanding of environment dynamics.

Method: ProPlay abstracts successful trajectories into procedures organized in a procedure graph that captures causal transitions among task stages. Each transition carries a reliability record embedding that estimates task-specific contribution from past outcomes. Before each episode, the model simulates future procedural trajectories over the graph as structured soft guidance (procedure-level preplay); after execution, it refines the graph using environment feedback, closing the perception-action-learning loop.

Result: Experiments on public benchmarks show that ProPlay consistently improves environment understanding and self-evolution capability compared to strong baselines.

Conclusion: ProPlay demonstrates that integrating procedural world modeling with closed-loop refinement enables more effective self-evolving agents in partially observable settings, with open-sourced code to facilitate further research.

Abstract: Self-evolving agents are expected to improve through interaction without external supervision, but this remains difficult in partially observable environments where agents must explore actively, learn from limited feedback, and decide when to trust prior experience. Existing LLM-agent methods often rely on memory or planning modules, yet they rarely close the loop between them to continually refine an internal understanding of environment dynamics. We introduce ProPlay, a procedural world model that supports procedure-level preplay, where agents can rehearse future procedural paths using the learned world knowledge. Rather than representing experience as isolated rules or low-level action constraints, ProPlay abstracts successful trajectories into procedures and organizes them in a procedure graph that captures causal transitions among task stages. Each transition is associated with a reliability record embedding to estimate its task-specific contribution from past outcomes. Before each episode, ProPlay simulates future procedural trajectories over known graph structures as structured soft guidance; after execution, it refines the graph using environment feedback. Experiments on public benchmarks show that ProPlay consistently improves environment understanding and self-evolution capability over strong baselines. Our code has been released in https://github.com/antman9914/proplay.

</details>


### [115] [SymQNet: Amortized Acquisition for Low-Latency Adaptive Hamiltonian Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12808)
*Yash Vardhan Tomar,Dheeraj Peddireddy,Vaneet Aggarwal*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SymQNet uses offline reinforcement learning to learn a fast acquisition policy for adaptive Hamiltonian learning, dramatically reducing online decision latency (47-73× speedup) while maintaining Bayesian posterior feedback, making adaptive quantum device calibration practical for low-latency workloads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adaptive Hamiltonian learning requires recomputing Bayesian design rules after every posterior update, which takes seconds per decision. Across hundreds of experimental shots, this computational overhead becomes a prohibitive wall-clock bottleneck, limiting practical deployment of adaptive controllers for quantum device calibration and characterization.

Method: The authors propose SymQNet, an amortized reinforcement-learning framework that decouples policy learning from online execution. Offline, a posterior-conditioned acquisition policy is trained using RL. Online, the policy forward pass is fast, avoiding expensive Bayesian optimization per step while retaining posterior feedback. The method is benchmarked against bounded Fisher-information search and bounded two-step BALD on transverse-field Ising models.

Result: At five qubits, SymQNet reduces acquisition-only decision latency by 47.1× over bounded Fisher-information search and 72.6× over bounded two-step BALD. At twelve qubits, full simulated steps take 1.02 s for SymQNet versus 13.27 s for bounded two-step BALD.

Conclusion: Learned acquisition policies via amortized RL can make adaptive Hamiltonian learning practical for repeated low-latency workloads, overcoming the computational bottleneck that has limited real-world deployment of Bayesian adaptive controllers in quantum device characterization.

Abstract: Adaptive Hamiltonian learning is central to calibrating and characterizing quantum devices. In an adaptive controller, choosing the next experiment is itself a computation. Bayesian design rules are recomputed after every posterior update, and that step can take seconds. Across hundreds of shots, those seconds become a significant wall-clock cost for adaptivity. We introduce SymQNet, an amortized reinforcement-learning approach for low-latency adaptive Hamiltonian learning. SymQNet learns a posterior-conditioned acquisition policy offline, then uses a fast policy forward pass online while retaining Bayesian posterior feedback. On transverse-field Ising benchmarks, SymQNet substantially reduces acquisition latency relative to bounded Fisher-information search and bounded two-step Bayesian active learning by disagreement (BALD).
