At five qubits, it reduces acquisition-only decision latency by $47.1\times$ and $72.6\times$ relative to these online baselines; at twelve qubits, full simulated steps take $1.02$ s for SymQNet versus $13.27$ s for bounded two-step BALD. Overall, we show that learned acquisition can make adaptive Hamiltonian learning practical for repeated low-latency workloads.

</details>


### [116] [CLARITree: Cholesky and Lookahead Accelerations for Regression with Interpretable Piecewise Linear Trees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12840)
*Yixiao Wang,Hayden McTavish,Varun Babbar,Margo Seltzer,Cynthia Rudin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents a novel algorithm for constructing near-optimal, sparse, piecewise linear regression trees using a lookahead search strategy combined with efficient rank-one Cholesky updates, achieving significant improvements in scalability and computational efficiency over existing optimal methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Optimal regression tree methods (dynamic programming/branch-and-bound) achieve superior accuracy but are computationally prohibitive for general linear regression trees. Recent lookahead strategies have shown promise in classification but remain underexplored for regression. The authors aim to bridge this gap with a scalable near-optimal approach for sparse, piecewise linear regression trees.

Method: A lookahead-style search strategy is combined with efficient rank-one Cholesky updates of the Gram matrix to construct near-optimal sparse piecewise linear regression trees. The method leverages the Cholesky factorization to efficiently update linear model fits when evaluating potential splits, avoiding costly full recomputation.

Result: The proposed algorithm achieves a favorable trade-off between computational efficiency, predictive accuracy, and sparsity. It scales significantly better than the current state-of-the-art optimal methods, with both theoretical analysis and empirical validation supporting these claims.

Conclusion: Lookahead search with rank-one Cholesky updates enables near-optimal piecewise linear regression tree construction at a fraction of the computational cost of existing optimal methods, making high-quality regression trees feasible at larger scales.

Abstract: Regression trees are among the most interpretable yet expressive model classes in machine learning. Historically, greedy induction has been the dominant approach for constructing well-performing regression trees. While optimal methods based on dynamic programming and branch-and-bound exist, they are computationally prohibitive for general linear regression trees, despite often achieving substantially better performance than greedy approaches. Recent work has shown that specialized lookahead strategies can dramatically improve runtime while maintaining near-optimal performance, primarily in classification settings. In this work, we develop a novel algorithm for near-optimal, sparse, piecewise linear regression trees that combines a lookahead-style search strategy with efficient rank-one Cholesky updates of the Gram matrix. We demonstrate, both theoretically and empirically, that our method achieves a favorable trade-off between computational efficiency, predictive accuracy, and sparsity, and scales significantly better than the current state of the art.

</details>


### [117] [TimeROME-DLM: Temporal Causal Tracing and Low-Rank Inference-Time Knowledge Editing for Masked Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12841)
*Zhengtao Yao,Liuyang Song,Hongbo Zhang,Chenhao Wei,Haoyan Xu,Guang Yang,Siheng Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TimeROME-DLM is a training-free, gradient-free, inference-time knowledge-editing framework for masked diffusion language models (MDLMs). It combines a Temporal Indirect Effect causal-tracing protocol with a closed-form, low-rank residual edit memory, enabling fast, VRAM-efficient fact editing/forgetting while preserving retained knowledge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing knowledge-editing methods (ROME, MEMIT, etc.) are designed exclusively for autoregressive LLMs and fail on MDLMs: they rely on assumptions invalid under iterative denoising, require prohibitive GPU memory for gradient computation, and cause model collapse at standard learning rates. No editing framework exists for MDLMs, which now rival AR LLMs.

Method: TimeROME-DLM couples (1) Temporal Indirect Effect (TIE) causal tracing — identifies the coordinate (layer, token position, denoising step) whose intervention maximally drives object prediction across later steps; and (2) a closed-form, low-rank residual edit memory that aggregates subject-key/target-delta pairs across forget facts, applying a single ridge-regularised update with sparsification at the identified coordinate during every diffusion forward pass. Backbone weights are frozen; only three hyperparameters (alpha, lambda, q) are tuned on a small validation set.

Result: On TOFU forget01 with TOFU-finetuned LLaDA-8B-Base, TimeROME-DLM reduces forget-set log-probability by ~83 nats while keeping retain-set log-probability within ~1 nat of baseline. It transfers zero-shot to LLaDA-8B-Instruct, Dream-7B, MMaDA-8B, DiffuLLaMA-7B, and LLaDA-MoE-1.4B. It achieves 4–14× wall-clock speedup over the strongest training-time baseline, uses zero additional VRAM, and scales sub-linearly to 400 facts across 50 sequentially inserted facts.

Conclusion: TimeROME-DLM successfully closes the locate-then-edit gap between AR LLMs and MDLMs, providing the first practical knowledge-editing and unlearning framework for masked diffusion language models at a fraction of the computational cost of prior approaches.

Abstract: Masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) such as LLaDA now rival autoregressive (AR) LLMs, but every existing knowledge-editing and unlearning method (ROME, MEMIT, etc.) targets AR transformers and either makes assumptions that fail under iterative denoising, or requires gradient updates whose backward-pass activations cost tens of GB of extra VRAM and which collapse MDLMs at standard learning rates. We introduce TimeROME-DLM, the first training-free, gradient-free, inference-time knowledge-editing framework for MDLMs. It couples two components: a Temporal Indirect Effect (TIE) causal-tracing protocol that identifies, for each fact, the coordinate whose intervention most strongly drives the object prediction at later denoising steps; and a closed-form, low-rank residual edit memory that aggregates subject keys and target deltas across all forget facts and applies a single ridge-regularised update at that coordinate at every diffusion forward, with sparsification to limit utility spillover. Backbone weights stay frozen; only three hyperparameters (alpha, lambda, q) are tuned on a small validation split. On TOFU forget01 with TOFU-finetuned LLaDA-8B-Base, TimeROME-DLM cuts forget-set log-probability by roughly 83 nats. The same configuration transfers to LLaDA-8B-Instruct, Dream-7B, MMaDA-8B, DiffuLLaMA-7B, and LLaDA-MoE-1.4B. It keeps retain-set log-probability nearly flat (within ~1 nat at the utility-safe operating point) across 50 sequentially inserted facts, delivers a four- to fourteen-fold wall-clock speedup with zero additional VRAM over the strongest converged training-time baseline, and scales sub-linearly to 400 facts. TimeROME-DLM closes the locate-then-edit gap between AR LLMs and MDLMs at a fraction of the computational cost.

</details>


### [118] [Interpretable Factor Decomposition for Decision Intelligence in Large-Scale Financial Markets: Evidence from China's A-Share Market](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12843)
*Xiao Han,Yao Xiao,Zhen Zhang,Moxuan Zheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An interpretable XGBoost pipeline with SHAP attribution decomposes Chinese A-share stock return predictability, finding behavioral signals dominate (58.2%) over valuation ratios (10.7%), with a long-short spread yielding +2.38%/month and Sharpe 2.23, robust to the Carhart model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To move beyond black-box machine learning in asset pricing by creating an auditable, interpretable decomposition of cross-sectional equity return predictability into factor contributions, and to understand the relative importance of different signal categories (behavioral vs. fundamental) in the Chinese A-share market.

Method: An XGBoost classifier is trained on 60-month rolling windows with 55-month out-of-sample periods on 3,632 Chinese A-share stocks (2009–2019). TreeSHAP is used for model attribution, decomposing predictions into feature-level contributions aggregated by signal category (behavioral, valuation, etc.) across 55 industry groups. Stress testing and ablation analysis are conducted to cross-validate rankings and reveal feature substitutability structure.

Result: XGBoost achieves a mean AUC of 0.547 and a long-short (top vs. bottom quintile) spread of +2.38%/month (Newey-West t = 5.94; annualized Sharpe 2.23). Alpha persists after Carhart four-factor adjustment (+2.31%/month; t = 7.48). SHAP decomposition shows behavioral signals (turnover, momentum) account for 58.2% of predictive attribution, versus 10.7% for valuation ratios. Ablation analysis corroborates the ranking but diverges from SHAP in ways that expose feature substitutability.

Conclusion: Interpretable ML pipelines can provide auditable, economically significant return predictability. Behavioral factors dominate Chinese equity markets, and combining SHAP with ablation yields richer insight into feature interdependencies than either method alone.

Abstract: We present an interpretable machine learning pipeline to decompose Cross-Sectional Equity Return Predictability into auditable factor contribution. We apply an XGBoost model with TreeSHAP attribution and conduct stress testing on 3632 Chinese A-share stocks from 2009 until 2019. Using 60-month, rolling windows over 55 months of out-of-sample data, XGBoost obtains a mean AUC of 0.547 and +2.38%/month (Newey-West t = 5.94; Annualized Sharpe 2.23) long-short spread for the top vs bottom quintiles. This alpha is persistent after adjusting for the Carhart four-factor model (+2.31%/month; t = 7.48). SHAP Decomposition indicates that behavioral signals (turnover and momentum) account for 58.2% of predictive attribution compared to 10.7% for valuation ratios, on average, across 55 industry groups. Ablation analysis serves to cross-validate this ranking and provides evidence that SHAP and ablation diverge in a manner that highlights feature substitutability structure that is largely invisible to either method used in isolation.

</details>


### [119] [Multimodal Graph Negative Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12863)
*Zhengyu Wu,Xu Wang,Hongchao Qin,Xunkai Li,Guang Zeng,Rong-Hua Li,Guoren Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: GraphMNL addresses node-level branch semantic imbalance in multimodal attributed graphs by using graph-aware negative learning — teaching inferior branches which classes are unlikely rather than forcing imitation of a potentially biased dominant branch, achieving state-of-the-art on Grocery and Reddit M datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In multimodal attributed graphs (MAGs), different semantic branches (topology, text, images) vary in informativeness and reliability across nodes. Existing methods use cross-branch agreement, forcing inferior branches to imitate dominant predictions, but when the dominant branch is biased, this propagates errors and suppresses useful semantics from other branches.

Method: GraphMNL introduces a graph-aware multimodal negative learning framework with four components: (1) a branch library to manage modality-specific representations, (2) graph-aware reliability arbitration to identify dominant vs. inferior branches per node, (3) gating of unstable cross-branch transfer, and (4) target-preserving negative learning that applies supervised loss on the correct class while using negative learning to suppress unlikely non-target classes when branch agreement is unreliable.

Result: GraphMNL achieves 72.47% accuracy on Grocery datasets and 76.60 F1 score on Reddit M datasets, outperforming existing methods in comprehensive experimental evaluations.

Conclusion: Decoupling target supervision from branch guidance via negative learning effectively mitigates node-level semantic imbalance in MAGs without forcing harmful imitation of biased dominant branches.

Abstract: Multimodal attributed graphs (MAGs) integrate graph topology with heterogeneous modality attributes, such as text and images, thereby enabling richer modeling of complex relational systems. However, such expressiveness also makes learning on MAGs depend on multiple semantic sources, including structural topology, textual and visual attributes, each of which can be regarded as a branch for node representation. Node-level branch semantic imbalance arises when these branches differ across nodes in semantic informativeness and reliability: a branch that provides discriminative semantics for one node may mislead another due to bias in modality quality or structural context. Existing methods often mitigate such heterogeneity through cross-branch agreement or alignment, implicitly treating the dominant prediction as reliable supervision. When the dominant branch is biased, forced imitation may propagate its bias to other branches and suppress original semantics that are useful for classification. We propose GraphMNL, a graph-aware multimodal negative learning framework that addresses this issue by using Negative Learning as cross-branch guidance. Instead of forcing inferior branches to imitate a teacher prediction, the model teaches them which classes a node is unlikely to belong to. GraphMNL builds a branch library, identifies dominant and inferior branches via graph-aware reliability arbitration, gates unstable transfer, and applies target-preserving negative learning over non-target classes. This design decouples target supervision from branch guidance so that supervised losses learn the correct class, while Negative Learning suppresses unlikely alternatives when branch agreement is unreliable. Through the comprehensive experimental evaluation, GraphMNL achieves the best performance on Grocery datasets with 72.47% accuracy and 76.60 F1 score on Reddit M datasets.

</details>


### [120] [SMGFM: Spectral Multimodal Graph Pretraining for Multimodal-Attributed Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12867)
*Zhengyu Wu,Xu Wang,Hongchao Qin,Xunkai Li,Guang Zeng,Rong-Hua Li,Guoren Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SMGFM is a spectral multimodal graph pretraining framework that decomposes node signals into graph-frequency bands, assigns band-level semantic roles, and performs frequency-routed cross-modal fusion. By treating low-frequency components as topology-consistent and high-frequency as modality-specific, it achieves SOTA on MAG benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In multimodal-attributed graphs (MAGs), structure-induced semantics (smooth, topology-consistent) and modality-intrinsic semantics (local, fine-grained) contribute differently to downstream tasks. Traditional coupling of topology and features fails to distinguish these roles, leading to problematic uniform smoothing or alignment during cross-modal fusion. A principled way to identify semantic roles before fusion is needed.

Method: SMGFM leverages graph-frequency analysis as a prior: low-frequency components capture topology-consistent semantics, high-frequency components preserve modality-specific semantics. It (1) decomposes each modality's node signal into graph-frequency bands using scalable Chebyshev filters, (2) constructs frequency-resolved modality tokens, (3) estimates coupling reliability via topology-conditioned routing, (4) performs band-modality interaction before fusion, and (5) uses frequency-routed objectives that align smooth consensus routes while preserving modality-specific routes.

Result: Extensive experiments on MAG datasets show SMGFM achieves state-of-the-art performance across both graph-level and modality-level downstream tasks.

Conclusion: Separating semantic roles by graph-frequency prior before cross-modal fusion effectively mitigates spatial-domain entanglement and avoids harmful uniform cross-modal alignment, leading to superior multimodal graph representation learning.

Abstract: Multimodal-attributed graphs (MAGs) couple graph topology with node semantics from text, images, and other modalities. Traditional graph learning contextualizes node semantics by coupling topology with node features. However, this coupling design becomes troublesome in MAGs, where structure-induced and modality-intrinsic semantics may contribute differently to downstream tasks. Structure-induced semantics promote relational consistency through smooth topological variation, whereas modality-intrinsic semantics often encode local, fine-grained distinctions that should not be uniformly smoothed or aligned. Therefore, the key challenge is to identify semantic roles before cross-modal fusion. To this end, we leverage graph-frequency variation as a prior, where low-frequency components capture topology-consistent semantics and high-frequency components preserve modality-specific semantics. Based on this intuition, we propose SMGFM, a spectral multimodal graph pretraining framework that decomposes each modality-specific node signal into graph-frequency bands and assigns band-level semantic roles before cross-modal interaction. Concretely, SMGFM constructs frequency-resolved modality tokens with scalable Chebyshev filters, estimates their coupling reliability through topology-conditioned routing, and performs band-modality interaction before fusion. Its frequency-routed objectives align smooth consensus routes while preserving modality-specific routes, mitigating spatial-domain entanglement and uniform cross-modal alignment. Extensive experiments conducted on the MAG datasets demonstrate that SMGFM achieves state-of-the-art performance across graph-level and modality-level tasks.

</details>


### [121] [Multi-Bitwidth Quantization for LLMs Using Additive Codebooks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12876)
*Liza Babaoglu,Shuangyi Chen,Ashish Khisti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Drop-by-Drop is a post-training quantization framework that produces a single LLM checkpoint supporting multiple inference-time bitwidths, using Matryoshka-style supervision and additive codebooks grounded in successive refinement theory, achieving competitive perplexity/accuracy with reduced storage overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are deployed across heterogeneous hardware with varying resource constraints, yet existing quantization methods typically produce fixed-bitwidth models requiring retraining or separate checkpoints per precision level. There is a need for adaptive, inference-time precision control from a single model without retraining, minimizing storage and memory overhead.

Method: Drop-by-Drop is a multi-bitwidth post-training quantization framework employing additive codebooks with nested, ordered subsets. It uses Matryoshka-style supervision in the loss function, theoretically grounded in information theory and successive refinement: Gaussian-distributed LLM weights can be optimally reconstructed with increasing fidelity as additional bits are added, under a weighted MSE distortion aligned with LLM loss functions. A single checkpoint serves multiple bitwidths via partial reconstructions from codebook subsets.

Result: The method significantly reduces storage and memory overhead by requiring only one checkpoint for multiple bitwidths. It maintains competitive perplexity and accuracy across major architectures including Qwen, LLaMA, Gemma, and Mistral.

Conclusion: Drop-by-Drop enables practical, adaptive precision control for LLM deployment across diverse hardware, combining theoretical rigor (successive refinement, rate-distortion) with efficient implementation (additive codebooks, Matryoshka supervision), achieving strong empirical performance without per-precision retraining.

Abstract: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed across heterogeneous hardware with varying resource constraints, the ability to adaptively manage the trade-off between performance and efficiency without retraining is critical. We propose Drop-by-Drop, a novel multi-bitwidth post-training quantization framework that enables inference-time precision control over LLM weights from a single trained model. Our method is theoretically grounded in information theory and successive refinement. We establish that LLM weights, which commonly follow a Gaussian distribution, can be optimally reconstructed with increasing fidelity as additional bits are incorporated, under a weighted mean squared error distortion motivated by LLM loss functions. To realize this in practice, Drop-by-Drop incorporates Matryoshka-style supervision into the loss function, exploiting the structure of additive codebooks. Drop-by-Drop produces a single model where ordered subsets of codebooks yield accurate partial reconstructions at each precision level. This approach significantly reduces storage and memory overhead by allowing a single checkpoint to serve multiple bitwidths, while maintaining competitive perplexity and accuracy across major architectures, such as Qwen, LLaMA, Gemma, and Mistral.

</details>


### [122] [LongSpike: Fractional Order Spiking State Space Models for Efficient Long Sequence Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12895)
*Xinrui He,Qiyu Kang,Xuhao Li,Zheng-Jun Zha*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LongSpike introduces fractional-order State-Space Modeling (f-SSM) into SNNs to overcome the memoryless bottleneck of first-order ODEs, enabling long-range dependency capture with efficient parallel training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard SNNs rely on first-order ODEs for neuronal dynamics, creating a "memoryless" bottleneck that severely limits their ability to model long-range dependencies in long-sequence tasks.

Method: The authors propose LongSpike, which integrates fractional-order State-Space Modeling (f-SSM) from control theory into SNNs. Fractional-order dynamics introduce long-memory kernels via hierarchical integration of past states. To avoid the typical computational overhead of fractional operators, they reformulate the model using a state-space representation that supports efficient, parallel training.

Result: LongSpike outperforms state-of-the-art SNNs on Long Range Arena (LRA), WikiText-103, and Speech Commands benchmarks while maintaining sparse synaptic computation.

Conclusion: Incorporating fractional-order dynamics via f-SSM is an effective strategy for equipping SNNs with long-range modeling capabilities without sacrificing the energy efficiency of spike-based computation.

Abstract: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) are well-regarded for their biological plausibility and energy efficiency in processing sequential data. However, dominant SNN architectures typically rely on first-order Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs) to govern neuronal state transitions. This first-order assumption imposes a "memoryless" bottleneck, limiting the model's capacity to capture the complex, long-range dependencies inherent in long-sequence tasks. In this work, we propose LongSpike, a novel SNN framework that integrates fractional-order State-Space Modeling, or f-SSM, from control theory into the spiking domain. By extending traditional integer-order SSMs to the fractional-calculus regime, LongSpike enables the hierarchical integration of neuronal dynamics with long-memory kernels. To mitigate the computational overhead and parallelization challenges typically associated with fractional operators, we leverage a state-space formulation that supports efficient, parallel training. Empirical evaluations on challenging benchmarks, including Long Range Arena (LRA), large-scale WikiText-103, and Speech Commands, demonstrate that LongSpike outperforms state-of-the-art SNNs in accuracy while preserving sparse synaptic computation. The code is available at https://github.com/xinruihe389-commits/LongSpike.

</details>


### [123] [Selecting Samples on Graphs: A Unified Dataset Pruning Framework for Lossless Training Acceleration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12913)
*Dongyue Wu,Zilin Guo,Xiaoyu Li,Jiajia Liu,Jingdong Chen,Nong Sang,Changxin Gao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a unified graph-based dataset pruning framework that models samples as nodes with intrinsic weights and pairwise relations as edge weights, casting pruning as a Maximum Weight Clique Problem. A greedy algorithm with formal approximation guarantees is derived, outperforming existing methods and reducing training time by over 40% on ImageNet-1k without accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dataset pruning criteria use either intrinsic signals (independent sample assessment) or extrinsic signals (pairwise diversity), each capturing only one aspect of sample utility. These methods lack robustness across different pruning ratios and data distributions, motivating a unified framework that integrates both signal types.

Method: The dataset is modeled as a weighted graph where node weights encode intrinsic value and edge weights encode extrinsic/diversity value. Dataset pruning is formulated as a Maximum Weight Clique Problem (MWCP). Although NP-hard, the structure enables a principled greedy algorithm based on sample-wise marginal gains. Under mild conditions, the unified objective is proven to have a formal approximation guarantee that applies across a broad family of importance metrics.

Result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms existing dataset pruning approaches while substantially reducing training cost, achieving over 40% training time reduction on ImageNet-1k with ResNet-50 without sacrificing accuracy.

Conclusion: A unified graph-based formulation integrating intrinsic and extrinsic sample utility signals provides a robust, principled, and practically effective solution for dataset pruning, with theoretical guarantees and strong empirical performance.

Abstract: The rapid growth of modern training datasets has significantly increased computational cost, motivating dataset pruning~(DP) methods which retain only a subset of informative samples to reduce training cost.
  Existing pruning criteria typically rely on either intrinsic signals that assess samples independently or extrinsic signals that promote diversity via pairwise relations.
  While effective in their own specific regimes, each captures only one aspect of sample utility and lacks robustness across different pruning ratios or data distribution.
  In this work, we present a unified graph-based DP framework.
  By modeling the dataset as a weighted graph, where node weights encode intrinsic value and edge weights encode extrinsic value, DP can be cast as a Maximum Weight Clique Problem (MWCP).
  Although MWCP is NP-hard, its structure admits a principled greedy solution based on sample-wise marginal gains.
  Under a few mild conditions, we further prove that this unified objective enjoys a formal approximation guarantee, which applies to a broad family of importance metrics and provides practical design guidelines.
  Extensive experiments show that our method outperforms existing DP methods while substantially reducing training cost, reducing training time by over 40\% without sacrificing accuracy on ImageNet-1k with ResNet-50.

</details>


### [124] [Where Computation Lives Inside TabPFN: Causal Localisation of Attention Head Function](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12917)
*Atharva Gupta,Dhruv Kumar,Murari Mandal,Saurabh Deshpande*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: First causal mechanistic analysis of TabPFN 2.5 reveals temporal specialization across attention heads: one dominant head carries 2–5× more causal necessity at its peak layer, with the peak shifting by task complexity, while other heads show symmetric late-layer profiles. Contrastive activation steering fails due to TabPFN's context-dependent in-context learning mechanism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While mechanistic interpretability has advanced significantly for language models, tabular foundation models like TabPFN remain a black box. The authors aim to conduct the first causal mechanistic analysis of a tabular foundation model to understand how feature-wise attention heads distribute computation across layers and whether insights from LM interpretability (e.g., activation steering) transfer to this domain.

Method: The authors apply activation patching, ablation, and attention entropy analysis to TabPFN 2.5 across two synthetic regression datasets of different complexity. They measure per-head causal necessity via patching, characterize temporal specialization patterns, and test contrastive activation steering for inference-time steerability.

Result: One attention head dominates in causal necessity (2–5× over others) at its peak layer. The dominant head's peak layer shifts depending on task complexity. Remaining heads exhibit symmetric late-layer activation profiles. Attention entropy and patching converge on identifying computational layers. Contrastive activation steering fails to transfer across samples, attributed to TabPFN's context-dependent attention mechanism rather than stable parametric directions.

Conclusion: TabPFN exhibits structured temporal specialization in its attention heads, with a single dominant head per task. However, unlike language models, it lacks stable parametric directions amenable to activation steering, because it relies on in-context learning where task representations are encoded dynamically through context-dependent attention patterns.

Abstract: We present the first causal mechanistic analysis of a tabular foundation model, investigating how TabPFN 2.5's feature wise attention heads distribute computation across layers. Using activation patching, ablation, and attention entropy across two synthetic regression datasets, we find clear temporal specialisation: one head's causal necessity dominates that of the others by 2 to 5 times at peak layer, with its dominant layer shifting across tasks of different complexity, while the remaining heads exhibit symmetric late layer profiles. Attention entropy and patching provide convergent evidence for the computationally active layers of the dominant head. We additionally investigate inference time steerability via contrastive activation steering, which fails to transfer across samples. We attribute this result to TabPFN's in context learning mechanism, which encodes task structure through context dependent attention rather than the stable parametric directions that make steering tractable in language models.

</details>


### [125] [LoRA-Muon: Spectral Steepest Descent on the Low-Rank Manifold](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12921)
*Franz Louis Cesista,Katherine Crowson,Cédric Simal,Stella Biderman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: LoRA-Muon adapts the Muon optimizer's spectral steepest-descent rule to low-rank finetuning, yielding a method where optimal learning rates transfer across ranks and model dimensions, outperforming dense baselines at rank-32 on TinyShakespeare while being more memory-efficient and accelerator-friendly than alternatives like Spectron and LoRA-RITE.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LoRA finetuning with standard factor-wise optimizers (e.g., AdamW) suffers from initialization sensitivity, poor learning rate transfer across ranks, and frequent underperformance relative to dense training. The authors aim to address these issues by designing a low-rank optimizer that serves as a faithful proxy for full-rank Muon/Shampoo-family optimizers.

Method: The authors derive LoRA-Muon by applying the Muon optimizer's spectral steepest-descent rule to the low-rank setting, paired with a split weight-decay rule. They also analyze Spectron (showing its dependence on arbitrary factor scaling) and demonstrate that LoRA-RITE's simplified QR-coordinate core implements the same spectral update, but LoRA-Muon avoids QR-decomposition and second-moment storage.

Result: In a compute-matched TinyShakespeare study, a rank-2 LoRA-Muon proxy recovers the dense best tested learning rate, and a rank-32 run achieves lower mean validation loss than the dense baseline in a seed-averaged sweep. Learning rates transfer robustly across rank, width, depth, and factor rescaling.

Conclusion: LoRA-Muon is a good low-rank proxy for full-rank Muon and Shampoo-family optimizers, overcoming key LoRA tuning difficulties while being more accelerator-friendly and memory-efficient than competing low-rank spectral methods.

Abstract: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) significantly reduces compute and memory costs for finetuning Deep Learning models but is often harder to tune than dense training: when using factor-wise optimizers such as AdamW, it is sensitive to initialization choices, its optimal learning rates transfer poorly across ranks, and it often fails to beat dense baselines. We derive LoRA-Muon by applying the Muon optimizer's spectral steepest-descent rule to the low-rank setting. Along with our split weight-decay rule, our main claim is that LoRA-Muon is a good low-rank proxy for full-rank Muon and Shampoo-family optimizers. Its optimal learning rates transfer across rank, width, depth, and factor-rescaling. In our compute-matched TinyShakespeare study, a rank-$2$ proxy recovers the dense best tested learning rate, and a rank-$32$ LoRA-Muon run attains lower mean validation loss than the dense baseline in the seed-averaged sweep. We further show that the Spectron optimizer depends on arbitrary factor scaling, so it would likely be a poor fit when finetuning starts from badly imbalanced factors, and that LoRA-RITE's simplified QR-coordinate core implements the same spectral update. LoRA-Muon computes that update without QR-decomposition and avoids storing second moments, making it more accelerator-friendly and memory-efficient.

</details>


### [126] [Order Is Not Control](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12923)
*Gareth Seneque,Lap-Hang Ho,Nafise Erfanian Saeedi,Jeffrey Molendijk,Tim Elson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper argues that aligning or perturbing neural systems reveals order-inducing objects, but order is not control. True control requires a receiver-gated response law—a denominator-indexed operator mapping state, action, bath, and receiver to displacement, sinks, effort, and basin projection. Evidence across biological panels (mouse, C. elegans, zebrafish) and LLM/adapter/stochastic-operator panels supports local admitted control and measurable response operators, while explicitly excluding coordinate identity, deployable pre-generation control, and thermodynamic claims.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current AI alignment, interpretability, steering, and neural perturbation research conflates discovering order-inducing objects with achieving genuine control. The authors contend that producing ordered outcomes does not constitute control; control demands a formal, receiver-gated response law that accounts for how interventions are admitted, saturated, sign-changed, leaked, or overdriven depending on medium, bath, and receiver state.

Method: The paper identifies receiver-gated response laws across multiple panels: (1) biological systems (mouse ALM, C. elegans, zebrafish) providing physical response-operator evidence without coordinate identity assumptions; (2) LLM panels where generated-output response vectors are tested under four material conditions, predicting component-sign accuracy (72.8–73.7%, rising to 84.3–84.8% on nonzero components) and held-out observer prediction of system-effect and target/oracle families (93.6% and 91.7%); (3) constitution-conditioned adapters as prepared media reshaping susceptibility; and (4) stochastic-operator panels separating measured opportunity from deployable action policies.

Result: LLM response vectors are predictable at 72.8–73.7% component-sign accuracy overall and 84.3–84.8% on nonzero components. Held-out observers predict system-effect families at 93.6% accuracy and target/oracle families at 91.7% accuracy. The evidence supports a driven-dissipative response-system account: drives act through prepared media, baths, and receivers, producing admitted movement, impedance, sinks, or overdrive. Local admitted control and measurable stochastic response operators are empirically supported.

Conclusion: Control is local, receiver-gated, and distinguishable from mere order induction. The response-law framework unifies findings across biological and artificial neural systems at the mesoscopic level. However, the work explicitly brackets out deployable pre-generation control, hidden/logit causal sufficiency, biological-to-LLM coordinate identity, and literal thermodynamic interpretations, delineating the scope of claims.

Abstract: AI alignment, interpretability, steering, and neural perturbation studies identify order-inducing objects. We argue that order is not control. Control requires a receiver-gated response law: a denominator-indexed operator mapping material state, action/drive, bath, and receiver state to response displacement, sinks, effort, and basin projection. We identify it across biological, LLM, adapter, and stochastic-operator panels. The laws are local: an intervention can be admitted, saturated, sign-changing, leaky, or overdriven depending on medium, bath, receiver state, action port, and comparator. Control is assigned when finite effort moves a target or outcome-readout class under the same denominator while damage, null/evasive, invalid format, overdrive, and unnecessary effort stay bounded. Mouse ALM, C. elegans, and zebrafish panels provide physical response-operator evidence while excluding coordinate identity and controller conclusions. LLM panels show generated-output response laws: across four material conditions, response vectors are predictable at 72.8-73.7% component-sign accuracy, rising to 84.3-84.8% on nonzero components; held-out observers predict system-effect and target/oracle families at 93.6% and 91.7% accuracy. Constitution-conditioned adapters reshape susceptibility as prepared media, and stochastic-operator panels separate measured opportunity from deployable action policies. This gives a driven-dissipative response-system account at the mesoscopic control level: drives act through prepared media, baths, and receivers, producing admitted movement, impedance, sinks, or overdrive. The evidence supports local admitted control and measurable stochastic response operators, while leaving deployable pre-generation control, hidden/logit causal sufficiency, biological-to-LLM coordinate identity, and literal thermodynamic quantities outside scope.

</details>


### [127] [Is Spurious Correlation Removal Always Learnable?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12930)
*Yibo Zhou,Bo Li,Hai-Miao Hu,Hanzi Wang,Xiaokang Zhang,Ruifan Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Invariant learning faces a conditional computational barrier: even when the invariant structure is statistically identifiable, polynomial-time algorithms can fail where exhaustive search succeeds. Under sufficient environment diversity, the authors derive minimax risk bounds and identify a phase transition in sample complexity, validated empirically.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To formally characterize when and why invariant learning fails computationally despite statistical identifiability, and to quantify how environment diversity governs both identifiability and the curvature of invariance objectives.

Method: The authors construct a reduction from a black-box samplable supervised sparse recovery primitive to show a conditional computational hardness result. They then develop a theoretical framework using a separation parameter γ to analyze minimax risk, establishing rates of Θ(k(d-k)/(n|E|)) under sufficient diversity and local Gaussian regularity. A phase transition analysis at n* ∝ k(d-k)/(|E|γ²) is derived for label-induced shift settings.

Result: A computational barrier is proved: polynomial-time constant-accuracy recovery contradicts the sparse recovery primitive, while exhaustive search succeeds with polynomial samples. The minimax risk is characterized as Θ(k(d-k)/(n|E|)), with refined scaling proportional to 1/γ² beyond the phase transition. Synthetic and real-data experiments corroborate the theoretical gaps and transitions.

Conclusion: Invariant learning exhibits a fundamental computational-statistical gap driven by environment diversity (γ). Sufficient diversity enables efficient estimation with sharp risk bounds, while insufficient diversity triggers a phase transition in sample complexity. The authors recommend simple diversity diagnostics for practitioners.

Abstract: Invariant learning can fail even when the invariant structure is statistically identifiable. We show a conditional computational barrier: under a black-box samplable supervised sparse recovery primitive motivated by average-case sparse-recovery reductions, there exist \emph{samplable} multi-environment instances with a one-dimensional predictive invariant subspace ($k=1$) that are learnable with polynomial samples by exhaustive search, while any polynomial-time constant-accuracy recovery algorithm would contradict the primitive. We further quantify environment diversity by a separation parameter $γ$, which controls identifiability and the curvature of invariance objectives. Under sufficient diversity and local Gaussian regularity, the minimax risk is $\mathbb{E}[\dist(\hat{V},V_{\mathrm{inv}})^2]=Θ(k(d-k)/(n|\mathcal{E}|))$, and under label-induced shifts a phase transition occurs at $n^*\propto k(d-k)/(|\mathcal{E}|γ^2)$ with refined estimation error scaling proportional to $1/γ^2$. Synthetic and real datasets illustrate the predicted gaps and transitions and motivate simple diversity diagnostics.

</details>


### [128] [Circuit Synchronization Precedes Generalization: Causal Evidence from Fourier Structure in Grokking Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12966)
*Achyuthan Sivasankar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Frequency Synchronization Degree (FSD), a novel metric that predicts grokking in modular arithmetic transformers 500–3,000 steps before it occurs, and provides causal evidence that the inter-phase gap is a regularization phenomenon with a predictable 1/weight_decay scaling law.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Grokking—the sudden transition from chance to perfect validation accuracy in transformers trained on modular arithmetic—is attributed to Fourier circuits, but its timing, causal structure, and controllability remain poorly understood. The authors aim to provide a reliable early predictor and causal understanding of this phenomenon.

Method: The authors propose the Frequency Synchronization Degree (FSD), a normalized, permutation-tested metric that quantifies Fourier circuit synchronization without requiring prior circuit knowledge. They validate FSD across nine configurations (primes 53, 71, 97, 113, 131; three seeds each). Causal experiments involve forking training at the FSD-ceiling step and varying weight decay λ to establish a Δt ∝ 1/λ law. Architecture ablations test attention-only, MLP-only, and single-layer variants.

Result: FSD synchronizes 500–3,000 steps before grokking (mean lead +1,722 steps; sign-test p≈0.004) and precedes the restricted-logit loss baseline in all nine cases. Varying weight decay yields strictly monotone earlier grokking with Δt ∝ 1/λ, replicating across three primes (R²=1.00 and R²=0.99 for two clean cases). Attention-only models grok with strong FSD precursors; MLP-only models never grok; single-layer FSD lags, confirming the precursor is a multi-block circuit property.

Conclusion: FSD is the earliest known predictor of grokking, and the inter-phase gap is causally governed by a 1/λ regularization law, formalized as Δt ~ C/λ, consistent with (1/λ)·log(||W_mem||/τ). The multi-block circuit nature of the precursor is confirmed through architecture ablations.

Abstract: Grokking -- where a transformer on modular arithmetic suddenly transitions from near-chance to near-perfect validation accuracy -- is attributed to a Fourier circuit, but its timing, causal structure, and controllability remain poorly understood. We introduce the Frequency Synchronization Degree (FSD), a normalised, permutation-tested metric for Fourier circuit synchronisation requiring no prior circuit knowledge. Across nine modular addition configurations (primes p in {53, 71, 97, 113, 131}, three seeds), FSD synchronises 500-3,000 steps before grokking (mean lead +1,722 steps; all nine positive, sign-test p~0.004), and precedes a restricted-logit loss baseline (Nanda et al.'s excluded loss) in all nine cases, making it the earliest available predictor. We provide direct causal evidence that the inter-phase gap is a regularisation phenomenon: forking training at the FSD-ceiling step and varying weight decay lambda produces strictly monotone earlier grokking, with Delta_t proportional to 1/lambda. This law replicates across three primes (p in {53,97,131}; R^2=1.00 and R^2=0.99 for two clean cases), captured as Delta_t ~ C/lambda, consistent with (1/lambda)*log(||W_mem||/tau). Architecture ablations show an attention-only model groks with a strong FSD precursor; an MLP-only model never groks; a single-layer model's FSD lags, confirming the precursor is a multi-block circuit property.

</details>


### [129] [Predicting Cognitive Load from Speech and Interaction Dynamics in Dyadic Conversations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12971)
*Tahiya Chowdhury*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This study investigates predicting cognitive load from speech and interaction dynamics in natural dyadic conversations, finding that turn-taking patterns and participation balance are key signals for different dimensions of cognitive load.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work on estimating cognitive load from speech has been confined to controlled laboratory settings, leaving a gap in understanding how reliably cognitive load can be predicted during natural, unscripted collaborative conversations.

Method: Audio from 53 dyads performing nine collaborative tasks was analyzed. Static acoustic features, dynamic features, and conversational interaction features were extracted and used to train a two-head Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) encoder to predict cognitive load scores across multiple dimensions.

Result: Conversational interaction features provide useful signals for predicting cognitive load. Temporal demand is associated with turn-taking dynamics such as overlap and speaker switch frequency, while mental demand is linked to imbalanced participation between speakers. The model effectively predicts load related to time pressure, mental work, effort, and task performance.

Conclusion: Task structure and conversational interaction dynamics are critical for modeling cognitive load in natural collaborative settings, extending beyond what acoustic features alone can capture in traditional laboratory paradigms.

Abstract: Estimating cognitive load from speech has largely been studied in controlled laboratory settings, with limited understanding of its reliability in natural collaborative conversations. We investigate whether speech and interaction dynamics predict perceived cognitive load during dyadic conversations. We analyze audio from 53 dyads performing nine collaborative tasks and extract static acoustic, dynamic, and interaction features to train a two-head Gated Recurrent Unit encoder to predict cognitive load scores. Results show conversational interaction provides useful signals for predicting cognitive load related to time pressure, mental work, effort, and task performance. Temporal demand is associated with turn-taking dynamics such as overlap and speaker switch, while mental demand is linked to imbalanced participation between speakers. These findings highlight the importance of task structure and conversational interaction for modeling cognitive load in natural collaborative settings.

</details>


### [130] [EPM-JEPA: Operator-Side Experience Modulation in JEPA-Family World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12979)
*Vedant Pandya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Compares two methods for injecting experience into JEPA world models under distribution shift: operand-side residual injection (EI-JEPA) vs. operator-side LoRA weight modulation (EPM-JEPA). EPM-JEPA shows a null primary result but a consistent 1.90% improvement over baseline, while trajectory analysis reveals three dynamical processes rather than equilibrium convergence, motivating a successor model (PEM-JEPA).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard JEPA-family world models use static predictors that cannot adapt when test-time dynamics deviate from training. The authors aim to determine whether incorporating accumulated experience via operand-side (hidden state residuals) or operator-side (LoRA weight modulation) mechanisms yields better adaptation under distribution shift, and to understand the underlying dynamics of the adaptation process.

Method: Two architectures are compared on a Moving MNIST task with gravity shift: (1) EI-JEPA — compressed experience added as a residual to the predictor's hidden state; (2) EPM-JEPA — the same compressed experience representation generates low-rank weight deltas via LoRA applied to predictor weights. A pre-registered comparison uses the D_shift^{n=50} metric (lower is better), with three seeds. Trajectory analysis decomposes the adaptation dynamics into buffer cycling, EMA target drift, and intrinsic LoRA settling transient.

Result: Primary pre-registered result: EPM-JEPA (0.7848 ± 0.0078) vs. EI-JEPA (0.8238) yields Δ = 4.74% — Outcome C, a null result by the stated criterion. Secondary non-pre-registered observation: EPM-JEPA improves 1.90% over the no-memory baseline (0.8000) consistently across seeds, while EI-JEPA underperforms baseline. The D_shift trajectory reflects three independent dynamical processes rather than equilibrium convergence, with an intrinsic LoRA settling transient contributing +0.021 to the metric.

Conclusion: Weight-level modulation (EPM-JEPA) yields consistent but modest gains over baseline, whereas operand-side injection (EI-JEPA) is detrimental. The primary contribution is a mechanistic analysis showing that adaptation dynamics are dominated by transient processes (buffer cycling, EMA target drift, LoRA settling) rather than convergence to equilibrium. These insights motivate PEM-JEPA, a physics-grounded successor that addresses the dynamical-peak limitation inherent in current approaches.

Abstract: JEPA-family world models use a static predictor whose weights do not adapt when test-time dynamics diverge from training. We compare two mechanisms for incorporating accumulated experience into a JEPA predictor under distribution shift: operand-side injection, where a compressed experience representation is added as a residual to the predictor's hidden state (EI-JEPA), and operator-side modulation, where the same representation generates low-rank weight deltas via LoRA applied to the predictor's weights (EPM-JEPA). On a pre-registered comparison (Moving MNIST, gravity shift), EPM-JEPA (D_shift^{n=50} = 0.7848 +/- 0.0078, three seeds) differs from EI-JEPA (0.8238) by delta = 4.74% - Outcome C: a null result - by our stated criterion, a valid outcome. As a secondary, non-pre-registered observation, EPM-JEPA improves 1.90% over a no-memory baseline (0.8000), consistently across seeds, while EI-JEPA underperforms the baseline, indicating the benefit is specific to weight-level modulation. Our primary contribution is a mechanism analysis: the D_shift^{n=50} trajectory reflects three independent dynamical processes - buffer cycling, EMA target drift, and an intrinsic LoRA settling transient of +0.021 - rather than convergence to equilibrium. These findings motivate PEM-JEPA, a physics-grounded successor addressing this dynamical-peak limitation.

</details>


### [131] [Exposure Bias as Epistemic Underidentification in Recursive Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12990)
*Riku Green,Zahraa S. Abdallah,Telmo M Silva Filho*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Recursive multi-step forecasting suffers not only from distribution shift but from epistemic underidentification: one-step Bayes-optimal models fail to identify the correct recursive predictor because self-generated induced states lack the provenance information needed to disambiguate local targets. The paper formalizes this via induced states and provenance variables, decomposes the error, and shows empirically that provenance-aware correction can improve closed-loop performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Exposure bias in recursive forecasting is typically framed as a train/test distribution shift. The authors challenge this view, arguing it is incomplete: even under deterministic latent dynamics and Bayes-optimal one-step supervision, the recursive predictor may remain unidentified due to epistemic ambiguity arising from partial observability or state truncation.

Method: The paper introduces a formal framework with induced states Z (the minimal sufficient statistic for past observations) and provenance variables P (encoding the causal history distinguishing self-generated from observed contexts). It derives an error decomposition for induced states into three components: teacher-forcing/rollout mismatch, representation-class approximation error, and provenance information gaps. Empirically, it tests provenance-aware correction using a binary provenance encoding and analyzes closed-loop gains from both local adaptation and changes in visited induced states.

Result: Rollout enters a distinct induced-state regime; fixed induced states define a local corrective task; closed-loop gains arise from both local adaptation and altered induced-state visitation. Provenance-aware correction yields conditional (not uniform) performance improvements.

Conclusion: Exposure bias should be recast as reasoning under self-induced epistemic uncertainty. The provenance gap reveals inherent limits of one-step supervision for recursive forecasting, and provenance-aware methods offer a principled path toward more robust closed-loop predictors.

Abstract: Recursive multi-step forecasting is usually framed as distribution shift: models are trained on observed histories but deployed on their own predictions. We show this framing is incomplete by proving that, under partial observability or state truncation, recursive rollout is also an epistemic underidentification problem. Even with deterministic latent dynamics, one-step Bayes supervision identifies behavior only on observed contexts and need not identify the deployed recursive predictor once rollout queries self-generated induced states whose correct local targets are not determined by numeric state alone. We formalize this with induced states $Z$ and provenance variables $P$, and derive a decomposition of induced-state error into teacher-forcing/rollout mismatch, representation--class approximation, and provenance information gaps. Empirically, we show that rollout enters a distinct induced-state regime, that fixed induced states define a distinct local corrective task, and that closed-loop gains arise not only from local adaptation but also from changing the induced states visited during rollout. Using a simple binary provenance encoding, provenance-aware correction can further improve performance, though gains are conditional rather than uniform. These results recast exposure bias as reasoning under self-induced epistemic uncertainty.

</details>


### [132] [DeepJEB++: Foundation Model-Driven Large-Scale 3D Engineering Dataset via 2D Latent Space Augmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12994)
*Soyoung Yoo,Leekyo Jeong,Jinsu Ra,Dongeon Lee,Sunwoong Yang,Hyogu Jeong,Namwoo Kang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: DeepJEB++ is a foundation-model-driven framework that augments a small set of jet engine bracket designs into a large, simulation-labeled 3D dataset by performing augmentation in 2D latent space, lifting to 3D, and automatically assigning finite-element labels—achieving a 40× expansion with minimal compute.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data-driven engineering design suffers from a scarcity of large-scale 3D datasets that pair geometry with physics-based performance labels. Existing 3D augmentation methods struggle to preserve subtle geometric variations, and automating the simulation-labeling process is challenging because boundary conditions depend on the generated geometry.

Method: A three-stage pipeline: (1) Fine-tune a pretrained 2D latent diffusion model on multi-view renders of seed brackets, synthesize novel views via latent interpolation, and filter with a VLM quality checker; (2) Lift validated 2D images to 3D meshes using a domain-adapted generative foundation model; (3) Automatically recognize load/bolt interfaces and assign finite-element labels (mass, stress, displacement) without manual intervention.

Result: Starting from fewer than 400 seed designs, DeepJEB++ generates 15,360 simulation-labeled 3D brackets—a 40× expansion—using only a single GPU per stage. Quality is validated on manufacturability, label fidelity against SimJEB ground truth, and distributional consistency.

Conclusion: The framework demonstrates that 2D-latent-space augmentation combined with automated 3D lifting and labeling can overcome data scarcity in engineering design. The resulting dataset will be publicly released to advance reproducible engineering-AI research.

Abstract: Data-driven engineering design is constrained by the lack of large-scale 3D datasets that pair geometry with physics-based performance labels. In particular, existing 3D data augmentation techniques have limitations in preserving subtle and diverse geometric variations, and it remains difficult to automate the subsequent simulation-labeling process, where boundary conditions vary depending on the generated geometry. We present DeepJEB++, a foundation-model-driven data-augmentation framework that expands a small seed set of jet engine brackets into a large, simulation-labeled 3D dataset under constrained resources. Our key idea is to augment in the data-rich 2D latent space, then transfer to 3D. In Stage 1, we fine-tune a pretrained 2D latent diffusion model on multi-view renders and synthesize novel views by latent interpolation, retaining manufacturable designs through a vision-language-model (VLM) quality filter. In Stage 2, the validated images are lifted to 3D meshes by a domain-adapted generative foundation model. In Stage 3, an automated pipeline recognizes the load and bolt interfaces on each mesh and assigns finite-element labels -- mass, stress, and displacement -- without manual intervention. We assess augmentation quality along three intrinsic axes: manufacturability, label fidelity against the SimJEB ground truth, and distributional consistency. Starting from fewer than 400 seed designs, DeepJEB++ yields 15,360 simulation-labeled 3D brackets -- a 40x expansion -- using a single GPU per stage. The dataset will be made publicly available to support reproducible engineering-AI research.

</details>


### [133] [Reliability of Probabilistic Emulation of Physical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12997)
*Sam F. Greenbury,Radka Jersakova,Paolo Conti,Marjan Famili,Christopher Iliffe Sprague,Edwin Brown,Jason D. McEwen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper systematically compares generative models (diffusion/flow matching) and CRPS-trained ensembles for probabilistic spatiotemporal forecasting. CRPS ensembles yield more reliable uncertainties with faster inference when both are trained in latent space; generative models approach parity only when trained in ambient space (often infeasible). The authors release AutoCast and AutoSim as open-source frameworks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While both generative models and CRPS-trained ensembles have shown strong predictive accuracy for physical system forecasting, the reliability (calibration) of their uncertainty estimates has not been systematically compared under controlled conditions. This gap hinders informed model selection for scientific and engineering applications where calibrated uncertainty is critical.

Method: The authors develop a unified evaluation framework comparing diffusion-based generative models, flow matching, and CRPS-trained ensembles across diverse 2D spatiotemporal systems (e.g., fluid flow, reaction-diffusion). All models are matched for size and computational budget. Evaluation metrics include empirical coverage of predictive intervals (reliability), predictive accuracy, and inference latency. Comparisons span latent-space vs. ambient-space training and single-step vs. autoregressive rollout regimes.

Result: CRPS-trained ensembles achieve more reliable uncertainty coverage than latent-space generative models in both single-step and autoregressive settings, with significantly faster inference. Generative models trained in ambient space achieve comparable coverage to CRPS ensembles but incur much higher inference latency. CRPS ensembles show minimal degradation when trained in latent vs. ambient space. Both approaches exhibit good predictive accuracy overall.

Conclusion: CRPS-trained ensembles offer a favorable accuracy–reliability–efficiency trade-off for probabilistic physical emulation, particularly when inference speed matters. Generative models remain viable but require ambient-space training for well-calibrated uncertainties, which is often computationally prohibitive. The released AutoCast and AutoSim frameworks aim to standardize future benchmarking and accelerate research in this domain.

Abstract: Two dominant approaches have emerged for generating probabilistic forecasts of physical systems: generative models, such as diffusion or flow matching; and ensembles of deterministic models with stochasticity injected, trained using the continuous ranked probability score (CRPS) loss. While both approaches have demonstrated strong predictive accuracy, the reliability of their uncertainties has not been systematically assessed. We address this gap by developing a framework to evaluate both approaches across diverse 2D spatiotemporal physical systems, under matched model size and computational budget. We assess the reliability of probabilistic emulation by inspecting the empirical coverage of predictive intervals, while also considering accuracy and computational efficiency metrics. CRPS-trained ensembles typically achieve more reliable uncertainties on both single-step prediction and autoregressive rollouts, demonstrating better coverage than the standard alternative of training generative models in a latent space. Moreover, the CRPS approach offers significantly faster inference. When generative models are trained in ambient rather than a compressed latent space, which is often infeasible for high-dimensional problems, they exhibit comparable coverage to CRPS-trained ensembles, though with substantially larger inference latency. In contrast, when CRPS-trained ensembles are trained in latent space they do not show a marked degradation in coverage with respect to ambient space. Both generative models and CRPS-trained ensembles demonstrate good predictive accuracy. To facilitate future research and application, we release AutoCast, a modular framework implementing both generative models and CRPS-trained ensembles, alongside AutoSim, a flexible dataset generation package for rapid prototyping.

</details>


### [134] [scLLM-DSC: LLM-Knowledge Enhanced Cross-Modal Deep Structural Clustering for Single-Cell RNA Sequencing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13007)
*Ping Xu,Pengjiang Li,Tian Du,Zaitian Wang,Jiawei Gu,Ziyue Qiao,Pengfei Wang,Yuanchun Zhou*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: scLLM-DSC is a clustering framework that integrates LLM-derived gene semantics with transcriptomic topology via cross-modal contrastive alignment, significantly outperforming 11 SOTA baselines on scRNA-seq clustering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing scRNA-seq clustering methods rely on numerical statistical patterns and suffer from semantic agnosticism—they ignore the biological functions encoded by genes. While LLMs possess rich semantic capabilities, they cannot be directly applied to clustering due to the mismatch between generative pre-training and discriminative downstream tasks.

Method: scLLM-DSC combines two views: (1) a Knowledge-Driven Semantic View using NCBI gene priors and contextualized Cell2Sentence embeddings, and (2) a Structure-Aware Topological View from a graph-guided encoder. A cross-modal contrastive alignment mechanism enforces consistency between biological semantics and transcriptomic features in a unified latent space, enabling deep structural clustering.

Result: Extensive benchmarks show scLLM-DSC significantly outperforms eleven state-of-the-art baselines in clustering accuracy.

Conclusion: Integrating LLM-encoded gene semantics with topological structure through cross-modal contrastive learning offers a promising direction for semantically-informed single-cell clustering, overcoming the semantic blindness of purely data-driven approaches.

Abstract: Clustering is fundamental to scRNA-seq analysis, serving as a cornerstone for identifying cell populations and resolving tissue heterogeneity. However, existing methods focus on mining numerical statistical patterns, suffering from semantic agnosticism by neglecting the intrinsic biological functions encoded by genes. While Large Language Models (LLMs) offer promising semantic capabilities, their direct adaptation to cell clustering is hindered by the structural mismatch between generative pre-training objectives and discriminative downstream tasks. To bridge this gap, we propose scLLM-DSC, a novel LLM-Knowledge Enhanced Cross-Modal Deep Structural Clustering framework. Diverging from data-driven paradigms, scLLM-DSC establishes a semantically-grounded representation by synergizing two views: a Knowledge-Driven Semantic View derived from NCBI gene priors and contextualized Cell2Sentence embeddings, and a Structure-Aware Topological View extracted via a graph-guided encoder. Crucially, we introduce a cross-modal contrastive alignment mechanism to enforce consistency between biological semantics and transcriptomic features within a unified latent space. Extensive benchmarks demonstrate that scLLM-DSC significantly outperforms eleven state-of-the-art baselines in clustering accuracy.

</details>


### [135] [CausalMoE: A Billion-Scale Multimodal Foundation Model for Granger Causal Discovery with Pattern-Routed Heterogeneous Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13024)
*Bo Liu,Di Dai,Jingwei Liu,Jiarui Jin,Xiaocheng Fang,Guangkun Nie,Hongyan Li,Shenda Hong*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CausalMoE is a billion-scale multimodal Granger causal discovery foundation model that uses a Pattern-Routed Mixture of Heterogeneous Experts to handle distribution shifts and dynamic regimes in time series. It incorporates Causality-Aware Self-Attention for sparse graph recovery and integrates LLMs/VLMs for multimodal alignment, achieving SOTA in both fully supervised and few-shot settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing neural Granger Causal Discovery methods employ a one-size-fits-all paradigm, failing to capture distribution shifts and dynamic regime changes in real-world time series, which results in entangled representations and spurious causal graphs.

Method: (1) Pattern-Routed Mixture of Heterogeneous Experts: dynamically identifies latent temporal patterns and routes patches to specialized domain experts, decoupling regime-specific mechanisms from shared dynamics. (2) Causality-Aware Self-Attention: operates across variables with proximal optimization to yield sparse Granger causal graphs. (3) First integration of LLMs and VLMs to align numerical signals with textual and visual priors for regularized causal estimation.

Result: CausalMoE achieves new state-of-the-art performance on fully supervised benchmarks and successfully generalizes to few-shot settings where traditional methods fail.

Conclusion: Explicitly modeling patch-level heterogeneity via a mixture-of-experts architecture, combined with causality-aware attention and multimodal priors, yields a robust and generalizable foundation model for Granger causal discovery across diverse temporal regimes.

Abstract: Granger Causal Discovery (GCD) is fundamental for analyzing temporal dependencies in complex systems. However, existing neural GCD methods predominantly rely on a "one-size-fits-all" paradigm, struggling to capture distribution shifts and dynamic regime changes inherent in real-world time series. This often leads to entangled representations and spurious causal graphs. In this paper, we propose CausalMoE, a billion-scale multimodal Granger causal foundation model that explicitly models patch-level heterogeneity. CausalMoE introduces a Pattern-Routed Mixture of Heterogeneous Experts, which dynamically identifies latent temporal patterns and routes patches to specialized domain experts, effectively decoupling regime-specific mechanisms from shared dynamics. To ensure interpretable graph recovery, we design a Causality-Aware Self-Attention mechanism operating across variables, yielding sparse Granger causal graphs via proximal optimization. Furthermore, CausalMoE is the first to integrate LLMs and VLMs to align numerical signals with textual and visual priors, regularizing causal estimation in complex scenarios. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CausalMoE establishes a new state-of-the-art on fully supervised benchmarks, while effectively generalizing to few-shot settings where traditional methods fail.

</details>


### [136] [TWLA: Achieving Ternary Weights and Low-Bit Activations for LLMs via Post-Training Quantization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13054)
*Zhixiong Zhao,Zukang Xu,Zhixuan Chen,Xing Hu,Zhe Jiang,Dawei Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TWLA is a post-training quantization framework that achieves 1.58-bit weight ternarization and 4-bit activation quantization for LLMs. It tackles heavy-tailed activation distributions through three components: E2M-ATQ for minimal-error weight ternarization, KOTMS for reshaping weights and suppressing activation outliers via Kronecker-structured orthogonal rotation, and ILA-AMP for inter-layer aware mixed-precision activation quantization that prevents cascading errors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM ternarization methods keep activations at high precision due to heavy-tailed activation distributions, which fundamentally limits end-to-end inference acceleration. There is a need for a framework that simultaneously quantizes both weights (to 1.58-bit) and activations (to low precision) without significant accuracy degradation.

Method: TWLA integrates three components: (1) E2M-ATQ — a two-stage asymmetric ternary quantizer that first initializes in Euclidean space then relocates onto a manifold to minimize layer-output error; (2) KOTMS — applies Kronecker-structured orthogonal rotation to reshape weights into tri-modal (ternary-friendly) distributions while statistically suppressing activation outliers; (3) ILA-AMP — incorporates adjacent-layer second-order interaction costs into bit allocation and jointly optimizes for layer-wise activation quantization gain disparities caused by the shared orthogonal transform.

Result: Extensive experiments show that TWLA maintains high accuracy under W1.58A4 (1.58-bit weights, 4-bit activations) while delivering significant end-to-end inference acceleration.

Conclusion: TWLA successfully achieves joint low-bit quantization of both weights and activations in LLMs, overcoming the activation precision bottleneck that previously limited ternarization methods, enabling practical deployment with substantial inference speedups.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) exhibit exceptional general language processing capabilities, but their memory and compute costs hinder deployment. Ternarization has emerged as a promising compression technique, offering significant reductions in model size and inference complexity. However, existing methods struggle with heavy-tailed activation distributions and therefore keep activations in high precision, fundamentally limiting end-to-end inference acceleration. To overcome this limitation, we propose TWLA, a post-training quantization (PTQ) framework that achieves 1.58-bit weight compression and 4-bit activation quantization while maintaining high accuracy. TWLA comprises three components: (1) Euclidean-to-Manifold Asymmetric Ternary Quantizer (E2M-ATQ) minimizes layer-output error under weight ternarization via a two-stage optimization from Euclidean initialization to manifold relocation; (2) Kronecker Orthogonal Tri-Modal Shaping (KOTMS) applies a Kronecker-structured orthogonal rotation to reshape weights into ternary-friendly tri-modal distributions, while the shared rotation statistically suppresses activation outliers; and (3) Inter-Layer Aware Activation Mixed Precision (ILA-AMP) explicitly introduces adjacent-layer second-order interaction costs in bit allocation and jointly optimizes for the layer-wise disparity of activation quantization gains induced by the shared orthogonal transform, preventing cascades triggered by a few weak layers. Extensive experiments demonstrate that TWLA maintains high accuracy under W1.58A4, while delivering significant inference acceleration. The code is available at <https://github.com/Kishon-zzx/TWLA>.

</details>


### [137] [A green solvent screening tool for emerging materials via uncertainty aware, transformer enhanced transfer learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13060)
*Ioannis Kouroudis,Simon Ternes,Zhaosu Gu,Gohar Ali Siddiqui,Marina Ustinova,Angelo Lembo,Alessio Gagliardi,Aldo Di Carlo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a transfer learning approach using a foundation model pre-trained on QM9 to predict solubility descriptors (Hansen solubility parameters, Dielectric Constant, Gutmann Donor/Acceptor numbers) with minimal data. It incorporates uncertainty quantification and deploys a screening tool for identifying green solvent alternatives, successfully rediscovering known eco-friendly solvents and proposing new candidates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Solvent usage is projected to rise with emerging technologies (photovoltaics, batteries, catalysis), making the substitution of conventional solvents with greener alternatives critical. However, machine learning efficacy for solubility prediction is severely limited by scarce data on key solubility parameters.

Method: A foundational model pre-trained on QM9 targets is transferred (transfer learning) to solubility prediction tasks with minimal data requirements. The pipeline integrates uncertainty quantification for confidence assessment. A customizable screening and ranking tool is deployed for practical solvent substitution workflows, compatible with high-throughput laboratories.

Result: The method achieves high performance on extensively documented targets (Hansen solubility parameters, Dielectric Constant) and, importantly, on data-scarce targets (Gutmann Donor and Acceptor numbers). The authors augment solubility descriptor data by orders of magnitude. The tool successfully rediscovered known green solvents and proposed new candidates.

Conclusion: Transfer learning from a QM9 pre-trained foundation model effectively overcomes data scarcity in solubility prediction. Combined with uncertainty quantification and a practical screening tool, the approach demonstrates real-world relevance for identifying and validating eco-friendly solvent alternatives.

Abstract: Accurate prediction of solubility remains a central challenge across materials science and sustainable chemistry. In particular due to emerging technologies like organic and hybrid photovoltaics, batteries, and catalysis, solvent usage is expected to increase significantly within the coming years. Therefore, substituting solvents with greener alternatives is vital. This is where machine learning can have substantial impact. However, the limited data on critical parameters of solubility significantly constraints machine learning efficacy. In this work, we transfer a pre-trained foundational model on QM9 targets to our application with minimal data requirements. Additionally, the pipeline integrates uncertainty quantification, allowing the user to gauge the confidence of the predictions. As baseline, we succeed in predicting the Hansen solubility parameters and Dielectric Constant for which extensive databases exist. Importantly, we achieve high model performance on additional targets, such as Gutmann Donor and Acceptor numbers, where the available data is extremely limited. Overall, we augment data on solubility descriptors by orders of magnitude with high quality predictions. For effective dissemination, we deploy easy-to-use, easily integrateable with high throughput labs, customizable tool for ranking and screening possible solvent substitutes. Finally, we rediscovered known green solvent alternatives and proposed new candidates proving its relevance for finding eco-friendly solvents.

</details>


### [138] [Limits of spectral learning under noise](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13067)
*Sabin Roman,Ljupco Todorovski,Saso Dzeroski,Marta Sales-Pardo,Roger Guimera*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper analyzes how additive label noise degrades sparse spectral coefficient estimates in supervised regression. It derives a closed-form expression for the overlap between noisy and noiseless coefficient vectors after feature whitening, revealing a universal degradation curve governed by a single intrinsic noise scale. A fundamental noise threshold beyond which coefficient recovery becomes unstable is identified.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spectral methods are widely used for function approximation in scientific inference, yet the stability of estimated coefficients under noise is poorly understood. The authors aim to provide a principled, basis-agnostic theory of how noise corrupts learned spectral representations in sparse regression.

Method: The authors study supervised regression with additive label noise across multiple function bases (Fourier, Legendre, Bessel, Haar) and dimensions. They whiten the empirical feature geometry and derive a closed-form expression for the overlap (inner product) between noisy and noiseless coefficient vectors. The analysis links degradation to the effective number of active spectral modes and a single intrinsic noise scale parameter.

Result: Noise induces a predictable drift in the learned coefficient vector. A universal degradation curve emerges, governed by a single noise scale. Numerical experiments across Fourier, Legendre, Bessel, and Haar bases confirm the theoretical prediction. A fundamental noise threshold is identified beyond which coefficient estimates become unstable.
