Conclusion: Spectral learning exhibits a fundamental noise threshold that places intrinsic, inescapable limits on recovering functional structure from noisy data. This has broad implications for scientific inference where spectral decompositions are employed.

Abstract: Learning functional relationships from noisy data is a central problem in scientific inference. Spectral methods approximate unknown functions by expanding them in a basis and estimating the corresponding coefficients from data, but the stability of these coefficients under noise remains poorly understood. Here we study supervised regression with additive label noise using sparse spectral representations across multiple bases and dimensions. We show that noise induces a predictable drift in the learned coefficient vector whose magnitude depends on the effective number of active spectral modes. After whitening the empirical feature geometry, we derive a closed-form expression for the overlap between noisy and noiseless coefficient vectors, revealing a universal degradation curve governed by a single intrinsic noise scale. Numerical experiments across Fourier, Legendre, Bessel, and Haar bases confirm the theoretical prediction. The results demonstrate that spectral learning exhibits a fundamental noise threshold beyond which coefficient estimates become unstable, placing intrinsic limits on recovering functional structure from noisy data.

</details>


### [139] [Emotional regulation improves deep learning-based image classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13081)
*Riccardo Emanuele Landi,João M. F. Rodrigues,Marta Chinnici*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces Emotional Regulation, a novel framework that incorporates artificial subjective experience into emotion-augmented deep learning. By pre-training on affective stimuli and balancing emotional/non-emotional responses during downstream optimization, the method achieves state-of-the-art results on CIFAR-10/100 image classification using ResNet and ViT backbones, outperforming prior emotion-based approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing emotion-augmented deep learning methods rely exclusively on objective neurophysiological factors while ignoring the role of subjective emotional experience. The authors aim to bridge this gap by modeling artificial subjective experience to improve neural network generalization.

Method: The proposed Emotional Regulation framework uses pre-training on affective stimulus datasets to instill artificial subjective experience, then balances non-emotional and emotionally-influenced responses during downstream task optimization. Experiments pre-train ResNet and ViT architectures on four emotional datasets, with CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 as target benchmarks for image classification.

Result: Emotional Regulation yields consistent improvements over baseline ResNet and ViT backbones on both CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100. It surpasses all prior emotion-augmented deep learning methods on CIFAR-based image classification, establishing a new state-of-the-art for large-scale vision datasets.

Conclusion: Emotional Regulation demonstrates that modeling artificial subjective experience is a promising direction for emotion-augmented deep learning. The results reinforce evidence that affective states can meaningfully improve machine learning optimization and encourage future work on emotion-inspired architectures.

Abstract: Emotion significantly influences cognition, enhancing memory and learning under certain conditions. Drawing on this principle, emotion-augmented deep learning investigates how affective states can improve neural network architectures and learning paradigms, achieving better generalization than non-emotional models. However, existing methods often rely solely on objective neurophysiological factors, neglecting the role of subjectivity in emotion. To bridge this gap, the present study introduces Emotional Regulation, a novel framework for modeling emotion in deep learning through artificial subjective experience. The method employs pre-training based on affective stimuli, balancing non-emotional and emotionally-influenced responses in downstream task optimization. Extensive experimentation was conducted in image classification, pre-training ResNet and ViT architectures on four emotional datasets, using CIFAR-10 and -100 as target benchmarks. Results reveal improvements over the aforementioned backbones, providing evidence of Emotional Regulation as a promising method for defining emotion-augmented deep learning through artificial subjective experience. Furthermore, the proposed approach overcomes the related work in image classification based on CIFAR, revealing Emotional Regulation as the new state-of-the-art in emotion-augmented deep learning for large-scale vision datasets. The study also enforces evidence of the impact of affective states in improving machine learning tasks' optimization, encouraging further investigation on emotion-inspired architectures.

</details>


### [140] [Scale Buys Interpolation, Structure Buys a Horizon: Certified Predictability for Equivariant World Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13092)
*Hongbo Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proves that equivariant latent world models possess a mathematically guaranteed, computable multi-step prediction horizon certificate derived from the Lyapunov spectrum. This certificate is exclusive to equivariant structure—no amount of scale can replicate it. Empirically, equivariant models recover the true Lyapunov spectrum while dense baselines fail, and the certificate enables training-free auditing of pretrained world models, revealing that scale does not improve horizon calibration.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Average prediction error of world models masks whether individual predictions are trustworthy and over what time horizon. Practitioners lack a principled, computable certificate for the predictable horizon—how far into the future a model's rollouts remain reliable. The authors aim to show that equivariant structure, not scale, provides such a certificate, and that this certificate can be computed without calibration data to audit deployed models.

Method: For equivariant latent world models, the authors derive a multi-step certificate based on the predictor's Lyapunov spectrum. Theorem A establishes that T-step rollout error is constant over each symmetry orbit. The horizon is upper-bounded via Lyapunov exponents: T_j(ε) ~ log(1/ε)/λ_j, with a matching lower bound showing approximate equivariance is horizon-limited. They prove orbit-constant error characterizes equivariance (no non-equivariant model can exhibit it). Empirically, they test on 40-D Lorenz-96, comparing Z_N-equivariant networks against dense and recurrent baselines for Lyapunov spectrum recovery. They propose a budget-based certificate inflation analysis and a training-free audit protocol applied to TD-MPC2 (1M–317M parameters) and V-JEPA 2-AC (1B parameters, real robot data).

Result: On Lorenz-96, only the Z_N-equivariant network recovers the full Lyapunov spectrum (R²=0.98); dense and recurrent baselines fail. The certificate meets a fixed sensing budget that its inflated dense counterpart cannot, with zero calibration data. Auditing TD-MPC2 checkpoints shows calibration matches the certificate's scope taxonomy: well-calibrated where strongly expansive (ratio 0.94–1.02), optimistic where weakly expansive, correctly abstaining where contracting. Calibration does not improve with parameter count (1M to 317M). On V-JEPA 2-AC, the cross-validated audit correctly overrides an over-promising tangent spectrum, demonstrating the deployable object is the audited certificate, not the raw Lyapunov number.

Conclusion: Scale buys interpolation, not a calibrated horizon. Only equivariant structure provides a certified, computable predictable horizon via the Lyapunov spectrum. This certificate is faithful, a priori computable, budget-optimal, and enables training-free auditing of pretrained world models. The audit, not the raw spectrum, is the deployable object for safety-critical applications.

Abstract: Scale buys interpolation; structure buys a certified horizon. A world model's average error says nothing about whether a particular prediction can be trusted, or for how long. For equivariant latent world models we give a computable, multi-step certificate of the predictable horizon: $T$-step rollout error is provably constant over each symmetry orbit (Theorem A) and stratified channel-by-channel by the predictor's Lyapunov spectrum, $T_j(ε)\sim\log(1/ε)/λ_j$. The horizon is two-sided -- a matching lower bound makes approximate equivariance provably horizon-limited -- and the certificate is exclusive to structure: orbit-constant error characterizes equivariance, so no non-equivariant model has it at any scale. Empirically, on 40-D Lorenz-96 only a $\mathbb{Z}_N$-equivariant network recovers the full Lyapunov spectrum ($R^2{=}0.98$); dense and recurrent baselines fail.
  Because the spectrum is faithful, the certificate acts, a priori: under a fixed sensing budget a $c\times$-inflated certificate provably needs $c\times$ the budget, and the equivariant certificate meets a budget its inflated dense counterpart cannot -- with zero calibration data. The same read-out, unchanged, audits public pretrained world models training-free: TD-MPC2 checkpoints land on the certificate's own scope taxonomy -- calibrated where strongly expansive (ratio 0.94-1.02), optimistic where weakly expansive, correctly abstaining where contracting -- a map a deployed monitor replicates cell-by-cell, out-of-sample. Across the official 1M-317M multitask ladder, calibration does not improve with parameters. On V-JEPA 2-AC (1B, real robot data) the measured cross-check correctly overrides an over-promising tangent spectrum -- the cross-validated audit, not the raw number, is the deployable object. Scale buys interpolation, not a calibrated horizon.

</details>


### [141] [Authority, Truth, and Citation Bias: A Large-Scale Multi-Domain Benchmark for Studying Epistemic Susceptibility in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13104)
*Aryan Khurana,Aravind Ramana RN,Dhruv Kumar*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AuthorityBench is a 220K-prompt benchmark using a 2x2 factorial design (claim veracity × citation veracity) across four domains to isolate how citation-based authority signals affect LLM epistemic behavior. The study finds that citation presence—whether real or fabricated—consistently increases hallucination rates, with the strongest effect when fabricated citations accompany true claims (3–22 pp increase, reaching 35–77% in general knowledge). Legal claims are more robust; venue prestige and author demographics show negligible impact.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs are increasingly deployed in citation-augmented settings (e.g., RAG, search-augmented generation), but the effect of citation presence on model behavior—independent of factual content—remains poorly understood. The authors aim to isolate how the mere presence of an authority signal (a citation) influences epistemic reliability, disentangling it from the truth value of the cited content.

Method: The paper introduces AuthorityBench, a 220,564-prompt benchmark using a fully balanced 2×2 factorial design: crossing claim veracity (true/false) with citation veracity (real/fabricated/absent). It spans four domains (general knowledge, science, law, medicine), 40 prompt templates, four venue prestige tiers, and a country-coded author name dataset. Seven LLMs are evaluated across 12 structured research questions measuring agreement rates, hallucination, overcaution, and sensitivity to prestige/demographic signals.

Result: Citation presence, whether real or fabricated, consistently increases hallucination rates relative to the no-citation baseline. The strongest effect occurs when fabricated citations accompany true claims, raising hallucination rates by 3–22 pp (reaching 35–77% in general knowledge). Legal claims are comparatively robust. Venue prestige and author demographics show negligible impact on model behavior.

Conclusion: Authority signals in the form of citations can backfire: rather than grounding LLM outputs, they increase hallucination, particularly when fabricated citations are paired with true claims. This suggests that citation-augmented generation systems must account for the independent epistemic effect of citation presence, and that legal-domain claims exhibit greater robustness to such authority manipulation.

Abstract: Large language models are increasingly deployed in citation-augmented settings, yet the effect of citation presence on model behavior independent of factual content remains poorly understood. We introduce AuthorityBench, a 220,564-prompt multi-domain benchmark that isolates how citation-based authority signals influence epistemic behavior in LLMs. The benchmark uses a fully balanced 2x2 factorial design crossing claim veracity with citation veracity, the first to do so, across four domains (general knowledge, science, law, and medicine), with controlled variation over 40 prompt templates, four venue prestige tiers, and a country-coded author name dataset. Evaluating seven models on 12 structured research questions, we find that citation presence, whether real or fabricated, consistently increases hallucination rates relative to a no-citation baseline. The effect is strongest when fabricated citations accompany true claims, raising hallucination rates by 3 to 22 percentage points and reaching 35 to 77% in the general knowledge domain, while legal claims are comparatively robust and venue prestige and author demographics show negligible impact. All datasets and evaluation code are available at: https://github.com/floating-reeds/AuthorityBench

</details>


### [142] [Disparate Impact in Synthetic Data Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13105)
*Paul Andrey,Michaël Perrot,Batiste Le Bars,Marc Tommasi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper revisits disparate impact in synthetic data generation (SDG), arguing that fairness is achieved when synthetic and real distributions match — not when biases are "corrected." It analyzes why SDG methods fail to achieve distributional parity due to approximation error, sampling error from group proportions, and DP-induced estimation error, then proposes group-wise modeling as a mitigation strategy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing fair SDG approaches redefine the learning objective away from the true data distribution to remove biases, but this conflates fairness with distributional fidelity. The authors argue that non-disparate impact should instead be framed as achieving equal fidelity across groups — i.e., the synthetic distribution matches the real one equally well for all sensitive groups.

Method: The authors theoretically decompose sources of disparate impact into three error types: (1) approximation error from limited expressive power of SDG models relative to distribution complexity, (2) sampling error from group size imbalances, and (3) estimation error introduced by differential privacy mechanisms. They empirically illustrate these failure modes using probabilistic graphical model–based SDG methods on both synthetic and real-world datasets. A group-wise modeling strategy (training separate SDG models per sensitive group) is proposed and evaluated as a remedy.

Result: Disparate impact arises even without explicit bias in the training procedure. Smaller groups suffer from higher sampling variance, complex sub-distributions induce larger approximation gaps, and DP noise disproportionately degrades minority group utility. Group-wise SDG models consistently improve both overall utility and utility parity across groups in most settings.

Conclusion: Disparate impact in SDG is not solely a matter of biased data but also of statistical and algorithmic factors. Group-wise modeling offers a simple yet effective strategy to mitigate these disparities, and future fair SDG work should account for approximation, sampling, and privacy-induced errors rather than only focusing on distributional bias correction.

Abstract: We revisit the fairness notion of disparate impact for synthetic data generation (SDG), that assesses whether the utility of generated records is the same across sensitive groups. Our approach departs from existing work on fair SDG, that address the problem of correcting for undue biases in the observed distribution, hence redefining SDG as learning a distribution that is not that of the real data. By contrast, non-disparate impact is notably achieved when the synthetic and real distributions are the same. We expose reasons why SDG may fail to reach that solution and discuss why approximation and estimation errors occur and can be disparate across groups. We notably look into the expressive power of SDG methods relative to distribution complexity, sampling errors due to group proportions, and estimation errors induced by differential privacy mechanisms. We illustrate cases of disparate impact on both artificial and real-world data, focusing on SDG methods that rely on probabilistic graphical models. We also introduce a strategy of learning group-wise SDG models and illustrate how it can improve both the overall utility and its parity in many settings.

</details>


### [143] [Demystifying Hidden-State Recurrence: Switchable Latent Reasoning with On-Policy Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13106)
*Jiayu Yang,Chao Chen,Shengen Wu,Yinhong Liu,Yuxuan Fan,Lujundong Li,Songning Lai,Chengwei Qin,Zhijiang Guo*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SWITCH introduces explicit boundary tokens (<swi>/</swi>) that enable standard on-policy RL optimization and mechanistic analysis of latent chain-of-thought reasoning, outperforming prior hidden-state recurrence methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing latent reasoning approaches that replace visible chain-of-thought with continuous hidden-state recurrence suffer from two problems: they are difficult to optimize with standard on-policy RL and are opaque to causal interpretation. The authors aim to solve both issues simultaneously.

Method: The authors propose SWITCH, which uses discrete boundary tokens <swi> and </swi> to mark entry/exit into latent reasoning mode. This makes the GRPO policy ratio well-defined for on-policy RL. They train with a visible-to-latent curriculum and a Switch-GRPO objective that propagates gradients through the recurrent latent computation. The boundary tokens also enable mechanistic probing and causal intervention.

Result: SWITCH consistently outperforms prior hidden-state-recurrence latent reasoning approaches at comparable scale. Mechanistic analysis reveals: (i) <swi> is a sharply localized learned switching policy, (ii) the latent step performs problem-specific, causally important computation, and (iii) computation concentrates at a single hidden-state transition on entry.

Conclusion: Hidden-state-recurrence latent reasoning can be made both RL-trainable and mechanistically interpretable through simple discrete boundary tokens, opening new avenues for understanding how on-policy RL improves models internally.

Abstract: Latent chain-of-thought compresses reasoning by replacing visible reasoning traces with continuous hidden-state recurrence, but existing formulations are difficult to optimize with standard on-policy reinforcement learning (RL) and hard to interpret causally. Our key insight is that a single pair of explicit boundary tokens can address both issues at once: discrete entry and exit anchors make the latent block compatible with standard on-policy RL, and the same anchors offer a natural foothold for mechanistic analysis. Motivated by this, we propose SWITCH, a switchable latent reasoning framework. The model emits <swi> to enter latent mode and </swi> to exit. Because the boundaries are ordinary discrete tokens, the GRPO policy ratio is well-defined at every decision point. The same anchors also expose the latent steps to direct probing and causal intervention. We train the model with a visible-to-latent curriculum and a Switch-GRPO objective that propagates gradients through recurrent latent computation. SWITCH consistently outperforms prior hidden-state-recurrence latent reasoning approaches at similar scale. Mechanistic analysis through the boundary tokens further reveals three findings: (i) <swi> is a sharply localised, learned switching policy rather than a stylistic artefact; (ii) the latent step it opens performs problem-specific, causally important computation rather than acting as an inert placeholder; and (iii) that computation is concentrated at a single hidden-state transition on entry. Together, these results show that hidden-state-recurrence latent reasoning is both RL-trainable and open to direct mechanistic analysis, including of how on-policy RL itself improves the model from the inside.

</details>


### [144] [MP3: Multi-Period Pattern Pre-training forSpatio-Temporal Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13119)
*Lilan Peng,Yandi Liu,Qingren Yao,Chongshou Li,Tianrui Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MP3 is a plug-and-play pre-training plugin for spatio-temporal graph neural networks that addresses the "temporal mirage" problem—where similar short-window inputs lead to divergent future trends—by learning multi-period patterns from long time series, modeling heterogeneous global spatial correlations, and capturing cross-period causality, achieving consistent MAE (4.7%) and RMSE (5.0%) reductions across five STGNN baselines on five real-world datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Urban spatio-temporal forecasting suffers from the "temporal mirage" phenomenon: similar short-window input sequences can yield vastly different future outcomes. Existing STGNNs fail to capture this because short-window inputs inherently suffer from three limitations: incomplete period observation, heterogeneous global spatial correlation, and cross-period superposition causality. The authors aim to bridge this gap with a pre-training strategy that learns richer temporal patterns from longer sequences.

Method: MP3 is a plug-and-play pre-training plugin with two core components: (1) Multi-period pattern learning comprising three sub-modules—multi-period temporal modeling using edge convolution to distinguish different multi-period patterns, multi-period spatial modeling employing a bottleneck projector and global memory bank for efficient heterogeneous global spatial relation capture, and cross-period pattern interaction using a causality-enhanced Transformer to model dependencies across period patterns. (2) Seamless integration into existing STGNN backbones through a pre-training and fine-tuning paradigm.

Result: Evaluated on five STGNN baselines across five real-world datasets (including a large-scale CA dataset), MP3 delivers consistent and robust improvements. On average, MP3 reduces MAE by 4.7% and RMSE by 5.0%, demonstrating superior scalability and strong adaptability.

Conclusion: The temporal mirage problem in spatio-temporal forecasting stems from incomplete short-window observations and complex spatial-temporal dependencies. MP3 effectively addresses this through multi-period pattern pre-training and integrates seamlessly with existing STGNNs, offering consistent performance gains.

Abstract: Spatio-Temporal forecasting is crucial in diverse fields, such as transportation, climate, and energy. Urban spatio-temporal data exhibits temporal mirage: similar short-window inputs have divergent future trends, and vice versa. Existing spatio-temporal graph neural networks (STGNNs) cannot effectively identify such mirages. We argue that the core reason lies in the short-window inputs that have incomplete period observation, heterogeneous global spatial correlation, and cross-period superposition causality. To bridge this gap, we develop a novel Multi- Period Pattern Pre-training (MP3), a plug-and-play pre-training plugin for distinguishing temporal mirages. MP3 presents two core innovations: (1) The multi-period pattern learning is designed to learn multi-period patterns from long time series. Specifically, multi-period temporal modeling leverages edge convolution to identify different multi-period patterns. Multi-period spatial modeling uses a bottleneck project and a global memory bank to capture heterogeneous global spatial relations efficiently. Cross-period pattern interaction employs a causality-enhanced Transformer to capture dependencies across different period patterns. (2) This plugin can seamlessly integrate into existing STGNN backbones to strengthen their forecasting performance. The experiment on five STGNN baselines across five real-world datasets (including a large-scale dataset CA) verify the effectiveness, superior scalability and strong adaptability of MP3, which brings consistent and robust performance improvements across all evaluated baselines. On average, MP3 reduces the MAE 4.7% and the RMSE 5.0%. The code can be available at https://github.com/YAN-outlook/MP3.

</details>


### [145] [Select and Improve: Understanding the Mechanics of Post-Training for Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13125)
*Akshay Krishnamurthy,Audrey Huang,Nived Rajaraman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates the mechanistic underpinnings of reinforcement learning (RL) post-training for reasoning models, identifying two core mechanisms — strategy selection and strategy improvement — through controlled experiments on Qwen-2.5-1.5B, and shows how SFT data diversity and RL data difficulty activate these mechanisms respectively.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Reinforcement learning has become central to training reasoning and coding models, yet the underlying processes by which capabilities are acquired or enhanced during RL post-training remain poorly understood from a mechanistic perspective. The authors aim to demystify this black box.

Method: The authors conduct controlled math reasoning experiments using Qwen-2.5-1.5B as the base model. They systematically vary the properties of supervised fine-tuning (SFT) data and reinforcement learning data, then analyze how these variations affect model behavior to isolate and identify the operative mechanisms.

Result: Two core mechanisms were identified: (1) strategy selection — supervising the model on diverse reasoning strategies enables it to select among them; and (2) strategy improvement — increasing the difficulty of RL training data drives substantive improvement in strategy execution. SFT data diversity and RL data difficulty are the respective enabling factors.

Conclusion: The findings provide mechanistic insight into RL training for reasoning models and suggest practical interventions — namely, curating diverse SFT strategies and scaling RL data difficulty — to continue advancing reasoning capabilities.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning has rapidly emerged as a key component in the training of reasoning and coding models, yet it remains poorly understood from a mechanistic perspective. We study how and through what underlying processes capabilities are acquired or enhanced via reinforcement learning post-training. Our analysis, based on controlled math reasoning experiments with Qwen-2.5-1.5B, reveals two core mechanisms: strategy selection and strategy improvement. Our results highlight the role of SFT data and reinforcement learning data in activating these mechanisms, in particular showing how supervising the model on diverse reasoning strategies can enable strategy selection and how increasing difficulty in reinforcement learning data can enable strategy improvement. Taken together, our results provide mechanistic insight into RL training and suggest practical interventions to continue scaling reasoning capabilities.

</details>


### [146] [MiniPIC: Flexible Position-Independent Caching in <100LOC](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13126)
*Nathan Ordonez,Thomas Parnell*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MiniPIC is a minimal, flexible extension to vLLM that enables Position-Independent Caching (PIC) using positional-encoding-free KV cache and user-controlled cache-reuse primitives, achieving significant prefill throughput gains with minimal code changes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing prefix caching in vLLM cannot reuse KV entries for recurring structured inputs ("spans") unless they share identical prefixes. Production-grade PIC implementations require substantial server modifications or incur host-to-device transfer overhead by keeping KV state outside the server. A minimal, production-friendly PIC solution is needed.

Method: MiniPIC stores unrotated K vectors in the KV cache and applies RoPE to K tiles inside attention using per-request logical positions. It exposes three token-level primitives — block-aligned padding, span separator (SSep), and prompt depend (PDep) — that modify hashing behavior and block-level causal attention structure. These primitives, implemented in under 100 lines of core-engine changes plus a custom attention backend, support multiple PIC methods (Block-Attention, EPIC, Prompt Cache) within a single vLLM instance and integrate natively with KV cache CPU offload.

Result: On 2WikiMultihopQA, MiniPIC with interleaved scheduling improves prefill throughput by 49% over baseline vLLM, reduces cached-span time-to-first-token by up to two orders of magnitude, preserves linear prefill scaling for uncached spans, and incurs only 5.7% worst-case overhead.

Conclusion: MiniPIC demonstrates that minimal engine modifications combined with a custom attention backend can enable flexible, high-performance PIC in production-grade inference servers without the drawbacks of external KV state management or extensive code rewrites.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented and agentic workloads repeatedly prefill recurring predictable structured inputs (which we call "spans") such as documents and code files. Yet, prefix caching in engines such as vLLM cannot reuse their KV entries unless they share identical prefixes with another request, while Position-Independent Caching (PIC) implementations within production-grade inference servers typically either require substantial server code changes or keep KV state outside the server, incurring host-to-device transfer overhead. We present Minimalistic PIC (MiniPIC): a minimal, flexible and fast vLLM design built from two ingredients: positional-encoding-free KV cache and user-controlled cache-reuse primitives. MiniPIC stores unrotated K vectors in the KV cache, applies RoPE to K tiles inside attention using per-request logical positions, and exposes three user-facing and token-level primitives: block-aligned padding, span separator (SSep), and prompt depend (PDep), that modify hashing behavior and effective block-level causal attention structure. With fewer than 100 lines of core-engine changes plus a custom attention backend, these primitives are sufficient to realize multiple PIC methods, including Block-Attention, EPIC, and Prompt Cache, within the same running vLLM instance, while natively integrating with KV cache CPU offload implementations. On 2WikiMultihopQA, MiniPIC with interleaved scheduling improves prefill throughput by 49% over baseline vLLM, reduces cached-span time-to-first-token by up to two orders of magnitude, preserves the linear prefill scaling of uncached spans, and incurs only 5.7% worst-case overhead.

</details>


### [147] [When Does Routing Become Interpretable? Causal Probes on Block Attention Residuals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13168)
*Aydin Javadov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Block Attention Residuals make cross-layer routing directly observable. Probing two 0.6B checkpoints reveals that only the trained variant develops structured routing motifs. However, routing mass and causal importance sharply dissociate, showing that architectural exposure of routing is necessary but insufficient for mechanistic interpretation; causal intervention remains essential.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Block Attention Residuals expose cross-layer information flow as an inspectable tensor, promising direct interpretability. The paper investigates whether this architectural exposure alone suffices for mechanistic interpretation, or whether additional conditions (training signal, causal probing) are required.

Method: Two same-scale (0.6B) Block AttnRes Qwen3 checkpoints are compared under identical routing-ablation interventions: (1) a vanilla Qwen3 wrapped with a deterministic recency-bias schedule (routing-equivalent but content-independent), and (2) a Block AttnRes Qwen3 trained from scratch with routing optimized. Routing weights are probed and causally ablated to assess dissociation between routing mass and causal importance.

Result: The wrapped baseline's routing weights are content-independent and match the analytic schedule. The trained checkpoint exhibits three localized routing motifs: embedding-source via early-layer MLP, current-state via early-layer attention/MLP, and older-history via late-layer attention. Critically, a sharp dissociation emerges: the largest routing-mass slice is not the largest causal contributor, and one source family carries appreciable mass with zero detectable causal role.

Conclusion: Architectural exposure of routing is necessary but not sufficient for mechanistic interpretation. Structured depth routing emerges only when routing is part of training. Descriptive routing summaries must be treated as candidate hypotheses requiring causal validation, not as mechanistic evidence on their own.

Abstract: Block Attention Residuals (Block AttnRes) by replace fixed additive residuals with a learned softmax over earlier depth-source representations, surfacing cross-layer routing as an inspectable tensor in the forward pass. This is a tempting interpretability target: information flow normally inferred indirectly is now directly observable. We ask whether such exposure suffices for mechanistic interpretation. We probe two same-scale ($0.6$B) Block AttnRes checkpoints under identical routing-ablation interventions: a vanilla Qwen3 inference-wrapped through a deterministic recency-bias schedule that the codebase admits as a routing-equivalent loading path, and a Block AttnRes Qwen3 trained from scratch with routing as part of optimisation. The wrapped baseline's routing weights are content-independent and reproduce the schedule's analytic prediction. The trained AttnRes checkpoint instead exhibits three localised routing motifs: an embedding-source pathway through early-layer MLP, a current-state pathway through early-layer attention and MLP, and an older-history pathway through late-layer attention. Beyond this stratification, we find a sharp dissociation between average routing mass and causal importance: in both sublayers, the largest mass slice is not the largest causal contribution, and one source family carries appreciable mass with no detectable causal role under intervention. Architectural exposure of routing is therefore necessary but not sufficient for mechanistic interpretation: structured depth routing emerges only when routing has been part of training, and even then, descriptive routing summaries should be treated as candidate hypotheses to be tested by causal interventions, not as evidence of mechanism in their own right.

</details>


### [148] [Detecting Explanatory Insufficiency in Learned Representations: A Framework for Representational Vigilance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13172)
*Jacques Raynal,Pierre Slangen,Elsa Raynal,Jacques Margerit*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: VER is a conceptual diagnostic framework that monitors learned representations for persistent residual structures — patterns that evade standard evaluation metrics — to identify representational inadequacy as distinct from prediction error, noise, or distribution shift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Learned representations can appear operationally successful under conventional metrics (accuracy, robustness, etc.) while harboring persistent, unexplained residual structures that signal deeper explanatory insufficiency. Existing evaluation paradigms conflate these structural inadequacies with ordinary error, uncertainty, or noise, leaving a blind spot in representation quality assessment.

Method: VER formalizes a five-stage monitoring sequence: (1) representation identification — specifying the target representation; (2) explanatory-domain delimitation — defining the scope of what the representation purports to explain; (3) residual-structure detection — identifying non-random patterns in residuals; (4) explanatory-resistance evaluation — assessing whether these structures persistently resist the representation's explanatory capacity; and (5) vigilance signaling — issuing diagnostic alerts when inadequacy is detected. The framework is purely diagnostic and does not introduce new algorithms, losses, or architectures.

Result: This is a conceptual/theoretical paper; no empirical results are reported. The authors outline a path toward empirical evaluation through representational-vigilance benchmarks as future work.

Conclusion: VER treats representational adequacy as a distinct object of inquiry, complementary to existing evaluation methods. By distinguishing inadequacy from prediction error, uncertainty, noise, and distribution shift, it provides a structured lens for diagnosing when and how representations fail to capture underlying structure.

Abstract: Learned representations are central to modern machine learning and are commonly evaluated through predictive performance, robustness, uncertainty estimation, or generalization. However, a learned representation may remain operationally successful while progressively failing to organize persistent residual structures that are not fully captured by conventional evaluation metrics. This article introduces VER, the Vigilant Evaluator of Representations, a conceptual framework for monitoring representational adequacy in learned representations. VER does not propose a new learning algorithm, loss function, or model architecture. Instead, it formalizes a diagnostic process through which persistent residual structures may be identified, analyzed, and interpreted as potential indicators of explanatory insufficiency. The framework distinguishes representational inadequacy from ordinary prediction error, uncertainty, noise, and distribution shift. It introduces a monitoring sequence based on representation identification, explanatory-domain delimitation, residual-structure detection, explanatory-resistance evaluation, and vigilance signaling. VER is intended as a contribution to representation diagnostics in machine learning. Its objective is not to replace existing evaluation methods but to complement them by treating representational adequacy as an explicit object of inquiry. A path toward empirical evaluation through representational-vigilance benchmarks is also outlined.

</details>


### [149] [Getting Better at Working With You: Compiling User Corrections into Runtime Enforcement for Coding Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13174)
*Yujun Zhou,Kehan Guo,Haomin Zhuang,Xiangqi Wang,Yue Huang,Zhenwen Liang,Pin-Yu Chen,Tian Gao,Nuno Moniz,Nitesh V. Chawla,Xiangliang Zhang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TRACE mines user corrections from chat interactions, rewrites them as atomic rules, and compiles them into runtime checks to enforce preference compliance across sessions. It reduces preference violations from 100% to 2–37.6% on coding tasks, addressing a failure mode that memory-only approaches like Mem0 (57.5% violation rate) cannot reliably solve.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interactive LLM agents fail to reliably retain and comply with user preferences across sessions — a correction made in one session is often violated in the next. This gap between preference access (remembering) and preference compliance (acting on it) creates repeated user friction that memory-alone approaches do not adequately address.

Method: TRACE is a drop-in skill-layer pipeline for coding-agent runtimes with three stages: (1) mining user corrections from chat history, (2) rewriting them as atomic, executable rules, and (3) compiling those rules into runtime checks that gate agent task completion. These checks are sourced from the user's own corrections rather than pre-written by developers.

Result: On ClawArena coding tasks, TRACE reduces held-out preference violations from 100% to 37.6% (in-distribution) and 100% to 2.0% (out-of-distribution). On MemoryArena-derived tasks, it reduces in-distribution violations from 100% to 60.5% while matching or exceeding the strongest memory baseline on task pass rate. Mem0 alone leaves 57.5% of preference checks violated.

Conclusion: Compiling user corrections into runtime enforcement rules addresses a repeated-friction failure mode that memory alone does not reliably solve, reducing the need for users to restate corrections across future sessions.

Abstract: Interactive LLM agents are becoming part of daily work, but they do not reliably become easier to work with over time: a correction remembered in one session may still be violated in the next. We study this gap between preference access and preference compliance. In tasks derived from anonymized real-user friction cases, Mem0 memory still leaves 57.5% of applicable preference checks violated. We introduce Test-time Rule Acquisition and Compiled Enforcement (TRACE), a drop-in skill-layer pipeline for coding-agent runtimes that mines user corrections, rewrites them as atomic rules, and compiles them into runtime checks that must pass before an agent completes future tasks. Unlike runtime checks written ahead of time by developers, TRACE skills come from the user's own chat corrections. We evaluate TRACE with simulated user-in-the-loop experiments on ClawArena coding-agent tasks and MemoryArena-derived memory-intensive tasks. On ClawArena, TRACE reduces held-out preference violation from 100.0% to 37.6% on in-distribution tasks and from 100.0% to 2.0% on out-of-distribution tasks. On MemoryArena-derived tasks, TRACE reduces in-distribution violation from 100.0% to 60.5% while matching or exceeding the strongest memory baseline on task pass. These results suggest that compiling corrections into runtime enforcement can address a repeated-friction failure mode that memory alone does not reliably solve, reducing the need for users to restate the same correction across future sessions. Experiment code is available at https://github.com/YujunZhou/TRACE_exp, and the deployable skill is available at https://github.com/YujunZhou/tellonce.

</details>


### [150] [Understanding helpfulness and harmless tension in reward models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13209)
*Eshaan Tanwar,Pepa Atanasova*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Reward models trained with mixed helpfulness and harmlessness objectives exhibit alignment tension due to shared neurons that causally support one objective while impairing the other, explaining why multi-objective RLHF underperforms single-objective training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the internal mechanisms and conflicts between helpfulness and harmlessness objectives in reward models used for RLHF, moving beyond treating them as black boxes.

Method: Train reward models under three conditions (helpfulness-only, harmlessness-only, mixed-objective); use activation-based neuron identification and targeted ablation studies to determine causal roles of individual neurons for each objective; analyze the proportion and influence of shared vs. objective-specific neurons.

Result: Mixed-objective models underperform single-objective ones, confirming objective interference. Identified neurons causally support their corresponding objective while negatively affecting the opposing one. A substantial proportion of neurons are shared between objectives, and these shared neurons disproportionately influence behavior, driving alignment tension.

Conclusion: Alignment tension arises from shared neural representations that cannot simultaneously optimize both objectives effectively. This mechanistic understanding motivates future work on disentangled and controllable multi-objective alignment methods.

Abstract: Reward models are a key component of reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), aligning language models toward both helpful and harmless behaviour. However, the internal mechanisms underlying these objectives and their conflicts remain poorly understood. We study alignment tension in reward models trained under helpfulness-only, harmlessness-only, and mixed-objective settings. We find that mixed-objective models often underperform single-objective models, indicating interference between objectives. Using activation-based methods, we identify neurons associated with each objective and study their functional roles via targeted ablations. We find that these neurons causally support their corresponding objectives while often negatively affecting the opposing one. We find that a substantial proportion of neurons are shared between helpfulness and harmlessness, and that these shared neurons exert a disproportionate influence on model behaviour, contributing to alignment tension. Additionally, our results provide insights and mechanistic interpretation into how alignment objectives are represented in reward models and why multi-objective alignment remains challenging, motivating future work on disentangled and controllable alignment methods.

</details>


### [151] [Loss-Shift Transfer via Bayes Quotients](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13178)
*Vasileios Sevetlidis*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces "loss shift," a failure mode distinct from distribution shift, where the data distribution is fixed but the loss function changes. Using Bayes quotients, the authors formalize how a representation optimal for a coarser loss may be insufficient for a strictly finer loss, and for log loss they derive an exact quantitative identity equating excess risk to discarded conditional information.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transfer learning is typically studied under distribution shift. The authors identify an orthogonal and underexplored failure mode: even with a fixed joint distribution P(X,Y), changing the loss function can render a previously optimal representation inadequate, because different losses deem different information in X as Bayes-relevant.

Method: The paper formalizes loss shift using Bayes quotients, which order losses by refinement. A loss L₂ refines L₁ if Bayes optimality for L₂ implies Bayes optimality for L₁. For finite-output log loss, the excess risk is shown to equal the conditional information about Y discarded by the representation. Experiments across controlled, learned, synthetic-image, and real-image settings validate the theory.

Result: Strict refinement provides a qualitative obstruction: a source-minimal representation for a coarser loss is insufficient for a strictly finer target loss. For log loss, this becomes an exact identity. Experiments confirm that classification-equivalent representations can exhibit dramatically different log-loss performance under a fixed data distribution.

Conclusion: Loss shift is a fundamental failure mode in transfer learning orthogonal to distribution shift. The Bayes-quotient framework provides both qualitative and quantitative tools for understanding when and why representations fail to transfer across different loss functions, even under identical data distributions.

Abstract: Transfer learning is usually studied as a consequence of distribution shift. This paper identifies an orthogonal failure mode in which the data distribution is fixed and the loss changes. This setting is called \emph{loss shift}. A loss determines which information in \(X\) is Bayes-relevant, and two losses may therefore require different representations even under the same joint law \(P(X,Y)\). The idea is formalized using Bayes quotients, which allow losses to be ordered by refinement. In the Bayes-quotient formulation, strict refinement gives an immediate qualitative obstruction. A source-minimal representation for a coarser loss is insufficient for a strictly finer target loss. For finite-output log loss, this obstruction becomes an exact quantitative identity. The excess risk is the conditional information about \(Y\) discarded by the representation. Experiments in controlled, learned, synthetic-image, and real-image settings show the predicted effect, i.e., classification-equivalent representations can have different optimal log-loss performance under a fixed data distribution.

</details>


### [152] [The Geometry of Phase Transitions in Generative Dynamics via Projection Caustics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13191)
*Ryosuke Sakamoto,Kotaro Sakamoto*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Diffusion and flow-matching models exhibit abrupt, phase-transition-like behaviour during generation. The authors develop a geometric theory linking these sharp transitions to projection caustics — regions where the nearest-point projection onto the data manifold loses uniqueness. They introduce the Critical Boundary Detector (CBD), a practical diagnostic for score-direction instability, and demonstrate its utility across toy models, standard diffusion, and latent text-to-image diffusion models for localising mode commitment, predicting sensitive intervention windows, and enabling targeted control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continuous-state generative models like diffusion and flow-matching models evolve through smooth reverse-time dynamics, yet their samples display sudden qualitative changes — mode commitment, semantic collapse, and high sensitivity to perturbations in narrow time windows. Understanding and predicting this phase-transition-like behaviour is crucial for both theoretical insight into generative dynamics and practical control over sampling.

Method: The authors reframe denoising as gradient descent on a free energy landscape and invoke differential geometry to show that sharp transitions occur near projection caustics — loci where the nearest-point projection onto the data support becomes non-unique (i.e., points equidistant from multiple data regions). They then propose the Critical Boundary Detector (CBD), a diagnostic tool that identifies regions of score-direction instability by detecting where the score field exhibits sharp directional changes, signalling proximity to these geometric boundaries.

Result: Across toy models, standard diffusion models, and latent text-to-image diffusion models, CBD successfully (1) localises where mode commitment occurs in the generative process, (2) predicts time windows during which interventions are maximally effective, and (3) supports targeted control by identifying geometrically sensitive regions where small perturbations yield large downstream effects.

Conclusion: The work establishes a principled connection between the geometry of the data manifold and the dynamics of diffusion-based generation, showing that phase-transition-like behaviour in generative sampling is a geometric phenomenon rooted in projection caustics. The CBD provides a practical tool for diagnosing and exploiting this behaviour in real-world generative models.

Abstract: Continuous-state generative samplers, including diffusion and flow-matching models, evolve through continuous reverse-time dynamics, yet their samples often undergo abrupt qualitative changes: trajectories commit to modes, semantic alternatives collapse, and small perturbations in narrow time windows can produce large downstream effects. This paper develops a geometric account of such phase-transition-like behaviour. We view denoising as gradient descent on a free energy landscape and show that sharp transitions arise near projection caustics, where the nearest-point projection onto the data support ceases to be unique. Motivated by this perspective, we introduce the Critical Boundary Detector (CBD), as practical diagnostics for score-direction instability. Across toy models, standard diffusion models, and latent text-to-image diffusion models, CBD localises mode commitment, predicts intervention-sensitive windows, and supports targeted control in geometrically sensitive regions. Our results connect geometry of data and dynamics of diffusion generation.

</details>


### [153] [WHAR Arena: Benchmarking the State of the Art in Efficient Wearable Human Activity Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13194)
*Maximilian Burzer,Tobias King,Till Riedel,Michael Beigl,Tobias Röddiger*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A large-scale benchmark of 17 deep learning architectures across 30 WHAR datasets reveals that predictive performance has plateaued, with top models clustering tightly. Compact models like TinierHAR and Random Forests dominate the accuracy-efficiency Pareto frontier, while larger recurrent/hybrid models incur high costs without gains. Future progress lies in deployment efficiency and domain adaptation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Wearable Human Activity Recognition (WHAR) suffers from a comparability crisis: inconsistent datasets, custom preprocessing, and varying evaluation protocols make state-of-the-art claims unreliable and fragile. A standardized, large-scale benchmark is needed to rigorously assess where the field truly stands.

Method: The authors built an open-source benchmark integrating 30 diverse WHAR datasets under unified preprocessing, standardized model interfaces, and a shared cross-subject evaluation protocol. They evaluated 17 representative architectures across 4,760 training runs, measuring both predictive performance (macro-F1) and on-device deployment metrics (latency, peak memory, model size) on an Android reference device.

Result: No single architecture dominates WHAR. CNN-HAR achieves the highest mean macro-F1, but top models cluster tightly, suggesting a predictive performance ceiling has been reached. When jointly considering deployment efficiency, compact neural models (TinierHAR) and classical Random Forests define the Pareto frontier; larger recurrent and hybrid models incur disproportionate hardware costs without commensurate accuracy gains.

Conclusion: Predictive performance in WHAR has largely plateaued across contemporary architectures. The most promising avenues for future research are optimizing deployment efficiency for resource-constrained devices and improving model robustness to domain shifts, rather than pursuing marginal accuracy improvements through architectural innovation alone.

Abstract: Deep learning has become the dominant paradigm in Wearable Human Activity Recognition (WHAR), yet progress is obscured by a comparability crisis. Results are often reported using inconsistent datasets, custom data processing, and varying evaluation protocols, making state-of-the-art claims fragile. We address this with a large-scale, open-source benchmark that integrates 30 diverse datasets under standardized processing, unified model interfaces, and a shared cross-subject evaluation protocol. Evaluating 17 representative architectures across 4760 training runs, we jointly measure predictive performance alongside on-device latency, peak memory, and model size on an Android reference device. Our results reveal that the WHAR state of the art is distributed rather than dominated by a single architecture. While CNN-HAR achieves the highest mean macro-F1, top-performing models cluster tightly, indicating contemporary architectures have converged near a predictive performance ceiling. When accounting for deployment efficiency, compact neural models, such as TinierHAR, and classical Random Forests define the practically relevant Pareto frontier, whereas larger recurrent and hybrid models incur high hardware costs without corresponding performance gains. Consequently, while predictive performance has plateaued, substantial potential for future progress remains in optimizing deployment efficiency and improving adaptation to domain shifts. We release our full framework to support transparent reuse and extension.

</details>


### [154] [MaxProof: Scaling Mathematical Proof with Generative-Verifier RL and Population-Level Test-Time Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13473)
*Jiacheng Chen,Xinyu Zhang,Shunkai Zhang,Yanmohan Wang,Lin Li,Tiancheng Qin,Qin Wang,Zhengmao Zhu,Tianle Li,Jingyang Li,Zehan Li,Binyang Jiang,Jin Zhu,Han Ding,Fei Yu,Chenyu Du,Zijian Song,Jiayuan Song,Zhi Zhang,Yunan Huang,Weiyu Cheng,Pengyu Zhao,Yu Cheng*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: MiniMax-M3 uses a test-time scaling framework called MaxProof that combines proof generation, verification, repair, and tournament selection to achieve gold-medal-level performance on IMO 2025 (35/42) and USAMO 2026 (36/42).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Competition-level mathematical proof requires both correctness and rigor. Single-pass generation often yields proofs with subtle errors. The authors aim to overcome this by scaling test-time compute through a population-based search with integrated verification and repair, moving beyond single-model generation toward a self-improving proof pipeline.

Method: The M3 model is trained on three capabilities: (1) proof generation, (2) proof verification (via a defense-in-depth generative verifier designed for low false-positive rate), and (3) critique-conditioned proof repair. These are merged into a single model. At test time, MaxProof treats the model as a generator, verifier, refiner, and ranker. It maintains a population of candidate proofs, iteratively applies verification and repair, and selects the final proof via tournament selection, enabling test-time compute scaling.

Result: The M3 model with MaxProof test-time scaling achieves 35/42 on IMO 2025 and 36/42 on USAMO 2026, surpassing the human gold-medal threshold on both benchmarks.

Conclusion: Population-level test-time scaling, where a single model serves multiple cooperative roles (generator, verifier, refiner, ranker) with tournament selection, is a highly effective strategy for formal mathematical proof. The approach demonstrates that multi-capability training combined with structured search at inference can yield super-human results on prestigious mathematical olympiads.

Abstract: We present MaxProof, a population-level test-time scaling framework for competition-level mathematical proof in the MiniMax-M3 series. M3 first trains three proof-oriented capabilities -- proof generation, proof verification, and critique-conditioned proof repair -- using a defense-in-depth generative verifier engineered for low false-positive rate. These capabilities are merged into a single released M3 model. At test time, MaxProof treats the model as a generator, verifier, refiner, and ranker, searches over a population of candidate proofs, and returns one final proof through tournament selection. With MaxProof test-time scaling, the M3 model reaches 35/42 on IMO 2025 and 36/42 on USAMO 2026, exceeding the human gold-medal threshold on both.

</details>


### [155] [SupraBench: A Benchmark for Supramolecular Chemistry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13477)
*Tianyi Ma,Yijun Ma,Zehong Wang,Weixiang Sun,Ziming Li,Connor R. Schmidt,Chuxu Zhang,Matthew J. Webber,Yanfang Ye*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SupraBench is the first systematic benchmark for evaluating LLMs on supramolecular chemistry reasoning across four core tasks (binding affinity prediction, top-binder selection, solvent identification, host-guest description) plus a vision-based task, accompanied by SupraPMC, a 16M-token domain-specific corpus. Results show LLMs have substantial room for improvement, with distinct failure modes across task families.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Designing host-guest systems in supramolecular chemistry is time-consuming (days per candidate pair). LLMs offer a faster alternative but lack a systematic benchmark to evaluate their reasoning on fundamental supramolecular chemistry tasks. This gap motivates the creation of SupraBench.

Method: The authors collaborated with domain experts to design SupraBench with four fundamental tasks: binding affinity prediction, top-binder selection, solvent identification, and host-guest description, plus an auxiliary vision-based molecular identification task. They also curated SupraPMC, a 16M-token corpus from Europe PMC articles, for domain-adaptive pretraining. A broad range of open and proprietary LLMs were benchmarked.

Result: LLMs leave substantial headroom across all tasks. Domain adaptation pretraining on SupraPMC transfers effectively to in-distribution regression but negatively impacts strict letter-format output. The difficulty profile differs sharply across task families, revealing distinct failure modes that highlight specific gaps in current supramolecular chemistry reasoning capabilities.

Conclusion: SupraBench establishes a foundation for systematically evaluating and improving LLM reasoning in supramolecular chemistry. The benchmark, dataset, and identified failure modes provide clear directions for future model development, with code and data publicly released.

Abstract: Supramolecular chemistry, which includes the study of non-covalent host-guest assemblies, has advanced various applications. However, designing host-guest systems remains time-consuming, requiring days of dry-lab verification per candidate pair. Although LLMs have emerged as a fast alternative with strong performance on molecular binding tasks, no benchmark currently systematically evaluates LLMs for host-guest reasoning across fundamental supramolecular chemistry tasks, e.g., binding affinity prediction. To this end, we collaborate with domain experts to release the first Supramolecular Benchmark, called SupraBench, to evaluate LLMs in chemistry reasoning. Specifically, we design four fundamental tasks, i.e., binding affinity prediction, top-binder selection, solvent identification, and host-guest description, plus an auxiliary vision-based task for molecular identification. We also release SupraPMC, a curated 16M-token corpus of Supramolecular chemistry articles distilled from Europe PMC, to support the adaptation to the supramolecular domain. We benchmark a broad range of open and proprietary LLMs and find that LLMs leave substantial headroom across all tasks. Domain adaptation pretraining over SupraPMC transfers cleanly to in-distribution regression but trades off against strict letter-format output. Moreover, the difficulty profile differs sharply across task families, revealing distinct failure modes that indicate specific gaps in current supramolecular chemistry reasoning. Our source codes and benchmark datasets are available at https://github.com/Tianyi-Billy-Ma/SupraBench.

</details>


### [156] [From Uncertain Judgments to Calibrated Rankings: Conformal Elo Estimation for LLM Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13221)
*Bora Kargi,David Salinas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes SoftElo, a two-layer calibration framework that reduces systematic errors in LLM-as-a-judge evaluations by propagating win probabilities (instead of hard labels) through the Bradley-Terry model and applying conformal prediction for uncertainty bounds, achieving Elo ratings within 17.9 MAE of human judgments without requiring costly human annotation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-as-a-judge is a cost-effective alternative to human evaluation for ranking models, but suffers from systematic biases (position bias, self-preference, intransitivity) that miscalibrate rankings. The authors aim to quantify and correct this judge-human disagreement to provide reliable, low-cost evaluation with honest uncertainty estimates.

Method: A two-layer approach: (1) Local layer — instead of using hard binary outcomes in the Bradley-Terry model, the authors propagate calibrated win probabilities derived from the judge's own score differences, capturing per-battle uncertainty. (2) Global layer — split conformal prediction is applied to the residual gap between LLM-derived and human-derived Elo ratings across held-out models, producing distribution-free prediction intervals with marginal coverage guarantees. Evaluated on 55 held-out models from LMArena.

Result: The local calibration alone brings LLM-derived Elo ratings within 17.9 Elo MAE of human-derived ratings on 55 held-out LMArena models. The global conformal prediction layer provides valid prediction intervals that account for irreducible LLM-human disagreement.

Conclusion: The two-layer SoftElo framework enables developers to obtain calibrated Elo estimates with honest uncertainty bounds at low cost, without large-scale human annotation. Code is released for reproducibility.

Abstract: Evaluating new large language models typically requires costly human annotation campaigns at scale. LLM-as-a-judge offers a cheaper alternative, but judge scores carry systematic errors - such as position bias, self-preference, or intransitivity - that can strongly miscalibrate the resulting rankings. We quantify the resulting judge-human disagreement at two complementary levels. At the local level, we estimate per-battle uncertainty from the judge's own score differences by propagating calibrated win probabilities rather than hard labels into the Bradley-Terry procedure. This alone provides a drastic improvement to Elo estimation accuracy, bringing LLM-derived ratings within 17.9 Elo MAE of human-derived ones when averaged over 55 held-out models on LMArena. At the global level, we apply split conformal prediction to the residual gap between LLM-derived and human-derived Elo ratings across held-out models, producing prediction intervals with distribution-free marginal coverage guarantees that account for irreducible LLM-human disagreement. Together, these two layers yield a low-cost evaluation tool that provides developers with calibrated Elo estimates and honest uncertainty bounds, without access to large-scale human annotations.To facilitate reproducibility, we release our code at https://github.com/kargibora/SoftElo .

</details>


### [157] [Distributional Loss for Robust Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13223)
*Kathleen Anderson,Thomas Martinetz*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes a bimodal Gaussian loss for classification that replaces hard one-hot targets with a soft, distribution-based objective, improving robustness and mitigating overfitting, especially in low-data settings, without needing extra labels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard supervised classification enforces a strict one-hot mapping per sample, which ignores inherent class ambiguity, promotes overconfident predictions, and yields brittle decision boundaries. The authors aim to address these shortcomings without requiring additional label information or complex pipeline changes.

Method: Instead of a direct input-to-label mapping, the method models the target distribution over all classifier outputs as a bimodal Gaussian — one mode for the correct class and another for the remaining classes. The optimization objective encourages the classifier's output distribution to match this softer, bimodal target, implicitly encoding class relationships and penalizing overconfident incorrect predictions.

Result: The proposed loss yields consistent robustness improvements across experiments, with particularly significant gains in low-data regimes. These benefits are achieved with only minimal modifications to standard training pipelines.

Conclusion: Softening classification targets via a bimodal Gaussian formulation is an effective, low-overhead strategy for learning more robust and well-calibrated decision boundaries, especially when labeled data is scarce.

Abstract: This paper proposes a novel loss concept for supervised classification tasks. Rather than enforcing a direct mapping from each input sample to a single assigned label, we define an optimization objective over all classifier outputs as a bimodal Gaussian distribution. This softer target formulation implicitly captures class ambiguity, mitigates overfitting, and encourages the learning of more robust decision boundaries, all without requiring additional label information. Experimental results demonstrate consistent improvements in robustness, with particularly pronounced gains in low-data regimes, while requiring only minimal modifications to standard training pipelines.

</details>


### [158] [ReSET: Accurate Latency-Critical NVFP4 Reasoning via Step-Aware Temperature Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13233)
*Sihwa Lee,Janghwan Lee,Donghoon Yoo,Jae Gon Kim,Hanyul Ryu,Soojung Ryu,Jungwook Choi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ReSET proposes an entropy-based temperature-scaling method that adapts decoding temperature using both token-level and step-level entropy signals to mitigate NVFP4 quantization-induced reasoning degradation in large reasoning models, alongside a CUDA-core small-M NVFP4 kernel for latency-critical autoregressive decoding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large reasoning models (LRMs) improve complex problem-solving via long reasoning traces but incur high inference costs. While NVFP4 (low-precision floating-point 4) reduces computation and memory, it degrades reasoning accuracy and existing NVFP4 kernels fail to deliver latency benefits for small-batch autoregressive decoding — a critical bottleneck in LRM inference.

Method: The authors first analyze NVFP4 quantization effects on token-level uncertainty, identifying two failure modes: (1) incorrect sampling at low-entropy symbolic tokens, and (2) over-concentration on a small token set in high-uncertainty reasoning steps. They then propose ReSET, which estimates step-level uncertainty online and adapts decoding temperature using both token-level entropy and step-level entropy signals (reasoning-step entropy-based temperature scaling). Additionally, they design a CUDA-core small-M NVFP4 kernel optimized for latency-critical autoregressive decoding with small batch sizes.

Result: ReSET improves NVFP4 reasoning accuracy by up to ~2 points over the NVFP4 baseline across reasoning benchmarks and model scales. The CUDA-core small-M kernel achieves up to 2.5× kernel-level speedup over NVFP4 vLLM and approximately 2× end-to-end decoding speedup over BF16.

Conclusion: Combining online entropy-based temperature scaling with hardware-efficient NVFP4 kernels effectively bridges the accuracy-efficiency gap in low-precision LRM inference, enabling practical deployment of quantized reasoning models without sacrificing reasoning quality.

Abstract: Large reasoning models (LRMs) improve complex problem-solving by generating long intermediate reasoning traces, but this substantially increases inference costs. NVFP4 inference offers a promising approach to reduce both computational and memory costs through hardware-supported low-precision execution. However, directly applying NVFP4 to LRMs introduces two practical limitations: reasoning accuracy degrades under quantization, and existing NVFP4 kernels do not fully realize latency benefits in small-batch autoregressive decoding. In this work, we analyze the effect of NVFP4 quantization on token-level uncertainty during reasoning. We show that quantization increases incorrect sampling at low-entropy symbolic tokens, while causing over-concentration on a small set of tokens in high-uncertainty reasoning steps. Based on this observation, we propose \textbf{ReSET}, a reasoning-step entropy-based temperature-scaling method that estimates step-level uncertainty online and adapts the decoding temperature using both token-level and step-level entropy signals. To address the latency gap, we further design a CUDA-core small-$M$ NVFP4 kernel for latency-critical autoregressive decoding. Across reasoning benchmarks and model scales, ReSET improves NVFP4 reasoning accuracy by up to $\sim\!$2 points over the NVFP4 baseline. Our CUDA-core small-$M$ kernel further improves latency-critical decoding, delivering up to $2.5\!\times$ kernel-level speedup over NVFP4 vLLM and approximately $2\!\times$ end-to-end decoding speedup over BF16. Code is available at https://github.com/aiha-lab/ReSET.

</details>


### [159] [Beyond the Commitment Boundary: Probing Epiphenomenal Chain-of-Thought in Large Reasoning Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13603)
*Daniel Scalena,Sara Candussio,Luca Bortolussi,Elisabetta Fersini,Malvina Nissim,Gabriele Sarti*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Chain-of-thought reasoning exhibits a sharp "commitment boundary" where the final answer stabilizes early, followed by superfluous steps. This signal enables early-exiting CoT reasoning, reducing length by up to 55% without performance degradation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite the widespread use of chain-of-thought reasoning for inference-time scaling, the causal role of individual reasoning steps remains poorly understood. The authors aim to characterize how answers form across reasoning traces and whether all steps meaningfully contribute to the final outcome.

Method: The authors estimate each step's causal importance using early exit (measuring the probability of the final answer at each intermediate step). They identify a "commitment boundary" — a sharp transition where answer probability jumps from low-confidence guesses to a stable high-confidence answer. Attention probes are used to linearly decode answer-formation stages from intermediate hidden states. This signal is exploited to truncate reasoning at the commitment boundary.

Result: Across diverse tasks and model families, reasoning consistently exhibits a commitment boundary occurring well before the reasoning block ends, often in a single step. Subsequent "epiphenomenal" steps leave the final answer probability unchanged. Answer-formation stages are linearly decodable with high accuracy and generalize to unseen tasks. Early-exiting at the commitment boundary reduces CoT length by up to 55% on average with negligible performance impact.

Conclusion: CoT reasoning contains a predictable commitment point after which further steps are largely redundant. This insight enables practical inference-time efficiency gains through early stopping without sacrificing accuracy, and provides a mechanistic understanding of how answers crystallize during chain-of-thought generation.

Abstract: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning is the dominant paradigm for inference-time scaling in language models, yet the causal influence of individual steps on the final answer poorly understood. We estimate each step's causal importance via early exit and use this measure to study how answers form across the reasoning traces of several model families. Across diverse tasks, we find that reasoning typically crosses a \emph{commitment boundary} -- a sharp transition from transient intermediate guesses to a stable, high-confidence answer. This transition often happens in a single step, well before the model's reasoning block ends, and is followed by \emph{epiphenomenal} CoT steps that leave the final answer probability unaltered. Using attention probes, we show that answer-formation stages can be linearly decoded from intermediate reasoning steps with high accuracy and generalize robustly to unseen reasoning tasks. We exploit this signal to early-exit reasoning blocks at the commitment boundary, reducing the length of CoTs up to 55\% on average with negligible impact on model performance.

</details>


### [160] [Decoding Insect Song: A Multitask Semisupervised Orthoptera Bioacoustic Classifier](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13236)
*Olga Isupova,Danil Kuzin,Ella Browning,Tom Mills,Steven Reece*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PULSE is a semi-supervised multi-task framework for Orthoptera bioacoustics that combines weakly-supervised classification, self-supervised learning, and knowledge distillation, significantly outperforming general-purpose models while enabling ecological discovery through learned embeddings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing automated acoustic monitoring tools are narrowly trained and non-transferable, limiting their utility for ecological inference. There is a need for models that can leverage both labelled and unlabelled field data, adapt to specific taxonomic domains, and reveal ecologically meaningful structure beyond mere classification.

Method: PULSE employs a semi-supervised, multi-task learning framework integrating three components: (1) weakly-supervised species classification, (2) self-supervised learning on unlabelled field audio, and (3) knowledge distillation from a general-purpose bioacoustic model. The framework also incorporates active learning to iteratively improve performance. Learned embeddings are made explorable via an interactive visualization tool.

Result: The domain-adapted PULSE model substantially outperformed a state-of-the-art general bioacoustic model across all metrics: macro F1 (0.21 vs. 0.07), AUC (0.74 vs. 0.45), and AP (0.32 vs. 0.19). With active learning, performance rose further to F1=0.34 and AUC=0.84. The learned embeddings also captured ecologically meaningful structure.

Conclusion: Domain-adapted specialist models trained with semi-supervised multi-task learning and knowledge distillation can dramatically surpass general-purpose models for bioacoustic monitoring. The combination of improved classification and interpretable embeddings offers a powerful paradigm for ecological inference from passive acoustic data.

Abstract: Passive acoustic monitoring holds great promise for ecological inference, yet existing automated tools are typically narrowly trained and non-transferable. We address these limitations with PULSE, a semi-supervised, multi-task framework for Orthoptera bioacoustics, combining weakly-supervised species classification, self-supervised learning on unlabelled field audio, and knowledge distillation from a general-purpose bioacoustic model. Our domain-adapted specialist model outperforms a state-of-the-art general model across all metrics (macro F1: 0.21 vs. 0.07; AUC: 0.74 vs. 0.45; AP: 0.32 vs. 0.19), with active learning further raising F1 to 0.34 and AUC to 0.84. Beyond classification, the learned embeddings encode ecologically meaningful structure, exposed through an interactive visualisation tool for ecological discovery.

</details>


### [161] [Towards More General Control of Diffusion Models Using Jeffrey Guidance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13240)
*Raphaël Razafindralambo,Rémy Sun,Frédéric Precioso,Jes Frellsen,Pierre-Alexandre Mattei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Jeffrey guidance is a principled framework for diffusion model control that leverages Jeffrey's rule of conditioning to update marginal distributions toward a prescribed target while minimally perturbing the joint distribution. It extends beyond standard guidance, demonstrated via targeted embedding distributions (improving FID on CIFAR-10 and FFHQ) and fairness applications (enforcing attribute independence on CelebA-HQ).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
