Abstract: Catastrophic forgetting is often viewed as the destruction of previously learned knowledge during sequential learning. Building on the Accessibility Collapse framework, we investigate the geometric structure of recoverability in continual learning. Using Split CIFAR-100 and a sequentially trained ResNet-18, we analyze recoverability, representational drift, and recovery complexity across ten tasks. We introduce Recovery Subspace Dimensionality (k_t), a measure of the minimum number of singular directions required to preserve 90 percent of full probe performance. Contrary to our Recoverability Diffusion hypothesis, recovery dimensionality remains stable throughout training (mean k_t = 8.0) despite substantial representational drift. Principal-angle drift strongly predicts recoverability (r = -0.862), and a simple geometric model explains 82.2 percent of recoverability variance. These findings support the Stable Recovery Manifold hypothesis, suggesting that forgotten knowledge remains compactly decodable despite representational reorganization. The results indicate that catastrophic forgetting is primarily an accessibility and manifold-alignment problem rather than information destruction.

</details>


### [187] [Dense Supervision, Sparse Updates: On the Sparsity and Geometry of On-Policy Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13657)
*Guo Yu,Wenlin Liu,Yulan Hu,Hao-Xuan Ma,Jun-Peng Jiang,Han-Jia Ye*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: OPD produces small, coordinate-sparse, FFN-heavy updates that are geometrically full-rank but spectrally concentrated, lying away from principal singular subspaces and targeting near-zero source weights. Sparse subnetworks recover near-full OPD performance, but AdamW remains necessary due to heterogeneous gradient scales from dense supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: On-policy distillation (OPD) combines on-policy student trajectories with dense teacher supervision, but the mechanism by which this hybrid approach alters model parameters is not well understood. The authors aim to characterize the structural and geometric properties of OPD updates.

Method: The authors analyze OPD across multiple language and vision-language model pairs and tasks. They examine update sparsity (coordinate-level distribution across layers/modules), conduct subnetwork training experiments (training only discovered sparse subsets), perform optimizer ablations (SGD vs. AdamW), and study geometric properties via singular value decomposition, comparing update directions to principal singular subspaces of source weights and analyzing the relationship to source weight magnitudes.

Result: Key findings: (1) OPD updates are small and coordinate-sparse, distributed across layers with FFN-heavy emphasis; training only the discovered sparse subnetwork recovers nearly full OPD performance. (2) SGD underperforms AdamW, likely because dense supervision preserves heterogeneous coordinate-wise gradient scales where adaptive scaling helps. (3) Geometrically, updates are numerically full-rank but spectrally concentrated, lie mostly away from principal singular subspaces of source weights, and fall disproportionately on coordinates where source weights are near zero.

Conclusion: Dense teacher supervision does not turn OPD into ordinary dense parameter rewriting. Instead, OPD retains important geometric signatures characteristic of on-policy post-training, suggesting it operates through a distinct mechanism that combines sparse structural updates with specific geometric preferences.

Abstract: On-policy distillation (\textsc{OPD}) has recently become a prominent post-training recipe as it combines two desirable ingredients: on-policy student trajectories and dense teacher supervision, yet how this hybrid changes a model's parameters remains unclear. Across several language and vision-language model pairs and use cases, our analysis yields two main findings. On sparsity, \textsc{OPD}-style updates are small and coordinate-sparse. They are distributed across layers and are usually FFN-heavy. This sparse structure is operationally useful: training only the discovered subnetwork recovers nearly the same performance as full \textsc{OPD}. However, the sparsity-inducing SGD optimizer underperforms AdamW in our optimizer ablation, likely because dense teacher supervision preserves heterogeneous coordinate-wise gradient scales where AdamW's adaptive scaling remains useful. On geometry, the updates are numerically full-rank but spectrally concentrated; they lie mostly away from the principal singular subspaces of the source weights and fall disproportionately on coordinates where the source weights are close to zero. These findings suggest that dense teacher supervision does not turn \textsc{OPD} into ordinary dense parameter rewriting; instead, \textsc{OPD} retains important geometric signatures of on-policy post-training.

</details>


### [188] [Understanding Truncated Positional Encodings for Graph Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13671)
*James Flora,Mitchell Black,Weng-Keen Wong,Amir Nayyeri*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper investigates how truncating positional encodings (PEs) — a common practical necessity — fundamentally alters their theoretical expressive power. It proves that truncated spectral and walk-based PEs are no longer equivalent, and truncated spectral PEs lose their advantage over the 1-WL test. Experiments show mixing truncated PEs works best in practice.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Full positional encodings (e.g., Laplacian eigenspaces, adjacency matrix polynomials) are theoretically equivalent in expressive power and lie between 1-WL and 3-WL, but their O(n³) complexity forces practitioners to use truncated versions (e.g., first k eigenvectors). The theoretical properties of these truncated PEs are entirely unknown, creating a gap between theory and practice.

Method: The authors conduct a theoretical expressivity analysis comparing truncated versions of spectral PEs (Laplacian eigenvectors, effective resistance) and walk-based PEs (adjacency matrix powers) under the message-passing GNN framework. They use WL-test hierarchies as the expressivity yardstick and introduce k-harmonic distances as a case study to illustrate fine-grained differences among closely related truncated spectral PEs.

Result: Under truncation, spectral and walk-based PE families become fundamentally different in expressive power. Truncated spectral PEs are no stronger than the 1-WL test. The k-harmonic distance analysis reveals that even closely related truncated PEs can differ in expressivity. Experimentally, combining multiple truncated PE families outperforms any single one on real-world benchmarks.

Conclusion: Truncation breaks the theoretical equivalence between spectral and walk-based PEs, with spectral PEs degrading to 1-WL expressivity. Practitioners should consider mixing different truncated PE families rather than relying on a single one, as this yields better empirical performance.

Abstract: Positional encodings (PEs) enhance the power of graph neural networks (GNNs), both theoretically and empirically. Two of the most popular families of PEs - spectral (e.g., Laplacian eigenspaces, effective resistance) and walk-based (polynomials of the adjacency matrix) - are theoretically equivalent in expressive power, with expressivity between the 1-WL and 3-WL tests. However, this equivalence assumes the GNN uses the "complete" version of these PEs, which requires $O(n^3)$ time and space complexity. Instead, practitioners commonly use truncated variants of these encodings, such as the first $k$ eigenspaces or powers of the adjacency matrix. However, the theoretical properties of these truncated PEs are unknown. In this work, we initiate the study of these truncated PEs. Theoretically, we show that, under truncation, several families of PEs are fundamentally different in expressive power. As a corollary, we show that truncated spectral PEs are no longer stronger than the 1-WL test. We also study a family of spectral PEs, the $k$-harmonic distances, to highlight the differences in expressive power of even closely related truncated PEs. Finally, we experimentally show that a mix of truncated PEs is preferable to any single family on real-world datasets.

</details>


<div id='cs.HC'></div>

# cs.HC [[Back]](#toc)

### [189] [OpenRoundup: Multi-Table Data Wrangling Through Interactive Visualization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12648)
*Stephen Kasica,Charles Berret,Tamara Munzner*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: OpenRoundup is a browser-based, no-code tool for data journalists to consolidate multiple tables into a single analysis-ready output using a schema-first, values-on-demand paradigm. It introduces two conceptual contributions — eager table consolidation and a declarative vocabulary of Stack and Pack operations — and is evaluated through a replication study of 17 journalist workflows and a deployment study with four professional data journalists.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Data journalists frequently need to combine records from multiple independently published sources for accountability reporting, but existing interactive data wrangling tools treat a single table — not a collection of tables — as the primary unit of work. This gap forces journalists with conceptual understanding of joins but lacking programming skills to rely on developers, slowing down investigative workflows.

Method: The authors built OpenRoundup, a client-only, browser-based system using DuckDB-WASM, featuring five coordinated panels with live schema previews, ambient data quality alerts, and a recursive treemap visualization. It implements a schema-first, values-on-demand paradigm. The system introduces eager table consolidation (assembling a composite table early via incremental, interactive assembly) and a declarative vocabulary with two operations: Stack and Pack. Evaluation was conducted via a replication study reproducing 17 published journalist programming workflows using only the GUI, and a deployment study with four professional data journalists.

Result: The replication study demonstrated expressive coverage of real-world consolidation tasks. The deployment study confirmed utility for practitioners who understand joins conceptually but lack programming skills, and surfaced an unanticipated secondary value for data journalism education.

Conclusion: OpenRoundup fills a critical gap in interactive data wrangling tools by treating table collections as the primary unit of work. Its schema-first, code-free approach enables non-programming journalists to perform complex multi-table consolidation tasks, with additional potential as an educational tool in data journalism.

Abstract: Data journalists routinely integrate records across multiple independently published sources to support accountability reporting, yet no existing interactive wrangling tool treats the collection of tables -- rather than the single table -- as its primary unit of work. We present OpenRoundup, an open-source, browser-based system that enables data journalists to consolidate multiple tables into a single analysis-ready output without writing code. The interface comprises five coordinated panels that implement a schema-first, values-on-demand paradigm with live schema previews, ambient data quality alerts, and a recursive treemap visualization of the evolving operation tree. A client-only architecture powered by DuckDB-WASM runs in the browser, providing strong data privacy guarantees suited to sensitive journalism data. The system introduces two conceptual contributions: eager table consolidation, in which a composite table is assembled early in the wrangling phase via interactive, incremental assembly of multiple source tables; and a declarative vocabulary for table consolidation consisting of two operations, Stack and Pack. We evaluate the system through a replication study in which the authors reproduce 17 published journalist programming workflows using only the interface, and a deployment study with four professional data journalists. The replication study demonstrates expressive coverage of real-world consolidation tasks. The deployment study confirms utility for practitioners who understand joins conceptually but lack the programming skills to execute them, and surfaces an unanticipated secondary value for data journalism education.

</details>


### [190] [A Multiplexing Design Space: Theory, Method, and Application](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12719)
*Yiwen Xing,Afrah Farea,Saiful Khan,Min Chen*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: Proposes a systematic design method for visual multiplexing — displaying multiple data attributes simultaneously — applied to ML workflows comparing 2D scalar fields in PDE surrogate modeling. Through domain-grounded co-design with experts, the method yields optimal default multiplexing designs with user-controllable variations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visual multiplexing is commonly used in visualization but rarely explored systematically as design patterns. The design space is vast, and there is a need for a principled, application-constrained method to navigate it. Superimposing heatmaps (a naive multiplexing approach) is ineffective for comparing multiple 2D scalar fields, which is a frequent task in ML workflows for PDE surrogate modeling.

Method: A design method comprising: (1) a pre-design step for domain grounding and theoretical analysis; (2) three design steps to systematically explore the constrained design space of visual multiplexing for multiple 2D scalar fields; and (3) co-design and evaluation activities involving domain experts. Applied as a case study to ML workflows developing PDE-approximating models.

Result: The process identified relatively optimal default multiplexing designs for the target application context and revealed the need for small variations that domain experts can control through a user interface.

Conclusion: Constraining the design space by a specific application and involving domain experts throughout the design process is an effective strategy for systematically exploring visual multiplexing designs and producing both optimal defaults and flexible, user-tunable alternatives.

Abstract: Many visualization designs feature phenomena referred to as ``visual multiplexing'', where multiple pieces of information associated with the same data point are conveyed simultaneously. Although visualization designers are able to bring such phenomena, often unconsciously, into their designs, the design space of visual multiplexing is huge, and it is uncommon to explore visual multiplexing systematically as design patterns. In this paper, we propose a design method for exploring a smaller design space constrained by an application. As an illustrative case study, we focus on machine learning (ML) workflows for developing ML models that approximate partial differential equations (PDEs). In these workflows, ML researchers need to analyze the inter-relationships among multiple 2D scalar fields frequently. Since superimposing one heatmap on top of another is not an effective design, we formulate three design steps to explore the design space of visual multiplexing in the context of multiple 2D scalar fields. Our design method also includes a pre-design step for domain grounding and theoretical analysis, and involves domain experts in both co-design and evaluation activities. The design process enables us to identify relatively optimal default multiplexing designs as well as the need for small variations that domain experts can control through a user interface.

</details>


### [191] [Exploring How Agent Voice Accents Shape Human-AI Collaboration in K-12 Group Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12805)
*Prerna Ravi,Carúmey Stevens,Ben Hurt,Brandon Hanks,Grace Lin,Emma Anderson*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: Study of 33 teachers shows that LLM voice agent accent (British vs. Indian vs. African American) significantly shapes group collaboration dynamics: British accent led to tool-like, detached use while Indian and African American accents fostered anthropomorphization and peer-level integration, affecting trust, engagement, and reliance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While collaboration is essential in education, teachers struggle to facilitate productive peer interaction. LLM conversational agents offer new mediation possibilities, but prior work has only examined accent effects in one-to-one settings — not in group contexts relevant to Computer-Supported Collaborative Learning (CSCL). The gap is understanding how voice persona design influences group dynamics.

Method: Between-subjects mixed-methods study with 33 teachers. Participants engaged in group work mediated by a GenAI voice agent with one of three accent conditions: British, Indian, or African American. Data collected through surveys, group interaction analyses, and artifacts to triangulate findings on collaboration and agent perception.

Result: Accent shaped participants' mental models and the roles assigned to the agent. The British-accented agent was treated instrumentally as a tool with detached, utility-based engagement. Indian- and African American-accented agents were more readily anthropomorphized and integrated as peers, influencing trust, engagement, and reliance patterns over time.

Conclusion: GenAI's sociolinguistic design features — specifically accent — materially shape group dynamics in CSCL contexts. Designers of AI partners for collaborative learning must attend to these features to create culturally inclusive agents, as accent triggers different role expectations that cascade into trust and engagement outcomes.

Abstract: Collaboration is widely recognized as a cornerstone of 21st-century education, yet teachers still encounter persistent challenges in fostering productive peer interaction. LLM conversational peer agents introduce new possibilities for mediating in-person group work, raising questions about how persona design, particularly their voice characteristics, shapes learners' perceptions, trust, and interactional dynamics. While prior work has examined agent accent effects in one-to-one settings, little is known about how these effects manifest in groups. We conducted a between-subjects mixed-methods study with 33 teachers examining how a GenAI voice agent with different accents (British, Indian, and African American) influenced collaboration and agent perception. Across surveys, group interaction analyses, and artifacts, we find that accent shaped participants' mental models and the roles the agent assumed in group interaction. The British-accented agent was largely treated as a tool and engaged in detached, utility-based ways, whereas Indian- and African American-accented agents were more readily anthropomorphized and integrated as peers. These role expectations influenced trust, engagement, and reliance over time. This work advances understanding of how GenAI's sociolinguistic design features shape group dynamics in CSCL, with implications for designing culturally inclusive AI partners in group learning.

</details>


### [192] [From Prompts to Preferences: An Open-Source Platform for Generative AI-Enhanced Conjoint Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12972)
*Philipp Brauner*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: The paper presents an open-source, self-hosted web application for end-to-end conjoint survey design, deployment, and analysis, featuring generative AI integration for producing textual and visual stimuli, with a proof-of-concept study on care robot preferences.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Researchers without access to commercial conjoint analysis platforms face significant barriers due to high costs and lack of end-to-end survey infrastructure. Existing open-source alternatives lack integrated stimulus generation, survey deployment, and analysis capabilities, creating a need for an accessible, comprehensive tool.

Method: The authors developed a self-hosted web application that streamlines the full conjoint workflow: a setup wizard with AI-assisted attribute suggestion, parameterised base prompts fed to large language models (LLMs) for textual scenarios and text-to-image models for visual stimuli, optional LLM-facing level annotations to enrich generation, live data analysis dashboards, and a full export bundle (stimuli, prompts, responses) for transparency and reproducibility. Validation via a proof-of-concept study (N=55) on care robot preferences for ambient assisted living (AAL).

Result: The platform successfully enables end-to-end conjoint surveys with AI-generated integrated stimuli formats — both textual scenario descriptions and visual imagery. The proof-of-concept study (N=55) demonstrated practical feasibility for studying care robot preferences in AAL contexts. The export bundle mechanism supports reproducibility and open science practices.

Conclusion: The platform lowers technical barriers for researchers adopting conjoint methodology, but the authors caution that theoretical grounding remains the researcher's responsibility. Generative AI can broaden the methodological repertoire in HCI and related fields by enabling richer, integrated stimulus formats beyond traditional tabular conjoint designs.

Abstract: Conjoint analysis is a widely used preference measurement method in marketing research, political science, healthcare, and human-computer interaction. Despite broad adoption, researchers without access to commercial platforms face significant barriers, as existing tools are either expensive or lack end-to-end survey infrastructure. This paper presents an open-source, self-hosted web application for designing, deploying, and analysing conjoint surveys. Beyond conventional tabular stimuli, the platform uses generative AI to produce integrated stimuli formats: textual scenario descriptions generated by a large language model, and visual stimuli by a text-to-image model. A researcher-defined base prompt is parameterised with the conjoint profile, and optional LLM-facing level annotations enrich the generation. A structured setup wizard, AI-assisted attribute suggestion, and live data analysis lower the technical barriers for researchers new to conjoint methodology. A full export bundle including all stimuli, their generating prompts, and response data facilitates transparency and reproducibility. The platform is demonstrated through a proof-of-concept study on care robot preferences for ambient assisted living (AAL, N=55) using AI-generated visual stimuli. The paper discusses the role of AI assistance in conjoint design, arguing that theoretical grounding must remain the researcher's responsibility, and outlining how genAI-generated stimuli can broaden the methodological repertoire for HCI and related fields.

</details>


### [193] [Mod-Guide: An LLM-based Content Moderation Feedback System to Address Insensitive Speech toward Indigenous Ethnic and Religious Minority Communities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13397)
*Dipto Das,Achhiya Sultana,Ankit Singh Chauhan,Saadia Binte Alam,Mohammad Shidujaman,Shion Guha,Sunandan Chakraborty,Syed Ishtiaque Ahmed*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper examines how LLM-based content moderation fails to recognize culturally insensitive speech targeting minority communities, using Bangladesh's Hindu and Chakma communities as case studies. The authors co-created a corpus of insensitive speech with community members and developed Mod-Guide, a RAG-enhanced moderation tool that incorporates lived experiences to improve LLM sensitivity to minority perspectives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based content moderation systems primarily detect overt hostility but systematically overlook subtle forms of culturally insensitive speech — including implicit erasure, misrepresentation, and normative framing — that marginalize historically underrepresented communities. The authors argue that these epistemic limitations stem from LLMs' lack of access to minority communities' lived experiences and hermeneutical resources, creating a gap between computationally detectable harm and culturally experienced harm.

Method: The study employs a participatory co-design methodology with Bangladesh's Hindu and Chakma communities. First, community members co-created a culturally grounded corpus of insensitive speech. Then, the authors developed Mod-Guide, a moderation pipeline that uses Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) to inject community narratives and contextual knowledge into LLM-based moderation decisions. Evaluation used mixed methods with both minority and majority participants to assess contextual accuracy and differential perception across ethnic lines.

Result: RAG-enhanced moderation responses demonstrated improved contextual accuracy compared to baseline LLM moderation. Crucially, minority and majority participants perceived the same moderation responses differently along ethnic lines, revealing how cultural positionality shapes evaluations of content moderation fairness and adequacy.

Conclusion: The paper argues for redesigning content moderation systems around principles of restorative justice and hermeneutical inclusion — ensuring that minority communities' interpretive frameworks are embedded in moderation pipelines rather than relying solely on dominant cultural norms encoded in LLMs.

Abstract: Language operates as a mechanism of both marginalization and resistance, especially for minority communities navigating insensitive and harmful speech online. As content moderation increasingly depends on large language models (LLMs), concerns arise about whether these systems can recognize culturally insensitive speech-language that disregards or marginalizes the cultural and religious perspectives of historically underrepresented communities, often through implicit erasure, misrepresentation, or normative framing, rather than overt hostility. Focusing on Bangladesh's Hindu and Chakma communities -- the country's largest religious and Indigenous ethnic minorities, respectively -- this paper investigates the epistemic limits of LLM-based moderation systems and explores methods for incorporating minority perspectives. We co-created a culturally grounded corpus of insensitive speech with community members and integrated their narratives into moderation pipelines using retrieval augmented generation (RAG). Our tool, Mod-Guide, improves LLM sensitivity to minority viewpoints by leveraging contextual cues derived from lived experience. Through mixed-method evaluations involving both minority and majority participants, we demonstrate that RAG-enhanced moderation responses are more contextually accurate and perceived differently across ethnic lines. This work advances research in human-computer interaction, AI ethics, and social computing by foregrounding restorative justice and hermeneutical inclusion in the design of content moderation systems.

</details>


### [194] [Ride, Track, and Recover: Pilot Randomized Trial of a Wearable Digital Self-Management Intervention During a Veteran Endurance-Cycling Program](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13529)
*Alan Ta,Nilsu Salgin,Caleb Armstrong,Kala Phillips Reindel,Farzan Sasangohar*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: A study of veterans with PTSD found that combining smartwatch-based hyperarousal detection with a digital self-management intervention (plus cycling) led to better stabilization of hyperarousal trajectories and sustained symptom improvement compared to cycling alone or at-home monitoring. Higher symptom severity was associated with greater perceived accuracy of ML-detected events.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: PTSD in veterans involves persistent hyperarousal and comorbid mood symptoms that are difficult to monitor and manage outside clinical settings. There is a need for scalable, real-world approaches combining passive sensing with digital interventions to extend care beyond the clinic.

Method: Thirteen veterans in a Project Hero cycling event were randomized to digital intervention plus physical activity (n=7) or physical activity only (n=3), with a third at-home monitoring control cohort (n=7, of whom 4 provided data). Continuous smartwatch sensing (heart rate + accelerometer) detected hyperarousal events, confirmed in real time by participants. Weekly self-report measures (anxiety, depression, PTSD severity) were analyzed using generalized additive mixed models to characterize nonlinear trajectories over time.

Result: Baseline-normalized hyperarousal trajectories differed significantly across conditions: the digital intervention group showed structured stabilization, while the physical-only group showed late-study escalation. Both cycling groups had acute symptom improvements during the event, but the digital group maintained gains better. The at-home control group showed gradual symptom declines. Perceived ML detection precision varied individually and was positively associated with symptom severity—higher-severity participants confirmed more detected events.

Conclusion: Coupling wearable-based hyperarousal detection with digital self-management tools may support hyperarousal stabilization and symptom improvement. Personalization and human-centered design are critical for wearable mental health systems, given individual differences in perceived detection accuracy.

Abstract: Post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD) in veterans is characterized by persistent hyperarousal and comorbid anxiety and depressive symptoms that are difficult to monitor and manage outside clinical settings. Thirteen veterans participating in a Project Hero cycling event in Texas were randomized by computer-generated sequence in a naturalistic setting to two arms: (1) digital intervention plus physical activity, or (2) physical activity only, plus a third at-home monitoring control cohort consisting of 7 veterans selected from the broader Project Hero veteran community. Continuous smartwatch sensing combined heart rate and accelerometer features to detect hyperarousal events, which were confirmed in real time by participants. Weekly self-report measures of anxiety, depression, and PTSD severity were collected. Generalized additive mixed models characterized nonlinear trajectories over time. Baseline-normalized hyperarousal trajectories differed significantly across conditions, with the digital intervention group (n=7) showing structured stabilization compared to late-study escalation in the physical-only group (n=3). Both cycling groups exhibited acute symptom improvements during the endurance event; however, the digital intervention group demonstrated a higher overall maintenance of gains. The at-home control group (n=4) showed gradual symptom declines. Perceived precision of ML detections varied substantially across individuals and was positively associated with symptom severity, with higher-severity participants confirming a greater proportion of detected events. These results suggest that coupling wearable detection with digital self-management tools may support stabilization of hyperarousal and symptom improvement while emphasizing the importance of personalization and human-centered design in wearable mental health systems.

</details>


<div id='cs.CR'></div>

# cs.CR [[Back]](#toc)

### [195] [Influence Factors on RAG Poisoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12469)
*Pedro Pereira,Eva Maia,Isabel Praça,Adrien Bécue*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: A factorial experiment (432 configurations) examining how dataset, retriever type, retrieval depth, database composition, chunking, and generator model affect poisoning robustness in RAG systems. Key findings: retriever architecture and retrieval depth most influence poisoning exposure; dense/graph-based retrievers outperform BM25; replicating poisoned content across databases amplifies attacks while clean sources mitigate them; vulnerability arises from component interactions rather than any single factor.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RAG systems depend on external retrieved documents, creating a vulnerability to adversarial poisoning attacks that can manipulate both retrieval and generation. The paper aims to systematically understand which components and configurations drive this vulnerability, rather than treating it as a single-point failure.

Method: Full factorial experimental design spanning 432 configurations. Factors manipulated include: dataset, retriever type (BM25, dense, graph-based), retrieval depth (number of retrieved passages), database composition (poisoned vs. clean source ratios), chunking strategy, and generator model. Effects measured on both retrieval-level and generation-level metrics.

Result: Retriever architecture, dataset, and retrieval depth are the strongest factors for poisoning exposure at the retrieval level. Dense and graph-based retrievers show improved robustness over BM25. Larger retrieval depth increases the probability of retrieving poisoned passages. Generator choice and database composition most affect downstream attack success. Replicating poisoned content across multiple databases amplifies adversarial influence; additional clean sources mitigate it.

Conclusion: Poisoning vulnerability in RAG is an emergent property of the interaction between retrieval, generation, and knowledge-base configuration, not attributable to any single component. Defense strategies must therefore be holistic rather than component-specific.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems enhance large language models by grounding responses in retrieved documents from external knowledge sources at inference time. However, this reliance on retrieved content introduces vulnerabilities to poisoning attacks, in which adversarial documents can manipulate both the retrieval process and the generated outputs. This paper investigates poisoning robustness in RAG through a full factorial experimental study covering 432 configurations. We analyze the impacts of dataset, retriever type, retrieval depth, database composition, chunking strategy, and generator model on retrieval-level and generation-level metrics. The results show that retriever architecture, dataset, and retrieval depth are the strongest factors affecting poisoning exposure, while generator choice and database composition have a major impact on downstream attack success. Dense and graph-based retrievers generally improve robustness relative to BM25, whereas larger retrieval depth increases the likelihood of retrieving poisoned passages. We further show that replicating poisoned content across multiple databases amplifies adversarial influence, while additional clean sources can mitigate it. These findings highlight that poisoning vulnerability in RAG is not attributable to a single component, but instead arises from the interaction of retrieval, generation, and knowledge-base configuration.

</details>


### [196] [From Parameters to Feature Space: Task Arithmetic for Backdoor Mitigation in Model Merging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12498)
*Zhenqian Zhu,Yamin Hu,Yiya Diao,Weixiang Li,Haodong Li,Wenjian Luo*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: LFPM proposes a backdoor defense for model merging that optimizes an anti-backdoor task vector from a feature-space perspective, leveraging Cross-Task Linearity to suppress backdoors while preserving clean performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing backdoor defenses for model merging rely on direct parameter-space editing (e.g., task arithmetic) and fail to remove backdoors without significantly degrading clean-task accuracy. A more principled approach is needed that can suppress backdoors while maintaining task performance.

Method: LFPM introduces an anti-backdoor task vector optimized under the Cross-Task Linearity (CTL) framework, which exploits approximate linearity of features across tasks. The optimization uses gradient accumulation and loss path-integral along the interpolation path between the backdoored and anti-backdoor models, formulated from a unified feature-space perspective to jointly address backdoor suppression and clean-task preservation.

Result: Extensive experiments show LFPM consistently achieves strong robustness against backdoor attacks across both full fine-tuning and Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) settings.

Conclusion: LFPM effectively mitigates backdoor attacks in model merging by operating in feature space rather than parameter space, offering a robust defense that preserves clean-task performance across diverse fine-tuning paradigms.

Abstract: Model merging (MM) has gained significant attention as a cost-effective approach to integrate multiple task-specific models into a unified model. However, recent work reveals that MM is highly susceptible to backdoor attacks. Existing defenses based on task arithmetic often fail to eliminate backdoors without substantially degrading clean-task performance, owing to their reliance on direct parameter-space editing. To address this gap, we propose Linear Feature Path Minimization (LFPM), a backdoor mitigation framework for model merging, which introduces an anti-backdoor task vector into the backdoored merged model. Unlike prior approaches, LFPM formulates the backdoor robustness of the merged model from a unified feature-space perspective under the Cross-Task Linearity (CTL) framework, which leverages the approximate linearity of features across tasks. This perspective guides the optimization of the anti-backdoor task to suppress backdoors while preserving clean-task performance. Furthermore, we introduce an effective optimization mechanism based on gradient accumulation and loss path-integral, ensuring robust backdoor suppression along the interpolation path. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LFPM consistently exhibits strong robustness against backdoor attacks in both full fine-tuning and Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) settings.

</details>


### [197] [Beyond Attack Success Rate: Examining Trigger Leakage in Vision-Language Agentic Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12586)
*Jiamin Chang,Salil Kanhere,Piotr Koniusz,Jason,Xue,Hammond Pearce*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper formalizes "trigger leakage" in vision-language agentic systems, where backdoor triggers activate unintentionally on visually/semantically similar inputs. It introduces Neighbor Leakage Rate (NLR) as a metric and shows that standard fine-tuning creates overly broad activation regions, while adding hard-negative samples during training effectively narrows trigger specificity and reduces leakage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing backdoor evaluations for VLAS focus only on clean accuracy and attack success rate (ASR), missing whether a backdoor is "precise" — i.e., activates only on the intended trigger rather than leaking to neighboring inputs. This precision failure poses a system-level threat because leaked triggers can propagate through decision pipelines into executable programs and action sequences.

Method: The authors formalize trigger leakage and propose Neighbor Leakage Rate (NLR) to quantify it. They evaluate icon and text triggers under a 3% poisoning ratio against common visual transformations. Using textual triggers as a controlled probe, they analyze how standard fine-tuning learns broad activation regions. They then introduce edit-distance-one hard-negative samples during training to narrow the activation region and measure leakage reduction in image-editing and embodied-manipulation workflows.

Result: At 3% poisoning ratio, icon and text triggers remain robust to visual transformations but suffer severe neighbor leakage: NLR reaches 0.996 for icons and 0.944 for text. Standard fine-tuning learns a broad activation region, causing neighboring strings to invoke malicious behavior even without the exact trigger. Adding hard-negative samples substantially narrows this region and reduces leakage, including in downstream workflows where leaked triggers propagate into executable code and action sequences.

Conclusion: Trigger precision is a critical but overlooked dimension of backdoor attacks in VLAS. NLR provides an effective metric for measuring leakage, and incorporating hard-negative samples during training is a viable mitigation that improves trigger specificity without sacrificing robustness.

Abstract: Vision-Language Agentic Systems (VLAS) connect visual perception to planning, tool use, and physical actions. This means backdoor-type triggers can propagate through both decision pipelines and their connected interfaces, thus making visual backdoors a system-level threat. Current evaluations on such backdoors focus on clean accuracy and attack success rate (ASR), metrics that capture whether a trigger works, but not whether an attack is actually "precise" -- i.e. whether it triggers hidden behaviors only when intended. In this work, we formalize the failure of trigger precision as "trigger leakage": inputs that are visually or semantically close to the intended trigger and therefore inadvertently activate the attacker-specified behavior. To quantify this leakage, we introduce Neighbor Leakage Rate (NLR). Our experiments show that at a 3% poisoning ratio, icon and text triggers remain robust to common visual transformations, but their neighboring variants leak heavily, with NLR reaching 0.996 (icon) and 0.944 (text). Using textual triggers as a controlled probe, we show that standard fine-tuning learns a broad activation region rather than an exact trigger condition, causing neighboring strings to invoke the malicious behavior even when the exact trigger is absent. Adding edit-distance-one hard-negative samples during training substantially narrows this activation region and reduces leakage, including in image-editing and embodied-manipulation workflows, where leaked triggers can propagate into executable programs and action sequences.

</details>


### [198] [Amnesia: A Stealthy Replay Attack on Continual Learning Dreams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12655)
*Ahmed Sharshar,Naveen Kumar Kummari,Mohsen Guizani*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces "Amnesia," a replay composition attack against continual learning systems where a limited-privilege insider controls only replay index selection. The attack maximizes catastrophic forgetting under auditable constraints (KL/TV divergence budgets from a nominal class histogram). Two variants (KL exponential tilt and TV balanced mass redistribution) demonstrate a principled trade-off between degradation impact and detectability under batch and rolling-window audits.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing attacks on continual learning models alter inputs or training pipelines (poisoning/backdoors) without explicit auditable constraints, limiting realism. The robustness of experience replay to replay sampling interference — where an insider controls only which samples are replayed — remains underexplored. The authors aim to establish index-only replay control as a practical, auditable threat surface and characterize the impact-visibility trade-off.

Method: Amnesia operates under two budgets: a visibility budget δ (bounding TV or KL divergence of the realized replay class histogram from nominal p₀) and a mass budget f (fixing replay rate). The attack proceeds in two steps: (1) Compute lightweight per-class utilities (e.g., EMA loss or confidence) to identify harmful classes and tilt p₀ accordingly; (2) Project the tilted distribution back into the δ-ball using efficient KL-based (exponential tilt) or TV-based (balanced mass redistribution) optimizers. A windowed scheduler enforces rolling-window audit compliance.

Result: Across challenging CL benchmarks with strong replay baselines, Amnesia consistently reduces final accuracy (ACC) and worsens backward transfer (−BWT). The KL variant achieves high degradation while remaining largely undetected under per-batch and rolling-window audits. The TV variant causes greater damage but is easier to detect, especially under tight per-class constraints.

Conclusion: Index-only replay control constitutes a practical, auditable threat surface in continual learning systems. The work establishes a principled impact-visibility trade-off: the KL variant offers stealthier degradation, while the TV variant is more damaging but less covert. This exposes a fundamental vulnerability in replay-based CL systems that cannot be fully mitigated by existing audit mechanisms.

Abstract: Continual learning (CL) models often use experience replay to reduce catastrophic forgetting, but their robustness to replay sampling interference remains underexplored. Existing CL attacks alter inputs or training pipelines (poisoning/backdoors) and rarely include explicit auditable constraints, limiting realism. Here, auditability means a monitor can verify compliance from sampler-visible telemetry - e.g., logged replay index/label statistics - by checking that the realized replay class histogram stays close to a nominal baseline and that replay rate is unchanged per batch and/or over a rolling window. We study a limited-privilege insider who controls only replay index selection, not pixels, labels, or model parameters, while staying within auditable limits such as queue priorities. We introduce Amnesia, a replay composition attack that maximizes degradation under two budgets: a visibility budget delta bounding the TV/KL divergence from a nominal class histogram p0, and a mass budget f fixing the replay rate. Amnesia has two steps: (i) compute lightweight class utilities, such as EMA loss or confidence, to tilt p0 toward harmful classes; and (ii) project the tilt back into the delta-ball using efficient KL (exponential tilt) or TV (balanced mass redistribution) optimizers. A windowed scheduler enforces rolling audits. Across challenging CL benchmarks and strong replay baselines, Amnesia consistently lowers final accuracy (ACC) and worsens backward transfer (-BWT). The KL variant delivers high impact while remaining largely undetected under multiple audit schemes, including per-batch and rolling-window checks. The TV variant is more damaging but easier to detect, especially under tight per-class constraints. These results expose index-only replay control as a practical, auditable threat surface in CL systems and establish a principled impact-visibility trade-off.

</details>


### [199] [CAPED: Context-Aware Privacy Exposure Defense for Mobile GUI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12666)
*Siyu Shen,Fenghao Xu,Wenrui Diao,Kehuan Zhang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Mobile GUI agents that take screenshots risk leaking unrelated private information during task execution. CAPED is a phone-side pre-upload layer that selectively exposes only task-relevant UI content before screenshots are sent to remote agents, reducing incidental visual privacy leakage from 0.766 to 0.268 while maintaining task utility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Screenshot-based mobile GUI agents create a privacy boundary at every screen observation: screenshots may inadvertently expose contacts, messages, photos, health information, and other sensitive context unrelated to the user's request. Existing defenses like text anonymization miss visual cues, and generic privacy masking removes task-essential controls. This "incidental visual privacy exposure" problem lacks a targeted solution.

Method: CAPED is a context-aware pre-upload exposure control layer that runs on-device. Before screenshots leave the phone, it (1) extracts task requirements from the user's request, (2) uses screen context as a privacy prior, (3) parses visible UI elements, and (4) selectively exposes only content needed for the current task while masking incidental private content. The system treats screenshot upload as an explicit device-cloud boundary decision governed by task-driven selective exposure.

Result: In a controlled 28-task seeded privacy evaluation, Full CAPED reduced success-conditioned weighted seeded leakage from 0.766 (raw screenshots) to 0.268 while preserving high task utility. A broader AndroidWorld evaluation showed a remaining prototype-level utility cost, but results support selective exposure over all-or-nothing screen sharing.

Conclusion: Screenshot upload to cloud-based GUI agents should be treated as an explicit device-cloud boundary decision governed by task-driven selective exposure. CAPED demonstrates that context-aware pre-upload filtering can substantially reduce incidental privacy leakage while maintaining task utility, though prototype-level overhead remains.

Abstract: Screenshot-based mobile GUI agents can operate ordinary smartphone apps through the same visual interface as a human user, but this capability also turns every screen observation into a privacy boundary. During normal task execution, screenshots may expose contacts, messages, photos, files, recommendations, health cues, and other sensitive context that is unrelated to the user's request. We call this problem incidental visual privacy exposure. It is difficult to address with existing defenses: text anonymization misses many visual and inferential cues, while generic privacy masking can remove the evidence and controls that a GUI agent needs to complete the task.
  This paper presents CAPED, a context-aware pre-upload exposure control layer for mobile GUI agents. CAPED is designed as a phone-side protection layer: before screenshots are released to a remote multimodal agent, it extracts task requirements, uses screen context as a privacy prior, parses visible UI elements, and selectively exposes only content needed for the current task while masking incidental private content. We evaluate CAPED on AndroidWorld for broad task utility and with a controlled 28-task seeded privacy evaluation used as a measurement instrument for trajectory-level incidental leakage. In this seeded evaluation, Full CAPED reduces success-conditioned weighted seeded leakage from 0.766 under raw screenshots to 0.268 while preserving high task utility. A broader AndroidWorld run shows a remaining prototype-level utility cost, but the results support the central claim that screenshot upload should be treated as an explicit device--cloud boundary decision, governed by task-driven selective exposure rather than all-or-nothing screen sharing.

</details>


### [200] [SMSR: Certified Defence Against Runtime Memory Poisoning in Persistent LLM Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12703)
*Tarun Sharma*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces Multi-Session Memory Poisoning (MSMP), a new attack vector where adversaries inject malicious memories across user sessions in RAG agents. The authors propose Signed Memory with Smoothed Retrieval (SMSR), the first defense with certified robustness bounds, combining HMAC-SHA256 provenance to block unsigned injection and randomized memory ablation with verdict-based voting to limit authenticated adversary impact. Experiments across 15 enterprise scenarios demonstrate near-total attack mitigation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RAG agents with persistent, cross-session memory create a novel attack surface where adversaries can inject crafted memories through normal interaction channels. These poisoned memories, once retrieved, can steer agent responses for future users without modifying model weights or code. Existing defenses (RobustRAG, ReliabilityRAG) assume static knowledge bases and cannot certify against this adaptive, multi-session threat. Heuristic filters fail against fluent enterprise-style adversarial text. No prior defense provides certified robustness for this setting.

Method: SMSR comprises two components: (1) HMAC-SHA256 provenance attached at memory write time, blocking unsigned injection entirely; (2) randomized memory ablation with verdict-based majority voting at query time, which bounds the influence of authenticated adversaries. The authors provide a formal proof that no provenance-free retrieval-time filter can certify against adaptive injection, derive a hypergeometric certificate for Component 2's robustness bound, and formalize the "Consistent Minority Effect" showing how adversarial answers can win string-based voting as a numerical minority while verdict-based voting eliminates them.

Result: Across 15 enterprise scenarios (3,150 repeated trials), Component 1 reduced attack success from 93-100% to 0% for all unsigned variants. For authenticated adversaries with single injection, Component 2 held success to 8.0% (95% CI [5.8, 10.9], n=450), below the certified worst case. In an end-to-end query-only attack where the agent itself writes the poison, SMSR reduced success from 65.3% to 5.3% (n=150). Clean-query utility was 90% (Component 1 alone) and 85% (combined).

Conclusion: SMSR is the first defense providing certified robustness against multi-session memory poisoning in RAG agents. The two-component design effectively neutralizes both unsigned and authenticated injection attacks while maintaining acceptable clean-query utility. The formal certification framework and the Consistent Minority Effect formalization establish theoretical foundations for securing persistent-memory RAG systems.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) agents increasingly run with persistent memory that accumulates across user sessions. This creates a new attack surface: an adversary interacting only through normal channels can inject crafted memories that, once retrieved, steer the agent's responses for future users, without touching model weights or code. We call this Multi-Session Memory Poisoning (MSMP) and show that no existing defence certifies against it; static-corpus defences (RobustRAG, ReliabilityRAG) assume a fixed knowledge base, and heuristic filters are bypassed by fluent enterprise-style text. We present Signed Memory with Smoothed Retrieval (SMSR), the first defence with a certified robustness bound for this setting. Component 1 adds HMAC-SHA256 provenance at write time, blocking unsigned injection. Component 2 applies randomised memory ablation with verdict-based majority voting at query time, bounding the influence of authenticated adversaries. We prove that no provenance-free retrieval-time filter can certify against adaptive injection, derive a hypergeometric certificate for Component 2, and formalise the Consistent Minority Effect, whereby a consistent adversarial answer wins string-based voting as a numerical minority while verdict-based voting removes it. Across 15 enterprise scenarios (3,150 repeated trials), Component 1 cuts attack success from 93-100% to 0% for all unsigned variants. For an authenticated adversary with a single injection, Component 2 holds success to 8.0% (95% CI [5.8, 10.9], n=450), below the certified worst case. In an end-to-end query-only attack where the agent itself writes the poison rather than it being pre-seeded, SMSR reduces success from 65.3% to 5.3% (n=150, non-overlapping CIs) on a live agent stack. Clean-query utility is 90% (Component 1) and 85% (combined).

</details>


### [201] [PI-Hunter: Automated Red-Teaming for Exposing and Localizing Prompt Injections](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12737)
*Pengfei He,Lesly Miculicich,Vishesh Sharma,Ash Fox,George Lee,Jiliang Tang,Tomas Pfister,Long T. Le*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: PI-Hunter is an automated auditing framework that proactively exposes prompt injection vulnerabilities in LLM agents by iteratively evolving realistic, source-aware test cases to reveal latent malicious instructions embedded in external environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM agents increasingly interact with external tools, creating indirect prompt injection risks from untrusted sources. Current defenses only block at inference time, and red-teaming methods optimize for attack success rather than revealing how latent prompt injections emerge and propagate. Developers lack visibility into these vulnerability pathways.

Method: PI-Hunter constructs source-aware test cases and uses feedback-driven iterative evolution to induce agents to retrieve and reveal latent malicious instructions embedded in external environments, providing broader attack-surface coverage than existing approaches.

Result: Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks, agent architectures, attacks, and defenses show PI-Hunter substantially outperforms strong automated red-teaming baselines in vulnerability exposure and attack-surface coverage, while remaining effective under existing prompt injection defenses.

Conclusion: PI-Hunter provides a proactive approach to auditing LLM agent vulnerabilities, giving developers better visibility into prompt injection propagation and enabling more robust security assessments before deployment.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are rapidly evolving into agentic systems that interact with external tools and environments, introducing new security risks such as indirect prompt injection attacks through untrusted external sources. Existing defenses mainly focus on blocking malicious content at inference time, and current red-teaming methods primarily optimize attack success. As a result, developers have limited visibility into how latent prompt injections emerge and propagate through agents. We propose PI-Hunter, an automated agentic auditing framework for proactive vulnerability exposure in LLM agents. PI-Hunter constructs realistic source-aware test cases and iteratively evolves them through feedback-driven exploration to induce agents to retrieve and reveal latent malicious instructions embedded within external environments. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks, agent architectures, attacks, and defenses demonstrate that PI-Hunter substantially improves vulnerability exposure and attack-surface coverage over strong automated red-teaming baselines, while remaining effective under existing prompt injection defenses.

</details>


### [202] [Semantic Identification of IoT Devices from Behavioral Primitives](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12793)
*Samuel Witt,Hassan Habibi Gharakheili*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper proposes identifying IoT devices using semantic representations of Manufacturer Usage Description (MUD) profiles rather than traditional packet/flow-based traffic patterns. By constructing ACE-level embeddings from MUD behavioral primitives, semantic matching remains robust under sparse overlap, unseen ACEs, and drifted hostnames, outperforming exact matching in early and sparse-traffic scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional IoT device identification relies on low-level traffic patterns (packets, flow records) that vary across deployments, software versions, and user interactions, limiting robustness. MUD profiles offer stable, policy-level behavioral descriptions, but exact ACE matching degrades under sparse or missing overlap. A semantic approach is needed to preserve identification evidence when exact overlap fails.

Method: The authors use 28 publicly available MUD profiles with 1,023 ACE instances to construct ACE-level semantic embeddings from compact behavioral text. They analyze geometric properties, apply whitening calibration, and evaluate semantic ACE matching against exact matching under three controlled variations: unseen ACEs, drifted hostnames, and partial runtime observation. They further validate on real IoT traffic traces exceeding 800,000 flows.

Result: ACE-level semantic representations preserve device-level distinctions better than whole-profile embeddings and remain effective after whitening. Exact ACE matching performs well under high canonical overlap but degrades sharply with sparse or absent overlap. Semantic ACE matching retains useful identification evidence across all conditions, provides stronger signals in early observation stages, and keeps the correct device among top-ranked candidates under sparse-overlap runtime traffic.

Conclusion: Semantic ACE matching using MUD profiles offers a robust alternative to exact matching for IoT device identification, particularly valuable during early observation and under sparse traffic conditions where traditional exact-overlap methods fail.

Abstract: Accurate identification of IoT devices is important for security management and policy enforcement. Existing approaches typically learn device signatures from packets or flow records. These methods operate on low-level communication observations whose traffic patterns may vary across deployments, software versions, and user interactions. This paper studies device identification using Manufacturer Usage Description (MUD) profiles. MUD profiles describe device behavior using Access Control Entries (ACEs), where each ACE represents a behavioral primitive consisting of protocol, endpoint, direction, and port semantics derived from device communication policy. Our contributions are threefold. First, using 28 publicly available MUD profiles containing 1,023 ACE instances, we construct ACE-level semantic representations from compact behavioral text and analyze their geometric properties. ACE-level representations preserve device-level behavioral distinctions more effectively than whole-profile embeddings and remain effective after whitening calibration. Second, we evaluate semantic ACE matching under controlled runtime variations, including unseen ACEs, drifted hostnames, and partial runtime observation. Exact ACE matching performs well when the overlap with the canonical MUD profile remains high, but degrades sharply when the overlap becomes sparse or disappears. In contrast, semantic ACE matching preserves useful identification evidence across these conditions. Third, we evaluate the same approach on real IoT traffic traces comprising more than 800,000 observed flows. Exact overlap remains the strongest signal when stable overlap exists, while semantic ACE matching provides stronger identification evidence during the early stages of observation, frequently retains the correct device among the highest-ranked candidates, and remains effective under sparse-overlap runtime traffic.

</details>


### [203] [A Privacy-Preserving Framework Using Remote Data Science for Inter-Institutional Student Retention Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12845)
*John Fields,K M Sajjadul Islam,Ruchitha Thota,Victor Chen,Praveen Madiraju*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: A PySyft-based remote data science framework with semi-air-gapped architecture enables three universities to collaboratively predict student retention without direct data access, achieving comparable Macro F1 scores (0.690–0.695) while maintaining FERPA compliance, and introduces Data-Type-Aware Templates for privacy-prioritized synthetic data generation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Institutions need to collaborate on student retention prediction using sensitive educational data, but FERPA regulations prohibit direct data sharing. Existing approaches like federated learning may be over-engineered for small-scale collaborations, motivating a simpler privacy-preserving alternative.

Method: A remote data science (RDS) framework built on PySyft with a semi-air-gapped architecture (high-side/low-side servers). Researchers submit models to the high-side server where sensitive data resides; only privacy-sanitized outputs emerge. Three synthetic data methods were compared, including a novel approach called Data-Type-Aware Templates that prioritizes privacy over distributional fidelity. Validation used N=720 historical student records from a small private university across three collaborating institutions.

Result: Consistent classification performance was achieved across all three institutions, with Macro F1 scores ranging narrowly from 0.690 to 0.695, while strictly maintaining FERPA compliance throughout the collaboration.

Conclusion: RDS-based PPML is technically feasible for educational settings and offers a practical, simpler alternative to federated learning for small-scale inter-institutional collaborations, with the proposed Data-Type-Aware Templates providing a viable privacy-first synthetic data strategy.

Abstract: This study explores privacy-preserving machine learning (PPML) techniques using the PySyft platform to enable collaborative prediction of student retention between institutions. We developed a remote data science (RDS) framework with a semi-air-gapped architecture consisting of high-side and low-side servers, allowing researchers from three universities to build predictive models on sensitive student data without direct data access. Using historical data from a small private university (N=720), we evaluated three synthetic data generation approaches and validated the framework through inter-institutional collaboration. The results demonstrate consistent classification performance across institutions (Macro F1: 0.690--0.695) while maintaining strict Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA) compliance. We also propose Data-Type-Aware Templates, a novel synthetic data method that prioritizes privacy over distributional fidelity. Our findings confirm that RDS-based PPML is technically feasible for educational settings and offers a practical alternative to federated learning for small-scale inter-institutional collaborations. The code is available at https://github.com/jtfields/NAIRR240195-Privacy-Preserving-Machine-Learning.

</details>


### [204] [DIG: Oracle-Guided Directed Input Generation for One-Day Vulnerabilities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13037)
*Andrew Bao,Haochen Zeng,Peng Chen,Stephen McCamant,Pen-Chung Yew*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: DIG leverages LLM analysis of patches to synthesize oracles that make vulnerability-triggering conditions explicit, then uses oracle-guided generator evolution (high-level) and oracle-instrumented directed fuzzing (low-level) to generate PoC inputs, triggering 80 CVEs and discovering 6 zero-days.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing directed fuzzing approaches rely on target-distance feedback and random mutation without explicitly identifying or solving necessary constraints for triggering vulnerabilities. Agentic approaches suffer from goal drift in long-horizon reasoning. One-day vulnerabilities present a unique opportunity: patches encode the necessary preconditions, yet no prior work systematically exploits this.

Method: DIG operates in two tiers: (1) An LLM analyzes the vulnerability patch to synthesize an oracle that formalizes triggering preconditions. (2) High-level: an agent performs oracle-guided generator evolution, inferring and solving constraints to directly produce inputs satisfying the oracle. (3) Low-level: the oracle is instrumented into the target binary, and branch-distance feedback from the oracle guides random mutation in directed fuzzing.

Result: DIG outperformed 2 state-of-the-art agents and 10 fuzzers across 138 real-world CVEs. It triggered 80 vulnerabilities, surpassing the best baseline by 40% (57 vs. 80). DIG exclusively triggered 9 vulnerabilities no other technique could trigger. It achieved faster triggering in 92.9% of cases versus the average of other tools, with &gt;100x speedup in 48.8% of cases and a maximum speedup of 3,664x. DIG also discovered 6 previously unknown zero-day vulnerabilities in widely deployed libraries.

Conclusion: Explicitly extracting and instrumenting vulnerability preconditions from patches via LLM-synthesized oracles is a highly effective strategy for one-day PoC generation. The dual-level approach — combining agent-based constraint solving with oracle-guided fuzzing — overcomes the limitations of both pure directed fuzzing and pure agentic reasoning, significantly advancing the state of the art.

Abstract: One-day vulnerabilities pose significant risks due to delayed or incomplete patch adoption. Generating proof-of-concept (PoC) inputs is therefore essential for assessing real-world impact. The key challenge is identifying necessary constraints for triggering the vulnerability and solving them effectively. Existing directed fuzzing approaches prioritize inputs toward target locations, but neither explicitly identify necessary constraints nor solve them effectively, relying instead on target-distance feedback and random mutation. Agentic approaches show strong potential through code reasoning and structured input generation, but goal drift in long-horizon reasoning limits their effectiveness.
  DIG addresses this challenge by exploiting a key property of one-day vulnerabilities: patches often reveal necessary preconditions for triggering. DIG uses an LLM to analyze the patch and synthesize an oracle making these conditions explicit. The oracle supports effective PoC generation at two levels. At the high level, DIG performs oracle-guided generator evolution, where an agent infers and solves constraints to satisfy the oracle. At the low level, DIG instruments the oracle into the target program and uses branch-distance feedback to guide random mutation in directed fuzzing. Evaluation shows DIG outperforms 2 state-of-the-art agents and 10 fuzzers across 138 real-world CVEs. DIG triggers 80 vulnerabilities, surpassing prior results and outperforming the best baseline by 40% (57 vs. 80 CVEs). Notably, DIG exclusively triggers 9 vulnerabilities no existing technique can trigger. Compared to the average of other tools, DIG triggers vulnerabilities faster in 92.9% of cases, achieving over 100x speedup in 48.8% of cases, with a maximum speedup of 3,664x. Beyond one-day PoC generation, DIG uncovers 6 previously unknown vulnerabilities in widely deployed libraries, enabling zero-day discovery.

</details>


### [205] [LNTest: A Testbed for Evaluating Bitcoin Lightning Network-Based Botnets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12887)
*Thomas Bakaysa,Ahmet Kurt,Abdul-Salem Beibitkhan,Jesus Maria Romo Diaz de Leon,Tag Kalat,Joshua Kramer,Estela Rodriguez,Abraham Watkins,Abdullah Aydeger*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: LNTest is the first reusable testbed for Lightning Network-based botnets, revealing that D-LNBot's autonomous topology formation produces fragile clustered chains, command propagation scales linearly rather than as previously claimed, and topology type determines takedown resilience patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior LN-based botnet designs (LNBot, D-LNBot) exist only as simulation-evaluated prototypes, leaving open questions about real-world topology formation behavior, actual propagation complexity, and resilience to takedown attacks. A reproducible, containerized testbed is needed to empirically answer these questions.

Method: LNTest — a containerized testbed using Docker-based Core Lightning nodes over a shared Bitcoin Core regtest chain, supporting three overlay topology modes: deterministic chain, autonomous peer discovery, and user-supplied graphs. Controlled experiments compare topology formation, command propagation, and takedown resilience across botnet structures.

Result: (1) D-LNBot's autonomous formation yields clustered chains with clique-bridge structures, not uniform chains; bridge removal fragments the network. (2) Command propagation scales as Θ(n), not O(m log n), with no benefit from higher neighbor connectivity. (3) Takedown effectiveness is topology-dependent: uniform-degree chains resist targeted removal but are fragile under random failure; scale-free topologies show the reverse; autonomous clustered chains are fragile under both strategies.

Conclusion: LNTest provides the first empirical, reproducible framework for studying LN-based botnet behaviors. The findings correct prior assumptions about propagation complexity and topology formation, and demonstrate that autonomous, realistic topologies are the most vulnerable to takedown, offering actionable insights for defenders. LNTest is released as open source.

Abstract: Bitcoin's Lightning Network (LN) can be exploited as a covert, low-cost command-and-control (C&C) channel for botnets, as demonstrated by the LNBot and D-LNBot designs. However, both remain proof-of-concept prototypes evaluated only through simulation, leaving key questions about real-world topology formation, propagation complexity, and resilience to takedowns unanswered. We present LNTest, the first reusable testbed for LN-based botnets, built from Core Lightning nodes containerized with Docker over a shared Bitcoin Core regtest chain. LNTest supports three overlay topology modes (a deterministic chain, autonomous peer discovery, and user-supplied graphs), enabling controlled experiments across different botnet structures. Using LNTest, we report three main findings. First, D-LNBot's autonomous formation protocol does not produce the uniform chain from its design; instead, it creates a clustered chain in which cliques are linked by bridge nodes whose removal fragments the network. Second, command propagation scales linearly with botnet size ($Θ(n)$), not the $O(m \log n)$ previously claimed, and gains nothing from higher neighbor connectivity. Third, the overlay topology determines the effectiveness of takedown strategies: uniform-degree chains resist targeted removal but fragment under random failure, scale-free topologies show the opposite pattern, and the autonomous clustered chain is fragile under both, making it the most vulnerable of the three. LNTest is released as open source, with a script that reproduces all our experiments, to support reproducible research on LN-based botnet defenses.

</details>


### [206] [Who Pays the Price? Stakeholder-Centric Prompt Injection Benchmarking for Real-world Web Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13385)
*Zihao Wang,Yiming Li,Yutong Wu,Zheyu Liu,Kangjie Chen,Fok Kar Wai,Pin-Yu Chen,Vrizlynn L. L. Thing,Bo Li,Dacheng Tao,Tianwei Zhang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: StakeBench is a stakeholder-centric benchmark for evaluating prompt-injection attacks on LLM-based web agents. Unlike prior attack-centric benchmarks, it categorizes harm across different stakeholders (users, sellers, platforms), decomposes attacks into concrete objectives, and uses complementary outcome/process metrics. Results show no attack objective is reliably resisted, revealing distinct failure modes including stealthy parasitism, misaligned disruption, and compounded failure.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing prompt-injection security benchmarks adopt an attack-centric perspective, focusing on technical feasibility while ignoring how harm distributes across different stakeholders. In practice, the same exploit produces asymmetric consequences for different entities (users, sellers, platforms), and attack effectiveness varies by target. A stakeholder-centric evaluation framework is needed to capture these real-world nuances.

Method: The authors introduce StakeBench, a stakeholder-centric benchmark that: (1) distinguishes between affected entities (user, seller, platform), (2) decomposes attacks into concrete objectives, and (3) evaluates each case using complementary outcome-level and process-level metrics. The benchmark systematically categorizes and attributes harm in real-world web agent systems.

Result: Results reveal substantial, heterogeneous vulnerabilities: no single attack objective is reliably resisted by current agents. Failures distribute across three qualitatively distinct modes — stealthy parasitism (attack succeeds without disrupting the user's delegated task), misaligned disruption (task disrupted without attack success), and compounded failure (both adversarial objective and task integrity simultaneously violated). These patterns are missed by conventional attack-centric evaluation.

Conclusion: Conventional evaluation misses critical vulnerability patterns in LLM-based web agents. Stakeholder-aware assessment is necessary for real-world deployments, as prompt-injection risks produce asymmetric, victim-dependent harms that cannot be captured by attack-centric benchmarks alone.

Abstract: Web agents driven by large language models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in real-world environments, where they operate over untrusted web content and execute actions with direct consequences. This makes them vulnerable to prompt-injection attacks, in which seemingly benign content embeds adversarial instructions that manipulate agent behaviour. Existing security benchmarks adopt an \textit{attack-centric} perspective, focusing on the technical feasibility of injections while overlooking the nuanced distribution of resulting harms. In practice, however, prompt-injection risk is victim-dependent: a single exploit can produce asymmetric consequences for different stakeholders, and the same attack pattern may exhibit substantially different effectiveness depending on whom it targets. To capture these properties, we introduce \textbf{\sysname}, a \textit{stakeholder-centric} benchmark to systematically categorize and attribute harm in real-world web agent systems. It distinguishes between affected entities (e.g., user, seller, platform), decomposes the attacks into concrete objectives, and evaluates each case with complementary outcome- and process-level metrics. Our results reveal substantial and heterogeneous vulnerabilities: not a single attack objective is reliably resisted by current agents, and failures distribute across qualitatively distinct modes ranging from \emph{stealthy parasitism} (attack succeeds without disrupting the user's delegated task) to \emph{misaligned disruption} (task disrupted without attack success) and \emph{compounded failure} (both adversarial objective and task integrity simultaneously violated). These patterns are missed by conventional evaluation, highlighting the need for stakeholder-aware assessment of LLM-based agents in real-world deployments. Benchmark is available at https://github.com/StakeBench/SBC.

</details>


### [207] [MAStrike: Shapley-Guided Collusive Red-Teaming on Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12918)
*Chejian Xu,Zhaorun Chen,Jingyang Zhang,Freddy Lecue,Avni Kothari,Sarah Tan,Wenbo Guo,Bo Li*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: MAStrike is a closed-loop red-teaming framework for hierarchical multi-agent systems that uses agent-level Shapley values to identify vulnerable agent coalitions and generate coordinated, role-aware adversarial attacks, outperforming heuristic baselines across finance, software engineering, and CRM domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing red-teaming for MAS is limited to heuristic target selection and isolated message perturbation, failing to answer (1) which agents most critically affect system safety, and (2) how compromised agents can collude to bypass defenses. Hierarchical MAS deployed in high-stakes domains have an expanded, distributed attack surface vulnerable to privilege escalation and cross-agent collusion.

Method: MAStrike introduces: (1) the first agent-level Shapley value analysis for MAS, quantifying each agent's marginal contribution to system robustness under task-specific distributions; (2) guided identification of vulnerable agent coalitions with coordinated, role-aware adversarial manipulations; (3) iterative refinement via structured causal diagnosis that attributes failures to uncompromised agents blocking adversarial attempts; plus a comprehensive benchmark with controllable hierarchical topologies.
