Result: Extensive experiments across MAS built on multiple frontier models show MAStrike substantially outperforms heuristic baselines. The analysis uncovers non-trivial Shapley value distributions and higher-order interaction structures among agents, revealing critical vulnerabilities and coordination patterns overlooked by prior single-agent or template-based methods.

Conclusion: Agent-level Shapley value attribution effectively identifies safety-critical agents in hierarchical MAS, and coordinated collusive attacks guided by this attribution expose vulnerabilities that isolated perturbation methods miss, demonstrating the need for coalition-aware security evaluation in multi-agent deployments.

Abstract: Hierarchical multi-agent systems (MAS) are rapidly being deployed in high-stakes workflows across domains such as finance and software engineering. In these systems, safety and security are inherently distributed across role-specialized agents, significantly expanding the attack surface, particularly under coordinated adversarial behaviors such as privilege escalation and cross-agent collusion. Existing red-teaming approaches for MAS remain limited: they rely on heuristic selection of target agents and perturb isolated message streams, leaving critical questions unanswered as which agents are most responsible for system safety, and how compromised agents can coordinate to bypass defenses. We propose MAStrike, a closed-loop framework for collusive red-teaming in hierarchical MAS. We propose the first agent-level Shapley value analysis for MAS, quantifying each agent's marginal contribution to system robustness under task-specific distributions. GGuided by this attribution, MAStrike identifies vulnerable agent coalitions and generates coordinated, role-aware adversarial manipulations. These attacks are iteratively refined through structured causal diagnosis, attributing failure cases to uncompromised agents that block adversarial attempts. We further build a comprehensive MAS red-teaming benchmark and controllable environments spanning diverse hierarchical topologies and domains, including finance, software engineering, and CRM. Extensive experiments across MAS built on multiple frontier models show that MAStrike substantially outperforms heuristic baselines. Our analysis further uncovers non-trivial Shapley value distributions and higher-order interaction structures among agents, revealing critical vulnerabilities and coordination patterns that are overlooked by prior single-agent or template-based methods.

</details>


### [208] [ViPER: Vision-based Packing-Aware Encoder for Robust Malware Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12949)
*Fatima Qaiser,Bisma Tahir,Muhammad Abid Mughal,Nauman Shamim*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: ViPER is a malware detection system that converts binaries to grayscale images and uses a Vision Transformer with a dual-head architecture to jointly learn malware classification and packing detection. A packing-aware gating mechanism enables distinct decision boundaries for packed vs. unpacked samples, addressing the critical failure mode of executable packing. It achieves state-of-the-art results on 200K Windows PE images.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Visualization-based malware detection is an evasion-resistant alternative to disassembly-based pipelines, but executable packing produces high-entropy images that obscure structural patterns. Since packing is common in both benign and malicious software, packing state alone cannot reliably indicate maliciousness. Existing methods lack a unified supervised framework to address this challenge.

Method: ViPER uses a LoRA-adapted ViT-B/14 backbone with a dual-head architecture: one head for malware classification, another for packing detection. A packing-aware gating mechanism conditions malware predictions on the inferred packing state, creating separate decision boundaries for packed and unpacked inputs. Training uses frequency-weighted losses with stratified sampling over joint class-packing strata to handle packing label skew.

Result: On 200,000 Windows PE byteplot images, ViPER achieves balanced accuracy of 0.8521, ROC-AUC of 0.9260, AUPR of 0.9279 for malware detection, and packing detection AUC of 0.9949. It outperforms representative state-of-the-art baselines across all primary metrics.

Conclusion: Joint learning of malware classification and packing detection with a gating mechanism effectively addresses the packing-induced failure mode in vision-based malware detection. The approach demonstrates that conditioning malware predictions on inferred packing state significantly improves robustness and overall detection performance.

Abstract: Visualization-based malware detection maps raw binary bytes to grayscale images and applies learned visual classifiers, providing an evasion-resistant and disassembly-free alternative to conventional analysis pipelines. However, executable packing remains a critical failure mode: packed binaries produce high-entropy images that obscure the structural patterns these models rely on. Because packing is also prevalent in benign software (e.g., for compression or copy protection), packing state alone is not a reliable indicator of maliciousness, and existing approaches do not address this challenge within a unified supervised framework. We present ViPER, a Vision-based Packing-Aware Encoder for Robust malware detection. ViPER builds on a LoRA-adapted ViT-B/14 backbone with a dual-head architecture that jointly learns malware classification and packing detection. A packing-aware gating mechanism conditions malware predictions on the inferred packing state, enabling distinct decision boundaries for packed and unpacked inputs. To address packing label skew during training, we employ frequency-weighted losses with stratified sampling over joint class-packing strata. Evaluated on 200,000 Windows PE byteplot images, ViPER achieves a balanced accuracy of 0.8521, ROC-AUC of 0.9260, and AUPR of 0.9279, outperforming representative state-of-the-art baselines across all primary metrics, while attaining a packing detection AUC of 0.9949.

</details>


### [209] [SoK: The Constant Time Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13000)
*Billy Bob Brumley*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper systematizes constant-time programming models, identifies gaps between theoretical protections and real-world assumptions, and presents an offensive methodology that uncovers a specification-level timing vulnerability in private key loading affecting both OpenSSL and BoringSSL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite constant-time programming being the primary defense against timing side-channel attacks, the definition of "constant time" is inconsistent across academia and industry, creating a dangerous gap between what models protect against and what cryptographic specifications assume.

Method: The authors systematize existing constant-time models and their evolution, identify recurring gaps between model protections and specification assumptions, and develop an offensive methodology to discover timing vulnerabilities that originate outside the cryptographic primitive boundary. They then apply this methodology to real-world libraries (OpenSSL, BoringSSL).

Result: A specification-level vulnerability in private key loading was discovered and confirmed in both OpenSSL and BoringSSL. Counterintuitively, BoringSSL — which adopts an explicitly stricter threat model — exhibits a per-observation signal several orders of magnitude stronger than OpenSSL.

Conclusion: The gap between constant-time models and specification assumptions represents a systemic weakness. Even libraries with stricter threat models can paradoxically leak more through specification-level oversights, underscoring the need to align constant-time guarantees end-to-end, including beyond the cryptographic primitive itself.

Abstract: Constant time programming patterns is the primary defense against timing attacks on cryptographic implementations, yet what "constant time" means varies across academia and industry. This work systematizes constant time models and their evolution, identifies a recurring gap between what models protect and what specifications assume, and distills an offensive methodology for discovering timing vulnerabilities that originate outside the cryptographic primitive boundary. Applying this methodology, we locate a specification-level vulnerability related to private key loading, and confirm the leak in both OpenSSL and BoringSSL. Counterintuitively, BoringSSL's per-observation signal is several orders of magnitude stronger than OpenSSL's, despite an explicitly stricter threat model.

</details>


### [210] [The Emergence of Autonomous Penetration Capabilities in Large Language Model-Powered AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13079)
*Jiaqi Luo,Jiarun Dai,Zhile Chen,Jia Xu,Weibing Wang,Yawen Duan,Brian Tse,Geng Hong,Xudong Pan,Yuan Zhang,Min Yang*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper presents a new evaluation framework for assessing LLMs' autonomous penetration testing capabilities using 300 realistic target servers across two difficulty tiers. Evaluating 19 models reveals success rates between 10.7%–69.3%, with capability scaling alongside general model improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing evaluations of autonomous penetration capabilities in AI systems suffer from opaque methodologies, unrealistic scenarios, and excessive prior knowledge given to LLMs, making them unable to accurately measure how well modern AI can autonomously conduct cyberattacks in realistic, high-impact settings.

Method: The authors construct a two-component evaluation framework: (1) target servers with two tiers — Tier 1 has one secure service alongside a vulnerable service, Tier 2 has three secure services, totaling 300 servers; (2) a general-purpose agent scaffolding with standard cybersecurity tools and no target-specific prior knowledge. Nineteen open-weight and proprietary LLMs are evaluated on their ability to independently identify and exploit vulnerabilities to gain unauthorized access.

Result: Current LLMs achieve autonomous penetration success rates ranging from 10.7% to 69.3%. Additionally, autonomous penetration capability is found to improve in tandem with advances in overall model capability.

Conclusion: Modern LLMs already possess non-trivial autonomous penetration capabilities, and these capabilities are likely to grow as base model capabilities improve, underscoring the importance of robust evaluation frameworks for monitoring this critical red-line capability.

Abstract: Nowadays, the autonomous execution of cyberattacks capable of causing substantial real-world harm is widely regarded as one of the critical red lines that frontier AI systems must not cross. Within this broader red-line scenario, autonomous penetration represents a core enabling capability and subtask: the ability of LLM-powered AI systems to independently conduct adversarial operations against a target server without human intervention, identify and exploit vulnerabilities, and obtain unauthorized access or control. A growing body of work has sought to assess the autonomous penetration capabilities of AI systems. However, existing evaluations often employ opaque methodologies, rely on unrealistic or overly simplified penetration-testing scenarios, or provide LLMs with excessive prior knowledge and task-specific guidance, and cannot accurately capture the extent to which modern AI systems can autonomously perform this core capability within broader high-impact cyberattack scenarios.
  To address these limitations, we construct a new autonomous penetration evaluation framework consisting of two components: target servers and agent scaffolding. Specifically, on the target-server side, we design two levels of target environments based on the number of secure services without known vulnerabilities deployed alongside a vulnerable service: Tier~1 (one secure service) and Tier~2 (three secure services), resulting in a total of 300 target servers. Meanwhile, the agent scaffolding adopts a general-purpose agent architecture equipped with a set of general-purpose cybersecurity tools, without any target-specific prior knowledge. We evaluate 19 open-weight and proprietary LLMs, and find that current models achieve penetration success rates ranging from 10.7% to 69.3%. Moreover, we observe that autonomous penetration capability continues to improve alongside advances in overall model capability.

</details>


### [211] [The Invisible Ink of the Android Malware World: A Longitudinal Study on the Usage of Covert Communication Channels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13107)
*Zeya Umayya,Manan Aggarwal,Manan Chugh,Mann Nariya,Yogesh Kaushik,Sambuddho Chakravarty*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: First longitudinal study of covert channel (proxy, VPN, Tor) usage in Android malware (2009–2025), analyzing 3.5M APKs. Found exponential growth from 0.30% to 50%, spanning 511 malware families, and validated CCs across 17 countries via static+dynamic analysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Despite the critical threat posed by malware exploiting proxies, VPNs, and Tor to hide C2 communications, no prior longitudinal study has systematically examined how Android malware applications use covert channels to evade detection.

Method: Developed a multistage analysis pipeline combining static and dynamic analysis to examine both system-level and network-level features. Applied to a corpus of 3.5M Android malware samples spanning 2009 to July 2025. Static validation rules identified CC-using APKs; dynamic analysis validated live CC connections by capturing contacted IP addresses and confirming covert channel presence.

Result: Static analysis uncovered 288K APKs using covert channels across 511 malware families, with CC usage growing from 0.30% (2012) to 50% (2025). Dynamic analysis identified 19,308 unique IPs in 85 countries, with explicit CC validation for 59 IPs across 17 countries. Longitudinal study (16 years) revealed evolving CC strategies: some malware adopted multiple CCs simultaneously, others switched periodically — one family switched 40 times between 2019–2025.

Conclusion: Covert channel usage in Android malware has grown exponentially and become increasingly sophisticated, with adversaries dynamically adapting their evasion strategies over time. This highlights the urgent need for defensive mechanisms that account for evolving CC-based evasion techniques.

Abstract: Proxies, VPNs and Tor have long helped the privacy community and users in censored regions to fight censorship. However, the same tools can be maliciously exploited by malware and botnets to conceal their communication to external command and control servers. Despite being a critical concern fueled by the proliferation of malware based attacks, no longitudinal studies have analyzed how malware applications use covert channels (CC) to evade detection.
  We fill this gap by performing the first study of the usage of covert channels in the Android malware ecosystem. To that end, we develop a multistage pipeline that combines static and dynamic analysis to investigate both system and network-level features. We applied this pipeline on a corpus of 3.5M Android malware spanning 2009 to July 2025. Our carefully crafted static validation rules uncovered 288K APKs that used CCs spanning 511 malware families and CC usage growing exponentially from 0.30\% (2012) to 50\% (2025). Overall, in dynamic analysis, we identified 19,308 unique IP addresses being contacted in 85 countries, out of which we were able to explicitly validate the presence of CCs for 59 IP addresses across 17 countries. Further, we performed a longitudinal dataset study spanning over 16 years for CC based malware and found that CC usage has evolved, \textit{e.g.,} some malware adopted by using more than one CCs; others switched between them periodically (one family switched CC usage 40 times from 2019 to 2025).

</details>


### [212] [An Assessment Framework for Application-Level Cryptographic Agility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13425)
*Navaneeth Rameshan,Gregoire Messmer*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper proposes a seven-dimensional component-based framework to systematically assess cryptographic agility in APIs, evaluates six major cryptographic APIs, and identifies three critical gaps (no intent-based key creation, no policy-driven algorithm selection, no first-class algorithm transformation operations) that individually block post-quantum migration despite decades of API evolution.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The post-quantum cryptographic transition demands complete algorithm replacement across all software, but existing cryptographic APIs were never designed for agility. The term "cryptographic agility" conflates multiple independent capabilities (algorithm replacement, policy-driven selection, implementation substitution) without structured decomposition, making systematic evaluation and API development impossible. This lack of rigor directly impedes the quantum migration.

Method: The authors introduce a component-based assessment framework with seven orthogonal dimensions: three coupling dimensions (measuring what application code knows about algorithms/providers), one cross-cutting decoupling mechanism, one governance authority dimension, and two agility enablers (measuring actual migration capability). The framework is non-linear and captures non-hierarchical profiles. Six representative APIs are evaluated: PKCS#11, OpenSSL 3.0, JCA, Google Tink, AWS KMS, and HashiCorp Vault Transit.

Result: Evaluation reveals three pervasive, independent gaps across all six APIs: (1) no system supports intent-based key creation, (2) none provides policy-driven algorithm selection distinct from access control, and (3) none offers first-class operations for algorithm transformation of existing keys. Each gap alone is sufficient to prevent agile migration.

Conclusion: The post-quantum transition remains an unresolved software engineering problem because cryptographic APIs lack fundamental agility capabilities. The identified three gaps explain why migration remains blocked despite decades of progress, and the proposed framework provides a structured basis for designing truly agile cryptographic APIs.

Abstract: The impending post-quantum transition to new cryptography will require complete replacement of algorithms within all software. The cryptographic APIs used today make this transition challenging because they were not designed with agility as a concern. There is no method for systematically assessing cryptographic agility as an overall ability. In addition to this, the term itself refers to multiple independent capabilities. Specifically, it includes replacing algorithms, selecting by policy, and substituting implementations. This lack of structured decomposition limits both the evaluation of systems and the development of cryptographically agile APIs.
  We introduce a component-based assessment framework that characterizes application-level cryptographic agility along seven orthogonal dimensions: three coupling dimensions that measure what the application code knows about algorithms and providers, a cross-cutting decoupling mechanism, a governance authority dimension, and two agility enablers that measure actual migration capability. The framework is non-linear and captures non-hierarchical profiles: a system may achieve high operation decoupling yet low creation decoupling, or strong versioning without externalized configuration.
  We evaluate six representative APIs (PKCS#11, OpenSSL~3.0, JCA, Google Tink, AWS KMS, and HashiCorp Vault Transit) against the framework, revealing three pervasive and independent gaps: no system supports intent-based key creation, none provides policy-driven algorithm selection (as distinct from access control), and none offers dedicated/first-class operations for algorithm transformation of existing keys. These gaps are individually sufficient to prevent agile migration, explaining why the post-quantum transition remains a software engineering problem despite decades of API progress.

</details>


### [213] [Intent-Based Cryptographic API Design for Cryptographic Agility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13445)
*Navaneeth Rameshan,Gregoire Messmer*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper proposes design principles for cryptographically agile APIs to address the software engineering challenge of migrating to post-quantum cryptography. It derives principles from five architectural characteristics and demonstrates their implementation through Protocol Buffers API patterns, including intent-based scoped key creation, abstract policy APIs, and stable key identifiers with evolution tracking—enabling algorithm transitions without application code rewrites.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Organizations face a major challenge transitioning to post-quantum cryptography because existing cryptographic APIs are algorithm-specific, lack policy-based algorithm selection, and provide no mechanism for migrating existing keys. This makes the transition largely a software engineering problem, as detailed in the companion assessment framework.

Method: The authors derive design principles for cryptographically agile APIs from five fundamental architectural characteristics: Abstraction, Stability, Temporal Flexibility, Separation, and Extensibility. These principles are demonstrated through Protocol Buffers API design patterns, featuring an intent vocabulary based on scopes for algorithm-independent key creation, an abstract policy API for cryptographic governance, and stable key identifiers supporting evolution operations (rotation, transformation, migration).

Result: The proposed approach decouples key creation from algorithm identities, enables transparent algorithm substitutions within applicable scopes, and supports key evolution with full traceability of identity and history. This transforms cryptographic updates into an operational process requiring no application code changes.

Conclusion: By adopting these agile API design principles, organizations can make post-quantum cryptographic migration an operational rather than developmental concern, eliminating the need to rewrite application code when algorithms change.

Abstract: As organizations move toward post-quantum cryptography, they face the major challenge of updating cryptographic algorithms across large, complex software portfolios. However, most cryptographic APIs in use today were designed around specific algorithms. These APIs expect explicit use of specific algorithms, provide little or no support for policy-based algorithm selection, and offer no straightforward way to migrate existing keys to newer algorithms. This makes the transition to post-quantum cryptography challenging. The companion assessment framework identifies the barriers to cryptographic agility and explains why algorithm transition is largely a software engineering problem.
  To address the limitations of current cryptographic APIs, we identify the principles necessary to design a cryptographically agile API. The design principles are derived from five fundamental architectural characteristics (Abstraction, Stability, Temporal Flexibility, Separation, and Extensibility). We also show how the design principles can be implemented using several examples of Protocol Buffers API design patterns. In particular, we present an intent vocabulary that is based on scopes which allows for decoupling key creation from algorithm identities. It also supports transparent substitutions of algorithms in the applicable scope. Cryptographic governance is enabled by an abstract policy API that does not prescribe the policy format. Keys are represented by stable identifiers and support key evolution operations (rotation, transformation, migration), facilitating migration between algorithms and providers while tracking both the original key identity and its evolution history. With this approach, updating cryptography becomes an operational process without the need to rewrite application code.

</details>


### [214] [Differentially Private Hierarchical Heavy Hitters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13563)
*Ari Biswas,Graham Cormode,Yaron Kanza,Divesh Srivastava,Zhengyi Zhou*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: This paper studies the problem of releasing Hierarchical Heavy Hitters (HHH) under differential privacy, in both streaming and non-streaming settings. Key findings include: in the non-streaming case, relative error for residual count estimation is independent of hierarchy height and number of heavy hitters; in the streaming case, despite high global sensitivity of approximation functions, absolute error remains independent of available space.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While HHH detection in data streams has been extensively studied, the problem of privately releasing HHH when the underlying data is sensitive has remained unexplored. This gap motivates the authors to investigate differentially private mechanisms for HHH release.

Method: The paper analyzes HHH release under differential privacy in two paradigms: (1) the non-streaming (offline) setting, where the entire dataset is available, and (2) the streaming setting, where data arrives continuously and space-constrained approximation algorithms are required. The analysis leverages properties of local/global sensitivity of counting queries and HHH approximation functions.

Result: Two main results: (1) In the non-streaming setting, the relative error in estimating residual counts for any prefix is independent of both the hierarchy height and the number of heavy hitters. (2) In the streaming setting, although exact HHH has low global sensitivity (1-sensitive counting queries), streaming approximation functions introduce high global sensitivity linear in the space budget; despite this, the absolute error for frequency estimation remains independent of the available space.

Conclusion: The paper establishes the first differentially private mechanisms for HHH release, demonstrating that privacy can be achieved without error dependence on hierarchy depth or space constraints, thus enabling practical private HHH computation in both offline and streaming environments.

Abstract: The task of finding _Hierarchical_ Heavy Hitters (HHH) was introduced by Cormode et al. [VLDB 2003] as a generalisation of the heavy hitter problem. While finding HHH in data streams has been studied extensively, the question of releasing HHH when the underlying data is private remains unexplored. In this paper, we study differentially private HHH release in both the streaming and non-streaming setting. In the non-streaming setting, we show the surprising result that the relative error in estimating the residual count for any prefix is independent of the height of the hierarchy and the number of heavy hitters in the stream. Meanwhile, in the streaming setting, although the exact version of HHH has low global sensitivity (as counting queries are 1-sensitive), the approximation functions due to streaming have high global sensitivity, linear in the available space. Despite this obstacle, we show that the absolute error for estimating frequencies in the steaming setting is independent of the available space.

</details>


### [215] [Beyond the IT Checklist: Engineering a Reasonable Standard of Care for Cyber Safety](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13612)
*Matthew E. Jablonski,Linton Wells,Kathryn B. Laskey,F. Brett Berlin*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: Current U.S. cyber policy treats administrative compliance and incident documentation as proxies for safety, an approach inadequate for cyber-physical systems where digital failures cause physical harm. Analyzing 292 policy documents (2000–2025), the authors find obligations concentrated in the Anticipate phase with poor alignment to physics-based hazards in Withstand/Recover phases. They propose a modernized standard of care based on hazard traceability, structured assurance cases, and cyber resiliency engineering, paired with economic incentives.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: U.S. cyber policy for critical infrastructure relies on documentation of controls and incident reports as proxies for safety, but this framework fails for cyber-physical systems (CPS), where digital failures can cause kinetic (physical) harm. There is a need to examine whether current operationalizations of "reasonable care" adequately address the resilience requirements of CPS environments.

Method: The authors construct and code a corpus of 292 critical infrastructure policy documents spanning 2000–2025. They map how "reasonable care" is operationalized across the NIST SP 800-160 Vol. 2 resilience lifecycle (Anticipate, Withstand, Recover, Adapt phases), identifying where obligations are concentrated, what standards are referenced, and how hazards are addressed across sectors.

Result: Three major disconnects were identified: (1) miscalibrated delegated standards that are IT-focused and misaligned with physics-based hazards; (2) recovery defined as notification rather than engineered navigation of incidents; and (3) uneven adaptation requirements across sectors. Obligations concentrate in the Anticipate phase with emphasis on administrative compliance, while Withstand and Recover phases rely on delegated references to control catalogs poorly suited to cyber-physical threats.

Conclusion: The authors propose a modernized standard of care anchored in hazard-specific traceability, structured assurance cases, and cyber resiliency engineering. They recommend that federal policy pair these engineering obligations with targeted economic incentives to make resilient architectures for critical infrastructure a viable business decision rather than an unfunded mandate.

Abstract: Current U.S. cyber policy, centered on security, often treats documentation of controls and incident reports as a proxy for safety in the built environment. This paper argues that such an approach is inadequate for cyber-physical systems, where digital failures can produce kinetic harm. We construct and code a corpus of critical infrastructure policy documents (N=292, 2000-2025) to examine how "reasonable care" is operationalized across the NIST SP 800-160 Vol.~2 resilience lifecycle. The resulting maps show that obligations are concentrated in the Anticipate phase and emphasize administrative compliance, while Withstand and Recover phases rely heavily on delegated references to IT-focused control catalogs that are poorly aligned with physics-based hazards. We identify three major disconnects: miscalibrated delegated standards, recovery defined as notification rather than engineered navigation, and uneven adaptation requirements across sectors. We then propose a modernized standard of care anchored in hazard-specific traceability, structured assurance cases, and cyber resiliency engineering. Finally, we recommend that federal policy pair these engineering obligations with targeted incentives so that resilient architectures for critical infrastructure become a viable business decision rather than an unfunded expectation.

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [216] [ToolSense: A Diagnostic Framework for Auditing Parametric Tool Knowledge in LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12451)
*Ashutosh Hathidara,Sai Shruthi Sistla,Sebastian Schreiber,Sahil Bansal*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces ToolSense, a diagnostic framework that reveals parametric tool retrievers suffer from a knowledge-retrieval dissociation: they perform well on standard benchmarks with fully-specified queries but collapse (~50-64pp drop) on realistic, ambiguous queries, often performing worse than embedding baselines and scoring near-random on factual probes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing parametric tool retrieval benchmarks (e.g., ToolBench) use verbose, fully-specified queries and constrained decoding, which obscure whether LLM-based retrievers genuinely understand the tools they retrieve. This creates a false sense of capability and masks a critical bottleneck in agentic LLM deployment over large tool catalogs.

Method: The authors propose ToolSense, an LLM-powered diagnostic framework that auto-generates three benchmarks from any tool catalog: (1) a Realistic Retrieval Benchmark (RRB) with ambiguity tiers, (2) an MCQ probing benchmark, and (3) a QA probing benchmark. They apply ToolSense to ToolBench (~47k tools) and evaluate five parametric model training configurations.

Result: On RRB queries, several parametric configurations drop by ~50-64 percentage points compared to fully-specified ToolBench benchmarks, falling below the embedding-model baseline. Despite strong retrieval scores, some models perform near-random on factual probes, demonstrating a knowledge-retrieval dissociation.

Conclusion: Parametric tool retrievers exhibit a knowledge-retrieval dissociation: they can retrieve tools without genuine semantic understanding. ToolSense provides a more rigorous diagnostic regime, and the authors open-source both the framework and benchmarks to facilitate robust evaluation of tool retrieval systems.

Abstract: Large language models deployed as agents over large tool catalogs face a critical tool-retrieval bottleneck. As embedding-based retrieval approaches rely on compact encoders that may under-capture specialized tool semantics, parametric tool retrieval addresses this by encoding each tool as a virtual token appended to the LLM vocabulary, fine-tuned in two stages (memorization then retrieval SFT) to use the LLM as a retriever, achieving strong performance on standard ToolBench retrieval benchmarks. Yet these benchmarks use verbose, fully-specified queries, and their evaluation applies constrained decoding that restricts outputs to valid token paths, neither reveals whether the model actually understands its tools. We introduce \textbf{ToolSense}, an open-source LLM-powered diagnostic framework that takes any tool catalog as input and automatically generates three benchmarks: a Realistic Retrieval Benchmark (RRB) with queries at three ambiguity tiers, an MCQ probing benchmark, and a QA probing benchmark. Applying ToolSense to ToolBench (~47k tools) and evaluating five parametric model training configurations reveals a knowledge-retrieval dissociation: on RRB queries, several configurations collapse by ~50-64 percentage points compared to fully-specified ToolBench benchmarks, falling below the embedding-model baseline. Additionally, despite strong retrieval performance, some models score near-random on factual probes, suggesting a knowledge-retrieval dissociation. We open-source the ToolSense framework and the ToolBench diagnostic benchmarks at https://github.com/SAP/toolsense.

</details>


### [217] [Arbor: Tree Search as a Cognition Layer for Autonomous Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12563)
*Neha Prakriya,Chaojun Hou,Zheng Gong,Huasha Zhao,Xi Zhao,Mou Li,Zhenyu Gu,Emad Barsoum*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Arbor introduces a multi-agent framework using structured tree search as a shared cognitive layer for autonomous optimization in large stateful action spaces, achieving up to 193% inference throughput-latency Pareto improvement on LLM inference optimization through a checks-and-balances architecture pairing Orchestrator and Critic agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior autonomous optimization systems operate on isolated targets with stateless evaluation and cannot coordinate across the full inference stack (application, framework, compiler, kernel, hardware). Achieving peak LLM inference performance has historically required coordinated engineering teams. A single-agent approach plateaus early and crashes irrecoverably, motivating a multi-agent architecture with persistent shared memory and structured exploration.

Method: Arbor maintains an explicit search tree of scored hypotheses as shared working memory across agents, evolving with each measurement. Failures are treated as diagnostic signals that reshape subsequent exploration; prior successes shift the bottleneck distribution. The system pairs an Orchestrator agent (delegates optimization to Domain Specialists across the stack) with a Critic agent (performs root-cause analysis, introspection, and measurement validation) in a checks-and-balances architecture. Agent capabilities decompose into hard skills (domain expertise) and soft skills (coordination protocols).

Result: Arbor achieves up to 193% inference throughput-latency Pareto improvement over vendor-optimized baselines on full-stack LLM inference optimization. A single agent without the harness achieves only +33% throughput improvement and crashes irrecoverably within hours. The method generalizes across multiple hardware generations with run-to-run variance within 2 percentage points, demonstrating hardware-agnostic reproducibility.

Conclusion: Structured tree search as a shared cognitive layer with a multi-agent checks-and-balances architecture enables robust, reproducible, and hardware-agnostic autonomous optimization across complex, stateful action spaces, substantially outperforming both vendor baselines and single-agent approaches.

Abstract: Arbor is a multi-agent framework that introduces structured tree search as a cognition layer for autonomous agents operating in large, stateful action spaces. Prior autonomous optimization systems operate on isolated targets with stateless evaluation. Arbor instead maintains an explicit search tree of scored hypotheses that serves as the shared working memory across agents, evolving with every measurement, treating failures as diagnostic signal that reshapes subsequent exploration, and expanding as prior successes shift the bottleneck distribution.
  We validate Arbor on full-stack LLM inference optimization, a domain where achieving peak performance has historically required coordinated effort from engineering teams across the application, framework, compiler, kernel, and hardware stack. Arbor pairs an Orchestrator agent, which drives optimization by delegating to Domain Specialists across the inference stack, with a Critic agent that safeguards stability through root-cause analysis, introspection, and measurement validation -- a checks-and-balances architecture where neither agent can unilaterally drive the system. Agent capabilities are decomposed into hard skills (domain expertise) and soft skills (coordination protocols that determine how contributions compose), enabling fully autonomous multi-day campaigns. Arbor achieves up to 193% inference throughput-latency Pareto improvement over vendor-optimized baselines, while a single agent without the harness plateaus at +33% throughput improvement and crashes irrecoverably within hours. Arbor generalizes to multiple generations of hardware platform, and run-to-run variance is within 2 percentage points demonstrating that the method is hardware-agnostic and reproducible.

</details>


### [218] [Strategic Decision Support for AI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12587)
*Shayan Kiyani,Sima Noorani,George Pappas,Hamed Hassani*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a framework for strategic decision support where AI agents are the primary actors, inverting the traditional human-centric view. It formulates an optimization problem that minimizes support (human/tool) usage subject to controlling a counterfactual missed-support error, derives an optimal threshold policy, and develops an online adaptive algorithm with calibration-on-the-fly to reduce unnecessary support calls across scenarios like information gathering, human-AI collaboration, and tool use.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: In modern agentic systems, AI agents act autonomously on behalf of users while humans and tools serve as support—reversing the traditional decision-support paradigm. This role reversal raises reliability concerns: agentic errors carry real consequences, and agent behavior must remain aligned with human goals. The paper aims to formalize how agents should strategically decide when to seek support, balancing the cost of support against the risk of acting alone erroneously.

Method: The authors cast strategic decision support as an optimization problem minimizing expected support usage subject to a constraint on counterfactual missed-support error (the probability the agent acts alone when support would have materially improved the output). They prove the optimal policy is a threshold rule on the value of support. They then develop an online algorithm that adaptively learns this threshold using randomized exploration without distributional assumptions, and introduce a calibration-on-the-fly method to further reduce unnecessary support calls. The framework is instantiated across information gathering, human-AI collaboration, and tool-use scenarios.

Result: Experiments across diverse settings demonstrate that the proposed method reliably controls the target missed-support error while substantially reducing support usage in practice. The framework successfully unifies multiple agentic support scenarios under a single strategic lens.

Conclusion: The classical principles of decision support—cost-value tradeoffs and uncertainty quantification—can be productively revisited and adapted for agentic systems where AI is the central actor. The proposed threshold-based optimization framework with adaptive online control provides a principled, practical solution for agents to decide when to invoke human or tool support, ensuring reliability while minimizing unnecessary interventions.

Abstract: Traditionally, decision support studies how humans use machine learning models to make better decisions. In modern agentic systems, this division of roles is increasingly reversed: AI agents act on behalf of users, while humans and tools becomes support mechanisms around them. This role reversal brings reliability concerns to the forefront, since agentic errors can be consequential and agent behavior must remain aligned with human goals and constraints. Departing from the classical view of decision support, we revisit its two basic principles, the cost--value tradeoff of seeking support and the role of uncertainty quantification, in a setting where AI agents are the central actors. We propose a framework for strategic decision support for AI agents through an optimization problem that minimizes support usage subject to controlling a counterfactual missed-support error: the probability that the agent acts alone on instances where support would have materially improved its output. At the population level, we show that the optimal policy is a threshold rule on the value of support. Building on this structure, we develop an online algorithm that adaptively thresholds such a score and uses randomized exploration to control missed-support error without distributional assumptions. We further introduce a calibration-on-the-fly method that reduces unnecessary support calls online. We instantiate this framework across diverse scenarios, including information gathering, human--AI collaboration, and tool use, showing how each can be modeled through the same strategic decision-support lens. Experiments across these settings show that our method reliably controls the target error while substantially reducing support usage in practice.

</details>


### [219] [Pythagoras-Prover: Advancing Efficient Formal Proving via Augmented Lean Formalisation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12594)
*Joshua Ong Jun Leang,Zheng Zhao,Mihaela Cătălina Stoian,Qiyuan Xu,Haonan Li,Wenda Li,Shay B. Cohen,Eleonora Giunchiglia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Pythagoras-Prover introduces a compute-efficient open-source family of Lean theorem provers (4B and 32B autoregressive, plus a 4B diffusion-based prover) that uses curriculum SFT, dynamic proof filtering, and Augmented Lean Formalisation (ALF) for data augmentation. The 4B model surpasses DeepSeek-Prover-V2-671B on MiniF2F-Test with 167x fewer parameters, and the 32B model achieves 93.0% on MiniF2F-Test, setting a new open-source state of the art.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern Lean provers require substantial training and inference compute due to scarce verified proof data and long reasoning traces. Both SFT and proof sampling are expensive. The authors aim to build a compute-efficient, open-source prover family that works within practical compute budgets while still achieving competitive or state-of-the-art performance.

Method: The approach spans multiple innovations: (1) a Lean-verified corpus stratified by difficulty (easy/medium/hard) for curriculum SFT; (2) dynamic proof-reasoning filtering that keeps each instance under an 8k-token context budget while preserving informative traces; (3) Augmented Lean Formalisation (ALF), which perturbs known problems into formal statement variants and populates them via self-distillation, expanding training data without formal verification of every mutated instance; (4) two model paradigms — autoregressive (4B, 32B) and a diffusion-based prover (4B) that iteratively refines proofs at inference time.

Result: Pythagoras-Prover-4B surpasses DeepSeek-Prover-V2-671B at pass@32 on MiniF2F-Test (86.1% vs 82.4%) with ~167x fewer parameters. Pythagoras-Prover-32B achieves 93.0% on MiniF2F-Test and solves 93/672 PutnamBench problems, setting a new open-source SOTA. On MiniF2F-ALF (an ALF-mutated contamination-sensitive benchmark), all models lose accuracy, but the 32B remains strongest and the 4B matches Goedel-Prover-V2-32B.

Conclusion: Compute-efficient training strategies — curriculum SFT, dynamic proof filtering, and ALF-based data augmentation — enable small open-source models to match or exceed much larger proprietary provers. The release of models and the MiniF2F-ALF benchmark provides a more contamination-robust evaluation framework for the community.

Abstract: Modern Lean theorem provers achieve strong performance only with substantial training and inference compute, driven in part by scarce verified proof data and the long reasoning traces of formal proof search, making both supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and sampling expensive. We introduce Pythagoras-Prover, a compute-efficient open-source family of Lean theorem provers built for practical compute budgets. The family spans two generation paradigms: autoregressive models at 4B and 32B parameters, and a first proof-of-concept diffusion-based prover (4B) that iteratively refines Lean proofs at inference time. For training efficiency, we build a Lean-verified corpus stratified into easy, medium, and hard problems for curriculum SFT, so models acquire proof skills progressively from shorter, simpler proofs to longer, harder ones. During SFT, a dynamic proof-reasoning filtering scheme preserves informative proof traces while keeping each instance within an 8k-token context budget. We also introduce Augmented Lean Formalisation (ALF), which expands scarce verified corpora into variants of formal statements, populated via self-distillation for extra training signal without formally verifying every mutated instance. By perturbing known problems while preserving their formal character, ALF reduces reliance on any statement's surface form. Empirically, Pythagoras-Prover-4B surpasses DeepSeek-Prover-V2-671B at pass@32 on MiniF2F-Test (86.1% vs 82.4%) with ~167x fewer parameters, while Pythagoras-Prover-32B sets the open-source state of the art at 93.0% on MiniF2F-Test and solves 93 of 672 PutnamBench problems. We release MiniF2F-ALF, an ALF-mutated contamination-sensitive benchmark on which every evaluated model loses accuracy; here our 32B remains strongest and our 4B matches the prior state of the art, Goedel-Prover-V2-32B.

</details>


### [220] [The Illusion of Multi-Agent Advantage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13003)
*Prathyusha Jwalapuram,Hehai Lin,Chuyuan Li,Fangkai Jiao,Sudong Wang,Yifei Ming,Zixuan Ke,Chengwei Qin,Giuseppe Carenini,Shafiq Joty*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper challenges the assumption that Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) outperform Single-Agent Systems (SAS). Through rigorous evaluation, the authors show that automatically generated MAS consistently underperform Chain-of-Thought with Self-Consistency (CoT-SC) on both traditional reasoning and interactive workflow tasks, while being up to 10x more expensive. A diagnostic synthetic dataset reveals that current automated MAS design produces "architectural bloat" — superficial complexity lacking functional utility — exposing fundamental misalignment with multi-agent principles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prevailing wisdom holds that MAS are superior to SAS due to context protection, parallel processing, and distributed decision-making, but empirical support relies on benchmarks emphasizing isolated reasoning tasks that fail to assess these claimed advantages. The authors aim to rigorously test whether automatically generated MAS — designed for generalizability — actually deliver on these promises when compared against strong SAS baselines like CoT-SC.

Method: The authors conduct a systematic evaluation comparing automatically generated MAS against CoT-SC across (1) traditional reasoning datasets and (2) interactive multi-step workflow tasks (e.g., BrowseComp-Plus). To isolate failures from task-structure limitations, they introduce a diagnostic synthetic dataset explicitly featuring task decomposition, context separation, and parallelization potential. They then compare expert-architected MAS against automatically generated architectures, and perform systematic deconstruction of generated MAS architectures to identify structural deficiencies.

Result: Automatic MAS consistently underperform CoT-SC across all evaluated tasks despite being up to 10x more expensive. On the diagnostic synthetic dataset, expert-architected MAS outperform automatically generated architectures in both raw performance and cost-efficiency. Deconstruction reveals that automated design paradigms produce "architectural bloat" — superficial complexity that fails to translate into functional utility, indicating a fundamental misalignment with multi-agent principles.

Conclusion: Existing evaluation frameworks mask critical architectural gaps and inefficiencies in complex MAS by failing to account for the marginal utility of increased computational cost. Current automated MAS design paradigms prioritize superficial complexity over functional utility, and the field needs better evaluation methodologies that properly assess the core claimed advantages of multi-agent architectures.

Abstract: Prevailing wisdom posits that Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) are superior to Single-Agent Systems (SAS), citing advantages like context protection, parallel processing and distributed decision-making. However, empirical support for this claim relies primarily on comparisons with SAS baselines using benchmarks that prioritize isolated reasoning tasks, which do not adequately assess these advantages. Focusing on automatically generated MAS that are designed for enhanced generalizability over manually-designed counterparts, we perform a rigorous, systematic evaluation against SAS, specifically Chain-of-Thought with Self-Consistency (CoT-SC). Across traditional reasoning datasets and tasks with interactive multi-step workflows (e.g., BrowseComp-Plus), we demonstrate that automatic MAS consistently underperform CoT-SC despite being up to 10x more expensive. To isolate these failures from limitations inherent to task structure, we introduce a diagnostic synthetic dataset tailored for MAS featuring explicit task decomposition, context separation and parallelization potential. We show that expert-architected MAS consistently outperforms automatically generated architectures in both raw performance and cost-efficiency on this dataset, demonstrating that existing evaluation frameworks mask critical architectural gaps and inefficiencies of complex MAS by failing to account for the marginal utility of increased computational cost. Critically, systematic deconstruction of the generated MAS architectures reveals that current automated design paradigms produce architectural bloat that prioritizes superficial complexity which does not translate into functional utility, exposing a fundamental misalignment with multi-agent principles.

</details>


### [221] [PersonaDrive: Human-Style Retrieval-Augmented VLA Agents for Closed-Loop Driving Simulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12616)
*Mahmoud Srewa,Praneetsai Iddamsetty,Mohammad Abdullah Al Faruque,Salma Elmalaki*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PersonaDrive conditions a single VLA driving agent on retrieved demonstrations from a dataset where humans drove under aggressive, neutral, and conservative instructions, achieving style-diverse behavior without per-style retraining and outperforming baselines across all styles on Bench2Drive.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing simulators lack genuine behavioral diversity in non-ego traffic agents — rule-based or single-mode learned models produce homogeneous behavior, while prior style-variation methods rely on proxy signals (post-hoc labels, LLM-inferred rewards) rather than actual human demonstrations of explicitly instructed driving styles.

Method: A three-stage pipeline: (i) offline triplet mining on a style-instructed human driving dataset (collected on a driver-in-the-loop CARLA rig under aggressive/neutral/conservative instructions) using combined image-text similarity; (ii) training a lightweight retrieval head that fuses frozen visual features with a small control encoder over per-style databases; (iii) fine-tuning a single VLA backbone to treat retrieved context points as in-context behavioral demonstrations during waypoint prediction. At inference, style is selected by swapping the per-style database queried by the retrieval head — no retraining needed.

Result: On Bench2Drive, PersonaDrive (no-style) improves driving score by 4.6% over SimLingo and 2.5% over HiP-AD. Under style conditioning, it achieves the highest driving score in every style within a ~2% band, with its weakest style surpassing the strongest baseline (DMW) by 5.4%. Average speed and acceleration increase 18% and 25% respectively from conservative to aggressive instructions.

Conclusion: Retrieval-based conditioning on genuine style-instructed human demonstrations enables a single VLA agent to produce realistic, style-diverse driving behavior across aggressive, neutral, and conservative modes without per-style retraining, outperforming existing baselines while maintaining interpretable stylistic variation in speed and acceleration profiles.

Abstract: Closed-loop driving simulators typically populate their environments with non-ego traffic agents that behave largely the same way, produced either by rule-based traffic managers or by learned models trained toward a single behavioral mode. Recent work introduces style variation through post-hoc labels on observational data or LLM-inferred reward weights, but these signals act as proxies for what a style should reward rather than demonstrations of humans explicitly asked to drive in that style. We introduce PersonaDrive, a pipeline that conditions a vision-language-action (VLA) driving agent on retrieved demonstrations from a style-instructed human driving dataset, in which participants drive CARLA leaderboard routes under aggressive, neutral, and conservative instructions on a driver-in-the-loop rig. The pipeline has three stages: (i) offline triplet mining over per-style human driving data using a combined image-text similarity score; (ii) training a lightweight retrieval head that fuses frozen visual features with a small control encoder over per-style databases; and (iii) fine-tuning a single VLA backbone to treat retrieved context points as in-context behavioral demonstrations during waypoint prediction. At inference, the same backbone is conditioned on any style by swapping which per-style database the retrieval head queries, so selecting a style requires no per-style retraining while enabling human-style, style-diverse non-ego agents for closed-loop simulation. On Bench2Drive, PersonaDrive (no style) improves the driving score by 4.6% over SimLingo and 2.5% over HiP-AD, and under style conditioning attains the highest driving score in every style within a roughly 2% band (its weakest style surpassing the strongest baseline, DMW, by 5.4%), while average speed and acceleration rise by 18% and 25% from the conservative to the aggressive instruction.

</details>


### [222] [LLM-as-an-Investigator: Evidence-First Reasoning for Robust Interactive Problem Diagnosis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13220)
*Fabrizio Marozzo,Pietro Liò*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces LLM-as-an-Investigator, an evidence-first agentic methodology that counters user-driven sycophancy—where LLMs prematurely align with user hypotheses—by iteratively generating candidate hypotheses, asking targeted clarification questions, and updating probabilities until one explanation dominates. Evaluated on a benchmark of solved technical forum threads across mechanical, electrical, and hydraulic domains using a three-agent pipeline, the approach outperforms standard and reasoning-only baselines in diagnostic accuracy while reducing user-induced conversational bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs acting as technical assistants exhibit "user-driven sycophancy": they prematurely align with incomplete or unverified user hypotheses rather than collecting sufficient evidence and testing alternative explanations. This leads to incorrect problem diagnosis when users provide plausible but misleading information.

Method: The Solution Investigator Agent uses an evidence-first protocol: (1) estimates initial ambiguity, (2) generates candidate hypotheses, (3) asks targeted clarification questions, (4) updates hypothesis probabilities after each answer, and (5) iterates until one candidate is clearly stronger. A three-agent evaluation pipeline automates benchmarking: a Problem-Solution Extractor converts solved forum threads into structured cases, a Ground-Truth Evaluator simulates the user hiding the known solution, and the tested assistant recovers the solution through dialogue. Benchmarks span mechanical, electrical, and hydraulic domains.

Result: The investigator-based approach achieves higher diagnostic accuracy than direct prompting and reasoning-only baselines across multiple LLM backbones. Standard assistants are shown to follow misleading user hypotheses in diagnostic cases, while the evidence-first protocol effectively reduces this user-induced conversational bias.

Conclusion: An evidence-first, iterative investigation protocol that actively tests alternative hypotheses significantly improves LLM diagnostic accuracy and mitigates user-driven sycophancy in technical problem-solving scenarios.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as interactive assistants for technical problem solving. However, when users provide incomplete descriptions or plausible but unverified explanations, LLMs may prematurely align with these assumptions and propose solutions before collecting sufficient evidence. We refer to this behavior as user-driven sycophancy: the tendency of an LLM to reinforce a user-provided hypothesis instead of testing alternative explanations. This paper introduces LLM-as-an-Investigator, an evidence-first agentic AI methodology for robust problem diagnosis. The approach is implemented through a Solution Investigator Agent, which estimates the ambiguity of an initial problem description, generates candidate hypotheses, asks targeted clarification questions, and updates hypothesis probabilities after each answer. Rather than producing an immediate response, the agent continues the investigation until the evidence makes one candidate explanation stronger than the alternatives. To evaluate the approach, we build a benchmark from solved technical forum threads in mechanical, electrical, and hydraulic domains. We use a three-agent evaluation pipeline in which a Problem-Solution Extractor Agent converts solved threads into structured cases, a Ground-Truth Evaluator Agent simulates the user while hiding the known solution, and the tested assistant attempts to recover the solution through dialogue. The experiments compare standard assistants, reasoning-oriented LLMs, and the proposed investigator-based model across LLM backbones. In addition to diagnostic accuracy, we analyze how standard assistants follow misleading user hypotheses in diagnostic cases. The results show that the proposed approach identifies the problem more accurately than direct prompting and reasoning-only baselines, while its evidence-first protocol helps reduce user-induced conversational bias.

</details>


### [223] ["Did you lie?" Evaluating Lie Detectors across Model Scale and Belief-Verified Model Organisms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12618)
*Alan Cooney,David Africa,Geoffrey Irving*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Existing lie detector evaluations suffer from model organisms that don't reliably hold hidden beliefs opposite to their statements. The authors introduce verified reasoning model organisms and a comprehensive prompted-lying testbed, then evaluate four detectors. While detectors scale positively with model capability on prompted lies, activation- and logprob-based methods fail on trained organisms; only chain-of-thought judging remains robust, and even that is partly artifactual. Current detectors cannot support high-confidence belief claims.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robust lie detection in language models is critical for auditing and monitoring, but evaluation requires testbeds where models verifiably believe the opposite of what they say. Prior trained model organisms often fail this verification requirement, rendering previous positive and negative detection results ambiguous or uninterpretable.

Method: The authors construct (1) 13 reasoning model organisms with chain-of-thought-verified hidden beliefs that generalize to held-out tasks, and (2) Varied Deception, a prompted-lying testbed with diverse lie-inducing motivations. They evaluate four detectors—a chain-of-thought judge, a logprob classifier, and two activation probes (including a novel Did-You-Lie/DYL follow-up probe)—across 31 open-weight models (2B–1T parameters) on both testbeds.

Result: All four detectors show positive scaling with model capability on prompted lying. However, activation- and logprob-based detectors drop sharply on trained model organisms; DYL retains the most signal among them. Only the chain-of-thought judge remains strong (0.82 balanced accuracy), partly as an artifact of the verification process favoring CoT-readable beliefs.

Conclusion: Current lie detectors are insufficient for high-confidence claims about model beliefs. The authors recommend research directions to address these limitations and publicly release their datasets, model organisms, and trained detectors.

Abstract: Robust lie detectors for language models could enable powerful techniques for auditing, monitoring, and post-hoc investigation of model behaviour, but evaluating them requires testbeds where models verifiably believe the opposite of what they say. We show that existing trained model organisms often fail this requirement, leaving prior positive and negative detection results difficult to interpret. We address this with 13 reasoning model organisms whose hidden beliefs are verified in chain-of-thought and shown to generalise to held-out tasks, alongside Varied Deception, a prompted-lying testbed covering a broad range of lie-inducing motivations. On these testbeds we evaluate four detectors: a chain-of-thought judge, a logprob classifier, and two activation probes, including Did-You-Lie (DYL), a new method for training follow-up probes. On prompted lying, across 31 open-weight models spanning 2B to 1T parameters, all four detectors show positive scaling with model capability. However, every activation- and logprob-based detector drops sharply on our trained model organisms, with DYL retaining the most signal; only the chain-of-thought judge remains strong, with 0.82 balanced accuracy, partly as an artefact of our verification process favouring CoT-readable beliefs. Current lie detectors therefore cannot support high-confidence claims about model beliefs, and we suggest research directions that may address some of their current limitations. We release our datasets, model organisms, and trained detectors.

</details>


### [224] [Can I Buy Your KV Cache?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13361)
*Luoyuan Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a "prefill CDN" where document publishers precompute KV caches once, and AI agents buy access to load them, eliminating redundant prefill computation. This yields 9–50x compute savings with zero accuracy loss. Provider-side hosting avoids expensive KV egress, enabling a 10x discount to users while retaining substantial provider margins.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Millions of AI agents independently recompute the same prefill step on identical documents, rebuilding identical KV caches from scratch. This is extraordinarily wasteful: prefill attention scales O(L²) and dominates inference compute costs for long documents. The authors argue this redundant recomputation across agents is absurd and propose eliminating it entirely.

Method: The proposal is to let a document publisher precompute the KV cache once and host it server-side. Other agents then purchase the right to load this precomputed KV cache and continue generation, skipping prefill entirely. The authors verify token-exact equivalence (24/24 greedy tokens match, logit-level match) and benchmark compute savings on Qwen3-4B. They analyze two hosting models — shipping KV caches (fails due to incompressibility and egress costs) vs. provider-side hosting (viable, analogous to production prompt-caching).

Result: Loading a precomputed KV cache is token-exact identical to prefilling from scratch. On Qwen3-4B, reuse is 9–50x cheaper than prefill, with savings growing with sequence length. For a 3774-token document served to 80M agents, re-prefill costs ~$1.5M vs. ~$0.03M for reuse (49.7x savings). A 0.1x cache-read tariff relative to prefill cost delivers a 10x discount to users while preserving provider margin, since the physical compute saving is ~50x.

Conclusion: A prefill CDN for agents is technically viable and economically compelling. The open problems identified are lossless KV compression (to reduce storage costs) and a cross-party payment layer to enable publisher compensation. The measured ~50x compute savings creates a large economic envelope where both users and providers benefit.

Abstract: Right now, across the world, AI agents are repeating the same absurd act: to read one document, they each recompute it from scratch. Every agent re-runs prefill, the most compute-intensive step a large model takes, over identical text, only to rebuild a key-value (KV) cache identical to the one the agent before it just built. The same answer, computed a million times. We make a proposal that is almost offensively simple: compute it once. Let a publisher precompute a document's KV cache, and let every other agent buy the right to load it and skip prefill. It works, and it is token-exact: loading a precomputed KV and continuing matches prefilling from scratch (24/24 greedy tokens, and at the logits level), with no accuracy cost. On Qwen3-4B, reuse is 9-50x cheaper in compute than prefill, and the gap widens with length (prefill's attention scales with L^2), so a single reuse already pays it back. Then the part that matters: where the KV lives. Shipping it fails, because KV is nearly incompressible, so per-load egress costs more than the prefill it saves. Hosting it provider-side, exactly as production prompt-caching works, removes egress entirely. The size of the prize is set by our measured compute saving: serving one hot 3774-token document to 80M agents costs ~$1.5M to re-prefill but only ~$0.03M of reuse compute (49.7x less). The 0.1x cache-read tariff APIs charge passes a 10x discount to users while sitting inside this measured envelope, so the 10x is a floor that the measured ~50x compute saving clears, and the gap to the physical ~50x is provider margin: millions of dollars per popular document. We frame the resulting agent-native prefill CDN and leave lossless KV compression and a cross-party payment layer as the open problems.

</details>


### [225] [TrajGenAgent: A Hierarchical LLM Agent for Human Mobility Trajectory Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12657)
*Siyu Li,Toan Tran,Lingyi Zhao,Khurram Shafique,Li Xiong*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TrajGenAgent is a training-free, hierarchical LLM-agent framework that generates realistic individual mobility trajectories by combining LLM-based activity chain synthesis with deterministic spatiotemporal grounding, outperforming existing methods in fidelity, coherence, and behavioral realism.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale human trajectory data is essential for transportation, urban planning, and epidemic control, but collection is costly and privacy-sensitive. Existing LLM-based generators face a trade-off: prompt engineering retains zero-shot reasoning but lacks spatiotemporal precision, while fine-tuning improves statistical accuracy at high computational cost and potential loss of general reasoning ability. The authors aim to bridge this gap with a framework that achieves both semantic reasoning and precise spatiotemporal grounding without model fine-tuning.

Method: TrajGenAgent employs a two-stage orchestrator-worker architecture. In the first stage, an LLM synthesizes an individual- and weekday-conditioned activity chain from historical evidence using in-context learning. In the second stage, a deterministic workflow grounds each activity into a complete visit through four components: (1) personalized POI retrieval, (2) distance-aware location selection, (3) kinematics-aware travel-time propagation, and (4) LLM-based duration estimation. For evaluation, the authors introduce an anomaly-detection-based framework using two complementary detectors to assess behavioral and semantic plausibility beyond aggregate statistics.

Result: Experiments on benchmark and large-scale simulation datasets demonstrate that TrajGenAgent improves spatiotemporal fidelity, semantic coherence, and individual-specific behavioral realism compared to representative neural and LLM-based baselines, all while avoiding parameter updates.

Conclusion: A hierarchical LLM-agent design with decoupled semantic reasoning and deterministic spatiotemporal grounding can generate highly realistic individual trajectories without fine-tuning. The anomaly-detection evaluation framework further provides a more nuanced assessment of trajectory realism. This points toward efficient, privacy-respecting synthetic data generation for mobility applications.

Abstract: Human mobility data is important for transportation, urban planning, and epidemic control, but large-scale trajectory collection is often costly and privacy-constrained, motivating realistic synthetic trajectory generation. Existing LLM-based generators typically rely on either prompt engineering, which preserves zero-shot reasoning but lacks fine-grained spatiotemporal grounding, or trajectory-level fine-tuning, which improves statistical precision but incurs substantial computational cost and may weaken general reasoning. We propose TrajGenAgent, a semantic-aware hierarchical LLM-agent framework for human mobility trajectory generation without model fine-tuning. TrajGenAgent uses a two-stage orchestrator-worker design: an LLM first synthesizes an individual- and weekday-conditioned activity chain from historical evidence via in-context learning, and a deterministic workflow then grounds each activity into a complete visit using personalized POI retrieval, distance-aware location selection, kinematics-aware travel-time propagation, and LLM-based duration estimation. To evaluate realism beyond aggregate spatiotemporal statistics, we introduce an anomaly-detection-based evaluation framework using two complementary detectors to assess behavioral and semantic plausibility. Experiments on benchmark and large-scale simulation datasets show that TrajGenAgent improves spatiotemporal fidelity, semantic coherence, and individual-specific behavioral realism over representative neural and LLM-based baselines, while avoiding parameter updates.

</details>


### [226] [Neuro-Symbolic Agents for Regulated Process Automation: Challenges and Research Agenda](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13405)
*Alexander Rombach,Chantale Lauer,Nijat Mehdiyev*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes "compliance-by-construction," a paradigm where symbolic regulatory structures (rules, process models, constraints) are embedded as core architectural components of LLM agents, rather than used only as external guardrails. This structural approach prevents control-flow violations by design, while guardrails handle semantic errors. The authors identify neuro-symbolic research challenges and call for community engagement in regulated process automation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-based agents are increasingly deployed in regulated industries (e.g., quality management) where compliance is critical. Existing approaches treat regulations as external monitoring/guardrail mechanisms applied post-hoc, which is insufficient for preventing structural control-flow violations. The authors argue that symbolic structures already present in these domains—regulations, typed process models, compliance constraints—should be leveraged as first-class architectural primitives to fundamentally shape agent decision-making, not merely constrain it from the outside.

Method: The paper is primarily a position/vision paper that: (1) introduces the compliance-by-construction paradigm as a complement to guardrail-based monitoring; (2) argues that symbolic domain artifacts (regulations, typed process models, compliance constraints) should be integrated directly into agent architecture to prevent control-flow violations structurally; (3) identifies a structured set of neuro-symbolic research challenges at both foundational and capability levels that must be jointly addressed to enable this paradigm.

Result: The paper does not present empirical results. As a position paper, its contributions are conceptual: (1) the formulation of compliance-by-construction as a design paradigm; (2) a structured taxonomy of neuro-symbolic research challenges spanning foundational and capability levels; (3) a call to action positioning regulated process automation as a high-impact domain for the neuro-symbolic research community.

Conclusion: Compliance-by-construction offers a principled alternative to purely guardrail-based compliance, embedding regulatory structure into agent architecture to prevent violations at the control-flow level. Realizing this vision requires concerted neuro-symbolic research across multiple challenge areas. The authors urge the community to treat regulated process automation as a strategically important application domain.

Abstract: LLM-based agents are entering regulated industries where they automate judgment intensive quality management processes. We argue that symbolic structures already embedded in these domains, including regulations, typed process models, and compliance constraints, should be treated not merely as external monitoring mechanisms but as core architectural components that shape the agent's decision-making and behavior. We propose compliance-by-construction as a complementary paradigm to guardrail-based monitoring: a structural foundation that prevents control-flow violations, while guardrails remain essential for catching semantic errors. We identify a structured set of neuro-symbolic research challenges on foundational and capability level and show that addressing them jointly enables compliance-by-construction. We call on the neuro-symbolic community to engage with regulated process automation as a high impact research domain.

</details>


### [227] [Evoflux: Inference-Time Evolution of Executable Tool Workflows for Compact Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12674)
*Kushal Raj Bhandari,Ling Yue,Ching-Yun Ko,Dhaval Patel,Shaowu Pan,Pin-Yu Chen,Jianxi Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Evoflux is an inference-time evolutionary search method that repairs compact language model tool-use workflows through execution-grounded edits, raising feasibility from ~3% to 17-24% on MCP-Bench, outperforming distillation-based SFT/DPO under limited teacher traces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Compact LMs struggle with MCP-style tool use because small-corpus distillation teaches workflow format but fails to convey recovery behaviors needed to repair failed plans over dynamic tool catalogs. Existing small planners generate plausible graphs that break under tool resolution, schema validation, dependency tracking, or execution.

Method: Evoflux performs inference-time evolutionary search over typed workflow graphs via structured edits, execution feedback, adaptive mutation intensity, meta-guided redesign, and diversity pruning. It mines repair trajectories without relying on large teacher-trace budgets.

Result: On held-out MCP-Bench tasks (live MCP servers, 250 tools), Evoflux boosts execution feasibility from ~3% to 17-24% across small planners. SFT and SFT+DPO on search-mined data match, underperform, or collapse below zero-shot; ReAct reaches higher peaks but with greater variance and token cost.

Conclusion: Execution-grounded evolutionary search is more reliable than distillation-based fine-tuning for compact tool-use agents operating under scarce teacher-trace budgets.

Abstract: Compact language models (LMs) reduce cost, latency, and deployment risk for tool agents. Yet MCP-style tool use requires more than isolated function calling: an agent must discover tools from live catalogs, satisfy schemas, preserve dependencies across intermediate outputs, and ground final responses in executed evidence. Small planners often generate plausible workflow graphs that fail under tool resolution, parameter validation, dependency tracking, or execution. We argue that this failure mode is poorly handled by small-corpus distillation. A few hundred teacher traces can teach workflow format, but rarely cover the recovery behavior needed to repair failed plans over changing tool catalogs. We introduce Evoflux, an inference-time evolutionary search method that treats compact tool use as the repair of executable tool workflows. It evolves typed workflow graphs through structured edits, execution feedback, adaptive intensity, meta-guided redesign, and diversity pruning. On held-out MCP-Bench tasks spanning live MCP servers and 250 tools, Evoflux raises execution feasibility from roughly 3% to 17-24% across small planners. In contrast, SFT and SFT+DPO on the same search-mined data match, underperform, or collapse below zero-shot performance; ReAct reaches higher peaks, but with higher variance and token cost.
