These results show that execution-grounded search is more reliable under scarce teacher-trace budgets.

</details>


### [228] [Multiagent Protocols with Aggregated Confidence Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13591)
*Ali Elahi,Barbara Di Eugenio*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces three protocols for producing a single aggregated confidence score for multiagent debate (MAD) systems. By transforming raw confidence signals for cross-model comparability and combining them via soft voting or Bayesian fusion, the aggregated confidence significantly improves discriminability (AUARC) over single agents and standard baselines, while maintaining F1-score stability and recovering losses on ambiguous tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Confidence estimation is widely used in NLP for reliability and downstream decision-making, yet no existing method produces or evaluates a system-level confidence score for multiagent systems. Prior work applies confidence only internally (e.g., weighting messages, triggering debate, calibrating individual agents) but never aggregates individual signals into a single, holistic confidence for the multiagent system as a whole.

Method: The authors propose three protocols that output both a final answer and an aggregated confidence. They first transform raw confidence signals (sequence probability and self-report) to ensure cross-model comparability, then combine them via soft voting or a novel probability fusion method called Bayesian fusion. They also analyze parametric and non-parametric calibrators. Evaluation spans six homogeneous and heterogeneous debating pairs across five benchmarks and four task types, covering various model capabilities and sizes.

Result: The aggregated confidence is substantially more discriminative (higher AUARC) than the best single agent or standard debate baselines. F1-score remains stable, and the method recovers correctness losses that MAD incurs on more ambiguous tasks. Calibration improves F1 for both estimators (sequence probability and self-report), while AUARC is less reliant on calibration.

Conclusion: System-level confidence aggregation for multiagent debate is both feasible and beneficial, yielding more reliable and discriminative confidence scores without sacrificing task accuracy, while also mitigating MAD's weaknesses on ambiguous tasks.

Abstract: Confidence is used for reliability, oversight, and a range of downstream decision tasks in Natural Language Processing (NLP), yet no existing method produces or evaluates a confidence for the output of a multiagent system. Prior work uses confidence within multiagent debate (MAD) to weight messages, trigger debate, or calibrate individual agents, but it never aggregates these into a single confidence for the system itself. We introduce three protocols that produce a final answer along with a single aggregated confidence by first transforming raw confidence signals to make them comparable across models, then combining them via soft voting or a probability fusion we call Bayesian fusion. This aggregated confidence is substantially more discriminative (AUARC) than that of the best single agent or the standard debate baselines, while correctness (F1-score) stays stable and recovers the losses MAD incurs on more ambiguous tasks. Analyzing two estimators, sequence probability and self-report, alongside parametric and non-parametric calibrators, we find that calibration improves F1 for both estimators while AUARC is less reliant on it. We evaluate six homogeneous and heterogeneous debating pairs per benchmark, across five benchmarks and four task types, spanning a range of model capabilities and sizes.

</details>


### [229] [From AGI to ASI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12683)
*Tim Genewein,Matija Franklin,Alexander Lerchner,Laurent Orseau,Samuel Albanie,Adam Bales,Cole Wyeth,Stephanie Chan,Iason Gabriel,Joel Z. Leibo,Allan Dafoe,Marcus Hutter,Thore Graepel,Shane Legg*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This report investigates the transition from human-level AGI to artificial general superintelligence (ASI), characterizing ASI, outlining four potential pathways (scaling, paradigm shifts, recursive improvement, multi-agent collectives), and analyzing frictions/bottlenecks. It argues that AI progress may be a series of transformative changes rather than a single step change, requiring interdisciplinary global preparation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: With AGI becoming a concrete near-term target for major AI organizations, understanding what comes after AGI — specifically the transition to superintelligence — is critical. The profound societal impacts demand anticipatory analysis of pathways, frictions, and the nature of post-AGI progress to inform global preparedness.

Method: The report employs conceptual and theoretical analysis grounded in the formal understanding of Universal AI as an endpoint. It characterizes ASI, then systematically maps four distinct pathways from AGI to ASI (scaling, paradigm shifts, recursive self-improvement, and emergence from multi-agent collectives), followed by identification and discussion of potential frictions and bottlenecks along these pathways.

Result: The analysis suggests that AI progress may continue accelerating, making the "single transformative step change" model of AGI introduction potentially inaccurate. Instead, a series of transformative societal changes driven by AI-enabled breakthroughs across science and technology appears more plausible. Key open research questions are identified regarding whether frictions along ASI pathways will be negligible or substantial.

Conclusion: Due to large uncertainties in predicting ASI progress, continued acceleration cannot be ruled out. Preparing for a cascade of AI-driven transformative changes — rather than a singular AGI event — demands a massively interdisciplinary, globally-scoped endeavor.

Abstract: Over the last decade, building human-level artificial general intelligence has moved from far-fetched speculation to being a concrete next-decade target for many of the largest AI organisations. Achieving this goal would have profound and far-reaching impacts on human society, which raises many complex questions for the decade ahead. This report investigates how AI itself might continue to develop in a post-AGI world along the continuum of machine intelligence. The endpoint of this continuum, Universal AI, is theoretically well understood, which provides some formal grounding for the main focus of this report: the transition from human-level AGI to artificial general superintelligence, which, intuitively, can be understood as a system that is more intelligent and cognitively capable than large organisations of humans. After characterizing ASI, the report discusses four potential pathways from AGI to ASI: scaling AGI, AI paradigm shifts, recursive improvement, and ASI emerging from large-scale multi-agent collectives. The report then discusses possible frictions and bottlenecks along these pathways. Determining whether the impact of these frictions will be negligible or substantial raises a number of concrete open research questions. Due to large uncertainties for predicting ASI progress, it cannot be ruled out that AI progress might continue to accelerate over the next years. This could imply that the image of a single transformative step change, caused by the introduction of human-level AGI into our society, could be inaccurate. More apt might be the prospect of a series of transformative societal changes caused by AI-enabled progress and breakthroughs across many areas of science and technology. Preparing for this prospect requires a massively interdisciplinary endeavour of global scope and interest.

</details>


### [230] [Reward Modeling for Multi-Agent Orchestration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13598)
*King Yeung Tsang,Zihao Zhao,Vishal Venkataramani,Haizhou Shi,Zixuan Ke,Semih Yavuz,Shafiq Joty,Hao Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: OrchRM introduces a self-supervised reward modeling framework that evaluates multi-agent orchestration quality using intermediate execution artifacts, enabling efficient orchestrator training and test-time scaling without human annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Training orchestrators for LLM-based multi-agent systems is hindered by limited supervision and the high computational cost of sub-agent rollouts. Existing approaches lack efficient, scalable methods for evaluating orchestration quality without human labels.

Method: OrchRM constructs win-lose pairs from intermediate artifacts of multi-agent executions and uses them to train a Bradley-Terry reward model. This operates directly at the orchestration level rather than requiring costly sub-agent rollouts, enabling reward-guided orchestrator training and test-time scaling.

Result: OrchRM achieves up to 10x improvement in training token efficiency and up to 8% accuracy gain in MAS test-time scaling. Gains transfer across mathematical reasoning, web-based QA, and multi-hop reasoning domains.

Conclusion: Orchestration-level reward modeling represents a scalable and robust direction for multi-agent orchestration, with consistent performance improvements across diverse domains.

Abstract: Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) built on Large Language Models (LLMs) require effective orchestration to coordinate specialized agents, yet training such orchestrators is hindered by limited supervision and high computational cost. We propose Orchestration Reward Modeling (OrchRM), a self-supervised framework for evaluating orchestration quality without human annotations. OrchRM leverages intermediate artifacts from multi-agent executions to construct win-lose pairs for Bradley-Terry reward model training. Unlike existing MAS test-time scaling and orchestrator training frameworks that rely on costly sub-agent rollouts, OrchRM operates directly at the orchestration level, enabling efficient and high-performing reward-guided orchestrator training and MAS test-time scaling. OrchRM improves training efficiency by up to 10x in token usage while improving MAS test-time scaling performance by up to 8% in accuracy. These gains consistently transfer across multiple domains, including mathematical reasoning, web-based question answering, and multi-hop reasoning, demonstrating orchestration-level reward modeling as a scalable direction for robust multi-agent orchestration. Code will be available at https://github.com/Wang-ML-Lab/OrchRM.

</details>


### [231] [Deployment-Centered Evaluation: Predicting Query-Level Rejection Risk in a Clinical LLM System](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12702)
*Alyssa Unell,Miguel Fuentes,Brenna Li,Bridget Lin,Meena Jagadeesan,Sanmi Koyejo,Nigam Shah*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A deployment-centered evaluation of an LLM system in an EHR setting demonstrates that predicting user rejection of LLM responses is feasible (AUROC 0.719), with deployment-specific context (provider type, department, model used) improving predictions over query content alone, enabling targeted guardrails.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Static clinical LLM benchmarks have critical blindspots: they measure correctness rather than user acceptance, aggregate performance across heterogeneous queries, and require densely annotated datasets. These limitations fail to capture real-world clinical deployment conditions where user feedback is sparse but directly reflects utility.

Method: The authors embed an LLM system within an academic medical center's EHR and train a pre-response classifier to predict the risk of user rejection. The model leverages both query content and deployment-specific context (provider type, department name, language model variant) available before generation. A prospective analysis is conducted over 4.5 months of user feedback, with downstream evaluation in guardrail triggering and abstention use cases.

Result: The prediction model achieves an AUROC of 0.719. Deployment-specific context (as opposed to query content alone) significantly improves predictive performance for user rejection. The approach demonstrates practical utility for two downstream applications: triggering guardrails and abstention.

Conclusion: Predicting user rejection in clinical LLM deployments is feasible using pre-response signals, particularly when incorporating deployment-specific metadata. This opens the door to targeted, context-aware guardrails that can improve real-world clinical LLM systems.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into clinical systems, making it essential to evaluate the real-world utility of these systems. However, static benchmarks tend to measure correctness rather than user acceptance, aggregate performance across queries, and require densely annotated datasets -- leading to major blind spots for evaluating clinical systems. In this work, we perform a deployment-centered evaluation of an LLM system embedded within electronic health records at an academic medical center, where user feedback is sparse but closely reflects the deployment conditions. Specifically, we train a pre-response classifier that estimates the risk that a future interaction will result in the user rejecting the LLM response, based on query content and deployment-specific context available before generation. We conduct a prospective analysis of our model over 4.5 months of user feedback, finding that our prediction model achieves an AUROC of 0.719. Further, we estimate the benefit of such predictions in two downstream use cases (guardrail triggering and abstention). Our key conceptual insight is that making use of deployment-specific context (i.e., the provider type, department name, language model used for response), as opposed to only query content, improves the ability to predict whether the user will reject the system output. Altogether, our empirical case study demonstrates the feasibility of predicting user rejection using deployment-specific context, opening the door to targeted guardrails.

</details>


### [232] [Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning from Delayed Marketplace Feedback for Objective-Weight Adaptation in Three-Sided Dispatch](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13604)
*Haochen Wu,Yi Hou,Shiguang Xie*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DoorDash deploys an offline reinforcement learning system that adapts dispatch objective weights per store using delayed operational signals, improving batching efficiency and courier utilization without degrading delivery quality, with a conservative Double Q-learning approach to mitigate value overestimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Three-sided marketplace dispatch involves complex tradeoffs between delivery speed, courier utilization, and merchant congestion. Manually tuning dispatch objective weights does not scale across heterogeneous stores. The authors seek to automate this adaptation using real-world delayed feedback while preserving the existing combinatorial assignment optimizer and operational constraints.

Method: A store-level policy selects a discrete multiplier that shifts the dispatch optimizer's tradeoff between delivery quality and batching efficiency. The system uses offline batch RL trained on logged marketplace data with a shared centralized value function and decentralized store-level execution. Double Q-learning targets and a conservative regularizer (inspired by CQL) address out-of-distribution value overestimation. The policy interfaces with the production solver without replacing it.

Result: In a production switchback experiment, the offline-trained policy increased batching and reduced courier-side time costs without degrading customer-facing delivery quality metrics.

Conclusion: World feedback from live economic and logistics systems can be effectively leveraged via offline RL to safely adapt decision policies online. The approach demonstrates that a lightweight policy layer atop an existing combinatorial optimizer can yield operational gains while maintaining production safety and feasibility constraints.

Abstract: Dispatch in three-sided marketplaces provides a natural setting for reinforcement learning from world feedback: decisions are evaluated by delayed operational outcomes such as delivery speed, courier utilization, and merchant congestion. We present a deployed reinforcement learning system at DoorDash that adapts dispatch objective weights in a large-scale food-delivery marketplace using delayed signals. Rather than replacing the combinatorial assignment optimizer, a store-level policy learned from logged marketplace data selects a discrete multiplier that shifts the dispatch optimizer's tradeoff between delivery quality and batching efficiency. This interface enables offline policy learning under noisy, delayed, and coupled feedback while preserving production feasibility constraints and operational safeguards. We train a shared value function using centralized offline data and decentralized store-level execution, with Double Q-learning targets and a conservative regularizer to reduce out-of-distribution value overestimation. In a production switchback experiment, the offline-trained policy increases batching and reduces courier-side time costs without degrading customer-facing delivery quality. Results illustrate how world feedback from a live economic and logistics system can be used to safely adapt decision policies online.

</details>


### [233] [Is It You or Your Environment? A Bayesian Inference Framework for Genomically-Anchored Personalized Physiological Interpretation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13556)
*Aruna Dey,Suraj Biswas*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Personalized health AI suffers from a cold-start problem requiring weeks of data to establish physiological baselines. The authors propose using genomic profiles as Bayesian priors (genetic anchors) to initialize personalized physiological set points, enabling immediate interpretation of physiological measurements by distinguishing constitutional variation from environmental deviation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Personalized health AI systems require weeks of individual behavioral data before they can reliably distinguish a person's constitutional (genetically fixed) physiological baseline from environmentally driven deviations. This "cold-start" problem delays actionable health insights and prevents immediate personalization.

Method: A causal inference framework using genomic profiles as exogenous Bayesian priors: (1) An individual's genetic anchor Ĝ = μ + Σ(βᵢ × gᵢ) is computed from GWAS-derived effect sizes (βᵢ) and risk-allele counts (gᵢ). (2) Each incoming physiological measurement P yields a non-constitutional deviation δ = P − Ĝ, isolating environmental/state effects. (3) A dynamic weighting scheme Ĝ_t = w(t)·Ĝ_genomic + [1−w(t)]·P̄_t transitions from genome-dominated to empirical-baseline-dominated inference as behavioral data accumulate. (4) Priors are graded by evidence strength, distinguishing robustly replicated anchors (FTO, FADS1/2, FKBP5) from contested genes (SLC6A4, MAOA, DRD2).

Result: The architecture enables context-dependent interpretation of identical physiological measurements: an HRV of 55 ms generates opposite hypotheses (suppression vs. enhancement) depending on whether the individual's genomic prior predicts 80 ms or 30 ms. The framework operates across six physiological domains with evidence-graded anchors.

Conclusion: Genomic priors solve the cold-start problem in personalized health AI by providing an immediately available, causally exogenous baseline. The approach requires four deployment constraints: evidence-graded priors, dynamic decay toward empirical baselines, ancestry-matched effect sizes, and attribution framing rather than deterministic claims. The authors distinguish the valid inference boundary between association, Mendelian randomization, and individual token causation.

Abstract: Personalized health AI systems face a fundamental cold-start problem: machine learning models for physiological interpretation require weeks of individual behavioral data before they can distinguish constitutional variation from environmentally driven deviation. We propose a solution grounded in causal inference and Bayesian prior design. An individual's genomic profile serves as an exogenous genetic anchor -- a domain-informed, personalized prior that is fixed at conception, immune to reverse causation, and available before a single behavioral observation is collected. The anchor initializes a Bayesian belief state over an individual's physiological set point G-hat = mu + sum(beta_i * g_i), where beta_i are GWAS-derived effect sizes and g_i are risk-allele counts. Each incoming physiological measurement P produces a non-constitutional deviation delta = P - G-hat that separates the signal attributable to environment and state from the constitutionally fixed baseline. As behavioral data accrue, the prior decays according to G-hat_t = w(t)*G-hat_genomic + [1-w(t)]*P-bar_t, transitioning from genome-dominated to empirical-baseline-dominated inference. The same observed HRV of 55 ms generates a suppression hypothesis for a person whose prior predicts 80 ms, and an enhancement hypothesis for a person whose prior predicts 30 ms -- a reversal impossible without a personalized anchor. We develop this architecture across six physiological domains, grading genomic priors by evidence strength, distinguishing robustly replicated anchors (FTO, FADS1/2, FKBP5) from contested candidate genes (SLC6A4, MAOA, DRD2). We address the inference boundary between association, Mendelian randomization, and individual token causation, and define four constraints for deployment: evidence-graded priors, dynamic decay, ancestry-matched effect sizes, and attribution rather than deterministic output.

</details>


### [234] [Definitional alignment before capability alignment: a Design-Science framework for adjudicating claims about AGI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12713)
*J. E. Aguilera Briones*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper develops DAF-AGI, a conceptual artifact for assessing AGI definitions, comprising five ordinal adjudicative criteria and a governance audit framework. Demonstrated on five definitional families, it shows that current generative AI qualifies as AGI only under performance-based operationalizations, while other approaches either reject or remain indeterminate on that claim. The work introduces "definitional sovereignty" as a governance concept.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The AGI debate suffers from terminological under-specification: "AGI" lacks a stable referent, and competing operationalizations yield contradictory verdicts on the same system. This confusion is framed as both a design problem and a governance problem requiring structured resolution.

Method: Following Design Science Research Methodology, the paper constructs DAF-AGI, a second-order conceptual artifact with two components: (1) five ordinal criteria for evaluating the adjudicative fitness of AGI definitions, and (2) a structured governance audit covering authorship, interest, certification, external verification, and revision authority. The artifact is demonstrated on five prominent definitional families (performance-based, capability-ontology, psychometric, skill-acquisition, economic) and a deflationary boundary position, then stress-tested against the 2024-2025 claim that generative systems constitute AGI.

Result: The stress-test revealed that the claim of current generative systems as AGI was certifiable only under performance-based operationalizations. Capability-ontology, psychometric, and skill-acquisition approaches did not certify it; the economic family remained indeterminate; and the deflationary position refused binary adjudication entirely.

Conclusion: The contribution is a novel integration and operationalization rather than empirical validation. Independent application, inter-rater testing, and author-external cases remain necessary. The paper proposes "definitional sovereignty" as an enabling component of algorithmic sovereignty: the institutional capacity to contest, certify, and revise imported technological categories under public accountability.

Abstract: Claims that artificial general intelligence has already arrived and claims that it remains decades away are often defended from overlapping evidence. "AGI" lacks a single shared and stable referent and competing operationalizations can return different verdicts on the same system. This article treats that under-specification as a design and governance problem. Following Design Science Research Methodology, it develops DAF-AGI, a second-order conceptual artifact with two coupled components: five ordinal criteria for assessing the adjudicative fitness of candidate definitions and a structured governance audit of authorship, interest, certification, external verification and revision authority. The artifact is demonstrated on five prominent measurement families and one deflationary boundary position in a documented corpus and then stress-tested against a stylized strong arrival claim: that current generative systems constitute AGI because they outperform a well-educated adult on many cognitive tasks. On evidence from the cited 2024-2025 sources, the claim was certifiable only under a performance-based operationalization; capability-ontology, psychometric and skill-acquisition approaches did not certify it, the economic family remains indeterminate and the deflationary position refuses binary adjudication. The contribution is a novel integration and operationalization, not an empirical validation: independent application, inter-rater testing and author-external cases remain necessary. The paper further proposes definitional sovereignty as an enabling component of algorithmic sovereignty: the institutional capacity to contest, certify and revise imported technological categories under public accountability.

</details>


### [235] [Beyond Runtime Enforcement: Shield Synthesis as Defensibility Analysis for Adversarial Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13621)
*Achraf Hsain,Sultan Almuhammadi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper reinterprets shielded reinforcement learning not as a runtime safety mechanism but as a design-time analytical tool. Applied to network defense via a constrained two-player safety game, it yields a "defensibility verdict" and a "defensibility fingerprint" that captures both formal safety properties and operational behavior. The key insight is that formal defensibility and operational effectiveness are distinct dimensions, making shield synthesis valuable for answering architectural questions rather than merely constraining deployed agents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The authors argue that the prevailing view of shielded RL — as a runtime safety mechanism compiling temporal-logic specifications into action-restricting automata — is misguided. The true value of the automata-theoretic machinery (specification compilation, product game construction, attractor computation, winning-region extraction) lies in design-time structural analysis, not online constraint enforcement.

Method: The approach instantiates a constrained two-player safety game for network defense with asymmetric specification enforcement: the defender's specification defines unsafe regions, while the attacker's specification constrains legal adversary actions during attractor computation. Solving this game produces a defensibility verdict (a formal certificate of whether a topology-specification pair is defensible) and the associated winning region/shield. Beyond the binary verdict, topology-level metrics are extracted from the attractor structure and combined with post-convergence behavior from shield-constrained adversarial multi-agent RL, forming a "defensibility fingerprint." A what-if analysis examines how architectural changes affect both formal and operational dimensions.

Result: The what-if analysis reveals that formal defensibility and operational effectiveness are distinct security dimensions: small architectural changes can cause large shifts in operational outcomes while formal safety margins remain nearly unchanged. This demonstrates that shield synthesis is more valuable as an architectural reasoning framework than as a safe-agent deployment mechanism.

Conclusion: Shield synthesis should be reframed: its primary output is the defensibility verdict — a formal answer to architectural questions about whether, where, and how a system can be defended — rather than a runtime safe policy for a deployed agent.

Abstract: Shielded reinforcement learning is typically presented as a runtime safety mechanism that compiles temporal-logic specifications into automata restricting an agent's actions. We argue this is the wrong product. The same automata-theoretic machinery -- specification compilation, product game construction, attractor computation, and winning-region extraction -- is better read as a design-time analytical instrument whose outputs are structural insights about a system rather than runtime constraints on a deployed agent.
  We instantiate this through a constrained two-player safety game for network defense. The two specifications are enforced asymmetrically: the defender specification defines the unsafe region of the game, whereas the attacker specification restricts the adversary's legal actions during attractor computation. Solving the game yields a defensibility verdict -- a formal certificate that a topology-specification pair is or is not defensible -- with the associated winning region and shield.
  Beyond the binary verdict, we derive topology-level metrics from the attractor structure and combine them with post-convergence behavior from shield-constrained adversarial multi-agent reinforcement learning. Together these form a defensibility fingerprint capturing both a network's formal safety properties and its operational behavior under adaptive play.
  A what-if analysis shows that formal defensibility and operational effectiveness capture distinct aspects of security: small architectural changes can produce large shifts in operational outcomes while leaving formal safety margins nearly unchanged. Shield synthesis is thus most valuable not as a deployment mechanism for safe agents, but as a framework for answering architectural questions about whether, where, and how a system can be defended. The defensibility verdict is the output, not the safe policy.

</details>


### [236] [The Theory of Mind Utility: Formal Specification of a Mentalizing Mechanism](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12721)
*Nikolos Gurney,Stacy Marsella*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: ToM-U formalizes epistemic state inference (mentalizing) by constructing and evaluating Local Epistemic World Models (LEWMs) — directed typed graphs tracking agents, states, and epistemic relations — against observed behavior, generating falsifiable predictions about mentalizing failure from structural properties rather than auxiliary assumptions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing formal accounts of Theory of Mind (Bayesian ToM, simulation theory, theory-theory) either presuppose belief states rather than deriving them, or lack a formal computational-level specification for how epistemic state inference operates. The paper aims to fill this gap by specifying what mentalizing computes and why, independent of algorithmic or neural implementation.

Method: ToM-U introduces five formal components: (1) LEWM structure — directed typed graphs with agents and state nodes; (2) agent node properties including ordered information access history; (3) a bounded proliferation mechanism for recursive mentalizing; (4) three inference procedures for evaluating candidate LEWMs against observed behavior until sufficient confidence; and (5) a residue function capturing structured traces of failed mentalizing attempts. The framework operates at Marr's computational level.

Result: ToM-U generates directional, falsifiable predictions about mentalizing failure that follow directly from the model's structural properties. It positions itself as a domain-agnostic mechanism upstream of goal inference and other downstream social cognitive processes, distinguishing it from Bayesian ToM and adjacent accounts.

Conclusion: ToM-U provides a principled computational-level formalization of epistemic state inference that derives rather than presupposes belief states, using LEWMs as the core representational structure. Its residue function and bounded recursive architecture yield testable predictions about when and how mentalizing breaks down.

Abstract: Inferring others' beliefs requires more than reading surface signals; it requires tracking who told them what, in what order, and how credibly. The Theory of Mind Utility (ToM-U) formalizes this epistemic state inference problem at the computational level of analysis, specifying what mentalizing computes and why without commitment to algorithmic or neural implementation. ToM-U achieves this by constructing Local Epistemic World Models (LEWMs) -- directed typed graphs that represent agents, state nodes, and the epistemic relationships among them -- and evaluating discrete candidate LEWMs against observed behavior until one achieves sufficient confidence. Five formal definitions specify the LEWM structure, agent node properties including ordered information access history, a bounded proliferation mechanism for recursive mentalizing, three inference procedures, and a residue function that captures the structured trace left by failed mentalizing attempts. ToM-U differs from Bayesian Theory of Mind and adjacent formal accounts, which presuppose rather than derive belief states, and from simulation theory and theory-theory, which lack a formal apparatus for epistemic state inference. The architecture generates directional, falsifiable predictions about mentalizing failure that follow from structural properties of the model rather than auxiliary assumptions, and positions ToM-U as a domain-agnostic mechanism upstream of goal inference and other downstream social cognitive processes.

</details>


### [237] [Rethinking Psychometric Evaluation of LLMs: When and Why Self-Reports Predict Behavior](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12730)
*Rafal Kocielnik,Pengrui Han,Peiyang Song,Myrl G. Marmarelis,Ramit Debnath,Dean Mobbs,Anima Anandkumar,R. Michael Alvarez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs exhibit selective self-report/behavior coherence: Theory of Planned Behavior (a targeted, behavior-specific measure) achieves human-level coherence within shared conversations, whereas broad traits like Big 5 do not. Coherence across separate conversations survives only for training-anchored behaviors (e.g., implicit bias) and collapses under strong context priming (e.g., sycophancy). Persona prompting stabilizes self-reports but fails to align behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work found substantial dissociation between LLM self-reports and behavior using Big 5 personality traits, but this may reflect the weakness of broad traits (which predict specific behaviors poorly even in humans) rather than genuine LLM incoherence. The authors investigate whether a more targeted framework—Theory of Planned Behavior—can reveal coherence, and whether session context and identity induction moderate the relationship.

Method: The authors contrast Big 5 personality traits with the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), which measures intention targeted to a specific behavior. Experiments span four behavioral tasks and 11 frontier LLMs, systematically varying session context (within vs. across conversations) and identity induction (persona prompting).

Result: Three key findings: (1) Within a shared conversation, TPB reaches human-level SR-behavior coherence; Big 5 does not. (2) Across separate conversations, coherence survives only for behaviors anchored outside the immediate prompt (e.g., implicit bias from training) and collapses under strong context priming (e.g., sycophancy). (3) Persona prompting increases self-report consistency across conversations but does not align behavior with those self-reports.

Conclusion: Coarse personality frameworks like Big 5 are inadequate for testing LLM deployment behavior. More task- and behavior-specific instruments (e.g., TPB) are needed, and even these require cross-task, cross-context evaluation. The findings also reveal boundary conditions for SR-behavior coherence in LLMs.

Abstract: Anticipating LLM behavioral tendencies from low-cost psychometric probes is critical for safe deployment, but only if self-reports (SR) reliably predict behavior. Recent work documented substantial SR-behavior dissociation in LLMs, but relied on broad personality traits (Big 5) that predict specific behaviors weakly, even in humans. Furthermore, the isolation of conversational sessions combined with weak context matching left open whether LLMs truly lack coherence or whether the conditions needed to detect such coherence were not met. We contrast Big 5 with the Theory of Planned Behavior (TPB), which measures intention targeted to a specific behavior and predicts human behavior substantially better than broad traits. We run experiments across four behavioral tasks and 11 frontier LLMs, while also varying session context and identity induction. We find that SR-behavior coherence exists but is selective. 1) Within a shared conversation, the Theory of Planned Behavior reaches human-level coherence; Big 5 does not. 2) Across separate conversations, coherence survives only for behaviors anchored outside the immediate prompt, such as implicit bias shaped by training, and collapses when behavior is strongly primed by context, as with sycophancy. 3) Persona prompting makes self-reports more consistent across conversations, but does not bring behavior into alignment. These findings suggest that coarse personality frameworks, such as Big 5 may not be the best tools for testing deployment behavior. More task- and behavior-specific instruments are needed, and even these must be evaluated across tasks and contexts.

</details>


### [238] [Benchmarking AI Agents for Addressing Scientific Challenges Across Scales](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12736)
*Tianyu Liu,Allen Xin Wang,Antonia Panescu,Lisa Xinyi Chen,Wenxin Long,Xinyu Wei,Yueqian Jing,Ziyao Zeng,Jihang Chen,Sihan Jiang,Ziqing Wang,Siyi Gu,Siyu Chen,Xinyang Hu,Haoran Shao,Leqi Xu,Wangjie Zheng,Zhiyuan Cao,Ada Fang,Botao Yu,Kunyang Sun,Rex Ying,Arman Cohan,Qingyu Chen,Lingzhou Xue,Kaize Ding,Yuanqi Du,Wengong Jin,Zhuoran Yang,Marinka Zitnik,James Zou,Hua Xu,Hongyu Zhao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SciAgentArena is a benchmark of ~200 real-world scientific tasks with stepwise verification, revealing that current AI agents perform well on structured data-analysis workflows but struggle with novel insight generation, self-directed exploration, and open-ended research problems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI agent benchmarks fail to capture the complexity and extended reasoning of real scientific work, while scientific task benchmarks reduce research to static problems with limited interactive evaluation. There is a need for a systematic, interactive benchmark reflecting genuine scientific research demands.

Method: The authors introduce SciAgentArena, an agent-agnostic interactive environment comprising approximately 200 tasks drawn from multiple scientific domains. Tasks feature stepwise verification, and the benchmark supports evaluation of diverse AI agents in realistic research scenarios.

Result: Current agents perform well on well-specified data-analysis workflows with clear task structures and evaluation criteria. However, performance is uneven across scientific contexts: agents struggle with generating novel insights, sustaining self-directed exploration, and formulating robust solutions for open-ended research questions. Common failure modes are characterized.

Conclusion: SciAgentArena provides a practical framework for measuring progress in scientific AI agents and guiding the design of future agents capable of addressing complex scientific challenges. It identifies key areas for improving agent reliability, autonomy, and scientific reasoning.

Abstract: AI agents are increasingly being developed to accelerate scientific discovery, yet their practical capabilities in real research settings remain poorly understood. Existing benchmarks for AI agents rarely capture the complexity, heterogeneity, and extended reasoning required by scientific work, whereas benchmarks for scientific tasks often reduce research to static, direct problems and provide limited support for interactive evaluation. Here, we introduce SciAgentArena, a systematic benchmark for evaluating AI agents in real-world scientific research scenarios drawn from emerging needs across multiple domains. SciAgentArena comprises approximately 200 tasks with stepwise verification and an interactive, agent-agnostic environment for assessing diverse AI agents. Using this benchmark, we find that current agents can contribute effectively to well-specified data-analysis workflows, particularly when the task structure and evaluation criteria are clear. However, their performance remains uneven across scientific contexts: agents struggle to generate genuinely novel insights, sustain self-directed exploration, and formulate robust solutions for open-ended research questions. We further characterize common failure modes across agents and identify opportunities for improving their reliability, autonomy, and scientific reasoning. Together, SciAgentArena provides a practical framework for measuring progress in AI agents for science and for guiding the design of future agents capable of addressing complex scientific challenges. Full codes, tasks, and datasets can be accessed via this link: https://sciagentarena.github.io/.

</details>


### [239] [Reducing the Complexity of Deep Learning Models for EEG Analysis on Wearable Devices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12742)
*Farough Shayeste Roodi,Parham Zilouchian Moghaddam,Mahdi Mohammadi-nasab,Mehdi Modarressi,Mostafa Ersali Salehi Nasab,Masoud Daneshtalab*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Investigates deploying deep neural networks for EEG-based epileptic seizure detection on resource-constrained wearable devices, exploring accuracy-vs-complexity trade-offs via parameter quantization and electrode reduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Wearable healthcare IoT devices cannot meet the computational, energy, and memory bandwidth demands of DNN models, hindering deployment of deep learning for automated ECG/EEG analysis in practical wearable services.

Method: Evaluated state-of-the-art DNN models for EEG-based epileptic seizure detection under two compression strategies: (1) parameter quantization (reducing numerical precision of weights/activations) and (2) electrode reduction (decreasing the number of input EEG channels). Measured the resulting accuracy-vs-complexity trade-offs.

Result: Parameter quantization and electrode reduction, when applied judiciously, significantly reduce DNN computational complexity with minimal accuracy degradation. Explicit trade-off curves between accuracy and complexity reduction are demonstrated.

Conclusion: Deploying DNN-based online EEG analysis on wearable devices is feasible with careful application of compression techniques; the study provides empirical evidence of acceptable accuracy-complexity trade-offs for practical wearable seizure detection systems.

Abstract: Wearable healthcare devices are the fastest-growing Internet of Things (IoT) sector. Many automated healthcare services rely on two crucial biological signals, namely ECG and EEG, which reflect the activity of the heart and brain, respectively. Although deep neural networks are considered the primary way to process and analyze these signals, the very tight energy and computational power constraints in wearable devices are far below the computational, energy, and memory bandwidth demands of DNN models, thereby impeding the deployment of deep learning in many practical wearable services. This paper investigates the feasibility of deploying state-of-the-art DNN models in resource-constrained wearable devices. Notably, we explore the trade-off between accuracy and computational complexity of DNNs when parameter quantization and electrode reduction methods are used. Our investigation centers on several state-of-the-art DNN models designed for EEG signal analysis, specifically for detecting epileptic seizures. Our findings demonstrate that, when applied judiciously, these techniques can significantly reduce the complexity of the DNNs under consideration with minimal adverse effects on accuracy. These results reveal the explicit trade-offs between accuracy and complexity reduction encountered when adapting DNN-based online EEG analysis for wearable devices.

</details>


### [240] [Prefill Awareness in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12747)
*Andy Wang,Parv Mahajan,David Demitri Africa,Alexandra Souly,Jordan Taylor,Robert Kirk*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Frontier language models can detect when their prior assistant messages have been tampered with ("prefill awareness"), with Claude Opus 4.5 detecting 9-35% of opposing prefills at 0% false positive rate. Detection relies on stylistic mismatch, while behavioral reversion relies on preference mismatch, making this a significant confound for alignment, jailbreaking, and AI control evaluations that use prefilling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Many safety-relevant studies (alignment evaluations, jailbreaking tests, AI control protocols) rely on prefilling model outputs — inserting or editing prior assistant messages. If models can recognize this tampering, the validity of these methods is compromised. The authors aim to systematically measure whether frontier models possess "prefill awareness."

Method: The authors construct a binary preference benchmark across three prefill mechanisms (opposing, neutral, supporting relative to model baseline preferences), filtering for cases with consistent stances. They evaluate frontier models (primarily Claude Opus 4.5) on detection (explicitly reporting tampering) and resistance (reverting toward baseline behavior). Controlled ablations disentangle the roles of stylistic mismatch and preference mismatch. They also examine realistic agentic settings including misalignment-continuation evaluations and SWE-bench trajectories.

Result: Claude Opus 4.5 detects opposing prefills in 9-35% of cases with 0% false positive rate when prompted. Models frequently revert toward baseline behavior even without explicitly flagging the prefill as foreign. Stylistic mismatch primarily drives explicit detection (flagging), while preference mismatch primarily drives behavioral resistance (reversion). In agentic settings, models sometimes disavow prefilled assistant turns, with behavior strongly dependent on dataset, task success, and hidden formatting artifacts.

Conclusion: Prefill awareness is already a substantial confound for prefill-based safety methods. The authors recommend that model developers track this capability in frontier systems and that evaluations using prefilling should account for model awareness of tampered context.

Abstract: Safety-relevant studies of language models, including alignment and jailbreaking evaluations and AI control protocols, often rely on prefilling model outputs. If AI models can recognize and act on the fact their prior assistant messages have been inserted or edited, the effectiveness and validity of these methods could be compromised. We investigate whether frontier language models can distinguish between tampered and untampered assistant-side context, a capability we call prefill awareness. To do so, we construct a binary preference benchmark across three prefill mechanisms, filtering for cases where models show consistent stances. We find that frontier models show substantial prefill awareness: Claude Opus 4.5 detects prefills opposing its preferences in 9-35% of cases with a 0% false positive rate when prompted; additionally, models often revert towards baseline behavior without explicitly reporting that the prefill was foreign. Controlled ablations later also show that detection and resistance rely on different cues, where stylistic mismatch mainly affects whether models flag a prefill as foreign, while preference mismatch mainly affects whether they revert toward their baseline answer. We also examine more realistic agentic settings such as misalignment-continuation evaluations and SWE-bench trajectories, where frontier models sometimes disavow prefilled assistant turns in ways that depend strongly on dataset, task success, and hidden formatting artifacts. Our results indicate that prefill awareness is already a substantial confound for some prefill-based methods. We recommend that model developers track this capability in frontier systems.

</details>


### [241] [Constructing Evaluation Datasets for Procedural Reasoning: Balancing Naturalness, Grounding, and Multi-Hop Coverage](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12767)
*Sarah Elshabrawy,Rahul K. Dass,Ashok K. Goel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper compares three strategies for generating question-answer datasets from Task-Method-Knowledge (TMK) models for evaluating procedural reasoning in AI-supported learning systems. Strict TMK generation yields the highest grounding quality (96.5% grounded questions, 92.6% usable), while transcript-based methods produce more natural but less grounded items. The authors introduce a grounding validation framework and demonstrate that procedural richness does not guarantee representational grounding across 690 QA pairs spanning 23 topics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Evaluating procedural reasoning in AI-supported learning systems requires QA datasets that are both learner-like (natural, realistic) and grounded in instructional knowledge. Existing generation approaches trade off between these qualities, and there is no systematic understanding of how different TMK-based generation strategies impact dataset quality, particularly for multi-hop procedural reasoning.

Method: The authors compare three QA generation strategies: (1) strict TMK generation — questions derived directly from Task-Method-Knowledge models; (2) transcript-first generation — questions generated from learner transcripts with post-hoc TMK filtering; and (3) TMK-aware generation — combining transcripts with structured TMK guidance. They introduce a grounding validation framework using closed-set evidence units extracted from TMK models to measure answer support, question self-containedness, and multi-hop procedural reasoning targeting. Evaluation covers 23 instructional topics with 690 generated QA pairs.

Result: Strict TMK generation achieved the best overall quality: 96.5% grounded questions and 92.6% usable questions. Transcript-first generation produced more learner-like questions but resulted in more context-dependent or weakly grounded items. TMK-aware generation yielded high raw multi-hop coverage but lower grounding scores. The findings demonstrate a clear trade-off: procedural richness and natural phrasing do not guarantee representational grounding.

Conclusion: Explicit representation-aware validation is essential for building evaluation datasets in AI-supported learning. The grounding validation framework provides a systematic method for assessing whether generated QA pairs are properly anchored in the underlying knowledge representation, and the results advocate for strict TMK-based generation when grounding quality is the priority.

Abstract: Evaluating procedural reasoning in AI-supported learning systems requires question-answer datasets that are both learner-like and grounded in the instructional knowledge the system is expected to use. We study how TMK-based question generation strategies affect dataset quality for procedural and multi-hop reasoning.
  We compare three strategies: strict generation from Task-Method-Knowledge (TMK) models, transcript-first generation with post-hoc TMK filtering, and TMK-aware generation that combines transcripts with structured guidance. To evaluate generated items, we introduce a grounding validation framework based on closed-set evidence units extracted from TMK models. The framework measures whether answers are supported by the underlying representation, whether questions are self-contained, and whether they target multi-hop procedural reasoning.
  Across 23 instructional topics and 690 generated question-answer pairs, strict TMK generation achieves the strongest overall quality, with 96.5% grounded questions and 92.6% usable questions. Transcript-first generation produces more learner-like questions but more context-dependent or weakly grounded items, while TMK-aware generation yields high raw multi-hop coverage but lower grounding. These results show that procedural richness and natural phrasing do not guarantee representational grounding, motivating explicit representation-aware validation for evaluation datasets in AI-supported learning.

</details>


### [242] [A Tutorial on World Models and Physical AI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12783)
*Il-Seok Oh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This tutorial abstract frames world modeling as a core principle for intelligent systems, distinguishing between explicit models (structured dynamics for rollout-based planning) and implicit models (predictive structure in learned representations). It highlights relevance to physical AI, the promise of foundation models for unified perception-prediction-action systems, and key open challenges in hierarchical reasoning, long-horizon planning, and autonomous goal formation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: World modeling is positioned as essential for moving intelligent systems beyond reactive control toward prediction, reasoning, and decision-making under real-world constraints in domains like robotics and autonomous driving. The abstract identifies a need to unify diverse world modeling paradigms and to address foundational challenges that currently limit progress toward artificial general intelligence.

Method: The tutorial introduces a coherent framework that unifies diverse world modeling approaches through the lens of shared predictive structure. It draws a central distinction between explicit world models (structured dynamics for rollout-based reasoning and planning) and implicit world models (predictive structure encoded within scalable learned representations), and discusses how foundation models can integrate perception, prediction, and action.

Result: The abstract does not present empirical results, as it is a tutorial overview. It frames the key conceptual findings: that explicit and implicit world models are complementary paradigms, that foundation models offer a pathway toward unified systems, and that major open challenges persist in hierarchical reasoning, long-horizon planning, and autonomous goal formation.

Conclusion: World modeling provides a unifying principle for building intelligent physical AI systems. Both explicit and implicit paradigms contribute complementary strengths, and recent foundation models suggest a route toward integrated perception-prediction-action systems. However, achieving artificial general intelligence will require breakthroughs in hierarchical reasoning, long-horizon planning, and autonomous goal formation.

Abstract: World modeling is emerging as a central principle for building intelligent systems capable of prediction, reasoning, and decision making. A central distinction can be drawn between explicit world models, which learn structured dynamics for rollout-based reasoning and planning, and implicit world models, which encode predictive structure within scalable learned representations. These complementary paradigms provide a foundation for physical AI in domains such as robotics and autonomous driving, enabling intelligence beyond reactive control under real-world constraints. Recent foundation models further suggest a pathway toward unified systems integrating perception, prediction, and action. Despite rapid progress, major challenges remain in hierarchical reasoning, long-horizon planning, and autonomous goal formation, which are critical for advancing toward artificial general intelligence. This tutorial presents a coherent framework in which diverse world modeling approaches are unified through shared predictive structure and differentiated by how such structure is represented and exploited.

</details>


### [243] [The Containment Gap: How Deployed Agentic AI Frameworks Fail Public-Facing Safety Requirements](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12797)
*Md Jafrin Hossain,Mohammad Arif Hossain,Weiqi Liu,Nirwan Ansari*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Three dominant agentic AI frameworks (LangChain, AutoGPT, OpenAI Agents SDK) lack architectural-level structural safety guarantees. Empirical validation shows memory-poisoning attacks can cause 88.9% wrongful denial rates in a simulated government benefits agent, while evading detection. Two lightweight defenses (<0.2ms overhead) eliminate these vulnerabilities, but the ecosystem currently fails secure-by-default expectations for high-stakes deployments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Agentic LLM systems are increasingly deployed in high-stakes public-facing domains (government services, healthcare, finance), yet it is unknown whether the frameworks used to build them provide structural safety guarantees at the architectural level. The authors seek to determine if current frameworks meet secure-by-default expectations, particularly against memory-based attacks.

Method: The authors derive six containment principles from a compositional model of agentic architectures. They audit three frameworks (LangChain, AutoGPT, OpenAI Agents SDK) against these principles. Empirical validation is conducted via a simulated government benefits agent built on LangChain, testing memory-poisoning attacks across multiple seeds and backends under both simple and complex five-factor policy scenarios. Two countermeasures — a memory integrity validator and a policy gate — are introduced and benchmarked for latency overhead.

Result: None of the three frameworks natively comply with any of the six containment principles, including memory integrity. A single memory-poisoning write achieves 88.9% targeted wrongful denial rate across all tested seeds/backends. Under a complex five-factor policy, the attack preserves aggregate accuracy while increasing targeted wrongful denials 3.5×, evading standard monitoring. The proposed memory integrity validator and policy gate eliminate both attack vectors with sub-0.2ms per-call overhead.

Conclusion: The current agentic framework ecosystem does not meet secure-by-default expectations for public-facing deployments. Architectural-level interventions — specifically memory integrity validation and policy gating — are necessary, lightweight, and effective. The authors call for prioritizing such structural safety guarantees to enable trustworthy deployment in high-stakes, socially impactful applications.

Abstract: Agentic large language model systems that autonomously invoke tools, maintain persistent memory, and execute multi-step plans are increasingly deployed in public-facing domains, including government services, healthcare triage, and financial advising. We ask whether the frameworks used to build these systems provide architectural-level structural safety guarantees. Applying six containment principles derived from a compositional model of agentic architectures, we audit three dominant frameworks (LangChain, AutoGPT, and OpenAI Agents SDK) and find no native compliance in any of them. Memory integrity, a defense against one of the most prevalent vulnerability classes, is not observed in any of the three evaluated frameworks. We validate these findings empirically: in a simulated government benefits agent built on LangChain, a single memory-poisoning write induces persistent targeted corruption across all tested seeds and backends, increasing the wrongful denial rate for targeted applicants to 88.9%. Under a complex five-factor policy, the same attack preserves aggregate accuracy while increasing targeted wrongful denials by 3.5x, rendering the corruption difficult to detect through standard monitoring. We then introduce two lightweight containment mechanisms: a memory integrity validator and a policy gate, which eliminate both attack vectors with sub-millisecond overhead (<0.2ms per call). We conclude that the current agentic framework ecosystem may not yet meet secure-by-default expectations for public-facing deployments and outline priority architectural interventions to enable trustworthy deployment in high-stakes, socially impactful applications.

</details>


### [244] [MLUBench: A Benchmark for Lifelong Unlearning Evaluation in MLLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12809)
*He Li,Haoang Chi,Qizhou Wang,Yunxin Mao,Zhiheng Zhang,Jie Tan,Tongliang Liu,Wenjing Yang,Bo Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Introduces MLUBench, a large-scale benchmark for evaluating lifelong unlearning in multimodal LLMs covering 127 entities over 9 classes. Reveals that existing methods suffer cumulative degradation due to the challenge of preserving multimodal alignment. Proposes LUMoE, a new method that significantly mitigates this degradation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As MLLMs are trained on massive multimodal data, data owners increasingly request removal of specific content. These unlearning requests arrive sequentially over time (lifelong unlearning), but existing benchmarks are too limited in scale and scope to properly evaluate this setting.

Method: The authors construct MLUBench, a benchmark with 127 entities across 9 classes for sequential unlearning evaluation. They then propose LUMoE (Lifelong Unlearning with Multimodal Experts), a method designed to mitigate cumulative degradation by better preserving multimodal alignment during sequential unlearning, unlike prior unimodal-focused approaches.

Result: Experiments on MLUBench reveal that existing unlearning methods suffer severe, cumulative degradation over sequential unlearning requests. LUMoE significantly mitigates this degradation compared to baseline methods.

Conclusion: MLLM lifelong unlearning is fundamentally more challenging than unimodal unlearning due to the multimodal alignment preservation constraint. LUMoE addresses this effectively, and the open-sourced MLUBench benchmark and code provide a foundation for future research in this direction.

Abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are trained on massive multimodal data, making data unlearning increasingly important as data owners may request the removal of specific content. In practice, these requests often arrive sequentially over time, giving rise to the challenging problem of MLLM Lifelong Unlearning. However, most existing benchmarks are limited in scale and scope, failing to capture the complexities of MLLM lifelong unlearning. To fill this gap, we introduce the MLUBench, a large-scale and comprehensive benchmark featuring 127 entities across 9 classes under lifelong unlearning requests. We perform extensive experiments using MLUBench and reveal that existing unlearning methods suffer from severe, cumulative degradation. More critically, we further identify the unique challenge of this problem: unlike in unimodal models, MLLM lifelong unlearning is constrained by the need to preserve multimodal alignment. Continually unlearning from one modality could degrade the entire model. To alleviate this challenge, we propose LUMoE, an effective method. Experiments demonstrate that LUMoE significantly mitigates the degradation problem faced by baselines. The source code and the MLUBench dataset are open-sourced in https://github.com/lihe-maxsize/Lifelong_Unlearning_main.

</details>


### [245] [Teach-and-Repeat: Accurately Extracting Operational Knowledge from Mobile Screen Demonstrations to Empower GUI Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12817)
*Yudong Zhang,Lei Hu,Daoyang Liu,Jiawei Liu,Yangfan Luo,Xingyu Liu,Zuojian Wang,Zhilin Gao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Teach VLM translates mobile screen demonstration videos into step-wise operational knowledge (action types, UI targets, textual arguments, execution orders). A data flywheel addresses training data scarcity, and a Chinese benchmark enables fine-grained evaluation. The Teach-and-Repeat paradigm uses this knowledge to guide downstream screen execution agents, achieving SOTA on operation semantics prediction and improving task success rates in Android World.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mobile UIs are highly diverse and heterogeneous across applications, making it difficult for existing VLMs to accurately infer operational actions from visual state transitions. There is a gap between static UI perception and dynamic action comprehension, and a lack of aligned training data for teaching models to extract step-wise operational knowledge from demonstration videos.

Method: The authors propose Teach VLM, a model that extracts operation-related keyframes from demonstration videos and translates them into natural-language operational knowledge. A systematic data flywheel enables scalable data acquisition. A novel Chinese Mobile Screen Teach Benchmark is introduced for fine-grained evaluation. The Teach-and-Repeat paradigm uses the generated operational knowledge as an interpretable procedural reference to guide downstream execution agents.

Result: Teach VLM significantly outperforms strong VLM baselines and achieves state-of-the-art performance on operation semantics prediction. The Teach-and-Repeat paradigm yields consistent Task Success Rate improvements for downstream agents in Android World evaluations.

Conclusion: Teach VLM and the Teach-and-Repeat paradigm together provide a practical pathway from raw demonstrations to reusable task automation, bridging the gap between visual demonstration understanding and executable agent behavior on mobile devices.

Abstract: Understanding the digital world on mobile devices is shifting from static UI perception to dynamic action comprehension. This capability enables models to convert visual state transitions into operational knowledge, defined as short natural-language sentences that describe action types, target UI elements, textual arguments, and execution orders. However, due to the highly diverse and heterogeneous UI designs across applications, existing vision-language models (VLMs) struggle to accurately infer these underlying operations. To bridge this gap, we introduce Teach VLM, a core model designed to translate mobile screen trajectories into step-wise operational knowledge by extracting and analyzing operation-related keyframes from demonstration videos. To address the scarcity of aligned training data, we develop a systematic data flywheel for scalable data acquisition. We further introduce a novel Chinese Mobile Screen Teach Benchmark for fine-grained evaluation. Building upon Teach VLM, we propose the Teach-and-Repeat paradigm, where the generated operational knowledge serves as an interpretable procedural reference to guide downstream screen-based execution agents. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that Teach VLM significantly outperforms strong VLM baselines, achieving state-of-the-art performance in operation semantics prediction. Furthermore, experiments in Android World show that our paradigm yields consistent Task Success Rate improvements for downstream agents. Together, Teach VLM and the Teach-and-Repeat paradigm offer a practical pathway from raw demonstrations to reusable task automation.

</details>


### [246] [GeoNatureAgent Benchmark: Benchmarking LLM Agents for Environmental Geospatial Analysis Across Frontier and Open-Weight Foundation Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12821)
*Gabriel Diaz-Ireland,Diego Prieto-Herráez,Mario García Peces,Javier Velázquez,Devika Jain*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The GeoNatureAgent Benchmark is the first benchmark for evaluating AI agents performing geospatial environmental analysis via structured tool calls to a real API. Testing 7 LLMs across 93 tasks in 18 categories, Claude Sonnet 4 leads (60.8%), DeepSeek V3.2 offers 93% of that capability at 11x lower cost, comparison tasks remain unsolved (0%), and structured API calling proves far more discriminative than existing GIS benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Environmental scientists spend disproportionate time on data wrangling rather than analysis. While AI agents could automate geospatial workflows, no benchmark exists that evaluates agents operating through structured tool calling against real, production-style geospatial APIs. Existing GIS benchmarks fail to test this realistic agentic paradigm.

Method: The authors introduce the GeoNatureAgent Benchmark with 93 tasks across 18 categories (municipality analysis, spatial reasoning, multi-turn conversation, cross-indicator synthesis, error handling, ranking, comparison, multilingual understanding, habitat analysis, task rejection). Tasks are evaluated against a self-hostable API serving 3 environmental indicators across Spain and Portugal via 16 tools. Seven LLMs are evaluated under 3 temperature-1.0 seeds, reporting both capability and per-case cost. Extensibility is demonstrated via BigEarthNet V2 integration.

Result: (1) Claude Sonnet 4 leads at 60.8% ± 0.8%, DeepSeek V3.2 at 56.3% ± 3.1%, no other model surpasses 51%. (2) The cost-accuracy Pareto frontier is dominated by open-weight models; DeepSeek V3.2 achieves 93% of Claude's capability at 11× lower cost ($0.011/case). (3) Comparison tasks remain universally unsolved (0% on close-value comparisons), exposing systematic reasoning limits. (4) Structured tool calling against real APIs is far more discriminative than general GIS benchmarks, with accuracies 25-35 points lower.

Conclusion: Structured tool calling against real geospatial APIs reveals significant capability gaps in current LLMs that general-purpose GIS benchmarks mask. The benchmark, harness, and self-hostable API are publicly released to spur progress on practical environmental analysis agents, with open-weight models showing strong cost-accuracy potential.

Abstract: Environmental scientists spend disproportionate effort on data wrangling rather than analysis, and AI agents that automate geospatial workflows remain unvalidated: no benchmark evaluates agents operating through structured tool calling against real APIs. We introduce the GeoNatureAgent Benchmark, the first benchmark for environmental analysis agents that operate via structured tool calls to a production-style geospatial API. It comprises 93 tasks across 18 categories, covering municipality analysis, multi-turn conversation, spatial reasoning, cross-indicator synthesis, error handling and recovery, ranking, comparison, multilingual understanding, habitat analysis, and task rejection. Tasks are evaluated against an open, self-hostable API serving three environmental indicators across Spain and Portugal via sixteen tools. We evaluate seven LLMs (Claude Sonnet 4, DeepSeek V3.2, GLM-5, Gemini 2.5 Pro, Qwen3-235B, GPT-OSS-120B, Llama 4 Scout) under three temperature-1.0 seeds, reporting capability and per-case cost as orthogonal axes. We find: (1) Claude Sonnet 4 leads at 60.8% +/- 0.8%, followed by DeepSeek V3.2 at 56.3% +/- 3.1%, with no other model above 51%; (2) the cost-accuracy Pareto frontier is occupied mostly by open-weight models, with DeepSeek V3.2 offering 93% of Claude's capability at 11x lower cost ($0.011/case); (3) comparison tasks remain universally unsolved (0% on close-value comparisons), exposing systematic reasoning limits; and (4) structured tool calling against a real API is more discriminative than general-purpose GIS benchmarks, with accuracies 25-35 points lower. We further show extensibility by integrating BigEarthNet V2 land cover for Portugal alongside Spanish CO2 and erosion indicators. The benchmark, harness, and self-hostable API are publicly available.

</details>


### [247] [Topical Phase Transitions in Artificial Intelligence Research: Large-Scale Evidence and an Early-Warning Signature for Emerging Topics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12828)
*Rasul Khanbayov,Hasan Kurban*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Analysis of 80,814 AI conference papers (2017–2025) reveals that major AI topics exhibit "phase transitions" — surging abruptly across venues after years of marginality — rather than smooth growth. LLMs and diffusion models exemplify this pattern. The authors develop an early-warning signature achieving 63% recall and flag reasoning, agentic AI, multimodal LLMs, RAG, and world models as emerging transitions for 2026–2028.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior literature tends to characterize scientific topic growth as gradual diffusion. The authors challenge this by hypothesizing that AI research exhibits punctuated equilibrium — topics remain dormant then surge abruptly. They aim to provide the first large-scale, cross-venue empirical characterization of how AI research reorganizes and to develop a predictive early-warning system for detecting nascent phase transitions before they peak.

Method: The authors analyze 80,814 accepted main-track papers from ACL, CVPR, ICLR, ICML, and NeurIPS (2017–2025). They define topical phase transitions via quantitative publication-dynamics criteria, distinguishing genuine abrupt surges from smooth compounding growth (e.g., reinforcement learning). They then derive an early-warning signature using four publication-dynamics criteria frozen on 2017–2021 training data, evaluating it out-of-sample on 2023–2025 transitions (precision: 27%, recall: 63%, base rate: 13.5%). The signature is applied prospectively to 2025 data.

Result: Major findings: (1) LLMs became the dominant cross-venue topic by 2025; (2) diffusion models rose with comparable abruptness; (3) language-model methods crossed into CV via vision-language models; (4) reinforcement learning compounded smoothly, distinguishing genuine phase transitions. The early-warning signature flagged five candidate transitions for 2026–2028: reasoning and test-time compute, agentic AI, multimodal LLMs, retrieval-augmented generation, and world models.

Conclusion: AI research advances through topical phase transitions rather than gradual growth. These transitions leave detectable pre-peak footprints, enabling prospective identification of emerging topics. The flagged topics (reasoning, agentic AI, multimodal LLMs, RAG, world models) warrant monitoring over 2026–2028. The authors release source code to support further research.

Abstract: Do research topics in artificial intelligence grow gradually, or do they advance through abrupt, detectable jumps? Analyzing 80,814 accepted main-track papers from five premier AI conferences (ACL, CVPR, ICLR, ICML, NeurIPS) spanning 2017 to 2025, we show major AI topics advance through topical phase transitions: remaining marginal for years, then surging across venues within one to three years. Large language models became the dominant cross-venue topic by 2025, diffusion models rose with comparable abruptness, and language-model methods crossed into computer vision via vision-language models, whereas reinforcement learning compounded smoothly, distinguishing genuine phase transitions from ordinary growth. This structure is our primary contribution: a large-scale, cross-venue characterization of how AI research reorganizes. We then ask whether a transition leaves a detectable footprint before it peaks. We define an early-warning signature, four publication-dynamics criteria frozen on 2017-2021 data, and evaluate it out of sample on 2023-2025 transitions, obtaining a precision of 27% and recall of 63% against a 13.5% base rate. Applied to 2025 data, the signature flags reasoning and test-time compute, agentic AI, multimodal LLMs, retrieval-augmented generation, and world models as topics to monitor over 2026-2028. The source code is also publicly available on GitHub at https://github.com/KurbanIntelligenceLab/ai-phase-transitions.

</details>


### [248] [Fantastic Scientific Agents and How to Build Them: AgentBuild for Rietveld Refinement](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12834)
*Woong Shin,Craig A. Bridges,Marshall T. McDonnell,Rafael Ferreira da Silva*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentBuild treats scientific agent construction as a workflow stage, using a scientist-authored contract (rubric, curriculum, knowledge base) to guide a meta-optimizer that builds the agent without burying the scientist's judgment. Instantiated for Rietveld refinement of XRD data, it demonstrates frontier-case exposure and contract reusability across base model updates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based agent development practices (fine-tuning, RL, prompt-and-go) obscure or eliminate the scientist's judgment from the workflow. There is a need for a principled construction method where the scientist's expertise remains the durable, version-controlled asset, and agent building becomes a reproducible, auditable stage rather than a black-box tuning process.

Method: AgentBuild treats agent construction as a formal workflow stage. The scientist authors a contract consisting of three components: (1) a version-controlled rubric for evaluation, (2) a difficulty-graded curriculum of tasks, and (3) a curated external knowledge base. A rubric-driven judge gates a meta-optimizer coding agent that edits the scientific agent within declared boundaries. The system is instantiated for Rietveld refinement of X-ray diffraction data using GSAS-II behind MCP (Model Context Protocol) and A2A (Agent-to-Agent) protocols, tested on a lithium lanthanum zirconium oxide (LLZO) signal-to-noise ladder.

Result: A blank-harness construction run successfully progressed through the LLZO signal-to-noise ladder, reaching a 4-hour scan as a frontier case. The rubric scores both fit credibility and trajectory scope, meaning frontier cases register as contract failures rather than pattern-fitting failures. The approach exposes workflow-scope limits transparently. The contract remains the durable, reusable asset when base models evolve — re-running AgentBuild constitutes a re-tune, not a rebuild.

Conclusion: AgentBuild demonstrates that treating agent construction as a rubric-gated, contract-driven workflow stage preserves the scientist's judgment as the durable asset in LLM-based scientific workflows. The approach offers reproducibility, auditability, and graceful degradation at frontier cases, with the contract outliving any particular base model generation.

Abstract: As scientific workflows shift from deterministic executables to LLM-based agents, the development practices on offer, such as fine-tuning, reinforcement learning, and prompt-and-go, bury the scientist's judgment. We propose treating agent construction as a workflow stage and introduce AgentBuild, which builds a scientific agent from a contract the scientist authors. The contract is a version-controlled rubric, a difficulty-graded curriculum, and a curated external knowledge base.
