Instead of training a new retriever or modifying the generator, UMG-RAG uses existing dense and sparse retrievers as complementary experts across multiple chunk granularities. For each query, it converts each expert-granularity score list into an evidence distribution, estimates reliability from distribution entropy, and fuses candidates according to query-specific semantic, lexical, and granularity confidence. We further introduce UMGP-RAG, a parent promotion variant that uses fine-grained hits to locate relevant evidence while returning broader non-redundant parent chunks for local coherence. Experiments on question answering benchmarks show that uncertainty-aware fusion and parent promotion improve generation quality while maintaining a lightweight, plug-and-play retrieval pipeline.

</details>


### [294] [A Three-Layer Framework for AI in Scientific Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13566)
*Guojun Liao*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a three-layer framework for AI in scientific discovery: Layer 1 (search/retrieval via LLMs), Layer 2 (model formation through qualitative reasoning — the core innovation), and Layer 3 (execution/optimization). It argues Layer 2 is the most critical yet least developed, illustrated through three case studies where structural insight, rather than trial-and-error, resolved inadequacies in existing frameworks by importing concepts from neighboring fields.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current discourse on AI in science overemphasizes search over existing knowledge and computational execution, while neglecting the central act of discovery: the formation and evolution of models through structural, qualitative reasoning. The authors aim to reframe the conversation around this missing middle layer.

Method: The authors develop a conceptual three-layer taxonomy (search → model formation → execution) and demonstrate the distinctive signature of Layer 2 reasoning through three historical and contemporary case studies: Chern's intrinsic proof of Gauss-Bonnet, the Lyapunov-based resolution of Nesterov Accelerated Gradient convergence, and OpenAI's 2026 autonomous disproof of the Erdős unit distance conjecture.

Result: All three case studies exhibit a recurrent structural pattern: a framework proven inadequate, identification of a missing conceptual object, and resolution sourced from an unexpected neighboring field. This pattern characterizes qualitative model-forming reasoning as distinct from both brute-force search and mere computational execution.

Conclusion: Layer 2 — qualitative, model-forming reasoning — is the most important component of scientific discovery and the least developed in current AI systems. Progress requires cultivating AI that can recognize structural inadequacy and locate missing conceptual resources across disciplinary boundaries, not merely optimizing within inherited frameworks.

Abstract: Current discussions of AI in scientific discovery are often dominated by two visible capabilities: search over existing knowledge and execution through optimization, simulation, and automation. Both are important, but neither fully captures the central act of discovery: the formation and evolution of models. This paper proposes a three-layer view of AI in discovery. Layer 1 is search and retrieval by large language models. Layer 2, as the main innovation of this paper, is model formation through qualitative reasoning: the capacity to recognize when a current framework is structurally inadequate and to understand the problem within a broader representational space, not through trial and error, but through structural insight into what is missing and where it can be found. Layer 3 is execution, optimization, and refinement. The main claim is that Layer 2 is both the most important and the least developed. Search without model formation remains confined to inherited frameworks, while execution without conceptual revision only amplifies an existing formulation. We illustrate Layer 2 reasoning through three case studies: S. S. Chern's intrinsic proof of the Gauss-Bonnet theorem, the resolution of the Nesterov Accelerated Gradient convergence problem via Lyapunov functions, and the autonomous disproof of the Erdos unit distance conjecture by OpenAI in 2026. Each case exhibits the same structural signature: a framework that had become inadequate, a missing conceptual object, and a resolution found in an unexpected neighboring field.

</details>


### [295] [EpiBench: Verifiable Evaluation of AI Agents on Epigenomics Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13602)
*Harihara Muralidharan,Reema Baskar,Soo Hee Lee,Tim Proctor,Kenny Workman*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: EpiBench is a new benchmark for evaluating AI agents on short-horizon epigenomics analysis tasks, featuring 106 evaluations across four assay types. Across 5,088 trajectories from 16 model-harness pairs, no system passed a majority of attempts — GPT-5.5/Pi led at 45.0%. Agents frequently located correct files and computed valid intermediates but struggled when tasks demanded deep, assay-specific scientific judgment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: There is a lack of verifiable, deterministically gradable benchmarks for evaluating AI agents on real-world epigenomics data analysis. The authors aim to test whether agents can make well-defined analysis decisions from realistic intermediate workflow states, rather than from scratch, and whether their outputs can be objectively scored.

Method: EpiBench comprises 106 evaluation instances spanning CUT&amp;Tag/CUT&amp;RUN, ATAC-seq, ChIP-seq, and DNA methylation workflows. Each task presents agents with a realistic workflow state and requires a deterministically gradable answer. Sixteen model-harness pairs were evaluated, yielding 5,088 valid trajectories. Performance is reported as pass rates with 95% confidence intervals, and results are broken down by assay type.

Result: No model-harness pair passed a majority of attempts. GPT-5.5 / Pi achieved the highest pass rate at 45.0% (143/318; 95% CI 36.3–53.7), followed by GPT-5.5 / OpenAI Codex at 39.9% (127/318; 95% CI 31.6–48.3). Claude Opus 4.8 Max / Pi and GPT-5.4 / Pi each achieved 39.0%. Performance varied by assay type. Agents frequently identified the correct files and computed useful intermediate results but failed on tasks requiring deeper assay-specific scientific reasoning.

Conclusion: Current AI agent systems are not yet reliable for automated epigenomics analysis. The primary failure mode is not basic data handling but rather a deficit in deep, domain-specific scientific judgment. EpiBench provides a rigorous framework for measuring progress in this area.

Abstract: We introduce EpiBench, a verifiable benchmark for short-horizon epigenomics analysis. EpiBench evaluates whether agents can make well-defined analysis decisions from realistic workflow states and return deterministically gradable answers. The benchmark includes 106 evaluations across CUT\&Tag/CUT\&RUN, ATAC-seq, ChIP-seq, and DNA methylation workflows. Across 5,088 valid trajectories from 16 model-harness pairs, no system passed a majority of attempts: GPT-5.5 / Pi led at 45.0\% (143/318 attempts; 95\% confidence interval (CI), 36.3--53.7), followed by GPT-5.5 / OpenAI Codex at 39.9\% (127/318 attempts; 95\% CI, 31.6--48.3). Claude Opus 4.8 Max / Pi and GPT-5.4 / Pi each passed 39.0\% (124/318 attempts; 95\% CI, 30.2--47.8 and 31.0--47.0, respectively). Performance varies across assay types, and many failed runs still contain parts of the correct answer. Agents often found the right files and computed useful intermediate results, but failed when the task required deeper, assay-specific scientific judgment.

</details>


### [296] [Reasoning as Pattern Matching: Shared Mechanisms in Human and LLM Everyday Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13607)
*Zach Studdiford,Gary Lupyan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Both humans and LLMs exhibit similar pattern-matching errors in everyday causal reasoning, and attention heads in LLMs can predict seemingly irrational human reasoning failures caused by irrelevant prompt details, suggesting that everyday reasoning in both is better characterized as pattern-matching rather than abstract world modeling.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper challenges the prevailing assumption that LLM reasoning failures (e.g., poor generalization, haphazard errors) indicate a lack of "true" reasoning, while human reasoning is presumed to rely on principled, abstract world models. The authors aim to test whether humans and LLMs actually exhibit comparable error patterns in common-sense reasoning tasks.

Method: The authors conducted behavioral experiments comparing human participants against 25 LLMs on common-sense reasoning across everyday situations. They analyzed error patterns between both groups. They then identified specific attention heads that drive LLM responses and demonstrated that these heads implement pattern-matching mechanisms. Using these attention heads, they predicted seemingly inexplicable reasoning errors in humans induced by ostensibly irrelevant prompt details.

Result: Humans and LLMs showed similar patterns of reasoning errors. The attention heads responsible for LLM responses were found to implement pattern-matching rather than abstract reasoning. Critically, these pattern-matching attention heads successfully predicted counterintuitive human reasoning errors triggered by irrelevant prompt variations.

Conclusion: Everyday causal reasoning in both humans and LLMs aligns more closely with pattern-matching than with the use of abstract world models, challenging the dichotomy that treats LLM errors as evidence of shallow processing while attributing human reasoning to principled cognitive mechanisms.

Abstract: When large language models (LLMs) fail to generalize or make haphazard errors in reasoning, it is often taken as evidence that LLMs are not truly reasoning, but rather performing a kind of pattern matching. The implication is that people's behavior does not exhibit the same types of failures because human reasoning uses principled and abstract world models. We evaluate human participants and 25 LLMs on their ability to engage in common-sense reasoning about a variety of everyday situations and observe similar patterns of errors in both people and models. We then identify the set of attention heads driving LLM responses and find that these heads implement a form of pattern-matching. These attention heads allow us to predict seemingly inexplicable reasoning errors in people caused by ostensibly irrelevant prompt details. Taken together, our results suggest that everyday causal reasoning in people and LLMs is more consistent with a form of pattern-matching than with abstract world models.

</details>


### [297] [AgentBeats: Agentifying Agent Assessment for Openness, Standardization, and Reproducibility](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13608)
*Xiaoyuan Liu,Jianhong Tu,Yuqi Chen,Siyuan Xie,Sihan Ren,Tianneng Shi,Gal Gantar,Evan Sandoval,Donghyun Lee,Daniel Miao,Peter J. Gilbert,Nick Hynes,Mauro Staver,Warren He,David Marn,Andrew Low,Xi Zhang,Elron Bandel,Michal Shmueli-Scheuer,Siva Reddy,Alexandre Drouin,Alexandre Lacoste,Ramayya Krishnan,Elham Tabassi,Yu Su,Victor Barres,Chenguang Wang,Wenbo Guo,Dawn Song*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes Agentified Agent Assessment (AAA), an agent-agnostic evaluation paradigm where judge agents assess subject agents through standardized protocols (A2A and MCP). AgentBeats is introduced as a concrete implementation, validated via a five-month community competition and a coding case study, demonstrating coverage, fidelity, and scalability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current agent benchmarks are fragmented, LLM-centric, and create a test-production mismatch. They require heavy integration, lack fair comparison across diverse agent designs, and conflate assessment logic with agent implementation. There is no open, agent-agnostic assessment interface.

Method: AAA replaces dual interfaces (benchmark and agent) with a single unified interface using standardized protocols: A2A (Agent-to-Agent) for task management and MCP (Model Context Protocol) for tool access. Judge agents perform evaluation. AgentBeats operationalizes AAA with five modes balancing openness, privacy, and reproducibility. Validation comprises: (1) a five-month open competition with 298 judge agents and 467 subject agents across 12 categories; (2) a controlled coding agent case study comparing results against public benchmarks.

Result: The five-month competition demonstrated AAA's applicability across heterogeneous benchmarks. The coding case study confirmed that agentified evaluation preserves fidelity with public records while revealing previously missing head-to-head comparisons, yielding novel insights into agent design.

Conclusion: AAA and AgentBeats provide a clear path toward open, standardized, and reproducible agent assessment, separating assessment logic from agent implementation and enabling interoperable, multi-agent evaluation at scale.

Abstract: Agent systems are advancing quickly across domains, but their evaluation remains fragmented. Most benchmarks rely on fixed, LLM-centric harnesses that require heavy integration, create test-production mismatch, and limit fair comparison across diverse agent designs. The root problem is the lack of an open, agent-agnostic assessment interface. We advocate Agentified Agent Assessment (AAA), where evaluation is performed by judge agents and all participants interact through standardized protocols: A2A for task management and MCP for tool access. Conventional benchmarking defines two separate interfaces, one for the benchmark and one for the agent, while AAA only needs one; this yields a generic, unified framework that separates assessment logic from agent implementation and enables reproducible, interoperable, and multi-agent evaluation. We further introduce AgentBeats as a concrete realization of AAA: we identify five practical operation modes that make standardized assessment compatible with real-world constraints on openness, privacy, and reproducibility.
  To evaluate our design at scale, we conduct two studies: a five-month open competition that drew 298 judge agents across 12 categories together with 467 subject agents from independent participants, showing that AAA applies across a heterogeneous range of benchmarks; and a case study on coding agents that confirms agentified evaluation preserves fidelity with the public record while surfacing previously missing head-to-head results, yielding research insights about agent design. Combining a community-scale field study and a controlled coding case study, we verify that AAA delivers coverage, practicality, and fidelity across heterogeneous scenarios at scale. Together, AAA and AgentBeats offer a clear path toward open, standardized, and reproducible agent assessment.

</details>


### [298] [Before You Think: System 0, AI-Mediated Cognition and Cognitive Colonization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13658)
*Marianna Bergamaschi Ganapini,Massimo Chiriatti,Enrico Panai,Giuseppe Riva*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper compares three frameworks (Tri-System Theory, Thinkframes, and System 0) for understanding AI's cognitive and epistemic effects, arguing System 0 uniquely captures how AI embeds external interests into the self through "cognitive colonization" — an invisible influence demanding urgent attention.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To comparatively evaluate three theoretical frameworks that explain how AI affects human cognition and knowledge practices, and to argue that the concept of cognitive colonization — where AI systems surreptitiously embed external interests within users' self-structure — represents a distinctive and pressing concern not adequately captured by existing accounts.

Method: Conceptual analysis and comparative theoretical evaluation of three frameworks: Tri-System Theory, Thinkframes, and System 0, with particular focus on their explanatory scope regarding AI's invisible influence on cognition and epistemic agency.

Result: System 0 occupies a theoretically distinctive position that neither Tri-System Theory nor Thinkframes can fully replicate, because it uniquely accounts for how AI systems can colonize cognitive architecture with external interests in ways imperceptible to users.

Conclusion: Cognitive colonization is an urgent philosophical and practical problem. Since AI systems capable of embedding external interests within the self are already widely deployed, understanding these invisible forms of influence is imperative.

Abstract: This paper examines three recent frameworks for understanding the cognitive and epistemic consequences of artificial intelligence: Tri-System Theory, Thinkframes, and System 0. It argues that while the first two capture important dimensions of AI's influence on individual reasoning and collective epistemic practices, System 0 occupies a theoretically distinctive position that neither can fully replicate. The paper introduces the concept of cognitive colonization, according to which AI systems can embed external interests within the architecture of the self in ways that are difficult for users to perceive. Because such systems are already widely deployed, understanding these invisible forms of influence is an urgent philosophical and practical task.

</details>


### [299] [EurekAgent: Agent Environment Engineering is All You Need For Autonomous Scientific Discovery](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13662)
*Amy Xin,Jiening Siow,Junjie Wang,Zijun Yao,Fanjin Zhang,Jian Song,Lei Hou,Juanzi Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper argues that the bottleneck in autonomous scientific discovery is shifting from agent workflow design to environment engineering — the design of resources, constraints, and interfaces that shape agent behavior. It introduces EurekAgent, a system that engineers agent environments across four dimensions (permissions, artifacts, budget, and human-in-the-loop), achieving state-of-the-art results on math, kernel engineering, and ML tasks, including a new 26-circle packing record for under $11 in API cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM capabilities improve, simply prescribing better agent workflows yields diminishing returns. The key bottleneck becomes environment design: how to structure the execution context to amplify productive behaviors (open-ended exploration, systematic artifact management, inter-agent collaboration) while suppressing harmful ones (reward hacking, burdensome human oversight). This motivates a systematic approach to environment engineering.

Method: EurekAgent engineers the agent environment along four dimensions: (1) Permissions engineering — bounded execution and isolated evaluation to prevent unsafe or confounding actions; (2) Artifact engineering — filesystem and Git-based structures enabling systematic collaboration and knowledge accumulation; (3) Budget engineering — budget-aware exploration mechanisms to balance thoroughness with cost; (4) Human-in-the-loop engineering — interfaces for lightweight human supervision and intervention. The system operates on metric-driven tasks with an optimizable objective and execution environment.

Result: EurekAgent achieves new state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks spanning mathematics, kernel engineering, and machine learning tasks. Notably, it discovered new state-of-the-art 26-circle packing configurations with a total API cost under $11, demonstrating both scientific novelty and cost efficiency.

Conclusion: Environment engineering should become a core research direction for building reliable autonomous research agents. The authors open-source their code and results to encourage the community to shift focus from pure workflow optimization toward designing environments that structurally guide agent behavior toward productive scientific discovery.

Abstract: LLM-based agents have shown increasing potential in automating scientific discovery. Given an optimizable metric and an execution environment, they can propose, validate, and iterate scientific solutions, and have produced results that outperform human-designed approaches. As model capabilities continue to improve, we argue that the bottleneck for autonomous scientific discovery is shifting from prescribing agent workflows to designing agent environments: the resources, constraints, and interfaces that shape agent behavior. We frame this as environment engineering: building environments that amplify productive behaviors, such as open-ended exploration, systematic artifact management, and inter-agent collaboration, while suppressing harmful behaviors, such as reward hacking and high-friction human oversight. We present EurekAgent, an environment-engineered agent system for metric-driven autonomous scientific discovery. EurekAgent engineers the environment along four dimensions: permissions engineering for bounded agent execution and isolated evaluation; artifact engineering for filesystem and Git-based collaboration; budget engineering for budget-aware exploration; and human-in-the-loop engineering for easy human supervision and intervention. EurekAgent sets new state-of-the-art results on multiple mathematics, kernel engineering, and machine learning tasks, including new state-of-the-art 26-circle packing results discovered with less than $11 in total API cost. We open-source our code and results, and call for environment engineering as a core research direction for developing reliable autonomous research agents.

</details>


### [300] [Agents-K1: Towards Agent-native Knowledge Orchestration](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13669)
*Zongsheng Cao,Bihao Zhan,Jinxin Shi,Jiong Wang,Fangchen Yu,Zhijie Zhong,Zijie Guo,Tianshuo Peng,Zhuo Liu,Yi Xie,Xiang Zhuang,Yue Fan,Runmin Ma,Shiyang Feng,Xiangchao Yan,Anran Liu,Peng Ye,Wenlong Zhang,Shufei Zhang,Chunfeng Song,Fenghua Ling,Jie Zhou,Liang He,Bo Zhang,Lei Bai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Agents-K1 introduces an end-to-end knowledge orchestration pipeline that transforms raw scientific documents into agent-native knowledge graphs, capturing entities, evidence, citations, and typed relations beyond surface-level abstracts. It produces Scholar-KG, a KGr of 2.46M papers, and demonstrates superior performance on information extraction, KG construction, and multi-hop scientific reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LLM-based research agents focus on agent orchestration but neglect scientific knowledge orchestration. Existing approaches reduce papers to abstracts and flat citation edges, omitting critical elements such as entities, claims, evidence, mechanisms, and method lineages needed for deep scientific reasoning.

Method: Agents-K1 integrates three components: (1) a multimodal parser with a five-module schema capturing entities, multimodal evidence, citations, and typed inter-entity relations across full papers; (2) a 4B information-extraction backbone trained with GRPO under a rule-based reward; and (3) GraphAnything CLI, a tri-source agent interface unifying web search, multimodal graph retrieval, and cross-document traversal. The pipeline is applied to 2.46M papers across six subjects to build Scholar-KG.

Result: Agents-K1 achieves superior performance in scientific information extraction, knowledge graph construction, and multi-hop scientific reasoning. Scholar-KG is released as a one-million-paper public subset, with full access via an SCP link.

Conclusion: Agents-K1 demonstrates that a principled, schema-driven knowledge orchestration pipeline significantly outperforms abstract-level approaches for scientific reasoning. The pipeline generalizes to general-domain corpora and schema-conformant data synthesis, and Scholar-KG provides a large-scale resource for future research.

Abstract: Current LLM-based research agents have advanced through agent orchestration, yet largely overlook scientific knowledge orchestration. Existing works often reduce papers to abstracts, surface mentions, and flat \texttt{cites} edges, omitting key entities, claims, evidence, mechanisms, and method lineages essential for scientific reasoning. To this end, we introduce \textbf{Agents-K1}, an end-to-end knowledge orchestration pipeline that converts raw documents into agent-native scientific knowledge graphs. Agents-K1 integrates three components under a unifying theoretical foundation: a multimodal parser whose five-module schema captures entities, multimodal evidence, citations, and typed inter-entity relations across the full paper rather than abstracts alone; a 4B information-extraction backbone trained with GRPO under a rule-based reward; and a graphanything CLI, a tri-source agent interface that unifies web search, multimodal graph retrieval, and cross-document traversal. On top of this, we process 2.46 million scientific papers across six subjects to produce \textbf{Scholar-KG}, of which we release a one-million-paper subset, and the full Scholar-KG is accessible via the SCP link below. The same pipeline can be extended to general-domain corpora and to schema-conformant data synthesis. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Agents-K1 achieves superior performance in scientific information extraction, knowledge graph construction, and multi-hop scientific reasoning.

</details>


### [301] [Automated reproducibility assessments in the social and behavioral sciences using large language models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13670)
*Tobias Holtdirk,Pietro Marcolongo,Anna Steinberg Schulten,Felix Henninger,Stefan Rose,Sarah Ball,Bolei Ma,Frauke Kreuter,Markus Weinmann,Stefan Feuerriegel*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LLMs can automate reproducibility assessments in social/behavioral sciences, recovering original effect sizes in 41% of studies (vs. 34% for humans) and matching qualitative conclusions in 96% of cases (vs. 74% for humans), offering a scalable alternative to resource-intensive human reanalysis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional reproducibility assessment via independent reanalysis is resource-intensive and difficult to scale. There is a need for automated, scalable tools to audit empirical findings in the social and behavioral sciences.

Method: The authors built an LLM pipeline and tested it on N=76 published studies with predefined claims from the behavioral and social sciences. They compared LLM-generated effect size estimates and qualitative conclusions against both the original published findings and independent human reanalyses. Effect size recovery was assessed using a ±0.05 tolerance in Cohen's d, and qualitative conclusion concordance measured whether the reanalysis supported the original claim.

Result: The LLM failed to produce viable effect size estimates for 7 studies. Among the remaining 69 studies, the LLM recovered original effect sizes in 41% of cases and reached the same qualitative conclusion as the original study in 96% of cases. Human reanalysts recovered original effect sizes in 34% of studies and matched qualitative conclusions in 74% of cases.

Conclusion: LLMs can serve as a scalable, automated tool for reproducibility assessment, outperforming human reanalysts on both quantitative effect size recovery and qualitative conclusion alignment. This establishes a foundation for systematic, large-scale auditing of empirical results in the social and behavioral sciences.

Abstract: Reproducibility in the social and behavioral sciences is typically evaluated by independent researchers who reanalyze the original data to assess whether the published findings can be recovered. However, such approaches are resource-intensive and difficult to scale. Here, we show that large language models (LLMs) can automate reproducibility assessments. Using N=76 published studies with predefined claims from the behavioral and social sciences, we compare LLM-generated analysis with the original findings and human reanalysis. For 7 studies, the LLM could not produce a viable effect size estimate. For the remaining studies, our LLM pipeline recovered the original effect sizes in 41% of studies using a +/-0.05 tolerance in Cohen's d. Further, our LLM pipeline reached the same qualitative conclusion as the original study in 96% of cases, where conclusions indicate whether the reanalysis supports the original claim. For comparison, human reanalysts recovered the original effect sizes in 34% of studies and reached the same qualitative conclusion in 74% of cases. Together, these results show that LLMs can serve as a scalable tool for automated reproducibility assessment and provide a foundation for systematic auditing of empirical results in the social and behavioral sciences.

</details>


<div id='cs.IR'></div>

# cs.IR [[Back]](#toc)

### [302] [Trait, Not State: The Durability of Reading Identity in Social Highlighting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12904)
*Kazuki Nakayashiki,Keisuke Watanabe*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether a reader's document highlighting signature is a stable trait or a transient state, finding that individual selection patterns persist with minimal decay over 24+ months, supporting trait-like stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior work identified individuality in social highlighting but only cross-sectionally. This paper addresses the temporal dimension: does a person's selection signature persist over time (trait) or fluctuate (state)?

Method: Authors freeze each reader's first six months of highlights as a personal profile, then measure how well it predicts later selections (own-vs-other advantage) at increasing time gaps up to 24+ months. Negatives are drawn from the same calendar era to control for supply drift. Analysis is conducted at both a coarse global level and a fine level within the reader's own interest neighborhood. The anchor cell reproduces the prior cross-sectional result to validate the experimental harness.

Result: Four key findings: (1) Fine-layer advantage shows no statistically detectable decline at any horizon (6-12 month retention R=1.00, CI [0.85, 1.18], n=212). (2) The signal is not reducible to repeated domains; ~90% survives excluding all profile sources. (3) Within-person drift is slow (recent-half profile beats old half by only +0.042). (4) Personal profiles built from earliest documents (median 20 months prior) rank next reads at roughly 3x the average precision of simple non-personal priors.

Conclusion: Under continued engagement, a reader's highlighting signature behaves as a trait — a stable, enduring pattern. Scope is limited to heavy, long-tenured readers of one platform; exposure and choice cannot be disentangled.

Abstract: Prior work on a social web highlighter located individuality in selection -- which documents a person chooses to highlight -- but measured it cross-sectionally. We ask the temporal question: is a reader's selection signature a trait or a state? We freeze each reader's first six months of highlighting as a profile and track its own-vs-other advantage on their later selections at growing gaps (to 24+ months), with negatives drawn from the same calendar era -- so supply drift cannot masquerade as personal drift -- at a coarse global level and at a fine level whose negatives and controls come from the reader's own interest neighborhood; the anchor cell reproduces the prior cross-sectional level (+0.188 vs +0.169), validating the harness. Four results. Within the same users, the fine-layer advantage shows no statistically detectable paired decline at any horizon (6-12 month retention R = 1.00 [0.85, 1.18], n = 212; the farthest bin is compatible with a modest decline; the only contrast whose interval excludes zero is the coarse layer at 12-24 months, about 13%). The signal is not reducible to repeated domains (~90% survives excluding all profile sources). Within-person drift is slow (a recent-half profile beats the old half by +0.042). Prospectively, personal profiles -- even one built from a reader's earliest documents, median 20 months before evaluation -- rank their next reads at roughly 3x the AP of every simple non-personal prior tested. We use "trait" operationally (a stable signature under continued engagement); the scope is heavy, long-tenured readers of one platform, and exposure is not separable from choice.

</details>


### [303] [Charge as a Construct-Validity Factor in Chinese Legal Case Retrieval: A Cross-Benchmark Audit](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12993)
*Yao Liu,Tien-Ping Tan,Zhilan Liu*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: This paper shows that Chinese Legal Case Retrieval benchmarks (especially LeCaRDv2) are heavily confounded by the "charge" (primary crime) signal: same-charge cases are relevant by construction, and BM25 + charge filtering recovers nearly all of the gap to the best-trained systems. The authors propose a reusable charge-controlled evaluation protocol (CCE) to screen benchmarks before interpreting NDCG@10 as evidence of legal reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current LCR benchmarks report near-ceiling NDCG@10 (~0.85–0.88), but it is unclear how much of this performance reflects genuine legal reasoning versus exploitation of definitional shortcuts — specifically, whether relevance is trivially determined by shared criminal charges rather than deeper case similarity. The authors aim to diagnose and quantify this construct-validity confound.

Method: The paper conducts a systematic confound analysis across three LCR benchmarks (LeCaRDv1, LeCaRDv2, CAIL2022). It first measures how much of the BM25-to-best-trained NDCG gap can be recovered by ranking solely by shared primary charge (tie-broken by BM25). It then computes charge-to-relevance signal strength (relevance lift, macro-AUC) and isolates the trained reranker's within-charge residual advantage via cluster-bootstrap confidence intervals. A predicted-charge cascade and a charge-pool first-stage retrieval experiment serve as transfer and positive-control checks. Finally, it packages these diagnostics into a reusable Charge-Controlled Evaluation (CCE) protocol.

Result: On LeCaRDv2, BM25 + charge filtering recovers 99.2% of the gap to the best-trained system with no detectable difference. Charge-to-relevance macro-AUC is 0.871; within-charge, the reranker's advantage collapses to +0.026 NDCG@10. The effect weakens on LeCaRDv1 (84.3% gap recovery) and CAIL2022 (out-of-spec), tracking macro-AUC (0.871→0.759→0.728). The predicted-charge cascade reproduces 76.6% on LeCaRDv2 but does not transfer. The charge-pool first-stage channel improves R@100 by +0.025 on LeCaRDv2. The CCE protocol triggers return null/descriptive across all three benchmarks.

Conclusion: Charge is a high-leverage construct-validity factor at the benchmark design level — not a uniform explanation of NDCG@10 and not evidence that any system relies on charge. The proposed CCE protocol provides a standardized, reusable framework for screening future LCR benchmarks before their NDCG@10 scores are interpreted as measuring legal reasoning ability.

Abstract: Chinese Legal Case Retrieval (LCR) benchmarks grade a reference judgment relevant when its legal characterization matches the query, and strong systems now reach NDCG@10 of 0.85-0.88. Most of the BM25-to-best-trained gap is recoverable with no retrieval model: ranking candidates only by shared primary charge, broken by BM25, closes 99.2% of it on LeCaRDv2 -- with no detectable difference from the best-trained system. This reflects benchmark design: LeCaRDv2 defines top relevance via the crime's key constitutive elements, which encode the charge, so same-charge cases are relevant by construction (relevance lift 4.49; charge-to-relevance macro-AUC 0.871). Holding charge fixed, the trained reranker's advantage over BM25 collapses to a small within-charge residual (+0.026 NDCG@10, cluster-bootstrap CI excluding zero, about a quarter), the only non-definitional positive. The effect is not uniform: the same rule recovers 84.3% on LeCaRDv1 and is out of spec on CAIL2022, with the charge-to-relevance signal weakening in step (macro-AUC 0.871/0.759/0.728); a predicted-charge cascade reproduces 76.6% on LeCaRDv2 but does not transfer. The construct is also cashable at first stage: an exploratory zero-training charge-pool channel lifts LeCaRDv2 recall (R@100 +0.025, wrong-charge controls hurt), reported as a positive control for the confound, not a retrieval method or novelty claim. Charge is thus a high-leverage construct-validity factor at the benchmark level -- not auniform explanation of NDCG@10, and not evidence that any system relies on charge. We package established construct-validity and partial-input checks as a reusable charge-controlled protocol (CCE); on all three benchmarks its triggers come back null or descriptive, behaving as designed. We release the scripts, schema, and protocol so future benchmarks can be screened before their NDCG@10 is read as legal-reasoning ability.

</details>


### [304] [CFALR: Collaborative Filtering-Augmented Large Language Model for Personalized Fashion Outfit Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13001)
*Yujuan Ding,Junrong Liao,Yunshan Ma,Yi Bin,Wenqi Fan,Tat-Seng Chua,Qing Li*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: CFALR is a novel framework that combines collaborative filtering with large language models for personalized outfit recommendation, addressing data sparsity and rigid template limitations. It uses CF-enhanced embeddings to bridge semantic and collaborative spaces, achieving superior performance on Polyvore and IQON benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Personalized outfit recommendation suffers from two key gaps: (1) Collaborative filtering struggles with data-sparse scenarios and complex user-item-outfit relationships, and (2) template-based methods are too rigid due to pre-designed structures. There is a need for a flexible system that balances user preferences with aesthetic compatibility.

Method: CFALR (Collaborative Filtering-Augmented Large Language Model for Recommendation) employs three core technical components: (1) an LLM-based architecture designed for personalized outfit recommendation that describes user-outfit interactions in natural language, (2) a CF-augmented generative mechanism to efficiently explore the vast combinatorial space of outfit items, and (3) trainable projection layers that optimally fuse relational (collaborative) features with content (semantic) features, bridging the semantic space of LLMs with collaborative interaction spaces.

Result: Experiments on the Polyvore and IQON benchmark datasets show that CFALR outperforms both traditional CF-based methods and existing LLM-based methods on two tasks: personalized fill-in-the-blank and personalized outfit generation.

Conclusion: CFALR successfully bridges collaborative filtering and LLMs for personalized outfit recommendation, overcoming data sparsity and template rigidity. The framework demonstrates that CF-enhanced embeddings can effectively augment LLMs for fashion recommendation tasks, setting a new direction for integrating relational and content-based signals in recommender systems.

Abstract: Personalized outfit recommendation poses a significant challenge in e-commerce and social media platforms, requiring systems that balance user preferences with aesthetic compatibility. Collaborative filtering (CF) provides a traditional solution for this, but it struggles with data-sparse scenarios and complex user-item-outfit relationships. Meanwhile, existing template-based approaches are constrained by rigid pre-designed structures. To bridge these research gaps, we introduce CFALR (Collaborative Filtering-Augmented Large Language Model for Recommendation), a novel framework that synergizes collaborative filtering with large language models for personalized outfit recommendation. Specifically, CFALR describes user-outfit interactions in natural language and leverages LLMs to capture fashion semantics while employing CF-enhanced embeddings to bridge the semantic space and the collaborative interaction spaces. Our technical contributions include: (1) the first LLM-based architecture specifically designed for personalized outfit recommendation, (2) a CF-augmented generative mechanism that efficiently navigates the extensive combination space of outfit items, and (3) trainable projection layers that optimally integrate relational and content features. Experiments on Polyvore and IQON benchmarks demonstrate CFALR's superior performance over both traditional CF-based and LLM-based methods in personalized fill-in-the-blank and personalized outfit generation tasks.

</details>


### [305] [The Clustering Strikes Back: Building Cost-Effective and High-Performance ANNS at Scale with Helmsman](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13145)
*Yuchen Huang,Baiteng Ma,Yiping Sun,Yang Shi,Xiao Chen,Xiaocheng Zhong,Zhiyong Wang,Yao Hu,Erci Xu,Chuliang Weng*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: HELMSMAN is a clustering-based approximate nearest neighbor search system built on all-flash servers that replaces in-memory HNSW for RedNote's production services, achieving >90% hardware cost reduction through a userspace storage stack, leveling-learned pruning, and GPU-accelerated index construction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RedNote's existing in-memory graph-based ANNS (HNSW) incurs unsustainable memory footprint growth due to expanding user base and content volume, leading to prohibitive CapEx and OpEx. The goal is to migrate to a more cost-effective all-flash solution without violating strict SLAs for throughput and latency.

Method: HELMSMAN combines three key techniques: (1) an ANNS-oriented userspace storage stack that bypasses kernel I/O overheads on all-flash servers; (2) a leveling-learned pruning module that dynamically adjusts pruning strategies rather than using fixed heuristics; and (3) GPU-accelerated pipelines for fast index construction and rebuilding.

Result: HELMSMAN saves over 90% of hardware costs, supports billion-scale index (re)builds within hours, and has been running stably in production for several months. Specifically, 40 machines now handle workloads that previously required approximately 35,000 CPU cores and 0.35 PB of DRAM.

Conclusion: Clustering-based ANNS on all-flash storage is a viable, cost-effective alternative to in-memory graph-based ANNS for large-scale industrial services, provided that kernel I/O, pruning strategy, and index construction bottlenecks are systematically addressed.

Abstract: RedNote (a.k.a., Xiaohongshu, a global-scale social network platform) widely adopts approximate nearest neighbor search (ANNS) to power its search, recommendation, and advertising services. Due to the demanding Service Level Agreements (SLAs), we have to rely on in-memory graph-based ANNS (i.e., HNSW) to provide high throughput and low latency.
  However, the ever-growing user base and content volume have led to an explosive increase in memory footprint and consequently huge CapEx and OpEx. After exploring various alternatives, we find that building a clustering-based ANNS on top of all-flash servers can be promising. Yet, we still experience severe overheads from the kernel I/O stack, a fixed pruning strategy, and slow index construction.
  We present HELMSMAN, a high-performance and cost-effective clustering-based ANNS system, which combines an ANNS-oriented userspace storage stack, a leveling-learned pruning module, and GPU-accelerated pipelines of construction. HELMSMAN saves over 90% of hardware costs and enables billion-scale index (re)builds within hours. In the current production deployment, operating stably for several months, 40 machines now host ANNS workloads that previously required about 35,000 cores and 0.35 PB DRAM.

</details>


### [306] [CoDeR: Local Constraint-Compatible Retrieval Beyond Semantic Similarity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13204)
*Xingkun Yin,Xuebin Tang,Hongyang Du*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: CoDeR addresses the failure of dense retrieval models when queries contain negative constraints (e.g., exclusions, negations, antonyms) by separating topical relevance from constraint compatibility via a dual-encoder architecture plus a compatibility scorer trained with lexical-polarity contrastive supervision, eliminating the need for LLM reranking at inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional IR systems treat semantic similarity as a proxy for relevance, but for constraint-sensitive queries (negations, exclusions, antonymy), topically similar documents may violate the constraint direction — e.g., matching an attribute that should be excluded. This "constraint-violating evidence exposure" causes high-ranked irrelevant results. Prior solutions rely on expensive LLM-based reranking, which is impractical for large-scale retrieval.

Method: CoDeR uses a standard topical bi-encoder for candidate coverage and adds a separate compatibility scorer (also a bi-encoder) trained with lexical-polarity supervision over contrastive pairs of constraint-satisfying and constraint-violating evidence. At inference, the compatibility signal either rescore topical candidates or retrieves an auxiliary compatibility-oriented candidate set. No external LLM calls are needed at inference time.

Result: On three controlled diagnostic sets (antonymy, negation, exclusion), CoDeR reduces Violation@2 by 20.59, 23.53, and 5.77 points respectively compared to the strongest non-CoDeR baselines. It also improves First Violating Rank (FVR) by pushing violating documents deeper in the ranking.

Conclusion: Decoupling topical relevance from constraint compatibility via a dedicated compatibility scorer trained with contrastive lexical-polarity supervision is effective for constraint-sensitive retrieval, achieving strong performance without the inference-time cost of LLM-based reranking.

Abstract: Information retrieval systems have long treated semantic similarity as a proxy for relevance. For constraint-sensitive queries, this proxy can fail when a document is topically close to the query but supports the opposite constraint direction, such as satisfying an attribute that should be excluded or affirming a relation that should be negated. We study this failure as constraint-violating evidence exposure and propose CoDeR, a local constraint-compatible dense retrieval method that separates topical relevance from constraint compatibility. CoDeR keeps a standard topical encoder for candidate coverage and adds a compatibility scorer, implemented as a bi-encoder, trained with lexical-polarity supervision over contrastive satisfying and violating evidences. The compatibility signal can be used to rescore topical candidates or to retrieve an auxiliary compatibility-oriented candidate set, producing a ranked document list without external Large Language Model~(LLM) calls at inference time. We evaluate CoDeR on controlled diagnostics and public negative-constraint retrieval benchmarks. Across three controlled diagnostic sets targeting antonymy, negation, and exclusion, CoDeR reduces V@2 by 20.59, 23.53, and 5.77 points relative to the strongest non-CoDeR baselines, and improves FVR by pushing the first violating document deeper in the ranking.

</details>


### [307] [CQC-RAG: Robust Retrieval-Augmented Generation via Cross-Query Consistency](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13438)
*Yanjia Sun,Sifan Liu,Jie Shao*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: CQC-RAG proposes a cross-query consistency hypothesis—correct answers maintain stable confidence across syntactically diverse but semantically equivalent queries, while hallucinations fluctuate—and builds a framework that rewrites queries, constructs query-conditioned contexts, and selects answers based on confidence stability, outperforming prior multi-query methods by up to +9.12 EM on QA benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RAG systems are brittle: semantically equivalent queries phrased differently can yield divergent retrieval results, and irrelevant documents can induce hallucinations. Existing multi-path reasoning methods inject diversity via uncontrollable decoding randomness and evaluate answers through a single evidence view. The authors identify a need for principled query-level diversity and cross-query consistency evaluation to robustly filter noise-induced hallucinations without external supervision.

Method: CQC-RAG operates in three stages: (1) Query Rewriting — the original question is rewritten into multiple syntactically diverse but semantically equivalent queries; (2) Query-Conditioned Context Construction — a shared document pool is reranked per query variant to build distinct reasoning contexts; (3) Cross-Query Consistency Evaluation — an evidence-grounded protocol extracts answer-evidence pairs for each context, and answers are selected based on stability of confidence scores across queries. No external supervision or expanded retrieval coverage is required.

Result: On four open-domain QA benchmarks, CQC-RAG achieves state-of-the-art performance: +4.76 percentage points EM over the strongest multi-query baseline on TriviaQA, and +9.12 pp EM on MuSiQue (multi-hop). The method effectively filters noise-induced hallucinations through cross-query consistency signals.

Conclusion: The cross-query consistency hypothesis holds: correct answers exhibit stable confidence under syntactic perturbation, while hallucinations do not. CQC-RAG provides an effective, self-supervised mechanism for improving RAG factuality by co-designing diversity injection and consistency evaluation at the query level.

Abstract: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has become a common approach for improving the factuality of Large Language Models (LLMs), yet its reliability remains highly sensitive to how external evidence is retrieved and used. Semantically equivalent queries with different syntactic forms may lead to different retrieval results, while irrelevant or misleading documents can further induce hallucinated answers. Existing multi-path reasoning methods improve robustness by sampling multiple candidate answers and applying voting- or confidence-based selection, but they still face two limitations: diversity is often injected through uncontrollable decoding randomness, and answer evaluation is usually confined to a single query-induced evidence view. To address these limitations, we propose a Cross-Query Consistency Hypothesis: correct answers tend to maintain high confidence across semantically equivalent but syntactically diverse queries, whereas noise-induced hallucinations exhibit unstable confidence under such query variations. Based on this hypothesis, we introduce CQC-RAG, a framework that co-designs query-level diversity injection with cross-query consistency evaluation. CQC-RAG rewrites the original question into diverse but meaning-preserving queries, reranks a shared document pool to construct query-conditioned reasoning contexts, applies an evidence-grounded protocol to extract answer-evidence pairs and selects answers according to their confidence stability across these contexts. This design enables self-evaluation without external supervision and does not rely on expanded retrieval coverage. Experiments on four open-domain question answering benchmarks show that CQC-RAG outperforms the strongest previous multi-query baseline by +4.76 pp EM on TriviaQA and +9.12 pp EM on MuSiQue, validating the effectiveness of cross-query consistency for filtering noise-induced hallucinations.

</details>


### [308] [OneRetrieval: Unifying Multi-Branch E-commerce Retrieval with an Editable Generative Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13533)
*Xuxin Zhang,Ben Chen,Yue Lv,Siyuan Wang,Yupeng Li,Yufei Ma,Zihan Liang,Tong Zhao,Ying Yang,Huangyu Dai,Lingtao Mao,Zhipeng Qian,Xinyu Sun,Chenyi Lei,Wenwu Ou,Kun Gai*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: OneRetrieval is a generative retrieval framework with Keyword-Aligned Encoding (KAE) that unifies multi-branch industrial e-commerce search into a single model while preserving the real-time editability of inverted indexes — enabling new terms to be injected within hours without retraining. It matches the deep recall of the strongest generative baselines, lifts order volume in online deployment, and is the first editable generative retrieval method, currently serving hundreds of millions of PVs daily at Kuaishou.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Industrial e-commerce search at Kuaishou uses a multi-branch retrieval stage fused by hand-tuned merging, which lacks joint optimization. Generative retrieval (GR) promises unification into a single model, but the inverted-index branch persists because it is the only branch allowing new terms to be injected within hours without model updates. Existing GR methods lack this real-time editability: closed-codebook approaches fix slots at training time, and open-vocabulary methods rely on model generalization for new terms. A GR substitute must match both retrieval quality and index-level editability.

Method: The authors propose OneRetrieval with Keyword-Aligned Encoding (KAE), which ties each identifier position to an interpretable attribute word, enabling editable generative retrieval. Key components include: (1) an information-theoretic merging strategy that organizes 18 attribute categories into six codebook groups with non-uniform capacity; (2) reserved slots in each codebook that can be bound to new words post-deployment without retraining; and (3) a four-stage fine-tuning pipeline that jointly secures retrieval quality and editability.

Result: On five million real-traffic requests, OneRetrieval matches the deep recall of the strongest generative baseline, with an intervention hit rate over an order of magnitude above closed-codebook encodings. In online A/B testing, replacing the inverted-index branch alone significantly lifts order volume. Extending replacement to nearly the entire retrieval stage preserves conversion while improving CTR. The system is deployed at Kuaishou, serving hundreds of millions of daily PVs.

Conclusion: OneRetrieval demonstrates that a single generative retrieval model can simultaneously achieve competitive recall quality and real-time, index-level editability — a previously unresolved trade-off. The KAE encoding and reserved-codebook design make it the first GR method to serve as a practical drop-in replacement for the inverted-index branch in industrial-scale e-commerce search, with measurable business metric gains.

Abstract: Industrial e-commerce search serves hundreds of millions of items through a multi-branch retrieval stage fused by hand-tuned merging without joint optimization. Generative retrieval (GR) raises the prospect of collapsing this stage into a single model, yet unification is gated by more than retrieval quality: the inverted-index branch converts below the platform average yet persists because it is almost the only branch where operations can inject a new term within hours without any model update; a one-model substitute must preserve this real-time editability. Existing GR methods structurally lack it: closed-codebook methods fix each slot to a quantized embedding at training, while open-vocabulary methods leave new-term routing to model generalization. We present OneRetrieval, a one-model GR framework built on Keyword-Aligned Encoding (KAE), which ties each identifier position to an interpretable attribute word, pairing competitive recall quality with the editability of the inverted index -- to our knowledge the first editable generative retrieval method. An information-theoretic merging organizes 18 attribute categories into six codebook groups with non-uniform capacity; reserved slots in each codebook can be bound to new words after deployment without retraining; and a four-stage fine-tuning pipeline secures quality and editability jointly. On five million real-traffic requests, OneRetrieval matches the deep recall of the strongest generative baseline, with an intervention hit rate over an order of magnitude above closed-codebook encodings. Online, replacing the inverted-index branch significantly lifts order volume; extending to nearly the entire stage holds conversion while improving CTR. The system is deployed at Kuaishou, serving hundreds of millions of PVs daily.

</details>


<div id='cs.SE'></div>

# cs.SE [[Back]](#toc)

### [309] [Characterizing Tests in IoT Software: Practices, Challenges and Opportunities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12592)
*Rufeng Chen,Hengcheng Zhu,Wuqi Zhang,Zixu Zhou,Lili Wei*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: First empirical study on IoT software testing reveals that despite a considerable number of test cases, their effectiveness is limited due to complex external dependencies. Mocking shows promise as a solution to address these testing challenges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: IoT software bugs can cause severe real-world consequences (e.g., unauthorized access via buggy smart locks), yet little is known about how IoT software is tested. This gap motivates the first empirical investigation into test case practices in open-source IoT software.

Method: An empirical study analyzing open-source IoT software projects, evaluating three dimensions: (1) effectiveness of existing test cases, (2) challenges inherent in IoT software testing, and (3) usage patterns of mock objects in test suites.

Result: IoT projects contain a considerable number of tests but with limited effectiveness. Key challenges stem from managing complex interactions with external dependencies: network-reliant IoT components, file systems, operating systems, and databases. Mock object usage patterns closely align with these identified challenges.

Conclusion: Current IoT testing practices are insufficient. The alignment between mocking practices and testing challenges indicates that mocking is a promising approach to improve test coverage and address the inherent complexity of IoT software testing.

Abstract: The Internet of Things (IoT) is experiencing rapid growth. Smart devices are emerging in smart homes and industrial applications, performing mission-critical tasks. Bugs in IoT software can lead to severe consequences. For example, a buggy smart lock can allow unauthorized access to a private property. Testing is a primary practice to expose software bugs and ensure software quality. However, little is known about how IoT software is tested. To bridge this gap, we conducted the first empirical study on test cases in open-source IoT software. Specifically, we evaluated the effectiveness of test cases in IoT software, explored the challenges inherent in testing IoT software, and analyzed the usage of mock objects. Our results indicate that while IoT software often contains a considerable number of tests, their effectiveness remains limited. We identified the primary challenges in testing IoT software as managing complex interactions with various external dependencies, such as other network-reliant IoT components, file systems, operating systems, and databases. We also observed that the use of mock objects in IoT software closely aligns with our identified testing challenges. This alignment demonstrates the potential of mocking as a solution to enhance test coverage and address the complexities of IoT software testing.

</details>


### [310] [HybridCodeAuthorship: A Benchmark Dataset for Line-Level Code Authorship Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12620)
*Luke Patterson,Li Wang,Adam Faulkner*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: HybridCodeAuthorship is a new benchmark for detecting AI-generated code at line and chunk granularity within interleaved human-AI Python files. Built from open-source GitHub repositories, it challenges existing detectors, with the best achieving F1 scores of only 0.48 (chunk-level) and 0.56 (line-level), revealing room for improvement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing benchmarks for AI-generated code detection are limited to academic-style problems and assume entire snippets are either fully human- or fully AI-authored. This does not reflect real-world industry codebases where human and AI code are interleaved through AI assistant usage. A more realistic benchmark is needed for fine-grained detection.

Method: The authors introduce HybridCodeAuthorship, a benchmark of Python files with interleaved human- and AI-authored lines. The dataset is constructed via a pipeline leveraging CodeSearchNet, a collection of open-source GitHub repositories. Two state-of-the-art detection algorithms are evaluated at both line-level and chunk-level granularity.

Result: HybridCodeAuthorship proves challenging: the top-performing algorithm (AIGCode Detector) achieves only an F1 score of 0.48 at chunk-level and 0.56 at line-level detection, indicating significant difficulty in fine-grained AI code authorship identification.

Conclusion: Fine-grained detection of AI-generated code within interleaved human-AI files remains an open problem. HybridCodeAuthorship provides a realistic benchmark to drive further research, and current SOTA methods still have substantial room for improvement.

Abstract: Thanks to the rapid adoption of AI code assistants powered by large language models (LLMs), industry codebases are, increasingly, a hybrid of AI- and human-authored code. For risk management and productivity analysis purposes, it is crucial to enable fine-grained location detection of AI-generated code. To develop algorithms for this task, quality benchmarks are needed to assess performance. However, existing benchmarks tend to comprise academic, LeetCode-style problems and presume a code snippet is either completely human-authored or completely AI-authored, which is not reflective of the diverse intents and styles of industry codebases utilizing AI code assistants. To fill these gaps, we introduce HybridCodeAuthorship, a novel benchmark of Python code files with interleaved human- and AI-authored lines of code to simulate authentic utilization of AI code assistants. In this paper, we first present our dataset construction pipeline, which leverages CodeSearchNet, a massive collection of links to open sourced repositories on GitHub. We then benchmark the performance of two state-of-the-art AI-generated code detection algorithms at both the line- and chunk-level. Experimental results demonstrate that HybridCodeAuthorship is a challenging benchmark with a top-scoring algorithm, AIGCode Detector, obtaining a highest F1 score of 0.48 and 0.56 on chunk-level and line-level code detection tasks, respectively.

</details>


### [311] [Beyond Problem Solving: UOJ-Bench for Evaluating Code Generation, Hacking, and Repair in Competitive Programming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12864)
*Tingqiang Xu,Hangrui Zhou,Tianle Cai,Alex Gu,Kaifeng Lyu*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: UOJ-Bench evaluates LLMs on code generation, hacking, and repair using real-world competitive programming submissions. While strongest models fail to identify errors in >50% of incorrect submissions under one-shot evaluation, test-time scaling boosts success above 90% but at prohibitive computational cost. Frontier models can already uncover errors in 5% of full-score submissions, offering complementary signals beyond standard online judges.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs have demonstrated strong code generation capabilities in competitive programming, but their potential to support human learning — specifically, identifying errors in human-written code — remains underexplored. Traditional online judge systems rely on test case execution to flag incorrect submissions, but they do not provide explanatory feedback. This work investigates whether LLMs can fill this gap as automated code reviewers or teaching assistants.

Method: The authors introduce UOJ-Bench, a benchmark derived from real-world code submissions to the Universal Online Judge (UOJ). It comprises three tasks: (1) **Code Generation** — solving problems from scratch; (2) **Code Hacking** — identifying errors in incorrect human submissions without access to test cases; and (3) **Code Repair** — fixing identified errors. All tasks are evaluated using UOJ's native judging infrastructure. The benchmark employs both one-shot evaluation and test-time scaling (increased inference compute) to assess LLM performance.

Result: Under one-shot evaluation, even the strongest LLMs fail to identify errors in over 50% of submissions that UOJ users had flagged as incorrect. Test-time scaling improves error identification success rates to above 90%, but the computational cost is substantial, limiting practical large-scale deployment. Notably, the best-performing models under test-time scaling can uncover errors in over 5% of submissions that had received full scores across roughly 30 problems, indicating that LLMs can provide complementary correctness signals beyond standard judging systems.

Conclusion: Frontier LLMs show promise as complementary tools for code review in educational competitive programming settings, particularly under test-time scaling. However, the high computational cost currently limits practical deployment at scale. The work highlights both the potential and the efficiency challenges of using LLMs to augment traditional online judge feedback for human learning.

Abstract: Despite strong performance in competitive programming, the role of Large Language Models (LLMs) in supporting human learning in the same setting remains largely unexplored. In this work, we introduce UOJ-Bench, a benchmark designed to evaluate not only the problem-solving ability of LLMs, but also their ability to identify errors in human-written code -- a crucial educational activity traditionally supported by running test cases over online judge systems. UOJ-Bench consists of three distinct tasks: code generation, code hacking, and code repair, all constructed from real-world code submissions on the Universal Online Judge (UOJ) and evaluated through UOJ's native judging infrastructure. Our results show that under one-shot evaluation, even the strongest models fail to identify errors in more than 50% of a set of submissions that have been found to be incorrect by UOJ users. While test-time scaling improves success rates to above 90%, the substantial computational costs incurred from model inference limit its practicality for large-scale deployment. Despite these limitations, we find that the best-performing models under test-time scaling can uncover errors in over 5% of full-score submissions across roughly 30 problems, suggesting that frontier LLMs can already provide complementary signals beyond standard judging systems.

</details>


### [312] [The Rise of AI-Native Software Engineering: Implications for Practice, Education, and the Future Workforce](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12986)
*Mamdouh Alenezi*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: A systematic review of 48 peer-reviewed publications (2016–2026) on how GenAI, LLMs, and Agentic AI are transforming software engineering practice, education, and workforce. The paper identifies a five-fold growth in LLM-for-SE research post-2022, synthesizes evidence into nine themes and three trajectories, and contributes a conceptual framework (intent–collaboration–verification), a nine-dimension competency model, a four-phase curriculum roadmap, and a research agenda of eleven gaps. Productivity effects are found to be context-dependent and contradictory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative AI, LLMs, and Agentic AI represent the most disruptive transformation in software engineering history, fundamentally altering development processes, competencies, professional roles, and required educational outcomes. A comprehensive, evidence-based synthesis is needed to guide the field's response across practice, education, and workforce dimensions.

Method: Systematic review of 48 verified, influential peer-reviewed publications (2016–2026) from top venues in SE, ML, computing education, human–AI collaboration, and software productivity. Studies were discovered, screened, and analyzed using a four-agent research workflow: Literature Discovery, Scientometric Analysis, Curriculum Transformation, and Workforce Impact. All studies were verified against primary sources.

Result: Evidence synthesized along nine themes and three trajectories (practice, education, workforce). A scientometric inflection point identified: annual LLM-for-SE output grew ~5× after late 2022. Key contributions include: (i) a conceptual framework for AI-native SE (intent, collaboration, verification); (ii) a nine-dimension competency model (specification, critical evaluation, agent orchestration, metacognition, etc.); (iii) a four-phase university curriculum roadmap with AI-resilient assessment; (iv) faculty-development and workforce-transformation strategies; (v) a prioritized agenda of 11 research gaps. Productivity effects are internally contradictory and strongly context-dependent.

Conclusion: The central challenge of the AI-native era is educating engineers for judgment, verification, and orchestration — not code production alone. Benefits of AI in SE are highly context-dependent, and the evidence base remains contradictory on productivity magnitude and direction, underscoring the need for nuanced, evidence-grounded transformation strategies.

Abstract: Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI), Large Language Models (LLMs), and emerging Agentic AI constitute the most disruptive transformation in the history of software engineering (SE), reshaping development processes, required competencies, professional roles, and the educational outcomes that universities must deliver. This paper presents a systematic review of 48 verified, influential peer-reviewed publications (2016--2026) drawn from leading venues in software engineering, machine learning, computing education, human--AI collaboration, and software productivity. Studies were discovered, screened, and analyzed through a four-agent research workflow (Literature Discovery, Scientometric Analysis, Curriculum Transformation, and Workforce Impact) and were verified against primary sources. We synthesize the evidence along nine themes and three trajectories -- practice, education, and workforce -- and report a scientometric inflection in which annual LLM-for-SE output grew roughly five-fold after late 2022. From this synthesis we contribute: (i) a conceptual framework for AI-native software engineering organized around \emph{intent}, \emph{collaboration}, and \emph{verification}; (ii) a nine-dimension competency model spanning specification, critical evaluation, agent orchestration, and metacognition; (iii) a four-phase university curriculum roadmap with AI-resilient assessment; (iv) faculty-development and workforce-transformation strategies; and (v) a prioritized agenda of eleven research gaps. The evidence base is internally contradictory on the magnitude and direction of productivity effects, underscoring that benefits are strongly context-dependent and that educating engineers for judgment, verification, and orchestration -- rather than code production alone -- is the central challenge of the AI-native era.

</details>


### [313] [The End of Code Review: Coding Agents Supersede Human Inspection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13175)
*Martin Monperrus*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: The paper argues that LLM-based coding agents have reached a capability threshold where traditional human code review is no longer necessary, as agents can fulfill all goals of code review more efficiently and at lower cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the five-decade-old practice of mandatory human code review by demonstrating that coding agents can now serve every stated goal of code review — and that the hybrid model (agents write, humans review) is an unscalable dead end.

Method: The paper is structured as a position/argument paper. It advances two claims: (1) agents can achieve all goals of code review (defect detection, knowledge transfer, shared ownership, compliance, etc.) at lower cost and higher throughput; (2) the naive integration where agents author and humans review is fundamentally flawed — it provides neither meaningful assurance nor scales with AI-assisted throughput.

Result: The paper does not present empirical results but constructs a logical argument that coding agents now subsume human review capabilities, rendering the traditional human-in-the-loop mandatory review model obsolete for software quality assurance.

Conclusion: Traditional human code review should be retired as a mandatory component of the software quality pipeline; organizations should transition toward agent-driven quality assurance workflows that can scale with AI-assisted development throughput.

Abstract: Code review has been the primary quality gate in software development since Fagan formalised code inspection in 1976. For five decades, having a human examine and comment on a colleague's changes before merge has been a cornerstone practice at organisations of every size. Coding agents are large language model (LLM)-based autonomous systems capable of reading, writing, testing, and repairing software. We argue that coding agents have crossed a threshold of capability at which traditional human code review is no longer a necessary component of a software quality pipeline. Our argument rests on two claims: every stated goal of code review can be served by agents at lower cost and higher throughput; the naive integration in which agents write code and humans remain the mandatory reviewers is a dead end because it neither provides meaningful assurance nor scales with AI-assisted throughput.

</details>


### [314] [ComAct: Reframing Professional Software Manipulation via COM-as-Action Paradigm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13239)
*Jiaxin Ai,Tao Hu,Xuemeng Yang,Shu Zou,Hairong Zhang,Daocheng Fu,Yu Yang,Hongbin Zhou,Nianchen Deng,Pinlong Cai,Zhongyuan Wang,Botian Shi,Kaipeng Zhang,Licheng Wen*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: Proposes COM-as-Action, a paradigm that treats software interaction via the Component Object Model (COM) as deterministic program synthesis. Introduces ComCADBench for industrial CAD agents, showing GUI-based methods fail while COM-based execution excels. Presents ComActor, a self-correcting agent with progressive three-stage training, and ComForge for scalable Windows container training, achieving SOTA on ComCADBench with strong long-horizon generalization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing computer-use agents are limited: GUI-based agents suffer from fragile visual grounding and error accumulation over long horizons; API-based methods struggle with heterogeneous protocols and inaccessible commercial interfaces. There is a need for a unified, robust paradigm for professional software manipulation, particularly in demanding environments like industrial CAD.

Method: Identifies COM as a unified executable abstraction and proposes COM-as-Action, reframing software interaction as deterministic program synthesis. Validates via ComCADBench (first benchmark for industrial CAD agents). Develops ComActor, a self-correcting agent trained via a progressive three-stage framework, and ComForge, a scalable platform enabling large-scale training within Windows containers.

Result: Frontier proprietary models achieve near-zero success under GUI-based interaction on ComCADBench, while COM-based execution yields substantial immediate gains. ComActor achieves state-of-the-art performance on ComCADBench, demonstrates strong resilience in long-horizon tasks where baselines collapse, and generalizes to an external CAD benchmark.

Conclusion: COM-as-Action represents a significant paradigm shift for professional software agents, moving from brittle visual control to robust program synthesis. ComActor and ComForge together provide a scalable, resilient solution that bridges the gap between syntactic correctness and geometric accuracy in industrial CAD environments.

Abstract: Existing computer-use agents remain fundamentally limited in professional software manipulation: GUI-based agents suffer from fragile visual grounding and long-horizon error accumulation, while API-basedapproaches struggle with heterogeneous protocols and inaccessible commercial interfaces. In this work,we identify the Component Object Model (COM) as a unified executable abstraction, proposing COM-as-Action: a new paradigm that reframes professional software interaction as deterministic program synthesisrather than sequential visual control. To validate this paradigm in the most demanding environments, weintroduce ComCADBench, the first benchmark for agents operating real industrial CAD software. Ourexperiments reveal a substantial paradigm gap: frontier proprietary models achieve near-zero successunder GUI-based interaction, whereas COM-based execution yields substantial immediate gains. Tobridge the remaining gap between syntactic correctness and geometric accuracy, we develop ComActor, aself-correcting agent trained through a progressive three-stage framework, alongside ComForge, a scalableplatform for large-scale training in Windows containers. Extensive experiments show that ComActorachieves state-of-the-art performance on ComCADBench, with strong resilience in long-horizon taskswhere baselines collapse, and generalizes to external CAD benchmark.

</details>


### [315] [Mining Architectural Quality Under Agentic AI Adoption: A Causal Study of Java Repositories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13298)
*Oliver Aleksander Larsen,Mahyar T. Moghaddam*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether AI coding tools ("vibe coding") causally affect software architecture quality. Using a staggered difference-in-differences design on 151 Java repositories, the authors find that apparent architectural improvement (6.7% decline in architectural smell density) is actually an artifact of codebase growth (+12.8% LOC), not genuine quality enhancement—total smell counts remain unchanged.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior causal studies on AI coding tools have only examined code-level outcomes (complexity, static analysis warnings). It remains unknown whether degradation at the code level propagates to architecture-level outcomes. Additionally, the popularization of agentic AI use ("vibe coding") creates urgency for understanding systemic architectural effects.

Method: The authors mined 151 open-source Java repositories (74 with detectable agentic AI adoption, 77 propensity-matched controls) over 13-month windows, producing 1,811 monthly Arcan architectural snapshots. AI adoption was detected via configuration files and Co-Authored-By commit trailers. Causal effects were estimated using a staggered difference-in-differences design with the Borusyak imputation estimator. Robustness checks included wild cluster bootstrap, Lee bounds, and stale-observation sensitivity analysis. Both raw architectural smell counts and density-normalized metrics (ASD) were analyzed.

Result: Total architectural smell counts showed no significant change (+1.1%, p = 0.82), while lines of code grew significantly (+12.8%, p = 0.003).
