The 6.7% decline in architectural smell density (ASD, p = 0.004) was therefore identified as a denominator effect—driven entirely by codebase expansion rather than genuine architectural improvement. Pre-trends were flat (Wald p = 0.90), supporting the parallel trends assumption. Per-type estimates and all robustness checks corroborated this pattern.

Conclusion: Density-normalized outcome metrics can be misleading when treatments affect system size. Causal mining studies of AI tool adoption must report raw counts alongside normalized metrics and explicitly decompose effects to distinguish genuine quality changes from denominator artifacts. The study demonstrates that AI coding tools, at current adoption levels, do not causally improve—or degrade—architectural quality.

Abstract: AI coding tools are now used by a majority of developers, and agentic use of these tools has popularized the practice colloquially called "vibe coding". Yet causal evidence on their effect on software architecture is scarce. Prior causal work has measured code-level outcomes (complexity, static analysis warnings); whether such degradation propagates to architecture-level outcomes remains unknown. We mine 151 open-source Java repositories, 74 with detectable agentic AI adoption (identified via configuration files and Co-Authored-By commit trailers) and 77 propensity-matched controls, across a 13-month per-repository window yielding 1,811 monthly Arcan snapshots. We estimate the causal effect of adoption on architectural smell density (ASD) with a staggered difference-in-differences design and the Borusyak imputation estimator, applying a causal design recently used for code-level metrics to the architecture level. Total smell counts are essentially unchanged (+1.1%, p = 0.82) while lines of code grow +12.8% (p = 0.003); the resulting 6.7% ASD decline (p = 0.004) is therefore a denominator effect rather than an architectural improvement. Per-type estimates and robustness checks (wild cluster bootstrap, Lee bounds, stale-observation sensitivity) corroborate the pattern; pre-trends are flat (Wald p = 0.90), consistent with parallel trends. Density-normalized outcomes can mislead when treatment affects system size: raw counts and explicit decomposition are required for causal mining studies of AI tool adoption. The complete replication package, including the curated 151-repository monthly panel, is publicly available.

</details>


### [316] [Toward Instructions-as-Code: Understanding the Impact of Instruction Files on Agentic Pull Requests](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13449)
*Ali Arabat,Mohammed Sayagh*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether providing AI-agents with instruction files improves the quality of agentic pull requests (PRs). Analyzing 15,549 PRs from 148 projects, the authors find no consistent benefit: roughly equal proportions of projects saw increased vs. decreased merge rates and other performance metrics. However, projects that did improve tended to have longer, better-structured instructions, motivating the concept of "Instructions-as-Code."


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI coding agents like GitHub Copilot are increasingly used to generate pull requests, and developers are creating instruction files to guide these agents. The motivation is to empirically assess whether these instruction files actually improve agentic PR outcomes—specifically merge rate, task complexity (code churn), and merge effort (time to merge, number of comments)—to determine if this practice is effective and worth formalizing.

Method: The authors analyzed 15,549 agentic pull requests from 148 projects in the AIDev dataset. They used a before-and-after comparison design, evaluating each project's PRs prior to and following the creation of instruction files. Three dimensions were measured: (1) merge rate, (2) task complexity (code churn, number of modified files), and (3) merge effort (merge time, number of review comments). They also explored the structural properties of instruction files (length, number of sections/subsections) in relation to outcomes.

Result: Instruction files did not produce a consistent improvement. Only 27.7% of projects increased their merge rate by at least 20%, while 26.35% saw a decrease. Similar mixed results held for code churn, modified files, merge time, and comment counts. Exploratory analysis revealed that projects with improved merge rates had substantially longer instruction files that were better structured with more sections and subsections.

Conclusion: The mere presence of instruction files does not guarantee better agentic PR outcomes. The findings call for treating the creation of instruction files as a deliberate software engineering activity—termed "Instructions-as-Code"—and motivate future research into how to systematically design, structure, and maintain these instructions to reliably improve AI-agent performance.

Abstract: AI-agents (e.g., GitHub Copilot) collaborate as teammates in different software engineering tasks, including code generation proposed through pull requests (Agentic-PRs). For better agent efficiency, developers create instruction files that guide the AI-agents, including how to navigate the project, locate the right components, run tests, respect best practices, and more. In this paper, we investigate the relationship between the creation of these instructions and the performance of AI-agents in creating better pull requests, which have a higher chance of success (i.e., the merge rate), address more complex tasks (e.g., code churn), and require less effort to be merged (e.g., time to merge). To this end, we analyze 15,549 agentic PRs from 148 projects in the AIDev dataset. Using the three dimensions, we compare each project before and after the creation of the instruction files. We find that specifying instructions for AI-agents does not necessarily lead to better results. With the instruction files, 27.7\% of the projects increased their merge rate by at least 20\%, while 26.35\% decreased it. The same observation is seen with the amount of changes (e.g., code churn, number of modified files) and with the efforts to merge an agentic PR (e.g., merge time and number of comments). From a first exploration, we find that projects that managed to increase their merge rate have substantially longer instruction files, which are also well structured into a higher number of sections and sub-sections. Our results motivate the need for research to assist practitioners in framing the development of instruction files as a software engineering activity (aka, \textbf{Instructions-as-Code}).

</details>


### [317] [Understanding the Rejection of Fixes Generated by Agentic Pull Requests -- Insights from the AIDev Dataset](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13468)
*Mahmoud Abujadallah,Ali Arabat,Mohammed Sayagh*

Main category: cs.SE

TL;DR: A qualitative and quantitative study of 306 non-merged AI-generated pull requests reveals 14 rejection reasons across four categories: incorrect implementation, CI/test failures, agent inability to perform, and low priority. Findings highlight the need for better approach guidance, constraint specification, CI validation instructions, and task prioritization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 46.41% of AI-agent-proposed fixes (from Copilot, Devin, Cursor, Claude) are rejected, representing significant wasted human review effort and computational resources. Understanding why these fixes fail is essential for integrating AI agents as effective teammates.

Method: A mixed-methods approach: qualitative analysis on a representative sample of 306 non-merged PRs created or co-authored by AI agents (Copilot, Devin, Cursor, Claude), followed by quantitative analysis of rejection reasons, drawn from the AIDev dataset.

Result: 14 distinct rejection reasons were identified, grouped into four high-level categories: (1) incorrect implementation (incomplete, wrong approach), (2) CI pipeline/test failures, (3) agent inability to perform (no code generated, session loss), and (4) low-priority fixes. These findings inform actionable guidance strategies at multiple levels.

Conclusion: Improving AI-agent fix acceptance requires: (1) hinting at appropriate solution approaches, (2) outlining constraints on approaches to avoid, (3) instructing agents on CI-based self-validation to prevent breaking changes, and (4) prioritizing tasks to avoid wasting both human review effort and agent compute resources.

Abstract: AI coding agents are increasingly used to generate pull requests (PRs) that propose code fixes in software projects. From a first exploration of the AIDev dataset, we find that 46.41\% of the fixes proposed by the agents Copilot, Devin, Cursor, and Claude are rejected. This represents a significant amount of wasted resources that require human reviews, verifications, and running tests and validations for fixes that are merely discarded. Our goal in this paper is to understand the failure modes of AI-agents, an understanding that is crucial for better integrating AI-agents as efficient teammates. In this paper, we conduct a qualitative study on a representative sample of 306 non-merged pull requests created or co-authored by the agents mentioned earlier, followed by a quantitative analysis of the reasons for rejection. Our qualitative findings identify 14 reasons divided into four high-level categories for rejecting AI-agent fixes. We observe that developers can reject fixes due to fixes whose implementation is incorrect (e.g., incomplete, wrong approach), fixes that do not pass the continuous integration (CI) pipelines and fail tests, fixes for which the agent is unable to perform the implementation (e.g., no code generated, sessions lost), and fixes whose priority is low. Our results shed light on the importance of better guiding the model at these levels: (1) proposing hints about the approach to follow for fixing an issue, (2) outlining constraints or limitations regarding the approaches that should not be taken, and (3) instructing the agent on how to validate the implementation through CI pipelines and without introducing a breaking change. Our results suggest the need for good prioritization of tasks so that generated fixes do not lead to wasted human review efforts or wasted agent resources (e.g., tokens, compute, or allowed number of requests).

</details>


<div id='cs.MA'></div>

# cs.MA [[Back]](#toc)

### [318] [SAIGuard: Communication-State Simulation for Proactive Defense of LLM Multi-Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12474)
*Ruxue Shi,Yili Wang,Mengnan Du,Qinggang Zhang,Rui Miao,Yixin Liu,Xin Wang*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: SAIGuard is a proactive defense framework for LLM-based multi-agent systems that intercepts risky inter-agent messages via communication-state simulation, detecting anomalies through reconstruction deviation and sanitizing/regenerating suspicious messages before propagation, outperforming reactive defenses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MAS defenses are reactive (post-execution detection/isolation), which can cause irreversible damage and degrade collaborative utility. A proactive approach is needed to intercept threats before they propagate through the agent communication network.

Method: SAIGuard performs communication-state simulation over the MAS interaction graph, estimates the impact of incoming messages on both local agent states and the global MAS state, detects risky messages via reconstruction deviations from benign communication patterns, and sanitizes or regenerates suspicious messages before they enter the system (rather than isolating agents).

Result: Experiments across diverse topologies and attack scenarios demonstrate that SAIGuard reduces attack success rates while maintaining MAS utility, outperforming reactive defense baselines.

Conclusion: Proactive interception via communication-state simulation and message sanitization/regeneration is an effective paradigm for MAS security, avoiding the limitations of reactive agent isolation approaches.

Abstract: LLM-based multi-agent systems (MAS) solve complex tasks through inter-agent collaboration, but their communication-driven nature also allows security risks to spread across agents and trigger system-wide failures. Existing MAS defenses mainly follow a reactive paradigm after execution by detecting and isolating harmful agents, which may cause irreversible damage and degrade collaborative utility. To address this, we propose a proactive defense framework for MAS security, namely a Simulation-aware Interception Guard (SAIGuard). SAIGuard performs communication-state simulation over the MAS interaction graph, estimates the impact of incoming messages on local agent states and the global MAS state, and detects risky messages via reconstruction deviations from benign communication patterns. Instead of isolating agents, SAIGuard sanitizes or regenerates suspicious messages before it propagation into system. Experiments across diverse topologies and attack scenarios show that SAIGuard reduces attack success rates while maintaining MAS utility, outperforming reactive defenses.

</details>


### [319] [Smarter Saboteurs, Better Fixers: Scaling & Security in Linear Multi-Agent Workflows](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12709)
*Timothy McAllister,Sina Abdidizaji,Ivan Garibay,Ozlem Ozmen Garibay*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: Larger LLMs are more susceptible to faithfully executing malicious instructions in multi-agent workflows (53.7pp performance drop at 27B), but adding a lightweight terminal "Fixer" stage nearly eliminates this vulnerability (0.6pp drop), restoring parity with clean performance and demonstrating that linear multi-agent structures can be resilient when correction mechanisms are present.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As LLM-based multi-agent systems are deployed in real-world settings, understanding how adversarial attacks (e.g., prompt injection, jailbreaking) interact with model scale and collaboration structure is an urgent safety concern. Prior work attributed brittleness to linear topologies, but the role of model scale and correction mechanisms was unclear.

Method: The authors conduct experiments across two open-weight model families at multiple scales on the HumanEval benchmark. They measure the "compliance-correction symmetry": the tendency of larger models to comply with malicious instructions in uncorrected linear pipelines, and the effect of appending a lightweight terminal Fixer agent that corrects downstream outputs.

Result: In uncorrected pipelines, larger models show a 53.7 percentage-point drop in control-to-malicious performance at 27B scale. Adding a Fixer stage collapses this gap to only 0.6pp, restoring statistical parity with control-level performance. This demonstrates that linear collaboration structures are viable and resilient at scale when correction is included.

Conclusion: The brittleness previously attributed to linear multi-agent topologies may stem not from the topology itself but from the absence of correction mechanisms. A simple Fixer stage can make linear workflows resilient to adversarial compromise, even as larger models become more dangerously compliant with malicious instructions.

Abstract: As LLM-based multi-agent systems (MAS) are deployed in the wild, the resilience of their collaboration structures against adversarial compromise becomes a critical safety concern. Attackers may leverage prompt-injection or jailbreaking to sabotage individual agents within MAS workflows, but the interaction between model scaling and system-level resilience remains poorly understood. This paper investigates how model scale affects the security of linear multi-agent workflows. Our experiments across scales of two open-weight model families on the HumanEval benchmark reveal a compliance-correction symmetry: larger models are far more likely to faithfully execute malicious instructions, with the control-to-malicious performance drop reaching 53.7pp at 27B in uncorrected pipelines. However, appending a lightweight terminal Fixer stage collapses this to 0.6pp and restores statistical parity with control-level performance, demonstrating that strictly linear collaboration structures can be viable and resilient to adversaries at this scale, and suggesting that the brittleness previously attributed to linear topology may stem from a lack of correction.

</details>


### [320] [The Internet of Agentic AI: Communication, Coordination, and Collective Intelligence at Scale](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12835)
*Quanyan Zhu*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: Proposes the Internet of Agentic AI (IoAI), an open ecosystem where heterogeneous autonomous AI agents discover, negotiate, and collaborate across diverse environments. The paper synthesizes foundations from multiple disciplines to define architectures, protocols, and trust mechanisms, highlighting key challenges like semantic interoperability, secure identity, and governance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous AI agents are shifting AI from isolated inference to distributed systems of reasoning and action. There is a need for a coherent architectural vision enabling heterogeneous agents to interoperate at scale across cloud, edge, device, organizational, and cyber-physical boundaries.

Method: The paper takes a synthesis approach, integrating foundations from single-agent agentic AI, multi-agent systems, distributed computing, communication networks, game theory, and security engineering. It analyzes deployment models, workflow lifecycles, communication protocols, interoperability layers, resource management, and trust architectures, with case studies in adaptive manufacturing and distributed operational coordination.

Result: The framework identifies central research challenges: controlled emergence, semantic interoperability, secure identity, incentive-compatible coordination, resource-aware orchestration, and governance for large-scale autonomous agent networks.

Conclusion: Realizing the IoAI vision requires addressing fundamental challenges in semantic interoperability, trust, incentives, resource orchestration, and governance to enable scalable, secure, and cooperative agent ecosystems across diverse environments.

Abstract: The rapid emergence of autonomous AI agents is transforming artificial intelligence from isolated model inference into distributed systems of reasoning, communication, and action. This paper develops the vision of the Internet of Agentic AI (IoAI): an open ecosystem in which heterogeneous agents discover one another, negotiate responsibilities, exchange context, invoke tools, and execute workflows across cloud, edge, device, organizational, and cyber-physical environments. We synthesize foundations from single-agent agentic AI, multi-agent systems, distributed computing, communication networks, game theory, and security engineering to characterize the architectures and mechanisms required for scalable agent ecosystems. The paper examines agent deployment models, workflow lifecycles, communication protocols, interoperability layers, resource-management challenges, and trust architectures, with case studies in adaptive manufacturing and distributed operational coordination. The resulting framework highlights the central research challenges of controlled emergence, semantic interoperability, secure identity, incentive-compatible coordination, resource-aware orchestration, and governance for large-scale networks of autonomous agents.

</details>


### [321] [Effects of Social Interactions in Self-Organising Railway Traffic Management](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13068)
*Fabio Oddi,Federico Naldini,Leo D'Amato,Grégory Marlière,Paola Pellegrini,Vito Trianni*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: This paper studies the predictive neighbourhood horizon in self-organised railway traffic management, finding that short horizons suffice for effective coordination, while longer horizons degrade local computational responsiveness without improving global schedule optimality.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Self-organised traffic management relies on trains negotiating future traffic plans via distributed consensus. The neighbourhood horizon — how far ahead trains look to detect conflicts and determine negotiation partners — is a key structural parameter, yet its impact on the trade-off between local tractability and global schedule coherence has not been systematically investigated.

Method: A closed-loop simulation framework evaluates variations of the predictive neighbourhood horizon, measuring effects across the entire decentralised coordination pipeline: conflict detection, local sub-problem formulation, and distributed consensus dynamics.

Result: Contrary to intuition, short time horizons are sufficient for effective decentralised coordination. Long horizons compromise local tractability and computational responsiveness without yielding improvements in global schedule feasibility or optimality.

Conclusion: The neighbourhood horizon introduces a distinct trade-off, but the balance favours shorter horizons — they maintain computational efficiency and responsiveness while preserving global schedule coherence, challenging the assumption that longer look-ahead always benefits coordination.

Abstract: Recent research is exploring self-organised traffic management as a solution for scaling to complex real-world networks. In such a system, trains predict their neighbourhood, produce traffic plan hypotheses, and agree via consensus with neighbours on a future traffic plan to be implemented. This paper investigates a structural parameter within this pipeline: the predictive neighbourhood horizon. The horizon is used by trains to identify future potential conflicts with neighbours, and to establish the local interaction topology, that is, the subset of trains to negotiate with. As the primary design variable, the horizon directly determines the size and density of the social interaction graph, whereas its impact on the complexity of local sub-problems and the distributed consensus dynamics represents a trade-off to be explored. Through a closed-loop simulation framework the study evaluates how variations of the horizon impact the overall decentralised coordination process, from initial conflict detection to distributed schedule consensus. The analysis focuses on investigating the potential trade-off introduced by the horizon choice: balancing local tractability and computational responsiveness with the need for global schedule coherence and feasibility in safety-critical environments. Contrary to intuition, our empirical results indicate that the short time horizons suffice, while long values compromise local tractability and computational responsiveness with no gain in global schedule optimality.

</details>


### [322] [$α$-fair heterogeneous agent reinforcement learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13076)
*Yao-hua Franck Xu,Tayeb Lemlouma,Jean-Marie Bonnin,Arnaud Braud*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: This paper introduces α-fairness into Heterogeneous-Agent Trust Region Learning (HATRL), creating a theoretically-grounded framework that smoothly interpolates between utilitarian efficiency and equitable welfare via a fair advantage function, with two practical algorithms (α-fair HATRPO and α-fair HAPPO) that outperform baselines in sequential social dilemmas.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard multi-agent reinforcement learning optimizes for utilitarian objectives (sum of rewards), leading to inequitable "leader-follower" dynamics where some agents are exploited. Existing fairness-based methods either break the stationarity of Markov Games or lack convergence guarantees. There is a critical gap between fair objectives and theoretically safe, monotonic-improvement learning frameworks.

Method: The authors bridge α-fairness with Heterogeneous-Agent Trust Region Learning (HATRL). They define a fair advantage function that dynamically weights each agent's utility based on its expected return, parameterized by α. As α varies, the global objective transitions continuously from pure utilitarian (α=0) to max-min fairness (α→∞). This is integrated into the HATRL surrogate objective with a trust-region constraint, ensuring monotonic improvement and convergence to Nash Equilibria. Two practical algorithms are derived: α-fair HATRPO (using TRPO-style constraints) and α-fair HAPPO (using PPO-style clipping).

Result: Experiments on CleanUp and CommonHarvest (sequential social dilemmas) show that α-fair HATRPO and α-fair HAPPO outperform their standard HATRL counterparts not only in fairness/social welfare metrics but also in utilitarian (total return) measures, demonstrating that equitable cooperation can coincide with higher collective efficiency.

Conclusion: The proposed framework successfully unifies fairness and trust-region learning in heterogeneous multi-agent settings, providing both theoretical guarantees (monotonic improvement, Nash convergence) and practical gains. It closes the gap between fair objective design and safe MARL, showing that socially aware learning need not sacrifice efficiency.

Abstract: Cooperation in multi-agent systems is typically optimized through utilitarian objectives that maximize overall efficiency but fail to account for reward distribution, often resulting in inequitable "leader-follower" dynamics. While fairness-based approaches encourage pro-social behaviors where every agent benefits from cooperation, many current algorithms - including those utilizing reward shaping - break the stationarity of Markov Games or lack rigorous theoretical guarantees. This creates a critical gap between fair objective methods and theoretically safe learning frameworks. We propose a novel framework that bridges $α$-fairness with Heterogeneous-Agent Trust Region Learning (HATRL), ensuring monotonic improvement and convergence toward Nash Equilibria. Our approach leverages a fair advantage function that dynamically weights agent utilities based on their expected returns, allowing the global objective to transition from purely utilitarian efficiency to $α$-fairness welfare based on the parameter $α$. We introduce two practical algorithms, $α$-fair HATRPO and $α$-fair HAPPO, and demonstrate through experiments in sequential social dilemmas like CleanUp and CommonHarvest that they perform better than HATRL's algorithms from a utilitarian point of view while achieving socially higher outcomes.

</details>


### [323] [See What I See, Know What I Think: Dense Latent Communication Across Heterogeneous Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13594)
*Siyi Chen,Xiaoyan Zhang,Meng Wu,Jonathan Tremblay,Valts Blukis,Stan Birchfield,Rene Vidal,Alvaro Velasquez,Sijia Liu,Qing Qu*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: The paper investigates heterogeneous KV-cache communication between different LLMs, proposing a dense alignment method with a lightweight cross-model cache transformation and two-phase training. It outperforms prior baselines, matches or exceeds text communication at 2-3× lower compute in context-aware settings, and uniquely succeeds in context-unaware transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-agent LLM systems incur lossy and expensive decode/re-encode costs when communicating via text. KV-cache communication is promising but prior work is either homogeneous (same model copies) or restrictively heterogeneous (shared input, caches used only for steering). The authors tackle the fundamental question of whether heterogeneous agents can be aligned for genuine "mind reading" — transferring both what an agent sees and how it thinks — across both context-aware and context-unaware settings.

Method: An information-structure analysis reveals a duality: context-aware transfer relies on sparse reasoning signals, while context-unaware transfer demands dense contextual knowledge preservation. The proposed method uses a lightweight cross-model cache transformation with two-phase training: (1) reconstruction — aligning the transformed KV-cache to reconstruct the sender's hidden states, and (2) generation — fine-tuning the receiver to generate correct outputs using the aligned cache. Evaluated across all six directions among Qwen3-4B, 8B, and 14B models on six in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks.

Result: The dense alignment method outperforms prior heterogeneous baselines across all model pairs and benchmarks. In context-aware settings, it matches or exceeds text communication performance at approximately 2-3× lower computational cost. Critically, it remains effective in context-unaware transfer where all prior methods collapse.

Conclusion: Heterogeneous KV-cache communication is viable for genuine cross-model knowledge and reasoning transfer when approached with dense alignment. The duality between sparse reasoning signals and dense contextual preservation provides a principled framework, and the lightweight transformation with two-phase training offers a practical solution that can surpass text communication efficiency.

Abstract: Multi-agent systems communicate mostly through text, paying a lossy and expensive decode and re-encode cost. KV-cache communication is a promising alternative, yet most prior work is homogeneous, using duplicate copies of the same model, and avoids the central challenge of cross-model latent alignment; existing heterogeneous methods are also restrictive, typically assuming shared input and using transferred caches mainly for steering. We study a more fundamental question: can heterogeneous agents be aligned well enough to perform real "mind reading" and transfer both what one agent sees and how it thinks? Our information-structure analysis reveals a duality: context-aware transfer is driven by sparse reasoning signals, while context-unaware transfer, where the receiver sees no input, requires dense contextual knowledge preservation. Motivated by this, we propose dense alignment for heterogeneous KV-cache communication via a lightweight cross-model cache transformation and two-phase training: reconstruction followed by generation. Across all six directions of {Qwen3-4B, 8B, 14B} and six in-domain and out-of-domain benchmarks, our method outperforms prior heterogeneous baselines, matches or exceeds text communication in context-aware settings at roughly 2 to 3 times lower compute, and remains effective in context-unaware transfer where prior methods collapse.

</details>


### [324] [Tuning Agent-Based Predator-Prey Models Toward Lotka-Volterra Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.13639)
*Corinna Mandl,Siddharth Chaturvedi,Marcel van Gerven*

Main category: cs.MA

TL;DR: This paper addresses the challenge of parameter tuning in large-scale agent-based predator-prey models to achieve stable Lotka-Volterra-like population cycles, using neural network controllers and a feature-based loss function for optimization within a GPU-accelerated ABM framework (ABMax).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large-scale agent-based models are increasingly feasible due to growing compute power, but they suffer from sensitivity to local rules and parameters, where small changes can cause runaway behavior, population collapse, or saturation at artificial bounds. The authors aim to systematically tune environmental and demographic parameters so that agent-based predator-prey dynamics stably reproduce classical Lotka-Volterra cycles.

Method: The authors implement a continuous predator-prey system with sheep (prey) and wolves (predators) as active agents possessing local sensing, internal energy reserves, and recurrent neural network (RNN) controllers. They optimize environmental and demographic parameters using a feature-based loss function that rewards sustained oscillations, appropriate phase lag, bounded populations, and long-term persistence. Optimization is performed first with random controllers, then with evolved controllers in a more naturalistic setting. The entire model is built on ABMax, a JAX-based ABM framework enabling efficient batched simulation on GPU/TPU hardware accelerators.

Result: The paper demonstrates that systematic parameter optimization with a feature-based loss can successfully tune agent-based predator-prey models to exhibit stable, bounded Lotka-Volterra-like cycles, avoiding collapse or saturation, for both random and evolved neural network controllers.

Conclusion: Feature-based loss optimization is an effective approach for calibrating complex agent-based models toward desired macroscopic dynamics. The JAX-based ABMax framework enables the computational efficiency needed for such large-scale parameter sweeps, making it practical to align bottom-up agent rules with target emergent behaviors.

Abstract: Recent growth in compute power has made it increasingly feasible to use large-scale agent-based models to simulate complex adaptive systems. A central difficulty is that such models contain many local rules and parameters, where small changes can lead to runaway behaviour, population collapse, or saturation at artificial bounds. We study this problem in a continuous predator-prey system where sheep and wolves are active agents with local sensing, internal energy, and recurrent neural network-based controllers. We ask whether environmental and demographic parameters can be tuned so that the resulting population dynamics resemble classical Lotka-Volterra cycles. We optimise these parameters with a feature-based loss that rewards sustained oscillations, phase lag, bounded populations, and long-term persistence, first for random controllers and then for evolved controllers in a more naturalistic setting. The model is implemented in ABMax, a JAX-based agent-based modelling framework that enables efficient batched simulation on hardware accelerators.

</details>
